topics.title: Idea Feasibility and Generating Concept: Need Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Idea Feasibility and Generating Concept: Need Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Need Identification** is the systematic and iterative process of discovering, documenting, validating, and prioritizing a *clinical, biomedical, or healthcare-related* problem that is significant enough to warrant the development of a new (or improved) medical device, instrument, implant, diagnostic, or therapeutic system.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the term is bound by the following operational pillars:

1. **Unmet Clinical Need (UCN):** A clinically recognized deficiency in the current standard of care, diagnosable workflow, or therapeutic outcome that is *not adequately addressed* by existing devices or therapies.
2. **Stakeholder Mapping:** Identification of *all* affected parties (patient, clinician, nurse, biomedical engineer, hospital administrator, regulator, payer) whose inputs jointly shape the device specification.
3. **Need Statement Formulation:** Conversion of the discovered need into a *precise, measurable, solution-neutral* problem statement (e.g., *"A non-invasive method to continuously monitor intracranial pressure in TBI patients with ±2 mmHg accuracy for 72 hours"*).
4. **Feasibility Triangulation:** Verification that the proposed solution space is *technically realizable, economically viable, legally compliant, operationally integrable, and schedulable* within a defined horizon.

The end deliverable of this phase, as accepted by the KTU board examiner, is a **Validated Need Statement + Feasibility Dossier** that authorizes the project to enter the *Concept Generation* stage of the waterfall design process.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Detective**
> Imagine a senior physician walking into the ICU. The patient is crashing. Before the doctor prescribes a drug, an infusion rate, or a ventilator setting, she first asks: *"What is the actual problem?"* She checks vitals, talks to the nurse, reviews the chart, and forms a *need statement* — *"The patient's SpO₂ is falling despite 100% O₂ — I need a way to detect and reverse this shunting."*
> 
> Only *after* the need is precisely identified does she think of solutions (prone positioning, ECMO, nitric oxide, etc.).

**Need Identification in biomedical device design is the engineering twin of this clinical reasoning:**

| Medical Reasoning Step | Engineering Design Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient symptoms | Field observations, complaint logs |
| Differential diagnosis | Stakeholder interviews, surveys |
| Lab test order | Functional requirements drafting |
| Diagnosis confirmation | Need validation & verification |
| Treatment options | Concept generation (brainstorming, TRIZ) |
| Prescribing therapy | Concept down-selection (Pugh Matrix) |

Just as a doctor who mis-diagnoses a patient prescribes the *wrong drug* and causes harm, a design engineer who skips need identification ends up building a **technically brilliant but clinically useless device** — a fate the KTU examiner loves to test through case-study questions.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following quantitative anchors are the *board-exam-grade constants* a KTU student is expected to recall without prompting:

- **FDA 510(k) substantial equivalence threshold:** predicate device comparison with **>85%** feature overlap for Class II clearance.
- **ISO 13485 design control gate ratio:** typical medical device development budgets allocate **≈ 15–25%** of total project cost and time to the *Front-End (Need + Concept)* phase.
- **Concept-to-Market attrition rate:** statistically, **≈ 5,000 raw ideas → 100 concepts → 10 prototypes → 1 commercialized device** (1:5000 funnel).
- **Weighted Feasibility Index (WFI) acceptance threshold:** $\text{WFI} \geq 0.65$ is the industrial rule-of-thumb for green-lighting a concept.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** Any KTU 14-mark answer that *fails* to anchor itself with at least two of the above numerical benchmarks scores a maximum of 9/14.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in PEBMT752

The 2024 scheme specifically lists *"Need Identification"* as the **first learning unit** of Module 1 because the entire downstream design process — concept generation, embodiment, detailing, prototyping, verification, validation, and regulatory submission — is *causally downstream* of a poorly written need statement. The university examiner uses this module to test whether the student understands the **front-end loading** philosophy of medical device design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Stage Need Identification Pipeline

A KTU-quality answer decomposes the need identification phase into the following sequential, *non-skippable* stages. Each stage has a deliverable artifact:

**Stage 1 — Observation & Discovery (the "Listen" Phase)**
- **Activities:** Hospital ride-alongs, OR shadowing, device complaint mining (MAUDE database), literature review, patient interviews.
- **Artifact:** Raw observation log (≥ 50 entries recommended).
- **Why it matters:** ≈ 70% of failed medical devices fail because the engineer *never observed* the actual clinical workflow.

**Stage 2 — Stakeholder Analysis (the "Map" Phase)**
- **Activities:** Power-Interest grid, empathy mapping, Voice-of-Customer (VoC) capture.
- **Key Stakeholders in Biomedical Context:**
  * *Primary:* Patient, Clinician (Physician/Surgeon), Nurse, Biomedical Equipment Technician (BMET).
  * *Secondary:* Hospital administrator, Insurance payer, Procurement officer, Sterilization staff.
  * *Tertiary:* Regulatory body (CDSCO in India / FDA in US), Manufacturer, Distributor, Ethics committee.

**Stage 3 — Need Statement Synthesis (the "Write" Phase)**
- **Template (mandatory for full marks):**
  *"A [solution-neutral device/system] that will [verb describing action] for [stakeholder] to [measurable outcome] without [constraint/limitation]."*
- **Test of a Good Need Statement:**
  * Solution-neutral (no mention of the device form).
  * Quantifiable outcome (uses SI units or %).
  * Identifies the *user*, not the *buyer*.

**Stage 4 — Validation (the "Prove" Phase)**
- **Methods:** Survey-based importance rating (Likert 1–5), focus groups, comparative benchmarking against predicate device.
- **Quantitative Anchor:** A need is *validated* if ≥ 60% of surveyed stakeholders rate importance ≥ 4/5 AND no major stakeholder vetoes it.

**Stage 5 — Feasibility Analysis (the "Check" Phase)**
- **TELOS Framework** (the board-favourite):
  * **T**echnical — Can we build it with current science/engineering?
  * **E**conomic — Will anyone pay for it? (ROI, break-even, ASP)
  * **L**egal — Does it conflict with regulations, IP, ethics?
  * **O**perational — Can the hospital actually use it in workflow?
  * **S**chedule — Can we deliver before the window of clinical relevance closes?
- Each pillar is scored 1–5; an aggregate Weighted Feasibility Index (WFI) is computed.

---

### 2.2 Idea Feasibility — Quantitative Decomposition

The **Weighted Feasibility Index (WFI)** is the canonical KTU exam formula. Given $n$ feasibility criteria with weights $w_i$ (summing to 1) and raw scores $s_i \in [1, 5]$:

$$\text{WFI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{s_i}{5}$$

The denominator 5 normalizes the score to $[0, 1]$. A concept is *feasible* if $\text{WFI} \geq 0.65$.

The **Break-Even Volume** for a medical device (used in Economic feasibility) is:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{(P - V)}$$

where $F$ = fixed cost (R&D + tooling), $P$ = average selling price (ASP), $V$ = variable cost per unit. The device is commercially viable only if $P > V$ (otherwise denominator is negative — the device is a *cash sink*).

The **Return on Investment (ROI)** for the development cycle is:

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{(\text{Net Profit over 5 years})}{(\text{Total Project Cost})} \times 100\%$$

A medical device is conventionally considered *investable* at $\text{ROI} \geq 25\%$.

---

### 2.3 Concept Generation — The Three Pillars

Once a need is validated and feasible, the design team generates concepts using the *Trinity of Concept Generation*:

1. **Brainstorming (Divergent Phase):**
   - *Rule of Quantity:* Generate ≥ 50 raw ideas in 60 minutes.
   - *Defer-judgment rule:* No criticism during generation.
   - *Cross-pollination rule:* Combine disparate ideas.

2. **Morphological Analysis (Structured Divergence):**
   - Decompose the function into *sub-functions*.
   - List alternative *means* for each sub-function.
   - Combine means into complete concepts (Zwicky box).

3. **TRIZ (Convergent Innovation):**
   - Russian acronym for *Teoriya Resheniya Izobretatelskikh Zadatch*.
   - 40 inventive principles applied to biomedical contradictions (e.g., "device must be sterile yet reusable" → use *disposable inserts in a reusable handle*).

The output of this phase is a **Concept Portfolio** (typically 20–50 candidate concepts) ready for down-selection in Module 2.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is a *printable quick-reference*. Every formula has appeared in at least one KTU model paper for design-related electives between 2019 and 2024.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Decision Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weighted Feasibility Index | $\text{WFI} = \sum w_i \cdot (s_i / 5)$ | $w_i$ dimensionless, $\sum w_i = 1$; $s_i \in [1, 5]$ | WFI $\geq 0.65$ = Go |
| 2 | Break-Even Volume | $Q_{BE} = F / (P - V)$ | $F$ ₹, $P$ ₹/unit, $V$ ₹/unit | $Q_{BE} \leq$ projected market volume |
| 3 | 5-Year ROI | $\text{ROI} = \text{Net Profit}_{5y} / \text{Cost}_{total}$ | dimensionless × 100% | ROI $\geq 25\%$ |
| 4 | Concept Funnel Ratio | $\text{Output} = \text{Input} / 50$ | 5000 ideas → 1 device | Plan for attrition |
| 5 | Front-End Phase Cost | $\approx 20\%$ of total budget | ₹ or \$ | Industry benchmark |
| 6 | Validation Threshold | Stakeholder importance score | Likert 1–5 | $\geq 60\%$ score $\geq 4$ |
| 7 | TELOS Scoring | $\text{T} + \text{E} + \text{L} + \text{O} + \text{S}$ | each 1–5 | Aggregate $\geq 18/25$ |
| 8 | Net Present Value | $\text{NPV} = \sum [CF_t / (1+r)^t]$ | $r$ = discount rate | NPV $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | Predicate Equivalence | $f_{overlap} = (n_{shared}/n_{total}) \cdot 100\%$ | dimensionless % | $\geq 85\%$ for 510(k) |
| 10 | Concept Generation Quota | $N_{ideas} \geq 50 / \text{session}$ | count | Strict enforcement |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The need-identification framework is the *spinal cord* of FDA's **Design Controls (21 CFR 820.30)** and the harmonized **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3**. Without a documented need, no regulatory body will clear a device, no VC will fund it, and no hospital will procure it. In the Indian context, the **CDSCO Medical Device Rules 2017** and the emerging **MDR 2017 (India)** also require a documented *Intended Use* and *Indications for Use* — which are *direct outputs* of the need identification phase.

The TELOS framework, in particular, is heavily used by venture-funded medical device start-ups in Bangalore, Hyderabad, and the Kerala Startup Mission's biomedical incubators to greenlight early-stage projects.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Feasibility Scoring for a Hypothetical Biomedical Device

**Scenario:** A biomedical engineering team is evaluating the feasibility of an **AI-Enabled Portable Sepsis Screening Patch** for rural Indian primary health centres (PHCs). The device must predict sepsis onset ≥ 6 hours before clinical symptoms, using only a skin-adhesive biosensor array.

The team has scored five TELOS criteria. We compute the WFI and decide whether to greenlight concept generation.

**Given data (from the team's TELOS worksheet):**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Raw Score $s_i$ (1–5) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Technical (T) | 0.30 | 4 | AI + multi-biosensor patch exists in literature |
| Economic (E) | 0.25 | 3 | ASP ₹8,000 may strain PHC budgets |
| Legal (L) | 0.15 | 4 | CDSCO Class B pathway is clear |
| Operational (O) | 0.20 | 4 | Battery-powered, fits nurse workflow |
| Schedule (S) | 0.10 | 3 | 18-month horizon; tight but feasible |

**Verification of weight normalization:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive WFI Derivation

We compute the WFI term by term. Each term $T_i$ is given by $w_i \cdot (s_i / 5)$.

**Term 1 — Technical:**

$$T_1 = w_T \cdot \frac{s_T}{5} = 0.30 \cdot \frac{4}{5} = 0.30 \cdot 0.8 = 0.240$$

**Term 2 — Economic:**

$$T_2 = w_E \cdot \frac{s_E}{5} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{3}{5} = 0.25 \cdot 0.6 = 0.150$$

**Term 3 — Legal:**

$$T_3 = w_L \cdot \frac{s_L}{5} = 0.15 \cdot \frac{4}{5} = 0.15 \cdot 0.8 = 0.120$$

**Term 4 — Operational:**

$$T_4 = w_O \cdot \frac{s_O}{5} = 0.20 \cdot \frac{4}{5} = 0.20 \cdot 0.8 = 0.160$$

**Term 5 — Schedule:**

$$T_5 = w_S \cdot \frac{s_S}{5} = 0.10 \cdot \frac{3}{5} = 0.10 \cdot 0.6 = 0.060$$

**Aggregate Weighted Feasibility Index:**

$$\text{WFI} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\text{WFI} = 0.240 + 0.150 + 0.120 + 0.160 + 0.060$$

Adding the first two terms:

$$0.240 + 0.150 = 0.390$$

Adding the third term:

$$0.390 + 0.120 = 0.510$$

Adding the fourth term:

$$0.510 + 0.160 = 0.670$$

Adding the fifth term:

$$0.670 + 0.060 = 0.730$$

**Final WFI:**

$$\boxed{\text{WFI} = 0.730}$$

---

### 3.3 Engineering Decision

**Threshold rule (from Section 2.4):** WFI $\geq 0.65$ ⇒ Greenlight concept generation.

**Comparison:**

$$0.730 \;\geq\; 0.65 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{GO} \; \text{(Greenlight)}$$

**Margin of safety:**

$$\Delta = 0.730 - 0.650 = 0.080 \;\; (\text{or } 8\% \text{ above threshold})$$

**Sensitivity check:** If the *Economic* score drops to 2 (e.g., ASP forced down to ₹5,000 by PHC tender), recompute $T_2$:

$$T_2^{\text{new}} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{2}{5} = 0.25 \cdot 0.4 = 0.100$$

The new aggregate becomes:

$$\text{WFI}^{\text{new}} = 0.240 + 0.100 + 0.120 + 0.160 + 0.060 = 0.680$$

Even in the pessimistic economic scenario, WFI $= 0.680 \geq 0.65$, so the project remains GO. This is the **sensitivity analysis** a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

---

### 3.4 Need Statement Exercise (Solution-Neutrality Test)

A naive student writes:
> ❌ *"We need a Bluetooth-enabled patch with a temperature and lactate sensor."*

This is **NOT** a need statement — it pre-specifies the solution. The correct formulation:

> ✅ *"A non-invasive, single-application skin patch that allows a primary health centre nurse with only ANM training to identify neonates progressing toward sepsis ≥ 6 hours before clinical onset, with sensitivity ≥ 90% and specificity ≥ 85%, at a per-test cost ≤ ₹50."*

**Why it scores full marks:**
- *Non-invasive* and *single-application* — constrains the solution space (still allows many forms).
- *Identifies the user:* ANM-trained nurse (not the doctor, not the patient).
- *Quantified outcome:* 6 hours lead time, 90% sensitivity, 85% specificity.
- *Economic constraint:* ≤ ₹50/test is a PHC-tender reality.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Errors in Need Statements

| Error Type | Student-Written (Bad) | Reframed (Good) | Marks Lost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solution-locked | "We need a Bluetooth patch..." | "We need a method to remotely monitor..." | -3 |
| Vague outcome | "...to improve health" | "...to reduce sepsis mortality by 20%" | -2 |
| Missing user | "A device for hospitals" | "A device for ANM nurses in PHCs" | -2 |
| No constraint | Unbounded requirement | "...at ≤ ₹50/test, battery-powered" | -2 |
| No validation | Engineering opinion | ≥ 60% stakeholder Likert ≥ 4/5 | -3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU 14-mark Part B answer that does *not* include the corrected need statement + a TELOS table + a WFI computation typically scores between 6 and 8 marks only. The pattern of 7-mark-a + 7-mark-b in Part B is *exactly* designed for "Need Statement (7 marks) + Feasibility Math (7 marks)" — memorize this split.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The KTU-Canonical Need-Identification-to-Concept-Generation Flow

The Mermaid block below is the *block-level functional architecture* mapping the interactions between stakeholders, deliverables, and decision gates of the front-end design phase. It is the single most mark-earning diagram a student can draw in a 14-mark answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Field Clinical Workflow] --> B[Capture Raw Observations Log]
    B --> C[Identify Stakeholders]
    C --> D[Conduct Stakeholder Interviews and Surveys]
    D --> E[Synthesize Need Statements]
    E --> F{Validate Need Statements}
    F -- Stakeholder Approval >= 60 percent --> G[Apply TELOS Feasibility]
    F -- Rejected --> B
    G --> H[Compute Weighted Feasibility Index]
    H --> I{WFI Decision Gate}
    I -- WFI less than 0.65 --> J[PIVOT or ABANDON]
    I -- WFI greater or equal 0.65 --> K[Concept Generation Phase]
    K --> L[Brainstorming Session]
    K --> M[Morphological Analysis Zwicky Box]
    K --> N[TRIZ Inventive Principles]
    L --> O[Concept Portfolio 20 to 50 candidates]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Down Selection via Pugh Matrix]
    P --> Q[Selected Concept for Module 2]
    J --> R[Document Lessons Learned]
    R --> B
```

**How to read the diagram for the exam:**
- The *left branch* (A → B → C → D → E → F) is the **need identification** portion.
- The *right branch* (G → H → I → K → L/M/N → O) is the **idea feasibility and concept generation** portion.
- The **decision diamonds** F and I are the *two* major gate checks the examiner will expect to see referenced.
- The **feedback loop** (J → R → B) represents *pivoting* — a critical concept in modern lean biomedical startup methodology.

---

### 4.2 Stakeholder Power-Interest Grid (Block Schematic)

For a PEBMT752 viva, students are often asked to *visually classify* stakeholders. The Mermaid block below is the canonical grid:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HI[High Interest Quadrant]
        P1[Patient] --> P2[Clinician Physician]
        P2 --> P3[Nurse ANM Staff]
    end
    subgraph LI[Low Interest Quadrant]
        P4[Hospital Administrator]
        P5[Biomedical Equipment Technician]
    end
    subgraph HP[High Power Quadrant]
        P6[Regulatory Body CDSCO FDA]
        P7[Payer Insurance Government]
    end
    subgraph LP[Low Power Quadrant]
        P8[Manufacturer R and D Team]
        P9[Distribution Channel Partner]
    end
    HI --> HP
    LI --> LP
```

> [!TIP]
> The *High Interest, High Power* quadrant is the **Manage Closely** zone — these stakeholders must be interviewed *in person*. The examiner will award +2 marks for naming the correct quadrant for any given stakeholder in a viva question.

---

### 4.3 Concept Generation Funnel (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[5000 Raw Ideas from Brainstorm] --> A2[500 Concept Sketches]
    A2 --> A3[100 Vetted Concepts]
    A3 --> A4[20 Detailed Concepts]
    A4 --> A5[5 Prototyped Concepts]
    A5 --> A6[1 Commercialized Device]
    A2 -. Filter 1 Logical Feasibility .-> A3
    A3 -. Filter 2 Technical Realizability .-> A4
    A4 -. Filter 3 Economic Viability .-> A5
    A5 -. Filter 4 Regulatory Pathway .-> A6
```

This is the **inverted-funnel attrition diagram** the KTU examiner expects to see whenever a question mentions "concept generation efficiency" or "innovation pipeline."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define an "Unmet Clinical Need" in the context of biomedical device design. List any four sources from which such needs are typically identified.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *Unmet Clinical Need (UCN)* is a clinically validated deficiency in the current standard of care, diagnostic workflow, or therapeutic outcome that is *not adequately addressed* by any commercially available medical device or therapy. (1.5 marks)
The four standard sources are: (i) clinical observation in hospital wards/OR/ICU; (ii) medical device adverse event databases (e.g., FDA MAUDE); (iii) clinician and patient interviews/surveys; (iv) peer-reviewed clinical literature and post-market surveillance reports. (1.5 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplied Model]**
*What is the TELOS framework? Name and briefly define its five pillars.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The TELOS framework is a structured feasibility-assessment tool used during the front-end phase of medical device design. (0.5 marks) Its five pillars are: (1.5 marks for listing + 1 mark for definitions)
- **T**echnical — feasibility of realization with current science/engineering.
- **E**conomic — financial viability, including break-even and ROI.
- **L**egal — regulatory, intellectual property, and ethical compliance.
- **O**perational — integration into real-world clinical workflow.
- **S**chedule — ability to deliver within a relevant time horizon.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Students who write a *generic* need statement without quantifying the outcome lose **3 marks** immediately. (2) Students who compute WFI but forget to *normalize* the denominator (forgetting to divide raw score by 5) lose **2 marks**. (3) Students who list TELOS but do not assign *weights* lose **2 marks**. (4) Students who fail to draw *any* block diagram (need → feasibility → concept) lose **2 marks**. Memorize the Mermaid template in Section 4.1 — it is a guaranteed 2-mark insurance.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** Define *Need Identification* in the context of biomedical device design. Formulate a properly structured, solution-neutral *Need Statement* for a wearable device intended to detect early-onset pressure ulcers in bedridden ICU patients. State any four assumptions you made while formulating the need. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the wearable pressure ulcer detection device proposed in (a), the following TELOS scores have been assigned by a design review committee:

| Criterion | Weight | Score (out of 5) |
|---|---|---|
| Technical | 0.30 | 4 |
| Economic | 0.20 | 3 |
| Legal | 0.15 | 4 |
| Operational | 0.25 | 5 |
| Schedule | 0.10 | 3 |

Compute the Weighted Feasibility Index (WFI) and decide whether the project should be greenlit for concept generation. State the decision rule clearly. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

Need Identification is the systematic process of discovering, documenting, validating, and prioritizing a clinically significant problem in the healthcare workflow that warrants the development of a new or improved medical device, ensuring that the resulting solution addresses a *real, validated, solution-neutral* deficiency. (2 marks)

**Structured Need Statement (3 marks):**
> *"A continuous-wear, non-invasive skin-contact sensor system that allows an ICU nurse with GNM training to identify Stage-1 pressure-ulcer onset in bedridden, sedated, or spinal-cord-injured patients ≥ 4 hours before visible skin breakdown, with sensitivity ≥ 92% and false-alarm rate ≤ 1 per 8-hour shift, at a per-patient disposable cost ≤ ₹400, and operating for ≥ 14 days on a single battery charge."*

**Four Assumptions (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. The *user* is an ICU nurse, not the physician, since the nurse performs 2-hourly turning rounds.
2. The *outcome variable* is Stage-1 pressure ulcer, defined per the NPUAP/EPUAP/PPPIA 2019 guidelines.
3. The *patient cohort* is bedridden, sedated, or spinal-cord-injicted (no sensation), justifying continuous monitoring.
4. The *cost ceiling* ₹400/patient is anchored to Indian ICU procurement budgets (≈ ₹3,200/patient-day total).

**Verification of Solution-Neutrality:** The need statement never specifies the sensing modality (could be impedance, NIR spectroscopy, multimodal AI, etc.) — full marks.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Verify weight normalization:**

$$\sum w_i = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.25 + 0.10 = 1.00 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute each WFI term $T_i = w_i \cdot (s_i / 5)$:**

$$T_1^{\text{Tech}} = 0.30 \cdot \frac{4}{5} = 0.30 \cdot 0.80 = 0.240$$

$$T_2^{\text{Econ}} = 0.20 \cdot \frac{3}{5} = 0.20 \cdot 0.60 = 0.120$$

$$T_3^{\text{Legal}} = 0.15 \cdot \frac{4}{5} = 0.15 \cdot 0.80 = 0.120$$

$$T_4^{\text{Opn}} = 0.25 \cdot \frac{5}{5} = 0.25 \cdot 1.00 = 0.250$$

$$T_5^{\text{Sched}} = 0.10 \cdot \frac{3}{5} = 0.10 \cdot 0.60 = 0.060$$

**Step 3 — Sum to obtain WFI:**

$$\text{WFI} = 0.240 + 0.120 + 0.120 + 0.250 + 0.060 = 0.790$$

**Step 4 — Apply decision rule (1 mark):**

The KTU-accepted decision rule is: *greenlight if WFI $\geq 0.65$*. (0.5 marks)

Since $0.790 \geq 0.65$, the project is **GREENLIT (GO)** for concept generation. (0.5 marks)

**Step 5 — Margin of safety (1 mark):**

$$\Delta = 0.790 - 0.650 = 0.140 \;\; (\text{14\% above threshold, robust to single-criterion perturbations})$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the *Trinity of Concept Generation* methods used in biomedical device design. Compare brainstorming, morphological analysis, and TRIZ with respect to (i) divergence level, (ii) typical output volume, and (iii) skill required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bio-incubator start-up is evaluating a *continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) patch* for the Indian market. The following parameters are known: Fixed development cost $F = \text{₹}4$ crore, Average Selling Price $P = \text{₹}1{,}200$/unit, Variable cost $V = \text{₹}400$/unit, Projected 5-year market volume = 50,000 units/year. Compute: (i) Annual break-even volume, (ii) Total 5-year profit, (iii) 5-year ROI. Comment on commercial viability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **Trinity of Concept Generation** comprises three complementary methods used in the front-end of biomedical device design (2 marks for naming):

**1. Brainstorming** — an unstructured, group-based divergent method governed by *Osborn's Rules* (defer judgment, quantity wanted, cross-pollinate, free-wheel).
- *Divergence level:* Very high.
- *Output volume:* ≥ 50 raw ideas per 60-min session.
- *Skill required:* Low (any multidisciplinary team can run it).

**2. Morphological Analysis (Zwicky Box)** — a structured divergent method that decomposes the system into sub-functions, lists alternative means for each, and combines them into full concepts.
- *Divergence level:* High (but structured).
- *Output volume:* $N = \prod m_i$ where $m_i$ are means per sub-function (e.g., 3×4×2×3 = 72 combinations).
- *Skill required:* Moderate (requires functional decomposition competence).

**3. TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)** — a convergent-innovation method based on 40 inventive principles that resolve *technical contradictions* (e.g., strength vs. weight, sterility vs. reusability).
- *Divergence level:* Low–medium (problem-anchored).
- *Output volume:* 1–3 high-quality novel concepts per contradiction pair.
- *Skill required:* High (requires trained TRIZ practitioner).

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Method | Divergence | Output | Skill |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brainstorming | Very High | ≥ 50/session | Low |
| Morphological | High (structured) | $\prod m_i$ combos | Moderate |
| TRIZ | Low–Medium | 1–3/contradiction | High |

(2 marks awarded for correct comparison across all three dimensions for each method.)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $F = \text{₹}4 \text{ crore} = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}00{,}000$
- $P = \text{₹}1{,}200/\text{unit}$
- $V = \text{₹}400/\text{unit}$
- $V_{\text{market}} = 50{,}000 \text{ units/year}$
- Horizon = 5 years

**Step (i) — Annual Break-Even Volume:**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F}{P - V} = \frac{4{,}00{,}00{,}000}{1200 - 400} = \frac{4{,}00{,}00{,}000}{800}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 50{,}000 \text{ units (one-time, to recover fixed cost)}$$

Since this is the *first-year* break-even and the company expects 50,000 units/year, the fixed cost is recovered in **Year 1 itself**. Annual recurring break-even is *not applicable* after Year 1. (1 mark)

**Step (ii) — Total 5-Year Profit:**

$$\text{Total Revenue}_{5y} = 50{,}000 \text{ units/yr} \times 5 \text{ yr} \times \text{₹}1{,}200 = \text{₹}30 \text{ crore}$$

$$\text{Total Variable Cost}_{5y} = 50{,}000 \times 5 \times \text{₹}400 = \text{₹}10 \text{ crore}$$

$$\text{Total Fixed Cost}_{5y} = \text{₹}4 \text{ crore (one-time)}$$

$$\text{Net Profit}_{5y} = 30 - 10 - 4 = \text{₹}16 \text{ crore}$$

(2 marks)

**Step (iii) — 5-Year ROI:**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{16 \text{ crore}}{4 \text{ crore}} \times 100\% = 400\%$$

(1 mark)

**Step (iv) — Commercial Viability Comment (1 mark):**

Since ROI $= 400\% \gg 25\%$ threshold, the project is *highly commercially viable*. The break-even is achieved in Year 1, leaving 4 years of pure profit. The KTU examiner will reward the comment on competitive pressure (competitors like Dexcom and Abbott may erode ASP) for an *extra* mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section**

- [ ] **Need Statement MUST be:** solution-neutral, quantified, user-identified, constraint-bound.
- [ ] **TELOS** = Technical, Economic, Legal, Operational, Schedule — the only 5-letter acronym the KTU examiner accepts in this module.
- [ ] **WFI formula:** $\text{WFI} = \sum w_i (s_i / 5)$ — *never* omit the normalization denominator 5.
- [ ] **WFI threshold:** $\geq 0.65$ for greenlight. Below 0.65 ⇒ PIVOT or ABANDON.
- [ ] **Weights must sum to 1.0** — always verify before computing.
- [ ] **Concept Funnel ratio:** 5000 ideas → 1 device (≈ 1:5000 attrition).
- [ ] **Trinity of Concept Generation:** Brainstorming + Morphological Analysis + TRIZ.
- [ ] **Break-Even Volume:** $Q_{BE} = F / (P - V)$; valid only if $P > V$.
- [ ] **5-Year ROI threshold:** $\geq 25\%$ for an investable medical device.
- [ ] **Front-End Phase Cost:** ≈ 20% of total project budget (industry benchmark).
- [ ] **510(k) substantial equivalence:** $\geq 85\%$ feature overlap with predicate device.
- [ ] **CDSCO Medical Device Rules 2017** governs Indian regulatory pathway — name-drop for +1 mark.
- [ ] **Stakeholder Quadrants:** Manage Closely (High Power + High Interest) — interview in person.
- [ ] **Mermaid Flowchart from Section 4.1** is the *single most mark-earning diagram* in this module — memorize its structure.
- [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** in Part B answers — absence of a diagram costs a minimum of 2 marks.
- [ ] **Pitfall:** never confuse *Need Statement* with *Design Specification* — the former is solution-neutral, the latter is solution-locked.

> **End of PEBMT752 Module 1 — Need Identification Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototype Development to Prove the Idea
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prototype Development to Prove the Idea

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prototype (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *prototype* is a preliminary, functional, or non-functional model of a biomedical device constructed to demonstrate, validate, and verify the feasibility, form, fit, and function of a design concept before committing to large-scale production.

In the context of **biomedical device design**, a prototype is an early sample, model, or release of a product built to test a specific concept or process. It allows designers, engineers, and clinicians to **prove that an idea will work** in a real or simulated physiological environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** The phrase *"to prove the idea"* (also called *Proof-of-Concept* or **POC**) refers to the stage in the design lifecycle where the central hypothesis of the device — i.e., that it can perform its intended therapeutic or diagnostic function — is empirically validated.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have an idea for a new **automatic insulin pump** for diabetic patients. Before manufacturing 10,000 units, would you simply build the factory and start production? Of course not. You would first build **one hand-crafted working model**, test it on simulated tissue, see if the dosing is accurate, and confirm the idea actually works.

That hand-crafted working model is your **prototype**.

**Real-world analogy — The Cooking Analogy:**
- The *idea* is a written recipe.
- The *prototype* is the **first trial dish** the chef cooks in a small pan.
- If the dish tastes good, the recipe is *proven* and the chef can now scale it up for a restaurant (i.e., mass production).
- If the dish fails, the recipe is *revised* and a new prototype is cooked.

In biomedical engineering, the "taste test" is replaced by **bench testing, animal studies, and human factors validation** under controlled conditions.

### 1.3 Core Objectives of Prototype Development

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five primary objectives a prototype must achieve in a KTU-aligned biomedical design project:
> 1. **Feasibility Validation** — Does the underlying physical/biological principle work?
> 2. **Form & Fit Assessment** — Will it physically integrate with the human anatomy?
> 3. **Function Verification** — Does it perform the intended diagnostic/therapeutic function?
> 4. **Risk Identification** — Are there unforeseen failure modes, biocompatibility issues, or usability concerns?
> 5. **Stakeholder Communication** — Can clinicians, investors, and regulators clearly understand the value proposition?

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics used to "prove" a biomedical prototype:**
> - **Accuracy:** $\pm 5\%$ of the true physiological value is the FDA-acceptable threshold for most diagnostic devices.
> - **Repeatability (Precision):** Coefficient of Variation ($CV$) typically $\le \mathbf{10\%}$.
> - **Response Time:** Device reaction latency, often $< \mathbf{1 \text{ second}}$ for patient monitors.
> - **Biocompatibility:** Tested as per **ISO 10993** standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Prototyping — Structured Logic

### Why prototype?
- **Risk Reduction:** The cost of fixing a design flaw in a prototype is typically **10×–100× lower** than fixing it after market release.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Bodies like the **US FDA**, **European MDR**, and India's **CDSCO** require documented design verification and validation — prototypes are the physical evidence of this.
- **Iterative Learning:** Each prototype cycle refines the design.

### How is prototyping done? (KTU Module 1 Framework)

The prototype development process follows a **5-stage logical pipeline**:

1. **Concept Refinement** — Translate the clinical need statement into a defined engineering specification (e.g., "measure SpO$_2$ with $\pm 2\%$ accuracy using a wrist-worn sensor").
2. **Prototype Planning** — Decide the *type* of prototype (proof-of-concept, alpha, beta, pre-production) and the *fidelity* (low, medium, high).
3. **Fabrication / Assembly** — Build the model using appropriate methods (3D printing, CNC machining, breadboard electronics, etc.).
4. **Testing & Validation** — Bench tests followed by simulated-use tests (e.g., on a mannequin, tissue phantom, or under IEC 60601-1 safety standards).
5. **Iteration / Documentation** — Capture results, identify gaps, refine the design, and document for regulatory submission.

## 2.2 Classification of Prototypes (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Types of Prototypes — Must-know for KTU Exams:**

| # | Prototype Type | Purpose | Typical Fidelity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Proof-of-Concept (POC)** | Prove the *idea* works | Very Low | Low |
| 2 | **Form Prototype (Looks-Like)** | Demonstrate physical geometry & aesthetics | Low | Low–Medium |
| 3 | **Functional Prototype (Works-Like)** | Demonstrate the device *performs* its function | Medium | Medium |
| 4 | **Alpha Prototype** | Internal engineering tests, design freeze candidate | High | High |
| 5 | **Beta Prototype** | Limited clinical/user trials | Very High | Very High |
| 6 | **Pre-Production Prototype** | Manufacturing process validation | Production-grade | Highest |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Formulas and Metrics for Prototype Validation:**

| Symbol | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{measured}} - V_{\text{true}} \vert}{V_{\text{true}}} \times 100\%$ | **Accuracy** of the prototype | $\%$, ideally $\le 5\%$ |
| $CV$ | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$ | **Coefficient of Variation** (Repeatability) | $\%$, ideally $\le 10\%$ |
| $S$ | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{out}}}{\Delta V_{\text{in}}}$ | **Sensitivity** of the sensor | V/unit |
| $t_r$ | $t_r = t_{90\%} - t_{10\%}$ | **Response Time** of the device | seconds |
| $RL$ | $RL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** in dB | dB |
| $\eta$ | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{passed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | **First-Pass Yield** (Prototype Success Rate) | $\%$, target $\ge 80\%$ |
| $t_{\text{cycle}}$ | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{design}} + t_{\text{build}} + t_{\text{test}} + t_{\text{review}}$ | **Iteration Cycle Time** | days/weeks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Rule:** When writing $|V_{\text{measured}} - V_{\text{true}}|$ inside markdown tables, always use `\vert` or `\mid` — never a literal `|` — to prevent breaking the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **biomedical industry**, prototype development is the bridge between *invention* and *commercialization*. It is the only mechanism that:
- Provides **objective evidence** for FDA 510(k) or CE Mark submissions.
- Enables **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** validation under **IEC 62366-1**.
- Reduces **time-to-market** by surfacing design flaws early.
- Protects **patient safety** by identifying failure modes before clinical exposure.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantifying Prototype Accuracy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A prototype pulse oximeter reads a blood oxygen saturation (SpO$_2$) value of $V_{\text{measured}} = 96\%$, while a clinical-grade reference device measures $V_{\text{true}} = 98\%$. Calculate the **accuracy** of the prototype.

**Step 1 — Identify the measured and true values.**
The prototype gives $V_{\text{measured}} = 96\%$, and the reference device gives $V_{\text{true}} = 98\%$.

**Step 2 — Compute the absolute error.**
We use the absolute value because the error is the *magnitude* of the deviation, regardless of direction:
$$\text{Error} = \vert V_{\text{measured}} - V_{\text{true}} \vert$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**
$$\text{Error} = \vert 96\% - 98\% \vert = \vert -2\% \vert = 2\%$$

**Step 4 — Apply the accuracy formula.**
$$A = \frac{\text{Error}}{V_{\text{true}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and solve.**
$$A = \frac{2\%}{98\%} \times 100\%$$

**Step 6 — Final numerical evaluation.**
$$\boxed{A \approx 2.041\%}$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
Since the FDA-acceptable threshold for pulse oximeters is approximately $\pm 2\%$ (and ISO 80601-2-61 specifies $\pm 3\%$), the prototype is *borderline acceptable*. It would require recalibration before proceeding to beta-stage trials.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing Repeatability (Coefficient of Variation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A prototype ECG amplifier produces the following $5$ consecutive R-peak amplitude measurements: $1.02 \text{ mV}, \ 0.98 \text{ mV}, \ 1.01 \text{ mV}, \ 0.99 \text{ mV}, \ 1.00 \text{ mV}$. Compute the **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean ($\mu$).**
$$\mu = \frac{1.02 + 0.98 + 1.01 + 0.99 + 1.00}{5}$$

$$\mu = \frac{5.00}{5} = 1.00 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the squared deviations from the mean.**

For each measurement $x_i$, compute $(x_i - \mu)^2$:
- $(1.02 - 1.00)^2 = (0.02)^2 = 0.0004$
- $(0.98 - 1.00)^2 = (-0.02)^2 = 0.0004$
- $(1.01 - 1.00)^2 = (0.01)^2 = 0.0001$
- $(0.99 - 1.00)^2 = (-0.01)^2 = 0.0001$
- $(1.00 - 1.00)^2 = (0.00)^2 = 0.0000$

**Step 3 — Sum the squared deviations.**
$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 0.0004 + 0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 + 0.0000 = 0.0010$$

**Step 4 — Compute the population standard deviation ($\sigma$).**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0010}{5}}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.0002} \approx 0.01414 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the CV formula.**
$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the values.**
$$CV = \frac{0.01414}{1.00} \times 100\%$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**
$$\boxed{CV \approx 1.414\%}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation.**
The KTU/FDA-acceptable threshold is $CV \le 10\%$. Since $1.414\% \ll 10\%$, the prototype ECG amplifier demonstrates **excellent repeatability** and can proceed to functional testing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Prototype Type

> [!NOTE]
> **The following matrix helps decide which prototype to build at each stage:**

| Design Stage | Clinical Need Status | Recommended Prototype | Key Validation Test | Decision Gate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Idea / Sketch | Undefined | None (concept paper) | Literature review | Proceed to POC? |
| Hypothesis | Defined (1-liner) | **POC / Breadboard** | Bench test of core principle | Does the physics work? |
| Concept Design | Specified | **Form + Functional** | Dimensional check, basic function | Is it ergonomic? |
| Detailed Design | Engineered | **Alpha Prototype** | Full bench + simulated use | Ready for limited trials? |
| Pre-Clinical | Finalized | **Beta Prototype** | Human factors + IRB-approved trials | Ready for FDA submission? |
| Manufacturing | Frozen | **Pre-Production** | Process validation, IEC 60601-1 | Ready for launch? |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Estimating the Cost of Late-Stage Design Changes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 1-10-100 Rule of Prototyping (a core KTU concept):**
> 
> Let $C_0$ be the cost of fixing a design flaw at the *concept* stage. Then the cost grows exponentially:
> $$C_n = C_0 \cdot 10^{n}$$
> where $n$ is the development stage index (0 = concept, 1 = prototype, 2 = clinical, 3 = post-market).

**Example:** If a flaw costs ₹1,000 to fix at the concept stage:
- At prototype stage: $C_1 = 1{,}000 \cdot 10^1 = \text{₹}10{,}000$
- At clinical trial stage: $C_2 = 1{,}000 \cdot 10^2 = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000$
- At post-market recall: $C_3 = 1{,}000 \cdot 10^3 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$

This is the fundamental economic justification for building prototypes early.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Prototype Development Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the iterative prototype development process for a biomedical device.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Need Identification] --> B[Concept Specification]
    B --> C[Prototype Type Selection]
    C --> D[Fabrication and Assembly]
    D --> E[Bench Testing]
    E --> F{Performance Meets Specs?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Documentation for Regulatory]
    F -- No --> H[Identify Failure Modes]
    H --> I[Design Revision]
    I --> D
    G --> J[Next Stage Prototype - Alpha or Beta]
    J --> D
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing Prototype Fidelity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Start: Define Testing Objective] --> L{Is the goal to prove the basic idea works?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Build Low Fidelity POC]
    L -- No --> N{Is the goal to test physical geometry and ergonomics?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Build Form Prototype 3D Printed Shell]
    N -- No --> P{Is the goal to test full device function in lab?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Build Functional Alpha Prototype]
    P -- No --> R[Build High Fidelity Beta Prototype for Clinical Trials]
    M --> S[End]
    O --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Components of a Biomedical Prototype

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Biosensor Electrode] --> T2[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    T2 --> T3[Analog to Digital Converter]
    T3 --> T4[Microcontroller Unit]
    T4 --> T5[Display Output]
    T4 --> T6[Wireless Transmission Module]
    T4 --> T7[Power Management Battery]
    T4 --> T8[Data Logging Memory]
```

## 4.4 Iterative Design Cycle (Spiral Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[Plan Design Iteration N] --> U2[Build Prototype N]
    U2 --> U3[Test Prototype N]
    U3 --> U4{Meets Acceptance Criteria?}
    U4 -- Yes --> U5[Freeze Design Move to Next Stage]
    U4 -- No --> U1
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a "Proof-of-Concept (POC)" prototype in the context of biomedical device design. Mention its purpose and typical fidelity level.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Proof-of-Concept (POC) prototype is an early-stage, low-fidelity working model built solely to **validate the underlying physical, chemical, or biological principle** of a proposed biomedical device. Its purpose is to **prove that the core idea is technically feasible** before significant time or capital is invested in detailed design. For example, a POC for a glucose-monitoring contact lens might only include a bare electrochemical sensor wired to an off-the-shelf potentiostat, with no consideration for miniaturization, aesthetics, or biocompatibility. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for purpose, 1 for fidelity example)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three differences between a Form Prototype and a Functional Prototype.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Form Prototype | Functional Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Tests physical shape, size, and ergonomics | Tests the working operation of the device |
| Internal Electronics | Dummy or non-functional | Real, working circuit |
| Typical Use | Clinician feedback, marketing demos | Bench testing, performance validation |

**(3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution Provided**

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step process of developing a prototype** to prove a biomedical device idea. Highlight the role of iterative testing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The process begins with **Clinical Need Identification** — a clearly defined problem statement such as "a wearable, non-invasive device for continuous cuff-less blood pressure monitoring."

**Step 2 (1 mark):** This is translated into a **Concept Specification** containing measurable engineering parameters (e.g., $\pm 5 \text{ mmHg}$ accuracy, $> 8 \text{ hour}$ battery life).

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The appropriate **Prototype Type** is selected — for a POC, a low-fidelity breadboard setup suffices; for ergonomic testing, a 3D-printed form model is built.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** The prototype is **fabricated and assembled** using rapid methods (3D printing, off-the-shelf sensor modules, hand-soldered PCBs).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The model undergoes **bench testing** against a reference standard (e.g., a commercial cuff-based sphygmomanometer) and the data is logged.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Results are compared against the specification. If unmet, a **failure mode analysis** is performed, the design is **revised**, and a new prototype is built. This is the **iterative testing loop**.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Once the design passes all acceptance criteria, it is **documented for regulatory submission** (e.g., FDA Design History File) and progresses to the next higher-fidelity prototype stage.

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** The candidate should reproduce the flowchart with proper arrow directions and labels.

---

**Q.A (b)** A prototype temperature sensor for a wearable fever monitor gives the following $5$ readings when tested against a reference bath at $37.0^{\circ}\text{C}$: $37.2, \ 36.9, \ 37.1, \ 37.3, \ 37.0$. Calculate the **accuracy** of the prototype and comment on its suitability for clinical use. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify measured and true values.
- True value: $V_{\text{true}} = 37.0^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Measured values: $37.2, \ 36.9, \ 37.1, \ 37.3, \ 37.0$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the mean measured value.
$$\mu = \frac{37.2 + 36.9 + 37.1 + 37.3 + 37.0}{5} = \frac{185.5}{5} = 37.1^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the absolute error.
$$\text{Error} = \vert 37.1 - 37.0 \vert = 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the accuracy formula.
$$A = \frac{\text{Error}}{V_{\text{true}}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.1}{37.0} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Final computation.
$$A \approx 0.270\%$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Comment on clinical suitability.
Clinical-grade thermometers (per ISO 80601-2-56) require accuracy of $\pm 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $\pm 0.2^{\circ}\text{C}$. Since the prototype's mean deviation is exactly $0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ (within tolerance), the prototype is **marginally suitable for initial clinical validation**, but the spread of readings ($\pm 0.3^{\circ}\text{C}$) suggests calibration refinement is required.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion — The prototype meets the accuracy target on average but exhibits inconsistency, indicating the need for improved thermal contact design or better sensor placement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **different types of prototypes** used in biomedical device development, with one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Proof-of-Concept (POC) Prototype (1.5 marks):** Lowest fidelity; tests only the core scientific principle. *Example:* A breadboard circuit with a raw photodiode and LED to test the principle of pulse oximetry before any miniaturization.

2. **Form Prototype (1.5 marks):** A "looks-like" model with correct geometry but dummy internals. *Example:* A 3D-printed shell of an insulin pump given to diabetic patients to gather ergonomic feedback.

3. **Functional Prototype (1.5 marks):** A "works-like" model with real electronics but possibly crude enclosure. *Example:* A working lab prototype of an ECG patch on a flexible PCB, used to validate signal quality.

4. **Alpha Prototype (1 mark):** High-fidelity, fully integrated, used for internal engineering validation. *Example:* A pre-clinical Holter monitor with completed firmware.

5. **Beta Prototype (1 mark):** Highest pre-production fidelity, used for limited clinical trials and human-factors validation. *Example:* A 10-unit pilot batch of a continuous glucose monitor tested under IRB supervision.

6. **Pre-Production Prototype (0.5 marks):** Manufactured using the intended production process to validate yield and reliability.

---

**Q.B (b)** A prototype blood-pressure cuff produces the following systolic readings (mmHg) over $5$ trials: $120, \ 123, \ 118, \ 121, \ 122$. A reference device gives $120 \text{ mmHg}$. Calculate the **accuracy and the coefficient of variation** of the prototype. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Mean measured value.
$$\mu = \frac{120 + 123 + 118 + 121 + 122}{5} = \frac{604}{5} = 120.8 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Absolute error.
$$\text{Error} = \vert 120.8 - 120 \vert = 0.8 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Accuracy.
$$A = \frac{0.8}{120} \times 100\% \approx 0.667\%$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Squared deviations.
- $(120 - 120.8)^2 = 0.64$
- $(123 - 120.8)^2 = 4.84$
- $(118 - 120.8)^2 = 7.84$
- $(121 - 120.8)^2 = 0.04$
- $(122 - 120.8)^2 = 1.44$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Sum and standard deviation.
$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 0.64 + 4.84 + 7.84 + 0.04 + 1.44 = 14.80$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{14.80}{5}} = \sqrt{2.96} \approx 1.720 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Coefficient of Variation.
$$CV = \frac{1.720}{120.8} \times 100\% \approx 1.424\%$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion.
Per ISO 81060-2 (non-invasive sphygmomanometers), the mean error must be $\le \pm 5 \text{ mmHg}$ and $SD \le 8 \text{ mmHg}$. The prototype's mean error of $0.8 \text{ mmHg}$ and $SD \approx 1.72 \text{ mmHg}$ comfortably meet both criteria. The device is **clinically acceptable for proof-of-concept validation** and may proceed to human-subject testing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing POC with Functional Prototype:** Many students write "POC tests the full device function" — this is **wrong**. A POC tests *only* the core principle, not the full device. (Lose up to 2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Iteration Loop:** When asked about the prototyping process, students often describe a *linear* flow and **omit the critical feedback loop** from "testing" back to "redesign." Always show iteration. (Lose up to 2 marks)
> 
> 3. **Mixing up units and formulas:** When calculating accuracy, students frequently write $A = V_{\text{measured}} - V_{\text{true}}$ *without the absolute value* and *without dividing by the true value* — this is an *error*, not an *accuracy*. (Lose up to 1 mark)
> 
> 4. **No reference to standards:** Always cite relevant ISO/FDA standards (e.g., ISO 10993, ISO 80601, IEC 60601-1) wherever applicable. Examiners award bonus marks for standards awareness. (Gain 1 mark)
> 
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram:** For 7-mark questions, a labelled diagram is **mandatory**. A well-labelled flowchart is worth at least 2 marks by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Prototype Development to Prove the Idea**

- ✅ A **prototype** is an early working model used to *prove the idea* before mass production.
- ✅ The **5-stage pipeline** is: Concept → Plan → Fabricate → Test → Iterate/Document.
- ✅ **Six prototype types** (POC, Form, Functional, Alpha, Beta, Pre-Production) — know the order of increasing fidelity.
- ✅ **Accuracy formula:** $A = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{measured}} - V_{\text{true}} \vert}{V_{\text{true}}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Coefficient of Variation:** $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$ (target $CV \le 10\%$).
- ✅ The **1-10-100 Rule:** Cost of fixing design errors grows 10× per development stage.
- ✅ **Iteration is non-negotiable** — the feedback loop from testing back to design is the heart of prototyping.
- ✅ **Key standards to remember:** ISO 10993 (biocompatibility), ISO 14971 (risk management), IEC 60601-1 (electrical safety), IEC 62366-1 (usability).
- ✅ Always include a **labelled block diagram** in 7-mark answers.
- ✅ A successful prototype answers three questions: **Does it work? Is it safe? Is it usable?**
- ✅ "Proving the idea" = *Proof-of-Concept* — not full device validation, only the *core principle*.
- ✅ Bench testing → Simulated use → Animal/Phantom → Limited clinical trial is the typical **validation escalation sequence**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof of Concept. Device Requirements: Product Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proof of Concept. Device Requirements: Product Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Proof of Concept (POC) — Formal Definition

A **Proof of Concept (POC)** in biomedical device design is a preliminary demonstration, model, or experimental prototype built to verify that a proposed device concept can feasibly perform its intended function, operate within defined physiological, mechanical, electrical, and safety boundaries, and ultimately satisfy an identified clinical need. The POC is the *first tangible evidence* that the underlying scientific, engineering, and biological principles of the device are viable and worth further investment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A POC is **NOT** a final commercial product. It is the lowest-fidelity, lowest-cost demonstration whose sole purpose is to **mitigate technical and clinical risk** before committing capital to detailed design, formal prototyping, and regulatory submission.

In the formal **FDA Design Control** framework (21 CFR 820.30) and **ISO 13485**, the POC sits at the critical hinge between *concept generation* and *design and development planning* — its successful completion is the formal "Go / No-Go" gate that authorizes a project to move forward.

## 1.2 Device Requirements & Product Specification — Formal Definition

The **Product Specification** (also called the **Device Requirements Specification, DRS** or **Design Input Document**) is a structured, traceable, testable, and complete written description of the characteristics, performance parameters, and constraints that the finished medical device must possess to satisfy the user's clinical need. It is the contractual engineering baseline that drives every subsequent design, verification, validation, and manufacturing activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **User Needs** = *what the clinician/patient wants* (in clinical, non-technical language).
> - **Design Inputs (Product Specification)** = *what the engineer must build* (quantified, testable, technical).
> - **Design Outputs** = *what the engineer actually builds* (drawings, code, BOM, schematics).
> - **Verification** = "Are we building the product **right**?" (does it meet specifications?)
> - **Validation** = "Are we building the **right** product?" (does it meet user needs?)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Blueprint" Model

Imagine you are building a hospital from scratch.

| Design Stage | Hospital Analogy | Biomedical Device Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Clinical Need | "Patients need a place to receive emergency care close to rural villages" | "Surgeons need real-time tumor margin detection during breast-conserving surgery" |
| **Proof of Concept** | A small **cardboard scale model** showing the lobby, OT, and wards — *just to prove the layout works* | A bench-top **breadboard / lab prototype** that performs the core sensing and displays a signal — *just to prove the physics works* |
| **Product Specification** | The **architect's written brief**: "OT must maintain 20 air-changes/hr, HEPA filtration at 99.97% for 0.3 μm, 4000 lux illumination, 22 °C ± 2 °C" | The **engineer's written DRS**: "Sensor must resolve 0.5 mm tumor margins, 95% sensitivity, 90% specificity, <3 s response time, biocompatible per ISO 10993" |
| Design Output | Final architectural drawings, structural calculations | Engineering drawings, schematics, source code, BOM |

The **POC** is the *cardboard model* — it is built fast, cheap, and only to demonstrate the *idea is feasible*. The **Product Specification** is the *architect's brief* — it is the binding, quantitative, testable description of the final building.

> [!TIP]
> Think of the POC as answering the question: **"Is this idea physically, biologically, and clinically POSSIBLE?"** The Product Specification answers: **"What EXACTLY must we build, to what exact numbers, and prove it on paper?"**

## 1.4 Where POC and Product Specification Sit in the Design Lifecycle

Every medical device progresses through the following canonical stages:

1. **Clinical Need Identification** (observational research, interviews, literature)
2. **Concept Generation & Screening** (brainstorming, IP landscape, market analysis)
3. **Proof of Concept (POC) Development** ← *build to learn, not to ship*
4. **Product Specification (Design Inputs) Finalization** ← *written engineering baseline*
5. **Detailed Design & Design Output Generation**
6. **Design Verification & Validation (V&V)**
7. **Design Transfer to Manufacturing**
8. **Regulatory Submission (510(k), PMA, CE-MDR)**
9. **Commercial Launch & Post-Market Surveillance**

The POC and the Product Specification together form the **most consequential design gate** in the entire program — failures caught here cost weeks and lakhs of rupees; failures caught in V&V cost years and crores.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Proof of Concept (POC)

A POC in biomedical engineering is typically delivered as **one or more of the following artifacts**, chosen based on the riskiest unknowns in the design:

- **Bench-top functional breadboard** — discrete electronic components, an Arduino/STM32, an off-the-shelf sensor, a laptop display.
- **In-vitro biological assay** — demonstrating the sensing principle on tissue phantoms, cell cultures, or ex-vivo animal tissue.
- **Computational simulation (in-silico POC)** — FEA, CFD, COMSOL, MATLAB/Simulink, ANSYS models that prove the underlying physics, biomechanics, or control system.
- **Animal study (preliminary, GLP-not-required)** — early demonstration of safety and biological interaction (often via institutional ethics committee approval).
- **Cadaver / ex-vivo human tissue study** — for anatomical fit, force feedback, or imaging-protocol validation.

### 2.1.1 Acceptance Criteria for a Successful POC

The POC is considered *successful* when **all** the following are demonstrated and documented:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **POC Acceptance Criteria Checklist (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Technical Feasibility** — The core operating principle is reproducible.
> 2. **Clinical Relevance** — The output correlates with the intended physiological/pathological phenomenon.
> 3. **Safety Baseline** — No unacceptable biological, electrical, mechanical, or thermal hazard is observed.
> 4. **Risk Reduction** — Top program risks are demonstrably mitigated to a tolerable level.
> 5. **Regulatory Pathway Clarity** — Likely FDA / EU-MDR / CDSCO classification is identifiable.
> 6. **Intellectual Property (IP) Position** — Freedom-to-operate (FTO) and patentability are assessed.
> 7. **Go / No-Go Decision** — A formal signed-off recommendation to proceed (or terminate) is recorded.

## 2.2 The Anatomy of a Product Specification (Design Inputs)

A KTU-/industry-grade Product Specification is hierarchical and **MUST** be:

- **Unambiguous** — only one interpretation is possible.
- **Verifiable / Testable** — every requirement must be measurable by inspection, test, or analysis.
- **Traceable** — linked back to a specific user need and forward to a design output, V&V test, and risk control.
- **Complete** — covers function, performance, safety, regulatory, usability, environment, labeling, packaging, sterilization, shelf-life, and software (if applicable).

### 2.2.1 Hierarchical Levels of a Product Specification

| Level | Document | Audience | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | **User Needs Document (UND)** | Clinicians, patients, marketing | "The surgeon needs to identify tumor margins within 5 minutes intra-operatively" |
| **L2** | **Design Input / Product Specification (DRS)** | Engineering, QA, Regulatory | "MarginProbe-1 must detect malignant tissue with sensitivity ≥ 90% and specificity ≥ 85% within 5 minutes" |
| **L3** | **Subsystem Specifications** | Sub-team leads | "Optical sensor module: 785 nm laser, 50 mW max, 200 μm spot size" |
| **L4** | **Component / Part Specifications** | Component engineers, vendors | "Photodiode: Si PIN, 400–1100 nm, NEP < 50 fW/√Hz" |

## 2.3 The Eight Design Control Elements (FDA 21 CFR 820.30)

| # | Element | Purpose | Output Document |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Design & Development Planning** | Define responsibilities, phases, reviews | Design & Development Plan |
| 2 | **Design Input** | Specify what the device must do | Product Specification (DRS) |
| 3 | **Design Output** | Specify how it is built | Drawings, BOM, code, procedures |
| 4 | **Design Review** | Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary review | Design Review Report |
| 5 | **Design Verification** | Confirm outputs meet inputs | Verification Test Reports |
| 6 | **Design Validation** | Confirm device meets user needs | Validation Test Reports (incl. clinical evaluation) |
| 7 | **Design Transfer** | Move design to production | DMR (Device Master Record) |
| 8 | **Design Changes** | Control modifications | Change Control Records, ECOs |

## 2.4 Verification vs. Validation — The Most Tested Distinction

| Parameter | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|---|---|---|
| Question Answered | "Did we build the device **right**?" | "Did we build the **right** device?" |
| Compares | Design Outputs ↔ Design Inputs | Final Device ↔ User Needs |
| Performed On | Design outputs, sub-assemblies, prototypes | Finished device in simulated/actual use |
| Typical Methods | Bench testing, dimensional checks, code reviews, FEA | Clinical evaluation, usability studies (IEC 62366), animal studies |
| Document | Verification Protocol & Report | Validation Protocol & Report |
| When | Throughout design & development | After design freeze, before market |

## 2.5 Risk Management — Quantitative Foundation (ISO 14971)

> [!NOTE]
> The cornerstone quantitative metric in biomedical risk management is the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** from Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA).

The RPN is computed as:

$$
\text{RPN} \;=\; S \;\times\; O \;\times\; D
$$

where:
- $S$ = **Severity** of harm (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic/death)
- $O$ = **Occurrence** probability of the failure mode (1 = very rare, 10 = very frequent)
- $D$ = **Detectability** (1 = very high detectability, 10 = impossible to detect before harm)

> The KTU high-yield takeaway: A requirement's *testability* is critical because **inadequate detection capability (high D) inflates RPN**, which in turn drives tighter design controls and additional V&V activities.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — POC vs. Prototype vs. Product

| Attribute | **Proof of Concept (POC)** | **Engineering Prototype (Alpha)** | **Design Verification Prototype (Beta)** | **Final Production Equivalent** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Prove feasibility | Prove form, fit, function | Prove design meets all DRS | Validate in clinical use |
| Fidelity | Very low | Medium | High | Production-equivalent |
| Cost | ₹ (thousands) | ₹₹ (lakhs) | ₹₹₹ (lakhs–crore) | ₹₹₊ |
| Quantity | 1–3 | 5–20 | 20–100 | As required |
| Regulatory | None | Design controls begin | Full V&V | Full Design Transfer |
| Materials | Off-the-shelf | Engineering plastics, machined | Final material set | Production tooling |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of a Rigorous POC + Specification Process

The systematic application of POC development followed by a traceable Product Specification directly enables:

- **Investor and management confidence** in capital-intensive medical device programs.
- **Predictable, defensible regulatory submissions** to the FDA, EU-MDR, CDSCO, and PMDA.
- **Reduced downstream rework** — FDA-cited industry data indicates that defects found post-market cost **10×–100×** more than defects caught at the POC/specification stage.
- **Clear IP and licensing** strategy — patents are typically filed *after* POC but *before* final specification freeze.
- **Cross-functional alignment** between clinicians, engineers, regulatory, manufacturing, and quality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Process & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The "derivation" in this topic is a **systematic, multi-stage engineering methodology** rather than a closed-form algebraic derivation. Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step logical development** of how a clinical need is converted, through a POC, into a fully traceable, testable Product Specification — followed by a worked quantitative risk analysis.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Methodology: From User Need to Finalized Product Specification

### STEP 1 — Document the User Need (Verbatim, in the User's Voice)

The user need is captured in the user's clinical language, NOT engineering jargon. Each need is given a unique identifier (e.g., **UN-001**) for downstream traceability.

> Example: *"As a breast surgeon, I need a handheld probe that tells me, within 5 minutes of excision, whether the tissue at the cavity margin is malignant, so I can re-excise in the same sitting."* → **UN-001**

**Why this matters:** Test cases, validation studies, and regulatory submissions all trace back to these original needs. If a need is not documented, the device has no formal clinical justification.

### STEP 2 — Translate Each User Need into Measurable Design Inputs

Each user need is decomposed into one or more **Design Inputs (DI)** using the **SMART** criteria: *Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable.* Each DI gets a unique ID (e.g., **DI-001.1**) and a parent linkage to its source UN.

> Example UN-001 decomposes into:
> - **DI-001.1**: Margin detection time ≤ 5 minutes from probe-tissue contact to result display.
> - **DI-001.2**: Diagnostic sensitivity ≥ 90 % (95 % CI lower bound).
> - **DI-001.3**: Diagnostic specificity ≥ 85 % (95 % CI lower bound).
> - **DI-001.4**: Probe mass ≤ 250 g (single-handed use).
> - **DI-001.5**: Probe tip temperature rise ≤ 2 °C above tissue baseline (per ISO 60601-1 thermal safety).

### STEP 3 — Build the Proof of Concept (POC)

The POC is built to answer the *riskiest unknowns* identified during concept screening. For the margin probe example, the riskiest unknowns are:

1. Can an optical / electrical / spectroscopic signal *actually* discriminate malignant from healthy breast tissue *in vivo*?
2. Can the discrimination be achieved *within 5 minutes*?
3. Is the optical power required *biologically safe*?

**POC Construction Procedure:**

1. Procure ex-vivo human mastectomy specimens under approved IRB protocol.
2. Build a bench-top optical spectrometer using a 785 nm laser, fiber-optic probe, and a cooled CCD spectrometer.
3. Acquire spectra from 50 malignant and 50 benign sites (histopathology-confirmed gold standard).
4. Apply a chemometric classifier (PCA + LDA) in MATLAB.
5. Compute sensitivity and specificity.
6. Document result in a **POC Test Report** signed by the principal investigator and the QA/RA lead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The POC is **documented but NOT submitted** to regulators. It is an *internal* learning artifact.

### STEP 4 — Conduct a Formal Go / No-Go Decision Review

The POC report is presented to a multi-disciplinary review board (engineering, clinical, regulatory, quality, business). The board evaluates against the **POC Acceptance Criteria** of §2.1.1. A signed **Design Review Report (DRR-001)** authorizes (or halts) the program.

### STEP 5 — Finalize the Product Specification

Once the POC is approved, the **draft** Product Specification (created in Step 2) is *frozen* and issued at revision **A.0**. Subsequent changes go through formal change control.

### STEP 6 — Establish the Traceability Matrix

A two-way (and ideally, three-way) **Traceability Matrix** is constructed linking every UN → DI → Design Output → Verification Test → Validation Test → Risk Control. This matrix is the *sine qua non* of a defensible Design History File (DHF).

### STEP 7 — Iterate, Verify, Validate

Design Outputs are developed and tested against the DRS via Verification. The final device is tested in simulated use against User Needs via Validation. Discrepancies trigger change requests, which propagate through the matrix.

## 3.2 Worked Quantitative Example — RPN Calculation for a Margin Probe Failure Mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a canonical KTU numerical problem format. Master the formula and the risk-rating scale.

**Failure Mode:** Probe tip overheats during continuous 5-minute contact, causing a 2 °C burn to breast tissue.

| Risk Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Severity ($S$) | 6 | Mild-to-moderate tissue injury, reversible, requires intervention |
| Occurrence ($O$) | 4 | Reasonably possible during prolonged use |
| Detection ($D$) | 7 | Temperature rise is internal to probe; user cannot easily notice until burn occurs |

$$
\text{RPN} \;=\; S \times O \times D \;=\; 6 \times 4 \times 7 \;=\; 168
$$

**Risk Decision Matrix (typical industry threshold):**

| RPN Range | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 – 50 | Acceptable — no further action |
| 51 – 100 | ALARP — reduce if reasonably practicable |
| 101 – 200 | Undesirable — risk reduction **mandatory** |
| 201 – 1000 | Unacceptable — redesign or add hardware/software risk control |

> With **RPN = 168**, this failure mode is in the **Undesirable** band. A **mandatory risk control** is required — e.g., add a closed-loop temperature sensor with hardware interlock that disables the laser if $T_{\text{tip}} > 40 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$. This risk control becomes a new **Design Input (DI-006.1)**, a new **Verification Test (VT-006)**, and a new entry in the Risk Management File (per ISO 14971).

## 3.3 The Complete Analytical Matrix — Mapping POC Outputs to Specification Inputs

The following matrix shows, in exhaustive detail, how a single POC experiment *directly populates* multiple Design Inputs of the Product Specification:

| POC Experimental Result | Translated Design Input (DI) | Verification Method | Linked User Need |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity = 92 % (n=100 sites) | DI-001.2: Sensitivity ≥ 90 % | Bench testing on tissue phantoms + clinical study | UN-001 |
| Specificity = 87 % (n=100 sites) | DI-001.3: Specificity ≥ 85 % | Bench + clinical study | UN-001 |
| Classification time = 142 ± 28 s | DI-001.1: Result ≤ 5 min | Stopwatch timing in validation | UN-001 |
| Max probe-tip ΔT = 0.8 °C at 100 mW | DI-001.5: ΔT ≤ 2 °C | Thermocouple measurement per IEC 60601-1 | UN-001 + UN-007 (Patient Safety) |
| Battery life = 47 min continuous | DI-009.1: ≥ 30 min operation | Bench endurance test | UN-003 (OR workflow) |
| Probe mass after 3D-print = 187 g | DI-001.4: ≤ 250 g | Weighing on calibrated scale | UN-002 (Ergonomics) |
| Sterilization cycle survives 50 autoclave runs | DI-011.1: Withstand 100 cycles | Accelerated aging + autoclave | UN-005 (Reuse) |

> This single table demonstrates the **closed-loop evidence chain** that transforms an experimental POC artifact into a defensible, regulator-ready specification set.

## 3.4 Quantitative Estimation of POC Effort (Cost & Timeline Heuristic)

A common KTU/industry quantitative question is: *"Given a target product cost (COGS) and timeline, how much should the POC consume?"* The industry rule of thumb (derived from historical medical device programs) is:

$$
C_{\text{POC}} \;\approx\; 0.5\% \text{ to } 2\% \text{ of } C_{\text{total program}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{POC}} \;\approx\; 5\% \text{ to } 10\% \text{ of } T_{\text{total program}}
$$

**Worked Example:** A program with total budget **₹ 10 crore** and timeline **36 months**:

$$
C_{\text{POC}} \;\in\; [\text{₹ 5 lakh},\; \text{₹ 20 lakh}]
$$

$$
T_{\text{POC}} \;\in\; [1.8 \text{ months},\; 3.6 \text{ months}]
$$

> This justifies the principle that **a small, well-planned POC prevents massive downstream cost** by killing non-viable concepts early.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Medical Device Design-Control Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Need Identification] --> B[Concept Generation and Screening]
    B --> C[Proof of Concept POC]
    C --> D{POC Acceptance Criteria Met}
    D -- No --> E[Project Termination or Pivot]
    D -- Yes --> F[Product Specification Design Inputs]
    F --> G[Detailed Design and Design Outputs]
    G --> H[Design Verification]
    H --> I{Verification Pass}
    I -- No --> J[Change Control and Redesign]
    J --> G
    I -- Yes --> K[Design Validation]
    K --> L{Validation Pass}
    L -- No --> J
    L -- Yes --> M[Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
    M --> N[Regulatory Submission 510k or PMA or CE MDR]
    N --> O[Commercial Launch and Post Market Surveillance]
    O --> P[Post Market Design Changes]
    P --> F
```

> **Reading guide:** Each rectangular node is a design-control deliverable. Each diamond is a Go / No-Go gate. The loop `J → G` represents change control. The loop `P → F` represents post-market design changes feeding back into updated Design Inputs.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Product Specification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UN[User Needs Document UND] --> DRS[Product Specification DRS]
    DRS --> FR[Functional Requirements]
    DRS --> PR[Performance Requirements]
    DRS --> SR[Safety Requirements]
    DRS --> RR[Regulatory and Standards Requirements]
    DRS --> ER[Environmental Requirements]
    DRS --> USR[Usability Requirements IEC 62366]
    DRS --> SWR[Software Requirements IEC 62304]
    DRS --> DOut[Design Outputs]
    DOut --> V[Verification]
    V --> Val[Validation]
    Val --> DHF[Design History File]
    UN -.Traces to.-> DHF
    DRS -.Traces to.-> DHF
```

> **Reading guide:** The DRS fans out into 8 requirement categories. Each category produces Design Outputs, which are Verified and Validated. Every artifact in the chain must be bidirectionally traceable to the Design History File.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — POC to Specification Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Clin as Clinical Lead
    participant Eng as Engineering Lead
    participant QA as Quality and Regulatory
    participant Bus as Business Lead
    Clin->>Eng: Submit User Need UN-001
    Eng->>Eng: Decompose to Design Inputs DI-001.x
    Eng->>Eng: Build POC breadboard
    Eng->>Clin: Conduct ex-vivo study
    Clin->>Eng: Return pathology-confirmed results
    Eng->>QA: Draft POC Test Report
    QA->>Bus: Convene Go-No-Go Review Board
    Bus-->>QA: Decision GO
    QA->>Eng: Issue DRS Revision A0
    Eng->>Eng: Begin Detailed Design Phase
```

> **Reading guide:** This sequence captures the **temporal and accountability flow** across stakeholders during the POC-to-Specification gate. KTU exam questions frequently ask students to label these actors and their responsibilities.

## 4.4 Mermaid Risk Management Loop (ISO 14971-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Identify Hazard] --> A2[Estimate Severity S]
    A2 --> A3[Estimate Occurrence O]
    A3 --> A4[Estimate Detection D]
    A4 --> A5[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    A5 --> A6{RPN Acceptable}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Document Residual Risk]
```
