```mermaid
    A6 -- No --> A8[Implement Risk Control]
    A8 --> A9[Re-evaluate RPN]
    A9 --> A5
    A7 --> A10[Risk Management Report]
```

> **Reading guide:** This iterative loop is the heart of ISO 14971. Every Design Input arising from a risk control enters the DRS and the Traceability Matrix, and is verified in the V&V phase.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Proof of Concept (POC) in the context of medical device design. List any four key acceptance criteria for a successful POC. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Proof of Concept (POC)** in medical device design is a low-fidelity, low-cost preliminary demonstration — physical, computational, or biological — built to verify that a proposed device's underlying technical, biological, and clinical principles are **feasible** before committing significant capital to detailed design and regulatory activities.

**Four key acceptance criteria for a successful POC:**

1. **Technical Feasibility:** The core operating principle (e.g., optical, electrical, mechanical, biological) is demonstrated as reproducible.
2. **Clinical Relevance:** The device's output correlates with the intended physiological or pathological phenomenon.
3. **Safety Baseline:** No unacceptable hazard (electrical, thermal, biological, mechanical) is observed.
4. **Formal Go / No-Go Decision:** A signed, multi-disciplinary design review authorizes the program to proceed to formal specification and detailed design.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the **first mark** for a clear one-sentence definition, and the **remaining two marks** for correctly listing acceptance criteria with brief, accurate justifications.

### **Q2. Distinguish between *Verification* and *Validation* of a medical device. Give one example of each. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|---|---|---|
| Question | "Did we build the device **right**?" | "Did we build the **right** device?" |
| Compares | Design Outputs ↔ Design Inputs | Final Device ↔ User Needs / Intended Use |
| **Example** | Bench-testing a temperature sensor to confirm it reads 37.0 °C ± 0.2 °C as per the DRS | Conducting a clinical evaluation in 30 patients to confirm the device correctly identifies tumor margins in actual surgical use |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Describe in detail the hierarchical structure of a **Product Specification** for a medical device. Explain the relationship between User Needs, Design Inputs, and Design Outputs, with a suitable example of a wearable pulse oximeter. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Design Controls** as mandated by the FDA Quality System Regulation (21 CFR 820.30) and ISO 13485. Discuss any four design-control elements in detail, with their input-output artefacts. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

The **Product Specification** (or **Device Requirements Specification, DRS**) is a hierarchical, traceable, and testable document that fully specifies what a medical device must be, do, and not do. It is structured into the following levels:

**Level 1 — User Needs Document (UND):** Captures the clinical, workflow, and patient needs in the **stakeholder's own language**, free of engineering jargon. Each need has a unique identifier (e.g., **UN-001**).

> Example for a wearable pulse oximeter:
> - **UN-001:** "As a chronic-COPD patient, I need to continuously monitor my blood oxygen saturation at home so that I can seek help before desaturation worsens."
> - **UN-002:** "As a clinician, I need the recorded SpO₂ data to be automatically transmitted to my dashboard within 5 minutes."
> - **UN-003:** "As a user, I need the device to be comfortable to wear for at least 24 hours continuously."

**Level 2 — Design Inputs (the Product Specification itself):** Each user need is decomposed into **SMART** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable) requirements. Each is uniquely identified (e.g., **DI-001.1**).

> Example decompositions of UN-001:
> - **DI-001.1:** SpO₂ measurement range 70 % – 100 %, accuracy ± 2 % (per ISO 80601-2-61).
> - **DI-001.2:** Continuous monitoring duration ≥ 24 hours on a single charge.
> - **DI-001.3:** Wireless transmission latency ≤ 5 minutes to clinician dashboard.
> - **DI-001.4:** Device mass ≤ 30 g.
> - **DI-001.5:** Biocompatibility of skin-contact materials per **ISO 10993-1**.
> - **DI-001.6:** Operating temperature 10 °C – 40 °C (per IEC 60601-1 environmental).

**Level 3 — Subsystem Specifications:** Each top-level DI is broken down into sub-system-level requirements (optical, electrical, firmware, mechanical, etc.).

> Example: DI-001.1 cascades into:
> - **DI-001.1.1:** Red LED wavelength 660 ± 5 nm; Infrared LED 940 ± 5 nm.
> - **DI-001.1.2:** Photodetector responsivity ≥ 0.4 A/W at 660 nm.
> - **DI-001.1.3:** Signal-to-noise ratio ≥ 40 dB at finger-tip.

**Level 4 — Component Specifications:** The lowest level — discrete component datasheet-level requirements for procurement (e.g., specific photodiode part number, microcontroller, battery cell).

**Relationship between User Needs, Design Inputs, and Design Outputs:**

The flow is **User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs**, each step being formally verified.

- **User Needs** define **what** the device must accomplish clinically.
- **Design Inputs** define **how well** it must accomplish it (quantified, testable).
- **Design Outputs** are the **realised artefacts**: schematics, PCB layouts, firmware source code, mechanical drawings, BOM, sterilization procedure, labeling, and packaging.

For the pulse oximeter, the **Design Output** corresponding to **DI-001.1** is the actual optical bench (LEDs, photodiode, finger housing) and the firmware that computes the SpO₂ ratio. The **Verification** test then proves the output meets the input; the **Validation** test proves the final integrated device meets **UN-001** in actual use.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Design Controls** are the systematic, documented set of practices mandated by the **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** (US) and **ISO 13485:2016** (international) that medical device manufacturers **must** apply during the design and development process to ensure that the resulting device is safe, effective, and meets user needs.

The **eight elements** of design controls (per 21 CFR 820.30) are:

1. **Design and Development Planning** — A formal plan that defines phases, deliverables, responsibilities, resources, and review schedules. *Output:* Design and Development Plan.

2. **Design Input** — The Product Specification, capturing functional, performance, safety, regulatory, usability, environmental, and software requirements. *Output:* Approved Design Input Document (DRS).

3. **Design Output** — The actual physical and procedural deliverables: drawings, BOM, schematics, source code, manufacturing procedures, labeling, packaging, sterilization instructions. *Output:* Design Output package, indexed in the **Device Master Record (DMR)**.

4. **Design Review** — Formal, documented, multi-disciplinary reviews at planned intervals. The reviewers must be independent of the designers where practicable. *Output:* Signed Design Review Report (e.g., DRR-001, DRR-002).

5. **Design Verification** — Confirmation that the Design Outputs meet the Design Inputs. Methods include bench tests, dimensional inspection, code review, FEA, and accelerated aging. *Output:* Verification Protocols and Reports.

6. **Design Validation** — Confirmation that the device meets the User Needs and Intended Use in simulated or actual use. For higher-risk devices this includes clinical evaluation, usability testing per **IEC 62366-1**, and software validation per **IEC 62304**. *Output:* Validation Protocols and Reports.

7. **Design Transfer** — Translation of the design into production-scale manufacturing specifications, procedures, and quality controls. *Output:* Updated DMR and transfer protocols.

8. **Design Changes** — All post-design-transfer modifications are controlled, reviewed, verified, validated (as applicable), and approved before implementation. *Output:* Engineering Change Orders (ECOs) and Design Change Records.

The **Design History File (DHF)** is the cumulative compilation of all design-control records for the device — it is the FDA's primary audit document during **QSIT** (Quality System Inspection Technique) inspections.

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **sequential process** of developing a Proof of Concept and converting its results into a finalized Product Specification for a **continuous glucose monitor (CGM)**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for the following failure modes of an infusion pump, and recommend a risk-control measure for each mode whose RPN exceeds 100. **(7 Marks)**
>
> | Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | FM-1: Air-in-line not detected | 8 | 5 | 3 |
> | FM-2: Occlusion alarm delay > 5 min | 7 | 6 | 5 |
> | FM-3: Battery dies mid-infusion | 6 | 4 | 6 |
> | FM-4: Software lock-up on power-on | 9 | 3 | 4 |

#### Model Solution — (a) — Flowchart (described in text for handwritten reproduction)

The sequential process for the CGM is:

1. **Clinical Need Identification:** UN-001 — "Diabetic patients need 24-hour continuous interstitial glucose monitoring with alerts for hypoglycaemia."
2. **Concept Generation & Screening:** Screen enzymatic (GOx-based), optical, and microneedle-based concepts against criteria (accuracy, calibration frequency, wear-time, cost).
3. **POC Construction:** Build a bench-top GOx-electrode sensor on a wearable patch; test on diabetic and non-diabetic volunteers (IRB-approved) for 6 hours.
4. **POC Testing & Data Analysis:** Measure MARD (Mean Absolute Relative Difference) against a reference YSI analyser.
5. **POC Acceptance Review:** Go / No-Go board reviews against criteria in §2.1.1.
6. **Design Input Finalization:** Convert UN-001 to SMART requirements — e.g., DI-001.1: MARD ≤ 10 %; DI-001.2: Wear-time ≥ 14 days; DI-001.3: Hypo-alert threshold 70 mg/dL with 90 % detection rate.
7. **Issue DRS Rev A.0** and establish the Traceability Matrix.

#### Model Solution — (b) — RPN Computations

Recall the RPN formula:

$$
\text{RPN} \;=\; S \;\times\; O \;\times\; D
$$

| Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN | Action Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FM-1 | 8 | 5 | 3 | **120** | **Risk Control Mandatory** |
| FM-2 | 7 | 6 | 5 | **210** | **Risk Control Mandatory — Redesign Likely** |
| FM-3 | 6 | 4 | 6 | **144** | **Risk Control Mandatory** |
| FM-4 | 9 | 3 | 4 | **108** | **Risk Control Mandatory** |

> All four modes exceed 100. Recommended risk controls:

- **FM-1 (RPN = 120):** Add a redundant ultrasonic air-in-line sensor with a hardware interlock that halts infusion if air bolus > 0.1 mL detected. Add a daily self-test.
- **FM-2 (RPN = 210):** Replace software-only occlusion detection with a dedicated force sensor on the distal line; reduce alarm latency to < 30 s.
- **FM-3 (RPN = 144):** Add a low-battery pre-alert at 30 % charge, a final alert at 5 %, and an audible "impending shutdown" chirp. Add battery health monitoring via periodic impedance check.
- **FM-4 (RPN = 108):** Add a hardware watchdog timer that auto-resets the MCU; add a power-on self-test (POST) sequence that disables the pump if any subsystem fails.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Writing only the final RPN value without showing the multiplication step** → Examiner cannot award partial marks if arithmetic is wrong. Always write: $S \times O \times D = 8 \times 5 \times 3 = 120$.
> 2. **Confusing the D scale:** Many students treat "Detection = 1" as "easy to detect." Correct interpretation: **D = 1 means the failure is *easily* detected (LOW risk), D = 10 means the failure is *impossible* to detect (HIGH risk)**.
> 3. **Forgetting to recommend a risk control for every RPN > threshold**, even if the failure seems obvious.
> 4. **Mixing up User Needs and Design Inputs** in long answers — User Needs are *clinical*, Design Inputs are *quantified and testable*.
> 5. **Skipping the Traceability Matrix concept** when asked about Design Controls — it is the most heavily weighted sub-element.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 High-Yield Items for KTU 2024 Module 1 — POC & Product Specification:**

- [ ] **POC = low-fidelity demonstration of feasibility**, NOT a final product.
- [ ] **Product Specification = the engineering baseline (Design Inputs)** that drives all downstream design, V&V, and regulatory activity.
- [ ] **User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs → Verification → Validation** is the *canonical waterfall* — know each arrow cold.
- [ ] **Verification = "Built it right?"** (outputs vs. inputs). **Validation = "Built the right thing?"** (device vs. user needs).
- [ ] **8 Design Control elements** per 21 CFR 820.30: Plan, Input, Output, Review, Verification, Validation, Transfer, Changes.
- [ ] **RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detection** (note: high D = bad).
- [ ] **Acceptable RPN thresholds** are company-defined; typical bands are ≤50 Acceptable, 51–100 ALARP, 101–200 Undesirable, >200 Unacceptable.
- [ ] **SMART requirements** — every Design Input must be Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Testable.
- [ ] **Hierarchy:** UND → DRS → Subsystem Specs → Component Specs.
- [ ] **Traceability Matrix** links every UN to its DI, Design Output, Verification, Validation, and Risk Control — the cornerstone of the **Design History File (DHF)**.
- [ ] **POC budget ≈ 0.5 % – 2 % of total program cost**, **POC timeline ≈ 5 % – 10 % of total program duration**.
- [ ] **Standards to know by name:** ISO 14971 (Risk Management), IEC 60601-1 (General Electrical Safety), IEC 62366-1 (Usability), IEC 62304 (Software Lifecycle), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility), ISO 13485 (QMS).
- [ ] **POC acceptance criteria** include technical feasibility, clinical relevance, safety baseline, risk reduction, regulatory clarity, IP position, and formal Go/No-Go sign-off.
- [ ] **Cost of late-stage defect** ≈ 10×–100× the cost of catching it at the POC/specification stage.
- [ ] **POC artefacts** can be: bench-top breadboard, in-vitro assay, in-silico simulation, animal study, or ex-vivo tissue study — choose based on the riskiest unknowns.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Specification Review

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of biomedical device design (as prescribed by **ISO 13485:2016** and **FDA 21 CFR Part 820**), a **Specification Review** is a systematic, documented evaluation of the complete set of design input and design output requirements for a medical device, conducted to ensure that the specifications are:
- Complete
- Unambiguous
- Verifiable
- Traceable
- Aligned with user needs, intended use, and applicable regulatory standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEBMT752 — Module 1):**
> *Specification Review is the structured process of critically examining, validating, and documenting the design specifications (functional, performance, safety, regulatory, and user requirements) of a biomedical device before progressing through the design control phases, ensuring traceability between user needs → design inputs → design outputs → verification & validation.*

A **design specification** is a document or set of documents that precisely describes what a device must do, how well it must perform, and under what conditions it must operate. The *review* is the act of formally scrutinizing these documents by a cross-functional team.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Building a Custom House"**
> Imagine you commission an architect to build a house. Before construction begins, you sit together and review the **blueprint specifications**:
> *How many rooms? What is the maximum budget? What is the seismic safety grade? Are the electrical points child-safe? Is the plumbing compatible with municipal water pressure?*
> You also cross-check that the architect's specifications **match your actual needs** (e.g., you need wheelchair access, but the blueprint only mentions stairs).
> A **Specification Review** in biomedical device design is exactly this — except the "house" is a heart pacemaker, MRI machine, or blood glucose monitor, the "architect" is a multidisciplinary engineering team, and the "municipal inspector" is a regulatory body like the **FDA**, **CE (EU MDR 2017/745)**, or **CDSCO (India)**.

---

## 1.3 Why Specification Review is a High-Yield KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In real-world medical device development, **more than 60% of design defects originate from poorly written or unreviewed specifications.** Under the KTU 2024 OBE framework, this topic directly maps to:
> - **CO1:** *Understand the design control process for biomedical devices*
> - **Bloom's Level:** *Understand (L2), Apply (L3), Analyze (L4)*
> - Marks Weightage: Frequently asked as a **7-mark or 14-mark ESE question** in Part B.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology — Quick Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Design Input (DI)** | The physical and performance requirements of a device, derived from user needs and intended use |
| **Design Output (DO)** | The results of the design effort — drawings, specifications, software code, procedures |
| **Design Review** | A documented, comprehensive, systematic examination of a design |
| **Verification** | Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that **design outputs meet design inputs** |
| **Validation** | Confirmation that the device meets **user needs and intended use** in the actual operating environment |
| **Traceability** | The ability to follow the life of a requirement through all stages of development |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Biomedical Device Specifications

Biomedical device specifications fall into **six primary categories**. Each category is critical to the Specification Review.

### 2.1.1 Functional Specifications
- Define **what the device must do**.
- Example: An infusion pump *shall deliver a programmed volume of fluid between 0.1 mL/h and 999 mL/h with an accuracy of $\pm 2\%$*.

### 2.1.2 Performance Specifications
- Define **how well** the device must perform.
- Example: An ECG monitor *shall display QRS complexes with a frequency response of 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz $\pm 3$ dB*.

### 2.1.3 Safety Specifications
- Define the **risk control measures** and safety limits (per **ISO 14971**).
- Example: A defibrillator *shall limit peak discharge energy to 360 J $\pm 10\%$*.
- Patient leakage current limits per **IEC 60601-1**: **0.1 mA (Type B)**, **0.01 mA (Type CF)**.

### 2.1.4 Regulatory & Standards Specifications
- Compliance with applicable **harmonized standards**:
  - **ISO 13485** — Quality Management Systems
  - **ISO 14971** — Risk Management
  - **IEC 60601-1** — Electrical Safety
  - **ISO 10993** — Biocompatibility
  - **IEC 62366** — Usability Engineering

### 2.1.5 User & Human Factors Specifications
- Defined per **IEC 62366-1**.
- Define intended user (clinician, patient, layperson), use environment, and usability goals.
- Example: *95% of trained users shall complete device setup within 5 minutes without critical use error.*

### 2.1.6 Material & Biocompatibility Specifications
- Material grade, surface finish, sterilization compatibility.
- Example: Implant housing *shall be Ti-6Al-4V ELI per ASTM F136*.

---

## 2.2 The Specification Review Process — 6 Logical Steps

1. **Preparation:** Collect all relevant documents — user needs, intended use, regulatory requirements, prior art, standards list, risk analysis.
2. **Cross-Functional Review:** Convene a panel including design, quality, regulatory, clinical, manufacturing, and usability engineering.
3. **Completeness Check:** Verify all categories of specifications are present (use a **checklist matrix**).
4. **Clarity & Unambiguity Check:** Reject vague terms like *"as appropriate"*, *"user-friendly"*, *"sufficient"*. Replace with measurable criteria.
5. **Traceability Verification:** Confirm each design input can be linked to a user need AND a design output (bidirectional traceability).
6. **Documentation & Approval:** Generate a **Design Review Report (DRR)** with action items, owners, and sign-offs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A specification that is **not measurable** is **not a specification** — it is a wish. The Specification Review must reject any requirement that cannot be objectively verified.

---

## 2.3 The "SMART-FV" Specification Quality Criterion

Every requirement passing the Specification Review must be:

| Letter | Criterion | Biomedical Example |
|--------|-----------|--------------------|
| **S** | **Specific** | "Pump shall alarm within 2 s of occlusion detection" |
| **M** | **Measurable** | "$\pm 2\%$ flow accuracy" |
| **A** | **Achievable** | Realistic given current technology |
| **R** | **Relevant** | Linked to a user need or risk control measure |
| **T** | **Traceable** | Linked to DI-DO-V&V chain |
| **F** | **Feasible** | Can be verified with available test methods |
| **V** | **Verifiable** | Has an objective acceptance criterion |

---

## 2.4 Specification Review vs. Design Review

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Confusion Clarified:**
> - A **Specification Review** is *focused only on requirements documents* (typically early in design).
> - A **Design Review** is a *broader, multi-stage examination* of the entire design (concept, detailed, final).
> - Specification Review is a **subset** of Design Review.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Knowledge Cheat Sheet

> Since Specification Review is largely a **process-oriented and qualitative topic**, the "formulas" here are structured **knowledge matrices** that KTU examiners love to test.

| # | Standard / Concept | Key Metric / Value | Use |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-----|
| 1 | **IEC 60601-1 Patient Leakage Current (Type BF)** | $\le 0.1$ mA | Safety specification |
| 2 | **IEC 60601-1 Patient Leakage Current (Type CF)** | $\le 0.01$ mA | Cardiac-protected safety |
| 3 | **IEC 60601-1 Earth Leakage Current (NC)** | $\le 0.5$ mA | Electrical safety |
| 4 | **Defibrillator Energy Tolerance** | $\pm 10\%$ of set energy | Performance spec |
| 5 | **Infusion Pump Flow Accuracy (typical)** | $\pm 2\%$ to $\pm 5\%$ | Performance spec |
| 6 | **Biocompatibility Tests (ISO 10993)** | Cytotoxicity, Sensitization, Irritation | Material spec |
| 7 | **Risk Acceptability per ISO 14971** | ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) | Safety spec |
| 8 | **Usability (IEC 62366-1)** | Zero use error leading to harm | User spec |
| 9 | **Design Review Participants (minimum)** | 3 independent functions | Process spec |
| 10 | **Software Safety Classification (IEC 62304)** | Class A, B, C | Software spec |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prevents costly redesigns:** A defect caught at specification stage costs ~**$1**; the same defect caught post-market costs ~**$1000+** (FDA data on recall costs).
- **Enables regulatory submission:** FDA 510(k) and EU MDR Technical Files require complete specification traceability matrices.
- **Drives test plan generation:** Every measurable spec becomes a test case in V&V.
- **Supports risk management:** Each safety spec is linked to a hazard in the **Risk Management File (RMF)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Example — Specification Review of a Digital Blood Pressure Monitor

> **Scenario:** A startup "MedTech Kerala Pvt. Ltd." is developing a wrist-type **digital blood pressure monitor** for home use. The design team has drafted an initial specification list. You are the KTU examiner — critique it as a Specification Review.

### Step 1 — Drafted Specifications Received

> [!NOTE]
> The following draft specifications are presented to the review panel:

1. The device shall measure blood pressure accurately.
2. The cuff shall be comfortable.
3. Battery life shall be long.
4. The display shall be clear and easy to read.
5. Pressure measurement range: 0 to 300 mmHg.
6. Pressure accuracy: $\pm 3$ mmHg.
7. Heart rate range: 40 to 200 bpm.
8. Heart rate accuracy: $\pm 5\%$.
9. Operating temperature: $10^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $40^{\circ}\text{C}$.
10. Memory: store 100 readings.
11. Compliance with IEC 60601-1.
12. Biocompatible cuff material.

### Step 2 — SMART-FV Compliance Check (Spec-by-Spec)

| Spec # | Original Wording | SMART-FV Verdict | Examiner's Comment |
|--------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | "Measure blood pressure **accurately**" | **FAIL** | "Accurately" is unmeasurable. Rewrite with numeric tolerance. |
| 2 | "Cuff shall be **comfortable**" | **FAIL** | Subjective. Replace with objective criteria (e.g., circumference range, pressure cycling time). |
| 3 | "Battery life shall be **long**" | **FAIL** | Replace with "$\ge 200$ measurements on a single charge". |
| 4 | "Display shall be **clear**" | **FAIL** | Replace with "LCD, minimum character height 8 mm, viewing angle $\ge 60^{\circ}$". |
| 5 | "0 to 300 mmHg" | **PASS** | Measurable, verifiable. |
| 6 | "$\pm 3$ mmHg" | **PASS** | Within clinically accepted standard (AAMI/ISO 81060-2). |
| 7 | "40 to 200 bpm" | **PASS** | Measurable. |
| 8 | "$\pm 5\%$" | **PASS** | Measurable. |
| 9 | "$10^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $40^{\circ}\text{C}$" | **PASS** | Matches home-use environment per IEC 60601-1-11. |
| 10 | "100 readings" | **PASS** | Measurable. |
| 11 | "IEC 60601-1" | **PASS (partially)** | Add **specific clauses** referenced (e.g., leakage current $\le 0.1$ mA). |
| 12 | "Biocompatible" | **FAIL (vague)** | Specify: *"Cuff shall pass ISO 10993-5 (cytotoxicity) and ISO 10993-10 (sensitization) tests."* |

### Step 3 — Rewrite Rejected Specifications

**Rewritten Spec 1 (Functional/Performance):**
> *"The device shall measure systolic and diastolic blood pressure with an accuracy of $\pm 3$ mmHg and a mean error $\le \pm 5$ mmHg per ISO 81060-2:2013 non-invasive sphygmomanometer standard."*

**Rewritten Spec 2 (User/Human Factors):**
> *"The cuff shall accommodate wrist circumferences of 13.5 cm to 21.5 cm, and the inflation cycle shall complete within 30 s without exceeding a peak pressure of 300 mmHg."*

**Rewritten Spec 3 (Performance — Battery):**
> *"On a fully charged battery, the device shall perform a minimum of 200 measurement cycles per IEC 60601-1-11 §4.10.2."*

**Rewritten Spec 4 (User Interface):**
> *"The display shall be a transflective LCD with a minimum character height of 8 mm, viewable at a viewing angle of $\ge 60^{\circ}$ under 300 lux ambient lighting."*

**Rewritten Spec 12 (Material/Biocompatibility):**
> *"All skin-contacting components of the cuff shall demonstrate no cytotoxic effect (ISO 10993-5 grade 0) and no sensitization (ISO 10993-10). The supplier shall provide a Declaration of Biocompatibility."*

### Step 4 — Traceability Matrix Construction

A **Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM)** is the formal output of a Specification Review.

| Req ID | Design Input | User Need | Risk Link | Design Output | Verification | Validation |
|--------|--------------|-----------|-----------|---------------|--------------|------------|
| REQ-001 | BP accuracy $\pm 3$ mmHg | Reliable home BP monitoring | R-02 (incorrect reading → wrong treatment) | Algorithm + sensor spec | ISO 81060-2 bench test | Clinical study with 85 subjects |
| REQ-002 | Cuff fit 13.5–21.5 cm | Comfort across population | R-05 (improper fit → injury) | Cuff design drawing | Dimensional inspection | User study with diverse cohort |
| REQ-003 | 200 cycles per charge | Field readiness | — | Battery sizing calc | Discharge test | Field use simulation |
| REQ-011 | Leakage current $\le 0.1$ mA | Electrical safety | R-01 (electric shock) | Isolation transformer | Dielectric test per IEC 60601-1 | — |

### Step 5 — Risk–Spec Linkage (ISO 14971 Excerpt)

For the most critical safety spec (electrical safety), the risk linkage is:

$$ R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{intrinsic}} \times (1 - P_{\text{control}}) $$

where:
- $R_{\text{residual}}$ = Residual risk after specification enforcement
- $R_{\text{intrinsic}}$ = Inherent risk from the hazard (electric shock)
- $P_{\text{control}}$ = Probability of risk control succeeding

For this device, by enforcing **leakage current $\le 0.1$ mA (REQ-011)**, the residual risk of electric shock is reduced to an **Acceptable** level per the risk acceptability matrix in the Risk Management Plan.

### Step 6 — Review Report Output

The Specification Review concludes with a **Design Review Report** containing:
- Attendees (min 3 independent functions)
- Specs reviewed: 12 (8 passed, 4 failed)
- Action items: 4 specifications to be rewritten and re-submitted within 10 working days
- Sign-off: Design Lead, Quality Manager, Regulatory Lead
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Specification Review Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Needs and Intended Use] --> B[Derive Design Inputs]
    B --> C[Categorize Specifications: Functional / Performance / Safety / Regulatory / User / Material]
    C --> D[Prepare Specification Document Set]
    D --> E[Convene Cross Functional Review Panel]
    E --> F{Apply SMART FV Check}
    F -->|Pass| G[Establish Traceability Links User Need to Design Input to Design Output]
    F -->|Fail| H[Return to Author for Rewrite]
    H --> D
    G --> I[Link Each Spec to Risk Control Measure]
    I --> J[Generate Requirement Traceability Matrix RTM]
    J --> K[Sign off Design Review Report]
    K --> L[Proceed to Detailed Design]
    L --> M[Verification and Validation Activities]
    M --> N[Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
```

## 4.2 Specification Categories — Hierarchical Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Biomedical Device Specifications] --> B[Functional]
    A --> C[Performance]
    A --> D[Safety]
    A --> E[Regulatory and Standards]
    A --> F[User and Human Factors]
    A --> G[Material and Biocompatibility]
    B --> B1[Inputs Outputs Modes]
    C --> C1[Accuracy Range Response Time]
    D --> D1[Electrical Mechanical Thermal]
    E --> E1[ISO 13485 ISO 14971 IEC 60601 ISO 10993]
    F --> F1[Intended User Use Environment Use Errors]
    G --> G1[Cytotoxicity Sensitization Irritation]
```

## 4.3 Traceability Matrix — V-Model Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[User Need 1] --> DI1[Design Input 1]
    U2[User Need 2] --> DI2[Design Input 2]
    U3[User Need 3] --> DI3[Design Input 3]
    DI1 --> DO1[Design Output 1]
    DI2 --> DO2[Design Output 2]
    DI3 --> DO3[Design Output 3]
    DO1 --> V1[Verification Test 1]
    DO2 --> V2[Verification Test 2]
    DO3 --> V3[Verification Test 3]
    V1 --> VA1[Validation User Trial 1]
    V2 --> VA2[Validation User Trial 2]
    V3 --> VA3[Validation User Trial 3]
```

## 4.4 Risk-Specification Linkage Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hazard Identification] --> B[Risk Estimation Severity x Probability]
    B --> C{Risk Acceptable per ISO 14971}
    C -->|No| D[Define Risk Control Measure]
    D --> E[Convert to Safety Specification]
    E --> F[Verify via Test]
    F --> G[Residual Risk Acceptable]
    G -->|Yes| H[Document in Risk Management File]
    G -->|No| I[Re Design or Reject]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Specification Review as applied to the design of biomedical devices. List any four key categories of specifications that are reviewed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Specification Review is a systematic, documented examination of all design input and output requirements of a medical device to ensure they are complete, unambiguous, measurable, verifiable, and traceable to user needs and regulatory requirements, in accordance with ISO 13485 and FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30.

**Four Categories (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Functional Specifications** — what the device must do
2. **Performance Specifications** — accuracy, range, response time
3. **Safety Specifications** — leakage current, energy limits, alarm thresholds
4. **Regulatory/Standards Specifications** — ISO 13485, IEC 60601-1, ISO 14971, ISO 10993

*(Other acceptable answers: Material/Biocompatibility, User/Human Factors.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM)? Why is it important in biomedical device Specification Review?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM) is a structured document that links every design input to its corresponding user need, design output, verification activity, and validation activity, ensuring bidirectional traceability throughout the design and development lifecycle.

**Importance — any three points (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Demonstrates to regulators (FDA, EU MDR, CDSCO) that all user needs have been addressed.
2. Detects missing or orphan specifications early, reducing redesign costs.
3. Forms the backbone of the **Design History File (DHF)** — mandatory for medical device audits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks on Specification Review questions by:
> (a) Confusing **Verification** with **Validation** (V&V).
> (b) Writing **vague specifications** (e.g., "good accuracy") instead of **numeric tolerances**.
> (c) Failing to mention **specific clauses** of standards (e.g., saying "IEC 60601-1" without citing the leakage current limit).
> (d) Omitting the **traceability** aspect — a specification without a linked user need loses 2–3 marks.
> (e) Skipping the **risk-management linkage** in safety specifications.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option 1**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 1]`**

A medical device company is developing a **portable pulse oximeter** for spot-check use in clinical settings.

**(a)** [7 marks] Prepare a complete set of design specifications for this device, covering at least three specification categories. For each specification, state the measurement unit, acceptance criterion, and the standard it is derived from.

**(b)** [7 marks] Construct a Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM) showing at least four specifications. Explain how each specification is verified and validated, with reference to V&V activities.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Design Specifications (7 marks — 2 marks for three well-written specs + 1 mark for units/standards)**

| Req ID | Category | Specification | Unit | Acceptance Criterion | Source Standard |
|--------|----------|---------------|------|----------------------|----------------|
| REQ-PO-01 | Performance (Functional) | SpO₂ measurement range | % | 35% to 100% | ISO 80601-2-61 |
| REQ-PO-02 | Performance (Accuracy) | SpO₂ accuracy at 70–100% | % | $\pm 2\%$ (RMS) | ISO 80601-2-61 §201.12.1.101 |
| REQ-PO-03 | Performance (PR Range) | Pulse rate range | bpm | 30 to 240 bpm | ISO 80601-2-61 |
| REQ-PO-04 | Performance (PR Accuracy) | Pulse rate accuracy | bpm | $\pm 3$ bpm or $\pm 2\%$ (whichever greater) | Manufacturer-defined; industry practice |
| REQ-PO-05 | Safety (Electrical) | Patient leakage current (Type BF) | mA | $\le 0.1$ mA (NC), $\le 0.5$ mA (SFC) | IEC 60601-1 §8.7 |
| REQ-PO-06 | Safety (Thermal) | Maximum skin-contact temperature | °C | $\le 41^{\circ}\text{C}$ for normal use | IEC 60601-1 §11.1 |
| REQ-PO-07 | User (Usability) | Time to obtain one reading | s | $\le 30$ s from finger insertion to display | IEC 62366-1 |
| REQ-PO-08 | Biocompatibility | Skin-contact sensor material | — | Pass cytotoxicity (ISO 10993-5) and sensitization (ISO 10993-10) | ISO 10993 |
| REQ-PO-09 | Environmental | Operating temperature | °C | $5^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $40^{\circ}\text{C}$ | IEC 60601-1-11 |
| REQ-PO-10 | Battery | Continuous operation on full charge | hours | $\ge 12$ h | Manufacturer requirement |

*(Examiner credit: 2 marks for a minimum of 3 SMART specifications across distinct categories; 1 mark each additional valid spec; 0.5 marks each for correct unit and standard reference.)*

**(b) Requirement Traceability Matrix (7 marks — 2.5 for matrix, 2.5 for V&V mapping, 2 for explanation)**

| Req ID | User Need | Risk Link (ISO 14971) | Design Output | Verification | Validation |
|--------|-----------|----------------------|---------------|--------------|------------|
| REQ-PO-02 | Accurate SpO₂ reading for clinical decisions | R-01: Inaccurate reading → wrong therapy | Algorithm + sensor calibration file | Bench test against a **co-oximeter** on 10 healthy volunteers per ISO 80601-2-61 | Clinical study comparing with arterial blood gas in 30 patients |
| REQ-PO-05 | Protection from electric shock | R-02: Patient harm from leakage | Isolation barrier schematic | Dielectric withstand and leakage current test per IEC 60601-1 | Inspection of production units during Design Transfer |
| REQ-PO-06 | Prevention of thermal burn | R-03: Skin burn from sensor | Thermal management design | Temperature rise test under fault conditions | Simulated-use testing with prolonged finger placement |
| REQ-PO-08 | No allergic reaction | R-04: Sensitization reaction | Material selection dossier | ISO 10993 biocompatibility test reports | Post-market surveillance |

**Explanation of V&V (2 marks):**
- **Verification** confirms that the design output *meets* the design input (e.g., bench-testing the SpO₂ accuracy on a simulator or against a co-oximeter).
- **Validation** confirms that the device *meets user needs in actual clinical use* (e.g., a clinical study comparing device readings with arterial blood gas).
- Together, the RTM provides **bidirectional traceability** — every user need can be traced forward to a verification test, and every test can be traced back to a user need.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option 2**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]`**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain in detail the **six categories of biomedical device specifications** that must be reviewed during a Specification Review. For each category, give one realistic example specification with a measurable acceptance criterion.

**(b)** [7 marks] Describe the **SMART-FV quality criteria** for medical device specifications. Apply these criteria to critically review the following specification: *"The ventilator shall deliver a comfortable tidal volume to the patient."* Rewrite the specification in a SMART-FV compliant form.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Six Categories (7 marks — 1 mark per category with 0.25 for example and 0.5 for measurable acceptance)**

1. **Functional Specifications:** Define *what* the device does.
   - *Example:* The infusion pump shall deliver fluid volumes in the range **0.1 mL/h to 1200 mL/h** with selectable flow-rate increments of **0.1 mL/h**.

2. **Performance Specifications:** Define *how well* the device performs.
   - *Example:* Flow accuracy shall be $\pm 2\%$ of the set rate per **IEC 60601-2-24**.

3. **Safety Specifications:** Define risk-control requirements.
   - *Example:* Patient leakage current shall not exceed **0.01 mA** (Type CF) per **IEC 60601-1 §8.7**.

4. **Regulatory/Standards Specifications:** Cite applicable standards.
   - *Example:* The device shall comply with **ISO 14971:2019** for risk management.

5. **User/Human Factors Specifications:** Define intended user and usability.
   - *Example:* **95%** of trained ICU nurses shall complete alarm acknowledgement within **5 s** of alarm onset, per **IEC 62366-1**.

6. **Material/Biocompatibility Specifications:** Define materials in contact with the body.
   - *Example:* All gas-path materials shall pass **ISO 10993-5** cytotoxicity and **ISO 10993-10** sensitization tests.

**(b) SMART-FV Criteria + Critical Review (7 marks — 2 marks for criteria, 2 marks for critique, 3 marks for rewrite)**

**SMART-FV Criteria (2 marks — list any 4 for full credit):**
- **S** — Specific (clearly stated)
- **M** — Measurable (numeric)
- **A** — Achievable (technically feasible)
- **R** — Relevant (linked to user need/risk)
- **T** — Traceable (linked in RTM)
- **F** — Feasible (verifiable with available test methods)
- **V** — Verifiable (has objective acceptance criterion)

**Critical Review of the Given Specification (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Verdict | Issue |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Specific | ❌ FAIL | "Comfortable" is subjective |
| Measurable | ❌ FAIL | No numeric quantity |
| Achievable | Unclear | Depends on patient condition |
| Relevant | Partially | Tidal volume is a clinical parameter, not comfort |
| Traceable | ❌ FAIL | No user-need link |
| Feasible | ❌ FAIL | Cannot test "comfort" objectively |
| Verifiable | ❌ FAIL | No objective test method |

**SMART-FV Compliant Rewrite (3 marks):**

> *"The ventilator shall deliver a tidal volume in the range of **20 mL to 1500 mL**, selectable in increments of **10 mL**, with a delivered-volume accuracy of $\pm 10\%$ of the set value per **ISO 80601-2-12 §201.12.1.103**, applicable to patients with ideal body weight (IBW) between 3 kg and 150 kg."*

This rewritten specification is specific, measurable, achievable, relevant to a clinical user need (precise ventilation), traceable to a design output (volume control algorithm), feasible to verify (volume bench test), and verifiable through the cited ISO standard.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Specification Review**

- ✅ Specification Review is a **documented cross-functional examination** of design input/output requirements.
- ✅ The seven specification categories are: **Functional, Performance, Safety, Regulatory, User/Human Factors, Material/Biocompatibility**, and (often added) **Software** specifications.
- ✅ Every specification must be **SMART-FV compliant** — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Traceable, Feasible, Verifiable.
- ✅ The review checks for **completeness, clarity, unambiguity, measurability, and traceability** to user needs.
- ✅ Output of the review is a **Design Review Report (DRR)** and a **Requirement Traceability Matrix (RTM)**.
- ✅ **Verification** = Design output meets design input.
- ✅ **Validation** = Device meets user needs in actual use.
- ✅ Key safety limits to memorize:
  - Leakage current (Type BF): **$\le 0.1$ mA**
  - Leakage current (Type CF): **$\le 0.01$ mA**
  - Earth leakage (NC): **$\le 0.5$ mA**
- ✅ Specification Review is governed by **ISO 13485 §7.3** and **FDA 21 CFR §820.30(c)** and **820.30(d)**.
- ✅ Every safety specification must be linked to a **risk control measure** in the Risk Management File (ISO 14971).
- ✅ Vague terms to **avoid**: *user-friendly, suitable, adequate, as appropriate, comfortable, sufficient*.
- ✅ Specification Review is **not** the same as Design Review — it is a subset focused on requirements only.
- ✅ A minimum of **three independent functions** must participate in a Design Review (per ISO 13485).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Specification. Software and/or Hardware Requirement Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Design Specification (DS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Design Specification** is a comprehensive, structured technical document that precisely defines the characteristics, features, performance parameters, safety requirements, regulatory compliance criteria, and operational constraints that a medical device must satisfy throughout its lifecycle. It serves as the **bridge document** between user needs/design inputs and the actual engineering implementation, translating abstract user requirements into measurable, testable engineering parameters.

### Components of a Design Specification

A complete Design Specification for a biomedical device typically encompasses:

1. **Functional Requirements** — What the device must do (e.g., measure ECG, deliver insulin, monitor SpO2)
2. **Performance Requirements** — Quantitative metrics (accuracy ±X%, sampling rate ≥Y Hz)
3. **Safety Requirements** — IEC 60601-1 compliance, leakage current limits, alarm priorities
4. **Usability Requirements** — IEC 62366-1 (human factors engineering)
5. **Environmental Requirements** — Operating temperature, humidity, altitude, EMC immunity
6. **Regulatory Requirements** — FDA 21 CFR Part 820, EU MDR 2017/745, ISO 13485
7. **Interface Requirements** — Mechanical, electrical, software, user interfaces
8. **Reliability & Maintainability** — MTBF, MTTR, service life, calibration intervals
9. **Materials & Biocompatibility** — ISO 10993 series for body-contacting materials
10. **Sterilization Requirements** — If applicable (EtO, gamma, autoclave compatibility)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "Building a House"**
>
> Think of designing a biomedical device like **building a custom house**:
> - The **User Needs** are the family's wishlist: "We want 4 bedrooms, a garden, solar power, wheelchair-accessible."
> - The **Design Specification** is the **architectural blueprint with exact dimensions**: "Master bedroom: 16 m², South-facing, double-glazed windows, R-30 insulation rating, door width ≥ 900 mm for accessibility."
> - The **Software/Hardware Requirement Specification** is the **detailed electrical and plumbing plan**: "Wire gauge 12 AWG for kitchen circuit, GFCI protection in all wet areas, water pressure 2.5 bar, pipe diameter 25 mm."
>
> Without the Design Specification, the engineer builds "something close" — but the inspector rejects it. Without the SRS/HRS, the software team writes code that talks to hardware that doesn't exist. **Specifications are the universal language that makes engineering reproducible and auditable.**

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **IEC 60601-1 Earth Leakage Current Limit:** Normal Condition ≤ **500 µA**, Single Fault Condition ≤ **1000 µA** for Class I equipment
- **Patient Auxiliary Current Limit:** Normal Condition ≤ **100 µA**, Single Fault Condition ≤ **500 µA** (Type B), ≤ **50 µA / 100 µA** for Type CF (Cardiac Floating)
- **ISO 13485:2016** — Quality management systems standard for medical devices
- **IEC 62304:2006/AMD1:2015** — Medical device software lifecycle processes (Software Safety Class A, B, C)
- **IEEE 830-1998** — Recommended practice for Software Requirements Specifications

---

## 1.2 Software Requirement Specification (SRS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Software Requirement Specification (SRS)** is a formal document that completely describes the **external behaviour of the software**, the **constraints on its operation**, and all **functional and non-functional requirements** that the software must satisfy. Per **IEEE 830-1998**, it serves as the contract between the development team and the customer/regulator, and forms the basis for **Verification & Validation (V&V)** activities.

### IEEE 830 SRS Structure (Standard Template)

1. **Introduction**
   - Purpose, scope, definitions, references, overview
2. **Overall Description**
   - Product perspective, functions, user characteristics, constraints, assumptions
3. **Specific Requirements**
   - Functional, performance, design constraints, software system attributes
4. **Appendices** (optional — data dictionaries, use cases)

---

## 1.3 Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS)** is a structured document that defines all **electrical, mechanical, electronic, and physical characteristics** of the hardware subsystems — including analog front-end, digital signal processor, memory, communication interfaces, power supply, sensors, actuators, and PCB layout constraints — that the biomedical device hardware must satisfy.

### Key Sections of an HRS

- Electrical characteristics (voltage rails, current draw, signal ranges)
- Sensor specifications (range, accuracy, resolution, bandwidth)
- Mechanical specifications (dimensions, weight, enclosure IP rating)
- Environmental compliance (operating/storage temperature, humidity, shock/vibration per IEC 60068)
- EMC compliance (IEC 60601-1-2)
- Reliability targets (MTBF ≥ 50,000 hours for medical-grade components)
- Bill of Materials (BOM) constraints

---

## 1.4 Difference Between Requirements and Specifications

| Aspect | **Requirement** | **Specification** |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Nature | *What* the system shall do | *How well / to what extent* it shall do it |
| Source | User / Stakeholder / Regulation | Engineering design decision |
| Testability | Often qualitative | Always quantitative & measurable |
| Example | "Monitor heart rate" | "Measure HR in range **40–200 bpm** with accuracy **±2 bpm** or **±2%**, whichever is greater" |
| Lifecycle stage | Concept / Feasibility | Design / Implementation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Design Control Framework (FDA Quality System Regulation)

> [!NOTE]
> Under **21 CFR 820.30 (Design Control)**, the FDA mandates a structured design process. Specifications sit at the heart of this framework as **Design Inputs** that drive **Design Outputs**, which are then verified and validated.

### Logical Flow of Design Control

1. **User Needs** → captured from clinical context, market research, regulatory mandates
2. **Design Inputs** → translated from user needs into engineering requirements (the SRS/HRS)
3. **Design Process** → converts inputs into outputs (schematics, code, prototypes)
4. **Design Outputs** → final drawings, BOM, software code, labels, instructions
5. **Design Verification** → "Did we build the device right?" (Did outputs meet inputs?)
6. **Design Validation** → "Did we build the right device?" (Does it meet user needs?)
7. **Design Transfer** → Specifications move from R&D into manufacturing

### Why Each Step Matters — The "Why/How" Breakdown

- **Why specifications before design?** → Prevents costly rework; enables parallel hardware/software teams to work independently against a stable contract.
- **Why quantitative metrics?** → Regulatory bodies (FDA, Notified Bodies) demand **objective evidence**. "Fast response" is not auditable; "Rise time ≤ 2 seconds from 10% to 90% of full scale" is.
- **Why separate SRS and HRS?** → Hardware and software teams have different expertise, timelines, and regulatory pathways (IEC 62304 vs. IEC 60601-1). Separation enables **modular V&V**.

---

## 2.2 IEC 62304 Software Safety Classification (Drives SRS Depth)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **IEC 62304 standard** mandates that the **depth and rigour of the SRS** must scale with the **Software Safety Class** of the medical device:

| **Software Safety Class** | **Risk of Harm** | **Examples** | **SRS Documentation Depth** |
|---------------------------|------------------|--------------|----------------------------|
| **Class A** | No injury or damage to health is possible | Hospital bed control, non-therapy cosmetic device | Basic SRS required |
| **Class B** | Non-serious injury is possible | Infusion pump rate display, ECG monitor | Detailed SRS + risk analysis |
| **Class C** | Death or serious injury is possible | Insulin dosing algorithm, ventilator breath delivery, AED shock decision | Exhaustive SRS + formal risk management (ISO 14971) + detailed V&V |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While specification documents are largely qualitative, certain **engineering parameters** appear repeatedly as **measurable design inputs**. The following table summarizes the key quantitative relationships and standards-derived limits.

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Standard Limit** | **Units / Boundary Condition** | **Applicable Standard** |
|------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| **Earth Leakage Current (Normal)** | $I_{EL} \leq 500 \,\mu A$ | microamperes (rms) | IEC 60601-1, Class I |
| **Earth Leakage Current (Single Fault)** | $I_{EL,SF} \leq 1000 \,\mu A$ | microamperes (rms) | IEC 60601-1 |
| **Patient Auxiliary Current (Type BF, Normal)** | $\vert I_{PA} \vert \leq 100 \,\mu A$ | microamperes (rms) | IEC 60601-1 |
| **Patient Auxiliary Current (Type CF, Normal)** | $\vert I_{PA} \vert \leq 10 \,\mu A$ | microamperes (rms) | IEC 60601-1 |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (ECG, minimum)** | $SNR \geq 40 \, dB$ | decibels | IEC 60601-2-25 |
| **Sampling Rate (Diagnostic ECG)** | $f_s \geq 500 \, Hz$ | samples per second | AHA / IEC 60601-2-25 |
| **Bandwidth (Diagnostic ECG)** | $0.05 \, Hz \leq BW \leq 150 \, Hz$ | hertz | AHA recommendations |
| **ADC Resolution (Minimum)** | $N \geq 12 \, bits$ for diagnostic ECG | bits | Engineering best practice |
| **Heart Rate Display Range** | $20 \leq HR \leq 300 \, bpm$ | beats per minute | IEC 60601-2-30 |
| **Blood Pressure Measurement Accuracy** | $\pm 3 \, mmHg$ or $\pm 2\%$ (whichever is greater) | mmHg or percent | ISO 81060-2 |
| **SpO₂ Accuracy (≥70% saturation)** | $\vert \Delta SpO_2 \vert \leq 3\%$ | percent | ISO 80601-2-61 |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF, Class C)** | $MTBF \geq 10000 \, hours$ (typical minimum) | hours | Manufacturer-defined |
| **Ingress Protection (Handheld device)** | $\geq IPX2$ (drip-proof) | IP code | IEC 60529 |
| **Operating Temperature (Clinical)** | $0^{\circ}C \leq T \leq 40^{\circ}C$ | degrees Celsius | IEC 60601-1 |
| **Storage/Transport Temperature** | $-20^{\circ}C \leq T \leq 60^{\circ}C$ | degrees Celsius | IEC 60601-1 |

### Calculation Aid — Quantization Error from ADC Resolution

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N}}$$

where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale input voltage range and $N$ is the ADC resolution in bits.

**Quantization Noise (RMS) for a uniformly distributed quantization error:**

$$V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$$

**Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) from measured SNR:**

$$ENOB = \frac{SNR_{measured} - 1.76}{6.02}$$

These three equations are routinely placed in the SRS as **performance specifications** for analog front-end signal acquisition in ECG, EMG, EEG, and PPG biomedical devices.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **biomedical device industry**, specifications are not just paperwork — they are **legal artefacts**:

- An SRS becomes part of the **FDA 510(k) submission** or **CE Technical File**.
- Traceability matrices link every user need → design input → design output → verification test → validation test.
- If a patient is harmed, the **SRS/HRS is the primary evidence** in product liability litigation. Vague specifications = legal exposure.
- Specifications also drive **procurement, supplier qualification, and post-market surveillance**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From User Need to Verifiable Design Specification

> [!NOTE]
> The transformation of a qualitative user need into a quantitative, testable design specification is the **single most important analytical skill** in biomedical device design. KTU examiners frequently test this transformation in **14-mark Part B questions**.

### Worked Example — Pulse Oximeter Finger Probe

**Step 1: Capture the User Need (Qualitative)**

> "The clinician needs to know the patient's blood oxygen saturation continuously, non-invasively, at the bedside, with immediate alarm on desaturation."

**Step 2: Decompose into Functional Sub-Requirements**

- Measure arterial oxygen saturation ($SpO_2$)
- Display numerical value on screen
- Generate audible + visual alarm below threshold
- Operate from internal battery ≥ 6 hours
- Be usable on adult, paediatric, and neonatal patients (with appropriate probes)

**Step 3: Quantify Each Sub-Requirement (becomes SRS/HRS content)**

For the $SpO_2$ measurement functional requirement:

| Sub-requirement ID | Text | Quantitative Target |
|--------------------|------|---------------------|
| **REQ-SRS-001** | $SpO_2$ measurement range | 35% – 100% |
| **REQ-SRS-002** | $SpO_2$ accuracy ($SpO_2 \geq 70\%$) | $\vert \Delta \vert \leq 3\%$ (per ISO 80601-2-61) |
| **REQ-SRS-003** | Update interval | ≤ 1 second |
| **REQ-SRS-004** | Low $SpO_2$ alarm threshold | Configurable, default 90% |
| **REQ-SRS-005** | Alarm sound pressure level | ≥ 65 dB(A) at 1 m |
| **REQ-HRS-001** | Red LED wavelength | $660 \pm 5 \, nm$ |
| **REQ-HRS-002** | Infrared LED wavelength | $940 \pm 10 \, nm$ |
| **REQ-HRS-003** | Photodetector spectral response | 600 nm – 1000 nm |
| **REQ-HRS-004** | ADC sampling rate | $\geq 500 \, Hz$ per channel |
| **REQ-HRS-005** | Battery operation | $\geq 6 \, hours$ continuous |
| **REQ-HRS-006** | Enclosure ingress protection | $\geq IPX2$ (per IEC 60529) |
| **REQ-HRS-007** | Operating temperature | $0^{\circ}C \leq T \leq 40^{\circ}C$ |

**Step 4: Derive Down-Stream Design Outputs (Verification Basis)**

For each requirement, the design output is checked against the requirement:

- **REQ-SRS-002** is verified by **bench testing against a co-oximeter reference** on ≥ 10 healthy subjects with induced desaturation (institutional ethics approval required), per ISO 80601-2-61 Annex EE.

**Step 5: Validate That the User Need Is Met**

- **Clinical usability study** with the intended user population in the intended use environment — the device shall allow clinicians to detect desaturation within ≤ 5 seconds of occurrence (REQ-SRS-003 + REQ-SRS-005 combined).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Quantifying ADC Resolution for ECG Acquisition

Given a maximum ECG signal amplitude of **5 mV peak-to-peak** and a desired amplitude resolution of at least **5 µV** (the smallest clinically meaningful ECG feature — e.g., late potentials), determine the minimum ADC resolution.

### Mathematical Derivation (Exhaustive, Step-by-Step)

Let $V_{LSB}$ be the voltage corresponding to **1 LSB (Least Significant Bit)** of the ADC.

Given parameters:
- Full-scale input voltage: $V_{FS} = 5 \, mV = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, V$
- Required amplitude resolution: $V_{res} \leq 5 \, \mu V = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, V$

For the ADC quantization to satisfy the resolution requirement, we require:

$$V_{LSB} \leq V_{res}$$

Substituting the defining relationship:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N}}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{N}} \leq V_{res}$$

Rearranging to solve for $N$:

$$2^{N} \geq \frac{V_{FS}}{V_{res}}$$

Taking the base-2 logarithm of both sides:

$$N \geq \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{V_{FS}}{V_{res}}\right)$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\frac{V_{FS}}{V_{res}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Performing the division:

$$\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-6}} = 10^{-3 - (-6)} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

Therefore:

$$N \geq \log_{2}(1000)$$

Computing $\log_{2}(1000)$ using the change-of-base formula:

$$\log_{2}(1000) = \frac{\log_{10}(1000)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3}{0.30103} \approx 9.9658$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, we round **up** to the next integer (rounding down would violate the resolution requirement):

$$N \geq 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{min} = 10 \, bits$$

### Practical Engineering Padding

In practice, the designer chooses $N = 12 \, bits$ or $N = 16 \, bits$ to:
- Accommodate **gain-stage variations** (front-end amplification is typically programmable: e.g., gain = 1×, 2×, 4×, 8×, 12×)
- Provide **headroom** against input over-range transients (e.g., defibrillator pulses, motion artefacts)
- Meet **IEC 60601-2-25 diagnostic ECG SNR requirement** of $\geq 40 \, dB$

### Verification Calculation — Effective Resolution

For $N = 12$ bits and $V_{FS} = 5 \, mV$ (after amplification), assuming ideal quantization:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2^{12}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{4096} \approx 1.22 \, \mu V$$

This is well below the 5 µV requirement, satisfying **REQ-HRS** for amplitude resolution.

The quantization RMS noise:

$$V_{q,rms} = \frac{1.22 \times 10^{-6}}{\sqrt{12}} \approx \frac{1.22 \times 10^{-6}}{3.4641} \approx 0.353 \, \mu V$$

Compared to a typical input-referred noise of the analog front-end (≈ 1–3 µV RMS), the quantization noise is **negligible** — confirming the ADC resolution choice is appropriate.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — IEC 62304 vs. IEC 60601-1 Documentation Scope

| **Documentation Artefact** | **Hardware (IEC 60601-1)** | **Software (IEC 62304)** |
|----------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Architecture document | Hardware block diagram, schematics | Software architecture (modules, interfaces, state diagrams) |
| Detailed design | Circuit diagrams, PCB layouts, BOM | Detailed design (data structures, algorithms, source code) |
| Requirement specification | Hardware Requirement Specification (HRS) | Software Requirement Specification (SRS) |
| Risk management | ISO 14971 — applies to all | ISO 14971 + software risk (ITEM 5.1 of IEC 62304) |
| Verification | Bench testing, EMC testing, safety testing | Unit testing, integration testing, code reviews |
| Validation | Clinical / usability evaluation | Software validation (system-level testing) |
| Change control | Engineering change orders | Configuration management plan, version control |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sample SRS Section (IEEE 830 Compliant)

Below is a condensed, KTU-style model **Section 3 (Specific Requirements)** of an SRS for a bedside ECG monitor:

### 3.1 Functional Requirements

- **SRS-F-001:** The system shall acquire 12-lead ECG signals simultaneously.
- **SRS-F-002:** The system shall display real-time ECG waveforms with a sweep speed of **25 mm/s** ± 5%.
- **SRS-F-003:** The system shall compute and display heart rate in the range **20 – 300 bpm**, updating every **1 second**.
- **SRS-F-004:** The system shall detect ST-segment elevation ≥ **0.2 mV** and trigger an alarm within **3 seconds**.

### 3.2 Performance Requirements

- **SRS-P-001:** Sampling rate shall be **≥ 1000 samples/s per channel** (diagnostic-grade, exceeding the IEC 60601-2-25 minimum of 500 Hz by 2× for ST analysis).
- **SRS-P-002:** System bandwidth shall be **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (−3 dB).
- **SRS-P-003:** Common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR) shall be **≥ 90 dB** at 50 Hz.
- **SRS-P-004:** Input impedance shall be **≥ 10 MΩ** at 10 Hz.

### 3.3 Interface Requirements

- **SRS-I-001:** USB 2.0 (or higher) interface shall be provided for data export to USB flash drive.
- **SRS-I-002:** HL7 / DICOM export shall be supported over Ethernet (RJ-45, 100 Mbps minimum).

### 3.4 Design Constraints

- **SRS-C-001:** All software shall be developed per **IEC 62304 Class B** processes.
- **SRS-C-002:** Touchscreen user interface shall comply with **IEC 60601-1-9** (Environmentally conscious design) and **IEC 62366-1** (Usability engineering).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — User Need → Design Specification → V&V Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Needs and Clinical Requirements] --> B[Design Inputs Engineering Requirements]
    B --> C[Design Specification DS]
    C --> D[Hardware Requirement Specification HRS]
    C --> E[Software Requirement Specification SRS]
    D --> F[Design Outputs Hardware Schematics BOM]
    E --> G[Design Outputs Software Code Documentation]
    F --> H[Design Verification Did we build the device right]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Design Validation Did we build the right device]
    I --> J{Acceptance Criteria Met}
    J -->|Yes| K[Design Transfer to Manufacturing]
    J -->|No| L[Corrective Action Re-specify]
    L --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a regulated phase of design control per **21 CFR 820.30** and **ISO 13485:2016 Section 7.3**. The arrow returning from L (Corrective Action) to B (Design Inputs) illustrates the **iterative nature** of specification refinement — a frequent KTU concept question topic.
