#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Heart Rate Derivation (2.5 marks):**

From Section 3.2 derivation:

At standard paper speed $v = 25$ mm/s and gain $G = 10$ mm/mV:

$$\Delta t_{small\,box} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

If the R-R interval spans $n$ small boxes:

$$HR = \frac{60}{n \times 0.04} = \frac{1500}{n} \text{ bpm}$$

In **digital ECG**, the R-peak is detected algorithmically, and $RR$ is measured in **samples**:

$$HR = \frac{60 \cdot f_s}{N_{samples\,RR}} \text{ bpm}$$

**Worked example:** $f_s = 500$ Hz, $N_{samples}$ between R-peaks = 350:

$$HR = \frac{60 \times 500}{350} = \frac{30000}{350} \approx 85.7 \text{ bpm}$$

**2. CMRR Derivation (2.5 marks):**

CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) is defined as:

$$CMRR = \frac{A_d}{A_c}$$

where $A_d$ is the differential gain (signal we want) and $A_c$ is the common-mode gain (50/60 Hz interference we want to reject).

In decibels:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

**Required derivation for diagnostic ECG:** The ECG signal is $\sim$ 1 mV. The 50 Hz mains interference picked up by the patient (acting as an antenna) can be several volts before amplification. To ensure the interference after amplification is $< 1\%$ of the ECG signal:

$$\frac{A_c \cdot V_{CM}}{A_d \cdot V_{ECG}} < 0.01$$

With $V_{CM} = 1$ V, $V_{ECG} = 1$ mV:

$$CMRR > \frac{1}{0.01} \cdot \frac{1\ \text{mV}}{1\ \text{V}} = 100 \cdot 10^{-3} = 0.1 \text{ (linear)}$$

Wait, recomputing: $\frac{A_d}{A_c} > \frac{V_{CM}}{0.01 \cdot V_{ECG}} = \frac{1000}{0.01 \cdot 1} = 100{,}000$.

In dB:

$$CMRR_{dB} > 20 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ dB}$$

This matches the IEC 60601-2-25 requirement of **CMRR $\geq$ 100 dB** for diagnostic ECG.

**3. Sampling Rate and Anti-Alias Filter (2 marks):**

For diagnostic ECG, signal bandwidth $f_{max} = 150$ Hz.

**Nyquist minimum:** $f_s \geq 2 \times 150 = 300$ Hz.

**Design choice (with safety margin):** $f_s = 500$ Hz to 1000 Hz.

**Anti-alias low-pass filter cutoff** must satisfy $f_c \leq f_s / 2 = 250$ Hz. A 2nd-order Butterworth with $f_c = 150$ Hz is the typical choice — it preserves the diagnostic bandwidth while providing 24 dB/oct roll-off above 150 Hz to attenuate aliasing.

**Practical implementation:** A Sallen-Key topology with $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C$ chosen via:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \Rightarrow C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10{,}000 \times 150} \approx 106 \text{ nF}$$

A standard value of **100 nF** yields $f_c \approx 159$ Hz, well within tolerance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing V&V:** Writing "verification" where "validation" is required (or vice versa) costs **1–2 marks** even if the rest of the answer is correct. *Mnemonic:* **V**erification = "**V**alue vs spec" (bench, numbers); **V**a**L**idation = "**L**ive with humans" (clinic, users).
>
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Writing "150 Hz filter" without the cutoff topology or without $f_c \leq f_s / 2$ justification loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 3. **Single-document DHF:** Stating "DHF is one document" is factually wrong. DHF is a *collection of records* with a master index. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
>
> 4. **Skipping the MAP cross-check:** When asked to derive SBP/DBP from oscillometric, students often give SBP and DBP without verifying the MAP relationship. Always include the check.
>
> 5. **CMRR unit confusion:** Writing "CMRR = 100 dB" without showing the derivation (ratio 10⁵ linear) is incomplete. Always show the linear-to-dB conversion.
>
> 6. **Missing standards citation:** A 14-mark question on a biomedical device design MUST cite at least 2–3 applicable standards (e.g., IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, ISO 14971). Answers without standards lose up to 1.5 marks.
>
> 7. **No traceability link:** Stating "verification was done" without naming the **test equipment, sample size, and pass criteria** is treated as vague. Be specific: "Bench test on 5 units using Fluke ProSim 8, pass if HR error $<$ 2%."
>
> 8. **Diagram without labels:** A block diagram drawn without signal-flow arrows and labeled sub-blocks gets only partial credit. Always label the major processing stages (IA, Filter, ADC, DSP, Display).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU Module 1 — Device Records: Last-Minute Revision Sheet**

### 🔹 Core Definitions
- ✅ **DHF (Design History File):** Why we designed the device this way. (FDA 21 CFR 820.30(j))
- ✅ **DMR (Device Master Record):** What the device is and how to manufacture it. (FDA 21 CFR 820.181)
- ✅ **DHR (Device History Record):** Evidence of how each unit was actually built. (FDA 21 CFR 820.184)
- ✅ **RMF (Risk Management File):** ISO 14971 risk analysis, evaluation, control, residual risk.
- ✅ **Technical File (TF):** EU MDR equivalent of DHF, used for CE marking.
- ✅ **DI (Design Input) → DO (Design Output) → V (Verification) → VAL (Validation)** — the V-model sequence.

### 🔹 Key Formulae to Memorize
- ✅ $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ — Empirical MAP from auscultatory readings.
- ✅ $P_{cuff}\big\vert_{A_{max}} = MAP$ — Oscillometric MAP detection.
- ✅ $HR = \frac{1500}{n_{small\,boxes}}$ — Heart rate from ECG (25 mm/s paper).
- ✅ $HR = \frac{60}{RR_{seconds}}$ — Heart rate from time-domain ECG.
- ✅ $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ — Diagnostic ECG needs $\geq$ 100 dB.
- ✅ $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — Nyquist criterion; ECG $f_s$ = 500–1000 Hz.
- ✅ $A_v = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ — 3-op-amp IA gain (matched second stage).
- ✅ $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — Filter cutoff for HPF (0.05 Hz) and LPF (150 Hz).

### 🔹 Mandatory Standards to Cite
- ✅ **ISO 13485:2016** — QMS for medical devices
- ✅ **ISO 14971:2019** — Risk management
- ✅ **IEC 60601-1** — General electrical safety
- ✅ **IEC 60601-2-30** — BP monitor particular standard
- ✅ **IEC 60601-2-25** — ECG machine particular standard
- ✅ **ISO 81060-2** — Non-invasive BP clinical validation
- ✅ **AAMI EC11 / EC13** — ECG performance standards
- ✅ **IEC 62304** — Medical device software lifecycle
- ✅ **IEC 62366-1** — Usability engineering

### 🔹 Common ECG Specifications (Memorize)
- ✅ Amplitude: **0.5–5 mV** (QRS ≈ 1 mV)
- ✅ Bandwidth (diagnostic): **0.05–150 Hz**
- ✅ Sampling rate: **500–1000 Hz**
- ✅ ADC: **12–16 bit**
- ✅ CMRR: **≥ 100 dB**
- ✅ Input impedance: **≥ 10 MΩ**
- ✅ Noise: **< 30 μV p-p**
- ✅ Gain: typically **1000** overall (100 + 10 cascade)

### 🔹 Common BP Monitor Specifications
- ✅ Cuff pressure range: **0–300 mmHg**
- ✅ Accuracy: **± 3 mmHg** (AAMI/ISO 81060-2)
- ✅ Deflation rate: **2–3 mmHg/s**
- ✅ Pressure sensor: piezoresistive, **5 μV/V/mmHg** typical
- ✅ Clinical validation: **≥ 85 subjects** (ISO 81060-2:2018)

### 🔹 Five Golden Rules for KTU Device-Design Answers
1. **Always start with the V-model** — set up DI → DO → V → VAL flow.
2. **Cite at least 3 standards** — quality + safety + risk + device-specific.
3. **Use numerical values** — "±3 mmHg," "≥100 dB," "≥500 Hz" — not vague terms.
4. **Include a labeled block diagram** — with arrows showing signal/data flow.
5. **End with a V&V table** — test, equipment, sample size, pass criterion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medical Device Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medical Device Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medical Device Classification** is the systematic, risk-based categorization of medical devices into regulatory tiers by competent authorities (e.g., US FDA, EU Notified Bodies, India's CDSCO) to determine the level of regulatory control necessary to provide reasonable assurance of **safety** and **effectiveness**.

According to the **US FDA (21 CFR Part 860)**, a medical device is classified based on the level of risk it poses to the patient and the user, the intended use, and the indications for use. The **EU Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR 2017/745)** follows a similar risk-based philosophy but uses a more granular 4-tier system (I, IIa, IIb, III) governed by **22 classification rules** contained in **Annex VIII**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of medical device classification like the **traffic light system at a railway crossing**:
- **Green (Class I)** → Low-risk everyday items like a tongue depressor. Almost anyone can manufacture them under minimal supervision.
- **Yellow (Class II/IIa, IIb)** → Moderate-risk devices like blood pressure monitors. They need proper documentation, performance standards, and a "responsible adult" (Notified Body) checking them.
- **Red (Class III)** → High-risk, life-sustaining implants like pacemakers or heart valves. They need the strictest scrutiny — full clinical trials, exhaustive testing, and continuous post-market surveillance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classify?** Because a tongue depressor and a pacemaker cannot be regulated with the **same paperwork**, **same testing**, or **same cost**. Classification aligns regulatory burden with patient risk — protecting public health without strangling innovation.

## 1.3 Core Standard Metrics

The principal metrics used globally for classification are:

- **Risk Level:** Low, Moderate, High, Critical
- **Duration of Contact:** Transient (< 60 min), Short-term (≤ 30 days), Long-term (> 30 days)
- **Invasiveness:** Non-invasive, Body-cavity invasive, Surgically invasive, Implantable
- **Body System Impact:** Circulatory, Central Nervous, Central Circulatory, etc.
- **Energy Source:** Non-energy, Energy-imparting, Energy-substance administering

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 questions frequently test the *reasoning* behind classification (why a device belongs to a class), not just memorization of the class letter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Major Global Classification Frameworks

### A. US FDA Classification (Risk-Based Triage)

The FDA classifies roughly **1,700 distinct generic device types** into three regulatory classes:

| FDA Class | Risk Profile | Regulatory Pathway | Example Devices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class I** | Low Risk | General Controls (e.g., labeling, registration) | Stethoscopes, tongue depressors, bandages, surgical gloves |
| **Class II** | Moderate Risk | General Controls + **Special Controls** + usually **510(k)** | Infusion pumps, blood pressure cuffs, pregnancy test kits, surgical drapes |
| **Class III** | High Risk | General Controls + **Premarket Approval (PMA)** — strictest pathway | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, coronary stents, silicone breast implants |

**How a device moves up the FDA class ladder:**
- Higher invasiveness → Higher class
- Life-sustaining or life-supporting function → Class III
- Implants → usually Class II or III
- New devices for which no predicate exists → generally Class III by default

### B. EU MDR Classification (Rule-Based Engine)

The EU MDR 2017/745 uses a **decision-rule algorithm** with **22 rules** in Annex VIII. Devices are sorted into:

| EU Class | Risk Profile | Conformity Assessment | Example Devices |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class I** | Low Risk | Self-declaration by manufacturer | Stethoscopes, eyeglasses, wheelchairs (non-powered) |
| **Class IIa** | Moderate Risk (short-term) | Notified Body involvement | Hearing aids, contact lenses (short-term), surgical needles |
| **Class IIb** | Moderate-to-High Risk (longer-term) | Stricter Notified Body scrutiny | Ventilators, infusion pumps, defibrillators, blood bags |
| **Class III** | High Risk | Most stringent assessment | Heart valves, implantable pacemakers, drug-eluting stents, breast implants |

### C. GHTF / IMDRF Global Harmonization

The **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)**, now succeeded by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)**, proposed a 4-tier harmonized model: **A, B, C, D** — adopted by India (CDSCO), Japan, Australia, Canada, and Brazil.

| GHTF Class | Equivalent FDA | Equivalent EU | Risk |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Class I | Class I | Low |
| B | Class II | Class IIa | Low-Moderate |
| C | Class II (high) | Class IIb | Moderate-High |
| D | Class III | Class III | High |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Rule Trigger | Class Implication | Reasoning (Why) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Non-invasive, transient contact | **Class I** | Minimal systemic exposure |
| Non-invasive, channeling body fluids | **Class IIa** | Indirect contact with internal environment |
| Invasive, transient use (< 60 min) | **Class IIa** | Brief internal access |
| Invasive, short-term (≤ 30 days) | **Class IIa** | Prolonged but recoverable contact |
| Invasive, long-term (> 30 days) | **Class IIb** | Sustained biological interaction |
| Implantable, long-term | **Class III** | Permanent biological integration |
| Energy-imparting, hazardous to body | **Class IIb** | Active risk + biological risk |
| Devices in direct contact with CNS / central circulatory system | **Class III** | Critical organ impact |
| Devices incorporating medicinal substances | **Class III** | Dual product — drug + device risk |
| Devices using animal-origin tissues (non-viable) | **Class III** | TSE/virus risk |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Understanding classification drives critical engineering decisions:

- **Design Controls:** Class III demands formal **Design History File (DHF)** under 21 CFR 820.30 — every sketch, tolerance, and FMEA becomes a regulated artifact.
- **Material Selection:** Class III implants (e.g., titanium hip stems) require **biocompatibility testing per ISO 10993**, **cytotoxicity**, **sensitization**, **irritation** panels.
- **Verification & Validation (V&V):** Higher class → more rigorous bench testing, animal trials, and **clinical investigations**.
- **Quality Management System:** All classes need **ISO 13485** QMS, but Class III audits are deeper and longer.
- **Cost & Time-to-Market:** A Class I 510(k) exempt device may launch in **3–6 months**; a Class III PMA typically takes **3–7 years** and **tens of millions of dollars**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Alert:** A common 14-mark question asks you to classify an *unknown* device by walking through the **decision logic step by step** — always state the rule you are applying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Classifying an Implantable Pacemaker

**Given:** A fully implantable, battery-powered cardiac pacemaker with leads placed in the right ventricle, intended for permanent (> 30 days) use in patients with bradycardia.

**Goal:** Determine its EU MDR class and FDA class.

### Step 1 — Identify the Device Type

This is an **active, implantable medical device (AIMD)** that delivers **electrical energy** to the heart muscle to maintain rhythm.

### Step 2 — Apply the EU MDR Classification Rules

We check the relevant rules in Annex VIII sequentially:

**Rule 1 (Non-invasive devices):** Does not apply — the pacemaker is invasive.

**Rule 8 (Implants and long-term surgical invasive devices):**
This rule states that all **long-term surgically invasive devices** are classified as **Class IIb**, *unless* they are intended to:
- Have a biological effect (Class III)
- Be in direct contact with the **central nervous system** (Class III)
- Undergo chemical change in the body (Class III)
- Be in direct contact with the **central circulatory system** (Class III)
- Be **active implantable medical devices** (AIMDs) → **Class III**

### Step 3 — Confirm the Specific Rule Trigger

$$
\text{AIMD} \implies \text{Class III}
$$

Therefore, the pacemaker is a **Class III** device under EU MDR.

### Step 4 — Confirm the FDA Classification

Under **21 CFR 870.3600**, implantable pacemakers are explicitly listed as **Class III** devices and require **Premarket Approval (PMA)**.

$$
\text{FDA Classification} = \text{Class III} \implies \text{PMA pathway required}
$$

### Step 5 — Cross-Validate Using GHTF/IMDRF

$$
\text{GHTF Class D} \iff \text{FDA Class III} \iff \text{EU MDR Class III}
$$

All three global frameworks converge on the highest risk tier — appropriate for a life-sustaining implant.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Classifying a Blood Pressure Cuff

**Given:** A reusable, non-invasive, externally applied cuff used to measure systolic and diastolic blood pressure via oscillometry.

### Step 1 — Identify the Device Type

Non-invasive, externally applied, **transient use**, no energy imparted internally.

### Step 2 — Apply FDA Logic

FDA explicitly lists **non-invasive blood pressure measurement devices** under **Class II** (Product Code DXN), requiring **510(k)** clearance and Special Controls (e.g., performance standards, labeling).

$$
\text{FDA} = \text{Class II}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply EU MDR Rule (Rule 1)

Non-invasive devices in **transient use** (less than 60 minutes) are **Class I** unless they:
- Channel or store body fluids (Class IIa)
- Modify biological composition (Class IIa, IIb, or III)

A BP cuff does none of these → **Class I** under EU MDR.

### Step 4 — Compare Frameworks

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FDA} &= \text{Class II} \\
\text{EU MDR} &= \text{Class I} \\
\text{GHTF} &= \text{Class A (low)} \text{ to } \text{B (low-moderate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The *same physical device* may carry **different class labels** under different regulatory bodies. Always specify the **jurisdiction** when stating a class.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic Flowchart Logic

For any unknown device, apply this **four-question decision tree**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Q1: Is the device invasive?} \rightarrow \text{No: Rule 1 (Class I or IIa)} \\
&\text{Q2: Is it long-term implantable?} \rightarrow \text{Yes: Rule 8 (Class III if AIMD or CNS/CCS)} \\
&\text{Q3: Does it deliver energy?} \rightarrow \text{Yes: Rules 9-12 (Class IIa or IIb)} \\
&\text{Q4: Does it incorporate drugs / animal tissue / blood derivatives?} \rightarrow \text{Yes: Rules 14-18 (Class III)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each rule is **applied in sequence** until one rule fires. The classification decision is **deterministic** — there is no "average" or "weighted" outcome.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Global Medical Device Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    devInput["Medical Device Under Review"] --> q1{"Is it invasive?"}
    q1 -- "No" --> q1a{"Channels body fluids or stores substances?"}
    q1 -- "Yes" --> q2{"Is it implantable or long-term invasive?"}
    q1a -- "Yes" --> r1a["Class IIa / Class II"]
    q1a -- "No" --> r1b["Class I"]
    q2 -- "Yes" --> q2a{"In contact with CNS, CCS, or active implant?"}
    q2 -- "No, short-term" --> r2a["Class IIa"]
    q2a -- "Yes" --> r2b["Class III"]
    q2a -- "No" --> r2c["Class IIb"]
    devInput --> q3{"Energy imparting or measuring?"}
    q3 -- "Yes, hazardous to body" --> r3a["Class IIb"]
    q3 -- "Yes, non-hazardous" --> r3b["Class IIa"]
    devInput --> q4{"Incorporates drugs, animal tissue, or blood derivatives?"}
    q4 -- "Yes" --> r4a["Class III"]
    q4 -- "No" --> q4b{"Diagnostic or sterilization use?"}
    q4b -- "Yes" --> r4b["Class IIa / Class II"]
    q4b -- "No" --> r4c["Class I"]
```

## 4.2 Three-Framework Risk Tier Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph US_FDA["US FDA"]
        f1["Class I"]
        f2["Class II"]
        f3["Class III"]
    end
    subgraph EU_MDR["EU MDR 2017/745"]
        e1["Class I"]
        e2["Class IIa"]
        e3["Class IIb"]
        e4["Class III"]
    end
    subgraph GHTF["GHTF / IMDRF"]
        g1["Class A Low"]
        g2["Class B Low-Moderate"]
        g3["Class C Moderate-High"]
        g4["Class D High"]
    end
    f1 -.-> e1
    f1 -.-> g1
    f2 -.-> e2
    f2 -.-> e3
    f2 -.-> g2
    f2 -.-> g3
    f3 -.-> e4
    f3 -.-> g4
```

## 4.3 Regulatory Pathway Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Device Conceptualization"] --> cls["Classify per Risk Framework"]
    cls --> c1["Class I / A"]
    cls --> c2["Class II / IIa-b / B-C"]
    cls --> c3["Class III / D"]
    c1 --> qms1["ISO 13485 QMS"]
    c2 --> qms1
    c3 --> qms1
    c1 --> ctrl1["General Controls Only"]
    c2 --> ctrl2["Special Controls + 510k"]
    c3 --> ctrl3["Premarket Approval PMA"]
    ctrl1 --> market["Market Entry"]
    ctrl2 --> clinical1["Performance Testing + Clinical Data"]
    ctrl3 --> clinical2["Extensive Clinical Trials"]
    clinical1 --> market
    clinical2 --> market
    market --> pms["Post-Market Surveillance"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight:** All devices — irrespective of class — share the same **ISO 13485 QMS backbone**. Only the *depth* of evidence and *type* of approval pathway diverge. This is the central architectural truth of medical device regulation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Class I and Class III medical devices. Give two examples for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Class I | Class III |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Risk Level** | Low | High |
| **Regulatory Control** | General Controls only | General Controls + Premarket Approval (PMA) |
| **Clinical Trial Data** | Usually not required | Mandatory, extensive |
| **Examples** | Stethoscope, tongue depressor, elastic bandage | Heart valve, implantable pacemaker, coronary stent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award the 1st mark for the *risk level* statement, the 2nd mark for *one correct example* each, and the 3rd mark for any meaningful distinguishing feature (e.g., PMA vs. 510(k)).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three parameters used for the classification of medical devices as per EU MDR.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Duration of contact** with the patient — transient, short-term, or long-term.
2. **Invasiveness** — non-invasive, body-cavity invasive, surgically invasive, or implantable.
3. **Involvement of an active energy source** — whether the device delivers energy to or from the body.

> [!NOTE]
> Any **three out of the seven** EU MDR criteria are accepted — duration, invasiveness, body system, energy source, biological effect, chemical change, or animal/human tissue origin.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]

#### (a) Explain the FDA classification system for medical devices. Describe each class with its risk profile, regulatory pathway, and at least two examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **US FDA** classifies medical devices into **three regulatory classes** under **21 CFR Part 860**, based on the level of risk and the degree of regulatory control needed to ensure safety and effectiveness.

**(i) Class I — Low Risk Devices**
- These devices pose minimal potential harm to the user and are subject to the **least regulatory controls**.
- They are subject only to **General Controls** — establishment registration, device listing, Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP), proper labeling, and reporting of adverse events.
- Most Class I devices are **exempt from the 510(k) premarket notification** requirement.
- **Examples:** Tongue depressor, elastic bandage, surgical gloves, stethoscope, bedpan.

**(ii) Class II — Moderate Risk Devices**
- These devices pose a higher risk than Class I but lower than Class III, requiring **General Controls + Special Controls**.
- **Special Controls** include performance standards, post-market surveillance, patient registries, special labeling requirements, and guidance documents.
- The standard pathway is **510(k) Premarket Notification**, where the manufacturer must demonstrate that the device is **substantially equivalent** to a legally marketed predicate device.
- **Examples:** Blood pressure cuff, infusion pump, powered wheelchair, pregnancy test kit, surgical sutures.

**(iii) Class III — High Risk Devices**
- These devices are typically **life-sustaining, life-supporting, or implantable** and pose the highest risk.
- They require **General Controls + Premarket Approval (PMA)** — the most stringent regulatory pathway.
- PMA requires extensive **scientific evidence**, including **bench testing**, **animal studies**, and **clinical investigations**.
- **Examples:** Heart valve, implantable pacemaker, coronary stent, deep brain stimulator, cochlear implant.

**Summary Table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class I} &\rightarrow \text{Low Risk} \rightarrow \text{General Controls} \\
\text{Class II} &\rightarrow \text{Moderate Risk} \rightarrow \text{General} + \text{Special Controls} + 510(k) \\
\text{Class III} &\rightarrow \text{High Risk} \rightarrow \text{General Controls} + \text{PMA}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### (b) Explain the EU MDR classification system. Discuss the four classes with respect to risk, conformity assessment procedure, and at least two examples for each class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **EU Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR 2017/745)** classifies medical devices into **four classes** based on the level of risk and intended use, governed by **22 classification rules in Annex VIII**. The classification follows a **rule-based decision engine** — devices are sorted by applying rules sequentially.

**(i) Class I — Low Risk**
- Includes most non-invasive devices and devices that do not interact with vital organs.
- The manufacturer may perform **self-declaration of conformity** (no Notified Body required for most Class I devices).
- **Examples:** Stethoscope, eyeglasses, non-powered wheelchair, simple wound dressing.

**(ii) Class IIa — Low-to-Moderate Risk**
- Includes short-term invasive devices and non-invasive devices that channel or store body fluids.
- Requires involvement of a **Notified Body** for assessment of conformity.
- **Examples:** Hearing aid, short-term contact lens, surgical needle, blood bag.

**(iii) Class IIb — Moderate-to-High Risk**
- Includes long-term invasive devices and energy-imparting devices that may be hazardous to the body.
- Requires **stricter Notified Body scrutiny** including audits of the QMS and review of technical documentation.
- **Examples:** Ventilator, infusion pump, external defibrillator, dialysis machine.

**(iv) Class III — High Risk**
- Includes **active implantable medical devices (AIMDs)**, devices in direct contact with the **central nervous or circulatory systems**, and devices incorporating **medicinal substances or animal tissues**.
- Requires the **most stringent conformity assessment**, including **clinical investigation data**, **regular audits**, and **scrutiny of the technical file**.
- **Examples:** Heart valve, implantable pacemaker, drug-eluting stent, silicone breast implant.

**Conformity Assessment Summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Class I} &\rightarrow \text{Self-Declaration} \\
\text{Class IIa} &\rightarrow \text{Notified Body} + \text{Technical Doc Review} \\
\text{Class IIb} &\rightarrow \text{Notified Body} + \text{Stricter Audits} \\
\text{Class III} &\rightarrow \text{Notified Body} + \text{Clinical Investigation} + \text{Scrutiny}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often confuse **510(k)** with **PMA** — be precise: *510(k) is for Class II, PMA is for Class III.*
> - Many students write **"Class II = CE Marking"** — this is **wrong**. CE Marking applies to **all classes** in the EU. The Notified Body involvement differs by class.
> - Do **not** write vague examples like "surgical instruments" — examiners expect **specific named devices**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]

#### (a) Classify the following medical devices as per EU MDR and justify your answer in each case: **(i) Surgical scalpel, (ii) Implantable cardiac defibrillator, (iii) External hearing aid, (iv) Drug-eluting coronary stent. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Surgical Scalpel**
- **Classification:** **Class I**
- **Justification:** It is a **non-invasive or transient surgically invasive** device used for cutting tissue during surgery. It has **transient contact** (single use, brief duration), no energy source, and no biological effect on the body.
- **Rule applied:** EU MDR Rule 1 (non-invasive devices) or Rule 4 (transient surgical invasive).

**(ii) Implantable Cardiac Defibrillator (ICD)**
- **Classification:** **Class III**
- **Justification:** It is an **Active Implantable Medical Device (AIMD)** intended for **long-term implantation**, delivering electrical energy to the heart to manage life-threatening arrhythmias.
- **Rule applied:** EU MDR Rule 8 (long-term surgically invasive + AIMD) directly assigns it to Class III.

**(iii) External Hearing Aid**
- **Classification:** **Class IIa**
- **Justification:** It is a **non-invasive active device** that delivers acoustic energy to the body. Although not hazardous, it qualifies under Rule 9 / 10 as a Class IIa device due to its energy-delivering nature.
- **Rule applied:** EU MDR Rule 9 (active therapeutic devices delivering energy).

**(iv) Drug-Eluting Coronary Stent**
- **Classification:** **Class III**
- **Justification:** It is an **implantable long-term invasive device** in direct contact with the **central circulatory system** (coronary arteries) and additionally incorporates a **medicinal substance** (anti-proliferative drug) to prevent restenosis.
- **Rule applied:** EU MDR Rule 8 (implantable, CCS contact) and Rule 14 (incorporation of medicinal substance) — both lead to Class III.

**Final Answer Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Surgical Scalpel} &\rightarrow \text{Class I} \\
\text{Implantable Cardiac Defibrillator} &\rightarrow \text{Class III} \\
\text{External Hearing Aid} &\rightarrow \text{Class IIa} \\
\text{Drug-Eluting Coronary Stent} &\rightarrow \text{Class III}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### (b) Describe the GHTF / IMDRF classification framework. Compare it with the FDA and EU MDR systems, highlighting the advantages of global harmonization. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**GHTF / IMDRF Framework:**
The **Global Harmonization Task Force (GHTF)** — now succeeded by the **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)** — proposed a **four-tier harmonized classification model** based on increasing risk: **Class A (low), B (low-moderate), C (moderate-high), D (high)**.

**Core principles of the GHTF/IMDRF model:**
- Risk-based, not jurisdiction-based.
- Aligns with ISO 13485 QMS philosophy.
- Promotes a single global dossier structure.

**Comparative Mapping:**

| GHTF Class | Risk | Equivalent FDA | Equivalent EU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A | Low | Class I | Class I |
| B | Low-Moderate | Class II (low) | Class IIa |
| C | Moderate-High | Class II (high) | Class IIb |
| D | High | Class III | Class III |

**Advantages of Global Harmonization:**

1. **Reduced Regulatory Burden:** A manufacturer can use a **single technical file** across multiple jurisdictions, reducing documentation duplication by up to **60–70%**.
2. **Faster Market Access:** Harmonization shortens the **time-to-market**, particularly for breakthrough innovations in low-resource geographies.
3. **Lower Cost of Compliance:** Avoids re-testing, re-labeling, and re-submission for each country.
4. **Improved Patient Safety:** Standardized post-market surveillance across borders enables faster detection of adverse events.
5. **Encourages Innovation:** Small and medium enterprises (SMEs) can enter international markets without prohibitive regulatory costs.
6. **Convergence on Standards:** Adoption of **ISO 13485**, **ISO 14971**, and **IEC 62304** becomes globally consistent.

**Final Summary Statement:**

$$
\text{GHTF/IMDRF} \rightarrow \text{Single Global Language} \rightarrow \text{Aligns FDA, EU MDR, CDSCO, TGA, PMDA, Health Canada}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students write **"GHTF is a regulatory authority"** — it is **not**. It is a *voluntary harmonization body* of regulators and industry.
> - Avoid vague phrases like *"harmonization is good"* — examiners want **specific advantages** (cost, time, patient safety, innovation).
> - Always state the **four GHTF classes in order** — getting them out of sequence costs marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Classification is risk-based**, not feature-based. The question is always *"How much harm can this device cause?"*
- **FDA has 3 classes** (I, II, III) and the **EU MDR has 4 classes** (I, IIa, IIb, III). The **GHTF** uses **A, B, C, D**.
- **Class I devices** are subject only to **General Controls** (FDA) or **self-declaration** (EU).
- **Class II devices** require **510(k)** (FDA) or **Notified Body involvement** (EU).
- **Class III devices** require **Premarket Approval (PMA)** (FDA) — the most stringent pathway.
- **Active Implantable Medical Devices (AIMDs)** are **always Class III** under EU MDR (Rule 8).
- **Devices in contact with the central nervous or central circulatory system** are **always Class III**.
- **Devices incorporating medicinal substances or animal tissues** are **always Class III**.
- **Duration of contact** escalates risk: *Transient → Short-term → Long-term → Implantable*.
- **Same physical device** can have **different class labels** in different jurisdictions — always specify jurisdiction.
- **All devices**, regardless of class, require **ISO 13485 Quality Management System** compliance.
- **510(k) ≠ PMA**: 510(k) demonstrates *substantial equivalence* to a predicate; PMA requires *independent safety + effectiveness proof*.
- **Classification drives everything downstream** — design controls, testing depth, clinical trials, labeling, post-market surveillance, and cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mnemonic — "I-I-I-III = Eyes, Invasiveness, Implant, Integration":**
> - **Eyes** (look at duration, invasiveness, energy)
> - **Invasiveness** (non-invasive → invasive → implantable → AIMD)
> - **Implant** (Rule 8 = Class III if long-term implant)
> - **Integration** (with CNS/CCS = Class III)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and System FMEA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design and System FMEA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: DESIGN AND SYSTEM FMEA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a structured, systematic, and proactive engineering methodology used to identify, evaluate, prioritize, and document potential failure modes of a system, subsystem, component, or process — and to determine the effects of those failures on overall system performance, patient safety, and regulatory compliance.

In the context of **biomedical device design**, FMEA is mandated by **ISO 14971** (Application of risk management to medical devices) and is a cornerstone activity within the **Design Controls** framework of **21 CFR 820.30 (FDA)** and the **EU MDR 2017/745**. It is not merely a "checklist" — it is a **living, iterative engineering artifact** that evolves throughout the design and development life cycle.

### Two Specific Variants Discussed in KTU Module 2

| Variant | Focus | Primary Use Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | Failure modes introduced by design choices (geometry, materials, tolerances, software logic) | Concept → Detailed Design → Verification |
| **System FMEA (SFMEA)** | Failure modes at the **system-level interaction** between subsystems, hardware, software, environment, and user | System Architecture Definition → Integration |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes **System FMEA** as a **higher-level, end-to-end functional analysis** that evaluates *interactions* between subsystems — whereas Design FMEA drills into the components themselves.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart Surgeon's Pre-Op Checklist"

Imagine a cardiac surgeon about to implant a **left ventricular assist device (LVAD)**. Before surgery, the engineering team must mentally "rehearse" every single way the device *could* fail once it is inside a living human:

- What if the **driveline cable** fatigues and fractures? → Effect: catastrophic pump stoppage
- What if the **controller firmware** misreads a sensor by 5%? → Effect: incorrect RPM, suction event, hemolysis
- What if the **battery contacts** corrode in humid tropical climates (like Kerala's monsoons)? → Effect: sudden power loss

FMEA is the **engineering equivalent** of that exhaustive pre-op risk rehearsal. Instead of a scalpel, we use a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** to mathematically rank each imagined failure.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** FMEA answers **three questions** for every component/system:
> 1. **HOW** can it fail? *(Failure Mode)*
> 2. **WHAT** happens if it fails? *(Effect)*
> 3. **HOW BAD, HOW OFTEN, HOW DETECTABLE?** *(Severity × Occurrence × Detection)*

---

## 2. Origin & Regulatory Anchors (KTU High-Yield Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FMEA was originally developed by the **U.S. Military** in the 1940s (military standard **MIL-STD-1629**). It was later adopted by **NASA** (Apollo program), the **automotive industry (AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook, 2019)**, and finally became **mandatory in medical devices** under **ISO 14971:2019**.

For the **KTU 2024 PEBMT752** course, the following regulatory anchors are examinable:

- **ISO 14971:2019** — Risk management process for medical devices
- **IEC 62366-1:2015** — Usability engineering (interlinks with FMEA)
- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820.30** — Design Controls
- **EU MDR 2017/745, Annex I** — General Safety and Performance Requirements
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Core Elements of an FMEA Worksheet

A compliant FMEA worksheet (per **AIAG-VDA 2019** and **IEC 60812**) contains the following columns:

1. **Item / Function** — What is the component or subsystem, and what is its *intended* function?
2. **Failure Mode** — The *manner* in which the item fails to meet its intended function (e.g., "short circuit," "no output," "erratic output," "premature wear").
3. **Effect of Failure** — The *consequence* on the system, patient, operator, or environment.
4. **Severity (S)** — A numerical rating (typically **1 to 10**) of how serious the effect is.
5. **Cause of Failure** — The *root mechanism* that triggers the failure mode.
6. **Occurrence (O)** — A numerical rating (**1 to 10**) of how frequently the cause happens.
7. **Current Controls (Detection)** — The existing design verification, validation, or inspection methods.
8. **Detection (D)** — A numerical rating (**1 to 10**) of how likely the failure is to be *detected before reaching the patient*.
9. **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** — The composite metric.
10. **Recommended Actions** — Mitigation steps to reduce S, O, or D.
11. **Action Taken / Residual Risk** — Post-mitigation re-scoring.

## 2.2 The Master FMEA Equation — Risk Priority Number (RPN)

The classical FMEA risk score is computed as:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

Where:
- **S** = Severity rating (1–10)
- **O** = Occurrence rating (1–10)
- **D** = Detection rating (1–10)
- Theoretical maximum RPN = **10 × 10 × 10 = 1000**

> [!CAUTION]
> **AIAG-VDA 2019 Update:** The modern automotive/medical FMEA handbook de-emphasizes the raw RPN and introduces an **Action Priority (AP)** approach (High / Medium / Low). However, KTU 2024 exams still primarily test the **classical RPN method** — and the **RPN formula is mandatory** for board calculations. Master both.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Range / Units | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Severity | 1 → 10 | 1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic (death) |
| $O$ | Occurrence | 1 → 10 | 1 = very rare (≈1 in 1,000,000), 10 = very frequent (≥1 in 2) |
| $D$ | Detection | 1 → 10 | 1 = almost certain detection, 10 = cannot detect |
| $RPN$ | Risk Priority Number | 1 → 1000 | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ |
| $SPR$ | Severity Priority Rank | 1 → 10 | AIAG-VDA 2019 alternate scoring |
| $AP$ | Action Priority | H / M / L | High / Medium / Low |
| $RR$ | Risk Ratio | unitless | $RR = RPN_{\text{after}} / RPN_{\text{before}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL for KTU exams:** In tables, the absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) must NEVER use the `|` symbol inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the parser. Always use **$\vert x \vert$** or **$\mid x \mid$**.

## 2.4 Standard Severity, Occurrence, and Detection Rating Tables

### Severity Scale (S) — Biomedical Variant

| Rating | Effect on Patient / User | Example in a Device |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | **Catastrophic** — death or permanent disability | Ventilator fails to deliver O₂ |
| 9 | **Critical** — severe injury, life-threatening | Infusion pump delivers 10× dose |
| 8 | **Major** — serious injury, requires intervention | Pacemaker lead fracture |
| 7 | **Significant** — temporary injury | Hypothermia during dialysis |
| 6 | **Moderate** — increases monitoring | False ECG alarm (alarm fatigue) |
| 5 | **Marginal** — minor injury, no medical intervention | Skin irritation from electrode gel |
| 4 | **Minor** — inconvenience to user | Device heavier than spec |
| 3 | **Small** — noticed by discerning user only | LED slightly dimmer |
| 2 | **Very small** — no impact on function | Cosmetic blemish on housing |
| 1 | **None** — no effect | None |

### Occurrence Scale (O)

| Rating | Failure Probability (per device) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | $\geq 1$ in 2 | Very high — failure almost inevitable |
| 9 | 1 in 3 |  |
| 8 | 1 in 8 | High — repeated failure |
| 7 | 1 in 20 |  |
| 6 | 1 in 80 | Moderate |
| 5 | 1 in 400 |  |
| 4 | 1 in 2,000 | Low |
| 3 | 1 in 15,000 |  |
| 2 | 1 in 150,000 | Very low |
| 1 | $\leq 1$ in 1,500,000 | Failure unlikely |

### Detection Scale (D)

| Rating | Detectability | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | **Absolute uncertainty** | No inspection method exists |
| 9 | Very remote | Remote chance of detection |
| 8 | Remote | Low detection capability |
| 7 | Very low |  |
| 6 | Low |  |
| 5 | Moderate |  |
| 4 | Moderately high |  |
| 3 | High |  |
| 2 | Very high |  |
| 1 | **Almost certain** | Automatic 100% detection (e.g., watchdog) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In biomedical device development, FMEA serves **four engineering purposes**:

1. **Prioritization** — Tells the engineer *where* to spend limited verification resources first (highest RPN first).
2. **Risk Reduction** — Drives the design of **mitigations** (redundancy, fail-safe, alarms, software interlocks).
3. **Traceability** — Creates a documented **Design History File (DHF)** artifact acceptable to FDA Notified Body auditors.
4. **Cross-functional Communication** — A single FMEA sheet becomes the **lingua franca** between mechanical, electronic, software, quality, and clinical teams.

> [!TIP]
> For a **Defibrillator**, an FMEA might reveal that a "software watchdog failure" (S=10, O=3, D=2) gives RPN = **60**, while a "battery contact corrosion" (S=9, O=5, D=7) gives RPN = **315**. Despite the *severity* being lower, the *detectability* issue makes battery corrosion the **higher-priority risk** to mitigate first.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of the RPN Decision Threshold

The classical RPN threshold for "action required" is industry-specific. Below is the standard derivation of the **K-T Ratio (Risk Reduction Index)**, which is often asked in KTU 14-mark problems.

### Step 1 — Define Initial Risk

$$
RPN_{\text{initial}} = S_i \times O_i \times D_i
$$

> **Text:** The initial RPN is the product of the as-designed Severity, Occurrence, and Detection scores.

### Step 2 — Apply Design Mitigation

After implementing one or more mitigations (e.g., adding a redundant sensor, tightening a tolerance, adding a software self-test), each of the three scores may be reduced.

$$
RPN_{\text{mitigated}} = S_f \times O_f \times D_f
$$

> **Text:** The mitigated RPN is recomputed using the new post-mitigation scores.

### Step 3 — Compute Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR)

$$
RRR = \frac{RPN_{\text{mitigated}}}{RPN_{\text{initial}}}
$$

> **Text:** The ratio compares the post-mitigation risk to the original. A value $RRR < 1$ means mitigation was successful.

### Step 4 — Equivalent Percentage Reduction

$$
\% \text{Risk Reduction} = (1 - RRR) \times 100\%
$$

> **Text:** A more interpretable metric for management review and Design History File (DHF) records.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Infusion Pump FMEA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Pattern Question:** Given the table below for an **insulin infusion pump**, compute RPN, identify the highest-priority failure, and recommend a mitigation.

| Item | Failure Mode | Effect | S | Cause | O | Current Control | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drive motor | Stalls mid-delivery | Under-infusion → hyperglycemia | 9 | Brush wear | 5 | Stall detection (software) | 4 |
| Battery | Sudden voltage drop | Pump stops → missed dose | 10 | Cell sulfation | 3 | Battery test at boot | 7 |
| Pressure sensor | Drifts high | False occlusion alarm → nuisance | 4 | Sensor aging | 6 | Calibration check | 6 |
| User interface | Button stuck | Patient cannot silence alarm | 8 | Debris ingress | 4 | Tactile feedback | 5 |
| Microcontroller | Lockup | Total pump failure | 10 | Firmware bug | 4 | Watchdog timer | 3 |

### Step-by-Step RPN Computation

**Line 1 — Drive motor stall:**

$$
RPN_1 = S_1 \times O_1 \times D_1 = 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180
$$

> **Text:** Compute the product of the three integer scores.

**Line 2 — Battery sudden drop:**

$$
RPN_2 = 10 \times 3 \times 7 = 210
$$

> **Text:** The severe consequence (S=10) is partially offset by rare occurrence (O=3) but compounded by poor detection (D=7).

**Line 3 — Pressure sensor drift:**

$$
RPN_3 = 4 \times 6 \times 6 = 144
$$

> **Text:** Low severity (only nuisance alarm) keeps this below the action threshold.

**Line 4 — UI button stuck:**

$$
RPN_4 = 8 \times 4 \times 5 = 160
$$

> **Text:** Moderate across all three dimensions.

**Line 5 — Microcontroller lockup:**

$$
RPN_5 = 10 \times 4 \times 3 = 120
$$

> **Text:** Despite catastrophic severity, the watchdog timer gives excellent detection (D=3), lowering RPN.

### Step 6 — Ranking and Identification

$$
RPN_2 (210) > RPN_1 (180) > RPN_4 (160) > RPN_3 (144) > RPN_5 (120)
$$

> **Text:** The **battery sudden voltage drop** is the **highest-priority risk** in this pump's FMEA.

### Step 7 — Recommended Mitigation and Re-scoring

**Mitigation:** Add a **dual-cell redundant battery pack with continuous impedance monitoring and a low-battery pre-alert at 20% state-of-charge.**

Re-scored values:
- $S_f = 10$ (severity unchanged — patient consequence is the same)
- $O_f = 1$ (impedance monitoring reduces occurrence to "very rare")
- $D_f = 2$ (continuous monitoring — almost certain detection)

$$
RPN_{2,\text{after}} = 10 \times 1 \times 2 = 20
$$

$$
\% \text{Risk Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{20}{210}\right) \times 100\% \approx 90.48\%
$$

> **Text:** The mitigation reduces risk by over 90% — a strong KTU answer for the "action taken" column.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Design FMEA vs. System FMEA

| Dimension | **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** | **System FMEA (SFMEA)** |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | **Component-level** failure modes (gears, ICs, welds, software modules) | **System-architecture-level** interactions (subsystem-to-subsystem, user-to-machine) |
| Initiated at | Detailed design phase | Concept / system architecture phase |
| Primary question | "How can this specific part fail?" | "How can the *integrated system* fail to deliver its clinical function?" |
| Resolution mechanism | Component substitution, derating, tolerance tightening | Redundancy, fail-safe architecture, alarms, interlocks |
| Typical user | Component / sub-system engineer | Systems engineer / risk manager |
| Example — Pacemaker | "Conductor fracture in the lead coil" | "EMI from MRI causes inappropriate pacing inhibition" |
| Reference standard | **IEC 60812**, AIAG-VDA 2019 | **MIL-STD-1629A**, ISO 14971 Annex C |
| Output artifact | Component-level RPN ledger | System Hazard Analysis + Risk Management File |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Module 2 specifically asks students to *contrast* the two. The exam often tests whether the student correctly identifies that **System FMEA is broader and considers external/environmental interactions**, while Design FMEA is **internal and part-specific**.

## 3.4 The Seven-Step FMEA Process (Procedural Derivation)

The complete FMEA execution workflow is formalized in **IEC 60812:2006** as seven sequential steps:

$$
\text{Step 1: Scope Definition} \rightarrow \text{Step 2: System Decomposition} \rightarrow \text{Step 3: Failure Mode Identification}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Step 4: Effect & Cause Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Step 5: Risk Scoring (S, O, D)}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Step 6: RPN Computation \& Ranking} \rightarrow \text{Step 7: Action \& Verification}
$$

> **Text:** Each step is documented and becomes a row/entry in the FMEA worksheet. The "loop" between Step 7 and Step 3 is **iterative** — re-scoring is mandatory after every mitigation.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FMEA Workflow — Sequential Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **seven-step FMEA execution loop** as a process flow, mapping interactions between the systems engineer, design engineer, and quality/regulatory stakeholder.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[stepA: Define Scope and Boundaries] --> B[stepB: System Decomposition into Subsystems and Components]
    B --> C[stepC: Identify Potential Failure Modes per Item]
    C --> D[stepD: Determine Effects and Root Causes]
    D --> E[stepE: Score Severity Occurrence and Detection]
    E --> F[stepF: Compute RPN and Rank Risks]
    F --> G[stepG: RPN above threshold]
    G -- Yes --> H[stepH: Recommend Mitigation Actions]
    H --> I[stepI: Implement Design Changes]
    I --> J[stepJ: Re-score and Re-compute RPN]
    J --> E
    G -- No --> K[stepK: Document and Archive in DHF]
    K --> L[stepL: FMEA Complete for this Iteration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice the **closed-loop feedback** from Step J back to Step E. This represents the **iterative re-scoring mandate** after every mitigation. KTU examiners will deduct marks if you draw FMEA as a strictly linear process.

## 4.2 Design FMEA vs. System FMEA — Comparative Architecture Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph sysFMEA[SYSTEM FMEA SCOPE]
        s1[stepS1: External Environment] --> s2[stepS2: Human Factors]
        s2 --> s3[stepS3: Subsystem to Subsystem Interaction]
        s3 --> s4[stepS4: Power and Data Flow Integrity]
    end

    subgraph desFMEA[DESIGN FMEA SCOPE]
        d1[stepD1: Mechanical Components] --> d2[stepD2: Electronic Components]
        d2 --> d3[stepD3: Software Modules]
        d3 --> d4[stepD4: Materials and Manufacturing Tolerances]
    end

    sysFMEA --> integrate[stepI: Integrated System Boundary]
    desFMEA --> integrate
    integrate --> out[stepOUT: Patient Safety and Clinical Outcome]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *top* block (System FMEA) handles "outside-in" risks — the world acting on the device. The *bottom* block (Design FMEA) handles "inside-out" risks — the device's own parts failing. Both converge at the **integrated system boundary**, which is exactly where patient harm is realized.

## 4.3 Risk Decision Matrix — RPN Threshold Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[stepStart: Compute RPN] --> judge{RPN Threshold}

    judge -- Greater than 200 --> high[stepHigh: RED - Immediate Action Required]
    judge -- 100 to 200 --> med[stepMed: YELLOW - Mitigation Recommended]
    judge -- Less than 100 --> low[stepLow: GREEN - Accept or Monitor]

    high --> action[stepAction: Design Change Mandatory]
    med --> review[stepReview: Engineering Review]
    low --> doc[stepDoc: Document in Risk File]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The threshold values (200, 100) above are **typical industry defaults**. Always state in your answer: *"Thresholds are organization-specific and must be defined in the Quality Management System (QMS)."*

## 4.4 Biomedical FMEA Causal Chain — Pacemaker Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cause1[stepC1: Lead Conductor Fatigue] --> fm1[stepFM1: Open Circuit]
    cause2[stepC2: Header Seal Degradation] --> fm2[stepFM2: Body Fluid Ingress]
    cause3[stepC3: Firmware Race Condition] --> fm3[stepFM3: Telemetry Loss]

    fm1 --> eff1[stepE1: Loss of Pacing - Bradycardia]
    fm2 --> eff2[stepE2: Short Circuit - Inappropriate Shock]
    fm3 --> eff3[stepE3: No Remote Monitoring]

    eff1 --> harm1[stepH1: Syncope or Cardiac Arrest]
    eff2 --> harm2[stepH2: Patient Pain and Tissue Damage]
    eff3 --> harm3[stepH3: Missed Clinical Follow-up]
```

> **Text:** Each *cause* maps to a *failure mode*, which produces an *effect*, which results in *patient harm*. This is the exact Cause → Mode → Effect → Harm chain that FMEA traces row-by-row.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing S, O, D ratings:** Many students invert the *Detection* scale. **Remember:** D = 10 means the failure is *nearly impossible* to detect (WORST), D = 1 means *almost certainly* detected (BEST).
> 2. **Forgetting to re-score:** Mitigation is meaningless without a *re-computed* RPN. Always show the new RPN explicitly.
> 3. **Treating FMEA as one-time:** FMEA is **iterative**. Failing to draw the feedback loop costs 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Mixing DFMEA and SFMEA scope:** In a System FMEA answer, do *not* list component-level failure modes (e.g., "capacitor C12 shorts"). Stay at the *subsystem interaction* level.
> 5. **No RPN threshold stated:** Always declare your organization's RPN action threshold in the answer.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA). State any two differences between Design FMEA and System FMEA.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** FMEA is a systematic, proactive engineering methodology used to identify potential failure modes of a product or process, evaluate their effects on system performance and patient safety, and prioritize mitigation actions using a Risk Priority Number (RPN) computed as $RPN = S \times O \times D$.

**Two differences (any two of the following):**

| Aspect | Design FMEA | System FMEA |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Component / part level | System architecture / interaction level |
| Initiated at | Detailed design phase | Concept / system definition phase |
| Focus | "How does this part fail?" | "How does the integrated system fail clinically?" |
| Reference | IEC 60812 | MIL-STD-1629A, ISO 14971 Annex C |

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark per difference)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]
**Explain the significance of the Detection (D) rating in an FMEA. Why is a *lower* D value considered better?**

**Model Answer:**

The Detection (D) rating quantifies the **probability that the failure mode will be detected *before* it reaches the patient or end-user**, by means of existing design controls, inspections, or automated self-tests.

- D ranges from **1 (almost certain detection)** to **10 (absolute uncertainty — no detection possible)**.
- A **lower D value is better** because it means the failure is *likely to be caught* by automated alarms, watchdogs, calibration checks, or built-in self-tests (BIST) before it manifests clinically.
- A high D (e.g., 8, 9, 10) indicates a **latent, silent failure** — extremely dangerous in devices like pacemakers, defibrillators, and ventilators, where undetected failure can be fatal.

**Example:** A microcontroller watchdog timer reduces D from 8 to 3, dropping the RPN significantly even if S and O remain unchanged.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for explanation + 1 mark for example)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Define FMEA. With a neat block diagram, explain the **seven-step FMEA execution workflow** as per **IEC 60812:2006**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a **patient monitor (ECG + SpO₂)** with the following partial FMEA data:

| Subsystem | Failure Mode | S | O | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ECG front-end amp | Saturation on high-amplitude QRS | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| SpO₂ sensor LED | Open circuit (no light) | 8 | 4 | 3 |
| Display driver | Flickering pixels | 3 | 5 | 6 |
| Alarm speaker | No sound output | 9 | 3 | 4 |
| Battery charger | Overvoltage | 9 | 4 | 6 |

Compute the **RPN** for each row, identify the **highest-priority failure**, and recommend **two specific mitigations** for it. Re-compute the RPN after mitigation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** FMEA is a systematic methodology that identifies potential failure modes, assesses their effects and causes, and prioritizes risk mitigation using the Risk Priority Number, defined as:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the workflow block diagram in Section 4.1. The seven steps are:

1. Define scope and boundaries of analysis.
2. Decompose system into subsystems and components.
3. Identify potential failure modes for each item.
4. Determine effects on system/patient and root causes.
5. Score Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D).
6. Compute RPN and rank in descending order.
7. Recommend actions, implement, and re-score (iterative loop).

**Explanation (2 marks):** The workflow is **iterative**, not linear. After each mitigation cycle, the engineer returns to Step 5 to re-score. The output feeds directly into the **Risk Management File** and **Design History File (DHF)** as required by **ISO 14971** and **21 CFR 820.30**.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute each RPN (3 marks):**

$$
RPN_{\text{ECG amp}} = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210
$$

$$
RPN_{\text{SpO2 LED}} = 8 \times 4 \times 3 = 96
$$

$$
RPN_{\text{Display}} = 3 \times 5 \times 6 = 90
$$

$$
RPN_{\text{Speaker}} = 9 \times 3 \times 4 = 108
$$

$$
RPN_{\text{Charger}} = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216
$$

**Step 2 — Rank in descending order (1 mark):**

$$
RPN_{\text{Charger}} (216) > RPN_{\text{ECG amp}} (210) > RPN_{\text{Speaker}} (108) > RPN_{\text{SpO2 LED}} (96) > RPN_{\text{Display}} (90)
$$

**Step 3 — Identify highest-priority failure (1 mark):**

The **battery charger overvoltage** failure has the highest RPN = **216**, driven by **high severity (patient harm from battery rupture/fire)** and **poor detection (silent until failure)**.

**Step 4 — Two specific mitigations (1 mark):**

1. Add a **hardware overvoltage protection IC (OVP)** at the battery input with a clamp voltage of 4.25 V/cell.
2. Implement a **software-based charger monitoring task** that runs every 100 ms and triggers a system shutdown + audible alarm if voltage exceeds safe threshold.

**Step 5 — Re-compute RPN after mitigation (1 mark):**

After mitigation:
- S remains 9 (overvoltage is still dangerous if it happens).
- O reduces to 2 (now very rare with OVP).
- D reduces to 2 (continuous software monitoring — almost certain detection).

$$
RPN_{\text{after}} = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36
$$

$$
\% \text{Risk Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{36}{216}\right) \times 100\% \approx 83.33\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Mitigation reduces RPN by ~83%, which is a strong KTU answer for residual risk acceptability.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **purpose of FMEA in biomedical device design**. List the **regulatory standards** that mandate or reference FMEA in medical device development. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **Design FMEA** and **System FMEA** with two examples each from a **hemodialysis machine** context. Construct a **sample System FMEA table** for the *dialysate preparation subsystem* of a hemodialysis machine with at least **three failure modes**, including computed RPN values and one mitigation. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Purpose of FMEA (4 marks):**

1. **Risk Identification & Prioritization** — Proactively uncovers latent failure modes before clinical use and ranks them by RPN to focus engineering effort.
2. **Patient Safety** — Directly supports **ISO 14971** risk management, preventing patient harm from device malfunction.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Creates documented evidence of design due diligence for FDA 21 CFR 820.30 and EU MDR 2017/745 audits.
4. **Design Improvement** — Drives design changes (redundancy, fail-safe, derating) that improve reliability, usability, and post-market performance.

**Regulatory Standards (3 marks — list any three):**

| Standard | Relevance |
|---|---|
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Primary risk management standard — FMEA is the principal technique |
| **IEC 60812:2006** | Methodology standard for FMEA execution |
| **IEC 62366-1:2015** | Usability engineering — interfaces with FMEA for use-error analysis |
| **FDA 21 CFR 820.30** | Design Controls — FMEA is required evidence in DHF |
| **EU MDR 2017/745, Annex I** | General Safety and Performance Requirements |
| **AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019)** | Modern FMEA methodology reference |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Differentiation with Hemodialysis Examples (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Design FMEA | System FMEA |
|---|---|---|
| Example 1 | "Conductivity sensor probe corrodes" (part-level) | "Dialysate temperature exceeds 42°C during preparation → hemolysis in patient" (system-level interaction) |
| Example 2 | "Solenoid valve O-ring hardens" (component) | "Air detector fails to alarm when venous line dislodges → air embolism" (system interaction with environment) |

**Sample System FMEA Table for Dialysate Preparation (4 marks):**

| Item / Function | Failure Mode | Effect on Patient | S | Cause | O | Current Control | D | RPN | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mix dialysate concentrate with RO water | Incorrect mixing ratio | Electrolyte imbalance, cardiac arrhythmia | 9 | Conductivity sensor drift | 5 | Periodic calibration | 6 | **270** | Add redundant conductivity sensor + online auto-calibration |
| Heat dialysate to body temperature | Over-temperature (>42°C) | Hemolysis, hyperthermia | 10 | Heater control failure | 4 | Software limit at 41°C | 5 | **200** | Dual independent temperature cut-offs (hardware + software) |
| Remove chlorine/chloramines | Carbon filter exhausted | Patient exposure to chloramine, hemolysis | 9 | Filter not replaced on schedule | 6 | Maintenance log check | 7 | **378** | Add total chlorine sensor with auto-shutdown + RFID-tagged filter replacement |

**Highest Priority:** The **chloramine breakthrough** failure (RPN = 378) is the top risk due to the combination of catastrophic severity, frequent occurrence (filter exhaustion is a known issue), and poor detection (current maintenance logs are unreliable).

**Recommended Mitigation:** Install a **continuous total chlorine amperometric sensor** at the dialysate inlet, set a software threshold at 0.1 ppm, and trigger an **audible alarm + automatic bypass to isolate the patient from contaminated dialysate** within 5 seconds.

**Re-scored RPN after mitigation:**

- S = 9 (unchanged)
- O = 2 (chloramine breakthrough now rare with auto-shutdown)
- D = 2 (continuous online detection)

$$
RPN_{\text{after}} = 9 \times 2 \times 2 = 36
$$

$$
\% \text{Risk Reduction} = \left(1 - \frac{36}{378}\right) \times 100\% \approx 90.48\%
$$

> **Conclusion for KTU valuation:** The chloramine mitigation reduces RPN by over 90%, demonstrating that the system-level mitigation (continuous monitoring + auto-shutdown) is far more effective than relying on periodic maintenance alone.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist in the **last 10 minutes** before your KTU exam.

- [ ] **FMEA = Failure Mode and Effects Analysis** — proactive, systematic, iterative.
- [ ] **Master Equation:** $RPN = S \times O \times D$, where each factor is on a **1–10** scale.
- [ ] **Theoretical RPN max = 1000.** Most industries use a **threshold of 100 or 200** for "action required."
- [ ] **D scale is INVERTED in intuition:** D = 10 is **worst** (cannot detect), D = 1 is **best** (almost certain detection).
- [ ] **S is NEVER reduced by mitigation** in classical FMEA — only O and D can be lowered.
- [ ] **DFMEA** = component / part level; **SFMEA** = system / architecture / interaction level.
- [ ] **FMEA is MANDATORY under ISO 14971** and **FDA 21 CFR 820.30 (Design Controls)**.
- [ ] **AIAG-VDA 2019** modernized FMEA with the **Action Priority (AP)** approach — but KTU 2024 still primarily tests **RPN**.
- [ ] **Workflow is ITERATIVE:** Re-score after every mitigation. Always include the feedback loop in diagrams.
- [ ] **Document everything** in the Design History File (DHF) — auditors WILL ask for FMEA records.
- [ ] **Seven-step process:** Scope → Decompose → Failure Modes → Effects/Causes → Score → Rank → Act & Re-score.
- [ ] **Common biomedical example:** Insulin pump, pacemaker, defibrillator, hemodialysis, infusion pump, ventilator, patient monitor.
- [ ] **Metric to highlight in 14-mark answers:** Always compute **% Risk Reduction** to show mitigation effectiveness.
- [ ] **For KTU diagrams:** Use Mermaid or hand-drawn block diagrams showing **Scope → Subsystems → Failure Modes → Effects → Patient Harm** as a left-to-right or top-down flow.
- [ ] **Examiner's pet peeve:** Never mix Design FMEA scope into a System FMEA answer (or vice versa). Stay at the right level of abstraction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hardware Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Biomedical Hardware Design

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Design** in the context of biomedical devices is the systematic engineering discipline of architecting, selecting, dimensioning, and interconnecting physical electronic and electromechanical subsystems — including sensors, analog front-ends (AFEs), microcontrollers/processors, power management circuits, communication modules, isolation barriers, and human-machine interfaces — to reliably acquire, condition, digitize, process, transmit, and display physiological signals while satisfying clinical safety, regulatory (IEC 60601-1), power-budget, and form-factor constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the hardware design process for a medical device is treated as a **constraint-satisfaction problem** where the designer must simultaneously optimize across five competing axes:

1. **Signal Integrity** (fidelity of acquired biopotential)
2. **Patient Safety** (leakage current, isolation, defibrillation protection)
3. **Power Efficiency** (battery life, thermal envelope)
4. **Miniaturization** (form factor, wearable compliance)
5. **Regulatory Compliance** (IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485 traceability)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Biomedical Device as a Human Nervous System"**
>
> Imagine the human nervous system: your **skin** senses the environment (this is your **sensor/transducer** in a medical device). The **spinal cord** amplifies and filters the rough signal (this is the **signal conditioning circuit**). The **brain** interprets the signal and makes a decision (this is the **microcontroller/DSP**). The **motor neurons** send commands to muscles (these are your **actuators and display**). Finally, the **speech organs** let you communicate the result to others (these are your **wireless/wired communication modules**).
>
> In a wearable ECG patch, the dry electrodes are the "skin", the instrumentation amplifier is the "spinal cord", the ARM Cortex-M0 is the "brain", the vibration motor for haptic alert is the "muscle", and the Bluetooth Low Energy radio is the "speech organ". A well-designed biomedical device is essentially an *engineered artificial nervous system* that interfaces safely with a real human one.

### 1.3 The Five Canonical Stages of Biomedical Hardware Design

| Stage | Sub-System | Example Components | KTU Exam Weightage |
|-------|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1. **Transduction** | Sensors / Electrodes | Ag/AgCl ECG electrodes, SpO₂ photodiode, thermistor | High |
| 2. **Conditioning** | Amplifier + Filter + Isolation | INA128, AD8221, optocoupler, isolation transformer | Very High |
| 3. **Conversion** | ADC / DAC | 16/24-bit sigma-delta ADS1292R, SAR AD7685 | High |
| 4. **Processing** | MCU / DSP / FPGA | MSP430, STM32, ADSP-BF706, Zynq | Very High |
| 5. **Actuation / I-O** | Display, Buzzer, BLE, WiFi | OLED, haptic motor, nRF52840, ESP32 | Medium |

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants and metrics appear in **every** KTU biomedical hardware numerical:
> - **Mains frequency in India:** **50 Hz** (power-line interference dominates ECG/EEG design).
> - **ECG bandwidth:** **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (diagnostic-grade).
> - **EEG bandwidth:** **0.5 Hz – 70 Hz** (clinical).
> - **EMG bandwidth:** **20 Hz – 500 Hz**.
> - **Maximum allowable patient leakage current (IEC 60601-1, Type CF, applied part on heart):** **10 µA (AC) / 10 µA (DC)** under normal conditions, **50 µA** under single-fault.
> - **Defibrillation-proof requirement:** **5 kV** transient withstand across patient electrodes.
> - **Standard CMOS logic low/high:** **0 V – 0.8 V (LOW)**, **2.0 V – 5.0 V (HIGH)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Biomedical Signal Acquisition Chain (Block-by-Block)

Every biomedical instrument, from a ₹500 pulse oximeter to a ₹50 lakh ICU monitor, follows a **fixed canonical chain**. KTU examiners love to test each block individually.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Chain:** *Patient → Electrode/Sensor → Pre-Amp → Isolation → Main Amp → Filter → ADC → DSP/MCU → Display/Comms*

#### 2.1.1 Block 1 — Electrode / Sensor Interface

The electrode-skin interface behaves as a **half-cell potential** in series with a **parallel RC network** (Helmholtz double layer).

- Half-cell potential of Ag/AgCl: **+0.223 V** (reference for ECG).
- Skin-electrode impedance at 1 Hz: **\Vert Z \vert \approx 100 k\Omega**, dropping to **\Vert Z \vert \approx 1 k\Omega** at 1 kHz (impedance decreases with frequency).
- For an instrumentation amplifier to reject common-mode noise, the electrode impedance mismatch must be **< 5 k\Omega**, otherwise the CMRR degrades sharply.

#### 2.1.2 Block 2 — Pre-Amplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier — INA)

The INA is the *heart* of biomedical front-end design. The KTU 2024 syllabus dedicates significant weight to its derivation.

**Differential gain of a 3-op-amp INA:**

$$
A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first stage provides **differential gain** with **unity common-mode gain**, the second stage (difference amplifier) provides **further differential gain and converts differential to single-ended**. This two-stage topology yields very high **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)**, typically **> 100 dB** at DC for premium INAs like AD8221.

**CMRR formula:**

$$
CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_{diff}}{A_{CM}}\right)
$$

**Input-referred noise (RTI) for a biopotential front-end** typically needs to be **< 1 µV RMS** in the 0.05–150 Hz band, which is below the smallest ECG P-wave amplitude.

#### 2.1.3 Block 3 — Filtering

Biomedical hardware uses three filter tiers:

| Tier | Type | Purpose | Typical Corner Frequency |
|------|------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1st | **High-Pass (HPF)** | Remove DC electrode offset (up to ±300 mV) and motion baseline wander | f_c = 0.05 Hz (ECG) |
| 2nd | **Low-Pass (LPF)** | Anti-aliasing + high-frequency noise suppression | f_c = 150 Hz (ECG), 70 Hz (EEG) |
| 3rd | **Notch** | Reject 50 Hz power-line interference | f_c = 50 Hz, Q = 8–30 |

**1st-order RC filter cutoff:**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

**Active 2nd-order Sallen-Key Butterworth (maximally flat) gain:**

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_b}{R_a}
$$

#### 2.1.4 Block 4 — Isolation Barrier (Patient Safety)

A medical device connected to mains MUST provide **galvanic isolation** between the patient-connected circuit and the mains-referenced circuit. The three IEC 60601-1 recognized Means of Patient Protection (MOPP) are:

1. **Basic insulation** (1 MOPP) — single layer of insulation.
2. **Reinforced insulation** (2 MOPP) — equivalent to double insulation.
3. **Protective earth** — chassis grounding.

Isolation can be implemented via:

- **Optocouplers** (e.g., 4N35, IL300) — bandwidth limited to ~100 kHz, good for digital isolation.
- **Isolation transformers** (e.g., ADuM1411, Texas Instruments ISO series) — capacitive/inductive isolation up to 5 kV RMS.
- **Analog Devices iCoupler** — integrated transformer isolation, multi-channel.

**Creepage distance on PCB** between isolated sides (IEC 60601-1, pollution degree 2): **8 mm minimum** for reinforced insulation at 250 V RMS mains.

#### 2.1.5 Block 5 — ADC and Sampling

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** mandates:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

In practice, biomedical systems use **oversampling** (f_s = 5–10 × f_max) to ease analog anti-aliasing filter design.

**Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) from SNR:**

$$
ENOB = \frac{SNR_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}
$$

**Quantization noise (LSB) for a bipolar ±V_ref ADC:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{2 V_{ref}}{2^n}
$$

#### 2.1.6 Block 6 — Processing & Power Budget

A KTU-favourite numerical is the **battery-life estimation**:

$$
t_{battery} \text{ (hours)} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{total} \text{ (mA)}}
$$

where $I_{total}$ is the sum of all subsystem currents.

### 2.2 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** The following table covers **≥ 85%** of biomedical hardware numericals asked in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | INA Differential Gain | $A_v = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2}$ | All resistances in $\Omega$ | 10 – 1000 |
| 2 | CMRR | $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A_d / A_{cm})$ | $A_d, A_{cm}$ dimensionless | > 100 dB |
| 3 | RC Cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F | 0.05 – 500 Hz |
| 4 | Sallen-Key Q (Butterworth) | $Q = \frac{1}{3 - A_v}$ | $A_v < 3$ for stability | Q = 0.707 |
| 5 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | 500 Hz ECG |
| 6 | ADC Quantization Step | $\Delta V = \frac{2 V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | V | 0.153 mV (n=16) |
| 7 | ENOB | $ENOB = \frac{SNR_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | 14 – 16 bits |
| 8 | Patient Leakage Limit (Type CF) | $I_{leak} \leq 10\ \mu A$ normal, $\leq 50\ \mu A$ SFC | µA | IEC 60601-1 |
| 9 | Battery Life | $t = C_{mAh} / I_{load}$ | hours | 24 h wearable |
| 10 | Thermal Noise (Johnson) | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V/√Hz | 4 nV/√Hz @ 1 kΩ |
| 11 | Op-amp Slew Rate Limit | $SR \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_{peak}$ | V/µs | > 1 V/µs ECG |
| 12 | Defibrillation Withstand | $V_{test} \geq 5 kV$ transient | kV | IEC 60601-1 |

Where the Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise uses:
- Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- Temperature: $T = 310$ K (body temperature, **37 °C**)
- Bandwidth: $\Delta f$ in Hz

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Hardware Design Mastery

- **Wearable ECG patches** (AliveCor KardiaMobile, Apple Watch) use the exact chain we described.
- **Implantable pacemakers** use ultra-low-power MSP430 + INA + isolation capacitors + hermetic titanium packaging.
- **ICU patient monitors** (Philips IntelliVue) use 24-bit sigma-delta ADCs (ADS1298) with 8 channels.
- **Defibrillators** use a high-voltage capacitor bank + H-bridge IGBT, switched via optoisolated gate drivers.
