# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define biomedical device. Differentiate between a medical device and a pharmaceutical drug with two examples each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — Examiner Distribution):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** A **biomedical device** is any instrument, apparatus, implant, machine, software, or related article intended by the manufacturer for the diagnosis, prevention, monitoring, treatment, or alleviation of disease or injury in human beings, and which achieves its principal intended action by **physical, mechanical, thermal, or chemical means** (not pharmacological).
>
> **Differentiation (2 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Medical Device | Pharmaceutical Drug |
> |---|---|---|
> | Mode of action | Physical / mechanical / chemical | Pharmacological / metabolic / immunological |
> | Examples | Cardiac pacemaker, hip implant, blood glucose meter | Aspirin, Insulin, Paracetamol |

### **Question 2 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is biocompatibility? List the three ISO 10993 Tier-1 tests with their ISO clause numbers.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Biocompatibility (1 mark):** The ability of a material to perform with an **appropriate host response** in a specific application. It is a *system property*, not an intrinsic material property.
>
> **ISO 10993 Tier-1 Tests (2 marks — ½ mark each + ½ mark for clause numbers):**
> 1. **Cytotoxicity** — ISO 10993-5
> 2. **Sensitization** — ISO 10993-10
> 3. **Irritation / Intracutaneous Reactivity** — ISO 10993-10

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Classify biomedical devices according to the US FDA. Explain with suitable examples the regulatory pathway for each class. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Classification (3 marks):**
> The US FDA classifies medical devices into three classes based on the **level of risk** and the **regulatory control** required to ensure safety and effectiveness.
>
> | Class | Risk | Description | Examples |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Class I** | Low | General controls; minimal harm potential | Stethoscope, elastic bandage, surgical gloves |
> | **Class II** | Moderate | General + special controls; performance standards | Infusion pump, blood pressure monitor, surgical sutures |
> | **Class III** | High | Life-supporting/sustaining or implantable; high risk | Pacemaker, heart valve, silicone breast implant |
>
> **Regulatory Pathway (4 marks):**
> - **Class I:** Only **General Controls** apply (registration, listing, GMP). Most are **exempt** from premarket notification.
> - **Class II:** Requires **510(k) Premarket Notification** — manufacturer demonstrates **substantial equivalence** to a predicate device.
> - **Class III:** Requires **Premarket Approval (PMA)** — the most stringent pathway. Manufacturer must provide **scientific evidence** (clinical trials, bench testing) of safety and effectiveness. Review period ≈ 6–18 months; cost ≈ $30–100 M.

#### (b) Explain the Foreign Body Reaction (FBR) with a neat sequence diagram. Discuss how the FBR influences the design of long-term implants. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **FBR Sequence (4 marks):**
> When any foreign material is implanted, the body mounts a stereotyped defensive reaction:
>
> 1. **Protein Adsorption (seconds):** Albumin, fibrinogen, and HMWK form a provisional matrix on the surface.
> 2. **Acute Inflammation (minutes to days):** Neutrophils, then macrophages migrate to the site.
> 3. **Chronic Inflammation (days to weeks):** Lymphocytes, plasma cells, foreign body giant cells (FBGCs) dominate.
> 4. **Granulation Tissue (1–2 weeks):** Fibroblasts and capillaries infiltrate.
> 5. **Fibrous Encapsulation (weeks to months):** A dense, avascular collagen capsule isolates the implant.
>
> **Design Implications (3 marks):**
> 1. **Surface modification** (e.g., hydrophilic coatings, nano-texturing) is used to control protein adsorption and reduce chronic inflammation.
> 2. **Drug-eluting coatings** (e.g., dexamethasone, sirolimus) are applied to suppress the local immune response for **stents** and **glaucoma drainage devices**.
> 3. **Porous architectures** (pore size 100–500 μm) allow vascular ingrowth, integrating the implant into host tissue rather than isolating it — this **eliminates** the capsule in devices like **porous acetabular cups** and **osseointegrated dental implants**.
> 4. **Material choice** (bioinert Ti, Al₂O₃ vs. bioabsorbable polymers) is matched to the desired FBR outcome — long-term isolation vs. gradual resorption.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Define biomaterial. Classify biomaterials with examples and explain any one class in detail. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** A **biomaterial** is a *natively occurring or synthetic substance* engineered to interface with biological systems for medical purposes — to **evaluate, treat, augment, or replace** any tissue, organ, or function of the body.
>
> **Classification (2 marks):**
>
> | Class | Examples |
> |---|---|
> | **Metals** | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Co-Cr-Mo, Nitinol |
> | **Polymers** | UHMWPE, PMMA, PLA, PGA, Silicone |
> | **Ceramics** | Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, Hydroxyapatite, Bioglass |
> | **Composites** | Carbon-fiber/PEEK, HA-coated Ti |
> | **Natural** | Collagen, chitosan, silk fibroin |
>
> **Detailed Explanation — Metallic Biomaterials (4 marks):**
> - **Titanium and its alloys (Ti-6Al-4V):** Most widely used for orthopedic and dental implants. Possess high specific strength, excellent corrosion resistance (due to a passive TiO₂ layer), and a Young's modulus (~110 GPa) much closer to bone than stainless steel. Biocompatible and **osseointegrative** when surface-treated.
> - **316L Stainless Steel:** Cost-effective; used for **temporary** fixation devices (bone plates, pins). The "L" denotes low carbon content (< 0.03%), reducing carbide precipitation and corrosion.
> - **Cobalt-Chrome (Co-Cr-Mo):** Used in articulating surfaces (femoral heads) due to high wear resistance and hardness.
> - **Nitinol (Ni-Ti):** Exhibits **superelasticity** and **shape memory**; used in self-expanding vascular stents and orthodontic wires.
> - **Concerns:** Metal ion release (Ni, Cr, V) can cause hypersensitivity; long-term systemic effects are an active research area.

#### (b) A Ti-6Al-4V tensile specimen of diameter 10 mm and gauge length 30 mm yields at a load of 24,500 N. Calculate the yield stress and the yield strain. Given the elastic modulus of Ti-6Al-4V is 110 GPa, find the elongation at the yield point. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area (1 mark):**
> $$A_0 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (10)^2}{4} = 25\pi = 78.54 \;\text{mm}^2$$
>
> **Step 2 — Yield Stress (2 marks):**
> $$\sigma_y = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{24{,}500}{78.54} = 311.9 \;\text{MPa}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Yield Strain from Hooke's Law (2 marks):**
> $$\sigma_y = E \varepsilon_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{311.9 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa}}{110 \times 10^9 \;\text{Pa}}$$
> $$\varepsilon_y = 2.835 \times 10^{-3} = 0.00284$$
>
> **Step 4 — Elongation at Yield (2 marks):**
> $$\Delta L_y = \varepsilon_y \times L_0 = 0.00284 \times 30 \;\text{mm}$$
> $$\Delta L_y = 0.0852 \;\text{mm} = 85.2 \;\mu\text{m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Frequently Cost 2–3 Marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — MPa to Pa (×10⁶), mm² to m² (×10⁻⁶). Many students correctly compute the formula but write the wrong final unit.
> 2. **Using the wrong area convention** — Engineering stress uses $A_0$ (initial area), **not** the instantaneous area. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 3. **Confusing the FDA Class III threshold** — Class III is for life-supporting / life-sustaining / implantable devices, **not** just for "complex" devices.
> 4. **Forgetting to state units** in the final answer — even if the numerical value is correct, omitting "MPa" or "mm" is penalized.
> 5. **Mixing up biocompatibility with bioactivity** — "Biocompatible" ≠ "bioactive". A bioinert material can be biocompatible (e.g., Ti) and so can a bioactive material (e.g., HA). They differ in their **host response**, not in their safety.
> 6. **Skipping the sequence in FBR questions** — Examiners award marks per stage. Writing only the first 2 stages of the FBR loses 2+ marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 1 hour before the exam.**

### Core Definitions to Memorize

- **Biomedical Device:** Any instrument/implant/software intended for medical purposes acting by **physical means** (not pharmacological).
- **Biomaterial:** A non-viable material used in a medical device intended to interact with biological systems.
- **Biocompatibility:** Ability of a material to elicit an **appropriate host response** in a specific application.
- **Osseointegration:** Direct structural and functional connection between ordered, living bone and the surface of a load-bearing implant (per Brånemark).
- **Foreign Body Reaction (FBR):** The stereotyped cascade from protein adsorption → inflammation → fibrous encapsulation.
- **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL):** Probability of ≤ 1 viable organism per million units, i.e., **10⁻⁶**.

### The Three Pillars of Design (Always Mention in Theory Answers)

1. **Material** — metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, natural.
2. **Mechanics / Electronics** — load, fatigue, signal integrity.
3. **Biology** — biocompatibility, FBR, sterilization.

### The Three Material "Bio-" Behaviors

- **Bioinert** (Ti, Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) — minimal reaction.
- **Bioactive** (HA, Bioglass) — chemical bond with bone.
- **Bioresorbable** (PLA, PGA, Mg alloys) — degrades & replaced.

### FDA Tripartite Classification (Always)

- **Class I** → Low risk → General controls.
- **Class II** → Moderate → 510(k) notification.
- **Class III** → High → PMA approval.

### ISO 10993 Tier-1 Tests (Cytotoxicity, Sensitization, Irritation)

These are the **first three tests** required for any new biomaterial.

### Stress-Shielding Concept (Wolff's Law)

A stiff metallic implant (E ≈ 110 GPa) carries the load and **robs the bone of mechanical stimulus** → bone resorption. Solution: **modulus-matched composites** (e.g., CF/PEEK).

### Sterilization Compatibility (Material ↔ Method)

- Metals/glass → autoclave.
- Polymers → EtO or gamma.
- Sensitive electronics → H₂O₂ plasma.

### Numerical Constants to Memorize

- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $T_{body} = 310$ K
- $E_{Ti-6Al-4V} \approx 110$ GPa
- $E_{316L\ SS} \approx 200$ GPa
- $E_{cortical\ bone} \approx 15$–$20$ GPa

### Top 5 Most-Asked KTU Question Types (Pattern Recognition)

1. Classify a given device and state the FDA pathway.
2. Explain FBR with sequence and design implications.
3. Derive stress / strain / modulus for a tensile test specimen.
4. Apply rule of mixtures to a composite implant.
5. Discuss biocompatibility testing pyramid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For Part A, write the **definition first**, then **two distinguishing points**. For Part B, structure every answer as **Definition → Classification → Diagram → Application → Design Implication**. Examiners reward **structured, marked-section** answers, not essays.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Verification Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Verification Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Design Verification Plan (DVP)** is a documented, systematic, and pre-defined protocol that specifies the methods, procedures, acceptance criteria, sample sizes, test conditions, and traceability links used to confirm that **design outputs** (the physical, functional, and performance characteristics of a medical device) conform to and meet the **design inputs** (the user needs, intended use, and specified requirements).

As per **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.3.5, 7.3.6)** and **FDA 21 CFR §820.30(f)**, verification is distinct from validation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Verification** = "Are we building the device **RIGHT**?" (Did we meet the design specifications?)
>
> **Design Validation** = "Are we building the **RIGHT** device?" (Does it meet user needs and intended use?)

The DVP is the **engineered bridge** between an abstract set of requirements (Design Inputs) and the production-ready Design Output. It transforms engineering intent into **objective, repeatable evidence**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a bridge** for a specific location. Before construction, you don't just trust your calculations; you create a strict testing schedule:

- **Design Inputs:** "Bridge must support 50 tons, withstand 150 km/h winds, and last 100 years."
- **Design Verification Plan:** Your master testing document that says: "We will load-test 3 scale models with 75 tons, run computer FEA simulations for wind loads, and salt-spray-test steel coupons for 1000 hours."
- **Design Verification Report (DVR):** The actual logbook where you record the test results: "Model #2 supported 78 tons before yielding."

In biomedical device development, the DVP is this **testing blueprint**. For example, when designing a heart valve:
- You state (Input): "Valve must open at ≤ 5 mmHg pressure gradient."
- The DVP specifies: "Test 30 valves in a pulse duplicator at 70 BPM, 37 °C, using ISO 5840-compliant saline, measure ΔP across the valve."
- The DVR later records whether the **acceptance criterion** was met.

> [!NOTE]
> The DVP is **proactive** — it is written **before** testing begins. This ensures statistical rigor, regulatory defensibility, and eliminates "testing to a favorable result" bias.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

The following mandatory elements must appear in any KTU-graded DVP:

- **Acceptance Criteria (AC):** The pass/fail threshold (e.g., **95% confidence interval**, **p < 0.05**, **± 5% tolerance**).
- **Sample Size (n):** Determined statistically (often via **ISO 16269-6** or **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4**).
- **Test Conditions:** Environmental envelope (temperature **23 °C ± 2 °C** / **37 °C ± 1 °C** for body-simulating tests, humidity **50% ± 10% RH**).
- **Verification Method:** One of the four canonical types — **Inspection, Test, Analysis, Demonstration**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** In every DVP question, explicitly name the **four verification methods** (Inspection, Test, Analysis, Demonstration) and map each design input to **at least one method** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark differentiator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Steps (The DVP Workflow)

A Design Verification Plan is executed through five structured phases:

**Step 1 — Requirements Decomposition (Input Mapping):**
Take the Design Input Document (DID) and break down each high-level requirement (e.g., "Must be biocompatible") into **verifiable, measurable sub-requirements** (e.g., "Must pass ISO 10993-5 cytotoxicity test with cell viability ≥ 70%").

**Step 2 — Verification Method Selection:**
Assign **one or more** of the four methods to each sub-requirement:

- **Inspection (I):** Visual or dimensional examination using calibrated tools (CMM, calipers, microscopes).
- **Test (T):** Empirical measurement of performance under defined conditions (bench testing, fatigue testing).
- **Analysis (A):** Engineering evaluation using established mathematical or computational models (FEA, CFD, statistical analysis).
- **Demonstration (D):** Functional observation showing the device operates as intended (e.g., a clinician successfully deploying a stent).

**Step 3 — Test Protocol Authoring:**
For each "Test" method, write a detailed **Test Protocol** that includes:
- Apparatus description
- Calibration records
- Sample preparation
- Procedure (numbered steps)
- Data collection sheets
- Acceptance criteria

**Step 4 — Acceptance Criteria Definition:**
Define quantitative (numerical) or qualitative (binary) thresholds. Use **Go/No-Go** gates.

**Step 5 — Traceability Matrix Construction:**
Build a **Traceability Matrix** linking each Input → Verification Method → Acceptance Criterion → Test Protocol Number. This matrix is the regulatory backbone required by **FDA Design Controls** and **ISO 13485**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression / Definition | Unit / Standard | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sample Size (Normal Distribution) | $n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$ | dimensionless | Determines $n$ where $Z$ is Z-score, $\sigma$ is std dev, $E$ is margin of error |
| Z-score (95% confidence) | $Z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$ | dimensionless | Two-tailed, $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| Z-score (99% confidence) | $Z_{\alpha/2} = 2.576$ | dimensionless | Two-tailed, $\alpha = 0.01$ |
| Binomial Sample Size | $n = \dfrac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(1-p)}$ | dimensionless | For reliability testing with success probability $p$ |
| Tolerance Interval | $TI = \bar{x} \pm k \cdot s$ | depends on metric | Captures $P\%$ of population with $C\%$ confidence |
| Failure Rate (Weibull) | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | failures / hour | Reliability verification for implants |
| B10 Life | $B10 = \eta \cdot (-\ln(0.9))^{1/\beta}$ | hours / cycles | Time at which 10% fail |
| Confidence Level (1−α) | $1 - \alpha$ | % | Statistical certainty of passing |
| Test Margin | $TM = 1 + \dfrac{\text{Measurement Uncertainty}}{\text{Specification}}$ | ratio | Ensures pass when actual performance may be at spec edge |
| p-value (t-test) | $p = 2 \cdot P(T > \vert t \vert)$ | dimensionless | Hypothesis test for mean comparison |
| Cochran's Sample Size | $n_0 = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | dimensionless | For proportion-based attribute testing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pipe Rule:** All absolute value expressions use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert t \vert$) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DVP is the **legal and engineering core** of regulatory submissions:

- **FDA 510(k) Submissions:** Reviewers check the DVP/DVR to confirm substantial equivalence testing was performed.
- **EU MDR Technical Documentation:** The DVP forms **Annex II, Section 6** evidence.
- **ISO 13485 Audit:** Auditors trace inputs to outputs via the DVP's traceability matrix.
- **Litigation Defense:** In product liability cases, the DVP serves as proof of "due diligence" and "state-of-the-art engineering practice."
- **Design Transfer:** The DVP ensures the device can be reliably **manufactured** (Design Transfer verification) — not just designed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sample Size for Performance Verification

**Problem Setup:** A biomedical device manufacturer must verify that the output current of a neural stimulator is within **5 mA ± 0.5 mA**. From prior prototype data, the standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.3$ mA. They require **95% confidence** with a **margin of error $E = 0.1$ mA**. How many devices must be tested?

**Step 1 — Identify the confidence parameter:**
At 95% confidence, two-tailed:
$$Z_{\alpha/2} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 2 — Identify variance and margin:**
$$\sigma = 0.3 \text{ mA}, \quad E = 0.1 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sample size formula:**
$$n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$n = \left( \dfrac{1.96 \cdot 0.3}{0.1} \right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**
$$1.96 \cdot 0.3 = 0.588 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 6 — Compute ratio:**
$$\dfrac{0.588}{0.1} = 5.88$$

**Step 7 — Square the ratio:**
$$n = (5.88)^2 = 34.57$$

**Step 8 — Round up (conservative convention):**
$$n = 35 \text{ devices}$$

**Interpretation:** 35 units must be tested to statistically guarantee (with 95% confidence) that the measured mean current is within ±0.1 mA of the true mean. Each unit's current must fall within the **5 mA ± 0.5 mA** specification for the design to be verified.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reliability Demonstration Test (Binomial RDT)

**Problem Setup:** A manufacturer must demonstrate that a pacemaker's connector has a **success probability $p = 0.99$** (i.e., 99% reliability over 10 years). They want **90% confidence** ($C = 0.90$) that the reliability is at least 99%. How many zero-failure units must be tested?

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$$p = 0.99, \quad C = 0.90, \quad \text{Zero failures allowed} \; (r = 0)$$

**Step 2 — Use the zero-failure binomial formula:**
$$n = \dfrac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(1-p)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$1 - C = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10$$
$$\ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**
$$1 - p = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$$
$$\ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

**Step 5 — Compute ratio:**
$$n = \dfrac{-2.3026}{-4.6052} = 0.5000$$

**Step 6 — Round up and interpret:**
$$n = 1 \text{ unit suffices for this loose target}$$

**Step 7 — Recompute for stricter target ($p = 0.999$):**
$$n = \dfrac{\ln(0.10)}{\ln(0.001)} = \dfrac{-2.3026}{-6.9078} = 0.333$$

For higher reliability (99.9%), testing more units is required:
$$n = \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.999)} = \dfrac{-2.9957}{-0.001} \approx 2996$$

So **~3000 units** with zero failures are needed to prove 99.9% reliability at 95% confidence. This is why **accelerated life testing** and **Weibull analysis** are critical for high-reliability implants.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Verification Trace Matrix Construction (Analytical Logic)

Given the Design Input list, the verification matrix maps each requirement. The transformation logic:

**Step 1 — Take the Design Input Document:**
$$I_i \in \{I_1, I_2, I_3, \ldots, I_n\}$$

**Step 2 — Assign verification method $M_j$ from the canonical set:**
$$M_j \in \{I, T, A, D\}$$

**Step 3 — Define acceptance criterion $AC_k$:**
$$AC_k = \{\text{numeric threshold} \vert \text{boolean pass condition}\}$$

**Step 4 — Form the traceability tuple:**
$$T_i = (I_i, M_j, AC_k, \text{Protocol}_m, \text{Report}_p)$$

**Step 5 — Verify completeness (coverage test):**
$$\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\vert \{I_i \mid T_i \text{ defined}\} \vert}{\vert \{I_i\} \vert} \times 100\%$$

A coverage of **100%** is mandatory before design transfer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Verification Method Selection Guide

| Design Input Type | Best Verification Method | Rationale | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dimensional (length, diameter) | **Inspection (I)** | Direct measurement with calibrated tool | Stent diameter must be $3.0 \pm 0.05$ mm |
| Performance (flow, voltage) | **Test (T)** | Requires controlled apparatus | Pump flow rate ≥ 5 L/min |
| Theoretical (stress, fatigue) | **Analysis (A)** | FEA / mathematical modeling | Von-Mises stress < yield strength |
| Usability (user interface) | **Demonstration (D)** | Human-in-the-loop observation | Surgeon deploys device in 3 minutes |
| Material composition | **Inspection + Analysis** | Spectroscopy + chemistry review | Ti-6Al-4V per ASTM F136 |
| Sterility | **Test (T)** | Bioburden / EO residuals test | SAL = $10^{-6}$ |
| Biocompatibility | **Test (T)** | ISO 10993 panel | Cytotoxicity cell viability ≥ 70% |
| Software | **Test + Analysis** | Unit test, integration test, static analysis | IEC 62304 compliance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Analysis" with "Test." **Analysis** uses **existing knowledge** (literature, math models, prior data) — no new empirical data is generated. **Test** generates **new empirical data** through experiment. Never interchange them in a DVP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DVP Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design Inputs DID] --> B[Requirements Decomposition]
    B --> C[Verification Method Selection]
    C --> D1[Inspection]
    C --> D2[Test]
    C --> D3[Analysis]
    C --> D4[Demonstration]
    D1 --> E[Test Protocol Authoring]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Acceptance Criteria Definition]
    F --> G[Sample Size Determination]
    G --> H[Traceability Matrix Build]
    H --> I{Document Approved?}
    I -->|No| J[Revise DVP]
    J --> B
    I -->|Yes| K[DVP Released]
    K --> L[Execute Test Protocols]
    L --> M[Collect Data]
    M --> N[Design Verification Report DVR]
    N --> O{AC Met?}
    O -->|No| P[Root Cause Analysis CAPA]
    P --> Q[Design Iteration]
    Q --> B
    O -->|Yes| R[Design Transfer]
```

## 4.2 Verification Methods Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Dimensional Input]
        I2[Performance Input]
        I3[Theoretical Input]
        I4[Usability Input]
    end

    subgraph Methods
        M1[INSPECTION]
        M2[TEST]
        M3[ANALYSIS]
        M4[DEMONSTRATION]
    end

    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Pass: Caliper reading]
        O2[Pass: Flow = 5.1 L/min]
        O3[Pass: Stress 250 MPa yield]
        O4[Pass: User completed task]
    end

    I1 --> M1 --> O1
    I2 --> M2 --> O2
    I3 --> M3 --> O3
    I4 --> M4 --> O4

    style M1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style M2 fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E
    style M3 fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C
    style M4 fill:#FDEEEE,stroke:#D62728
```

## 4.3 DVP Document Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DVP Document Header] --> B[Document Control Info]
    A --> C[Revision History]
    A --> D[Referenced Standards]

    D --> E1[ISO 13485]
    D --> E2[FDA 21 CFR 820]
    D --> E3[ISO 14971 Risk]
    D --> E4[Applicable Product Standard e.g. ISO 5840]

    A --> F[Section 1 Scope and Purpose]
    A --> G[Section 2 Definitions and Acronyms]
    A --> H[Section 3 Verification Strategy]
    A --> I[Section 4 Verification Matrix]
    A --> J[Section 5 Test Protocols]
    A --> K[Section 6 Acceptance Criteria Summary]
    A --> L[Section 7 Sample Size Justification]
    A --> M[Section 8 Test Conditions Environmental]
    A --> N[Section 9 Roles and Responsibilities]
    A --> O[Section 10 Deviation Handling]
    A --> P[Section 11 Approval Signatures]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords like `end` or `subgraph` used as node names). All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric strings without markdown formatting inside the quotes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Design Verification Plan (DVP). List the four canonical verification methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Design Verification Plan (DVP) is a pre-approved, documented protocol that defines how design outputs will be objectively tested, inspected, analyzed, or demonstrated to confirm conformance to design inputs, in accordance with **ISO 13485:2016** and **21 CFR §820.30(f)**. (2 marks)

The four canonical verification methods are:
1. **Inspection (I)** — visual / dimensional verification using calibrated tools
2. **Test (T)** — empirical measurement under controlled conditions
3. **Analysis (A)** — mathematical/computational evaluation
4. **Demonstration (D)** — functional observation in simulated use

(1 mark for the list)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Design Verification and Design Validation with a one-line example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Question Answered | Are we building the device **right**? | Are we building the **right** device? |
| Reference | Design Inputs (specifications) | User Needs / Intended Use |
| Stage | During design phase | After design freeze, before launch |
| Example (3 marks) | Bench test confirms stent diameter = 3.0 ± 0.05 mm | Clinician successfully deploys stent in a simulated artery |

(2 marks for distinction table + 1 mark for example)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the workflow of a Design Verification Plan and Design Verification Report (DVR) in a Class II medical device project. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pulse oximeter manufacturer must verify that SpO₂ readings are within ±2% of a reference arterial blood gas analyzer. From prior prototype runs, σ = 1.1%. They require 99% confidence with margin of error 0.5%. Determine the required sample size and outline the verification matrix for the following inputs: (i) Sensor wavelength accuracy, (ii) Display legibility, (iii) Software alarm logic, (iv) Battery endurance. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — DVP/DVR Workflow (7 marks):**

The verification cycle consists of **six sequential phases**:

1. **Design Input Finalization (1 mark):** Approved Design Input Document (DID) is the source. Each input is uniquely numbered (e.g., DI-001, DI-002).

2. **DVP Authoring (1 mark):** For each input, select verification method (I/T/A/D), define acceptance criterion, sample size, and reference a test protocol number (e.g., TP-007).

3. **DVP Approval (1 mark):** Cross-functional sign-off — Design Engineering, Quality Assurance, Regulatory, Clinical (if applicable).

4. **Test Execution (1 mark):** Trained technicians execute the protocols under controlled environmental conditions (typically **23 °C ± 2 °C**, **50% ± 10% RH**).

5. **Data Recording & DVR (1 mark):** Raw data, calibration certificates, photographs, deviations logged in the Design Verification Report.

6. **Acceptance Decision (2 marks):** Pass → Design Transfer authorization. Fail → CAPA triggered, design inputs/outputs revisited, DVP revised, and re-execution cycle begins.

```
DID → DVP → Approval → Test → DVR → Go/No-Go Decision
                                            ↓
                                  (Fail) → CAPA → Revise Inputs
                                            ↓
                                  (Pass) → Design Transfer
```

**Part (b) — Sample Size + Verification Matrix (7 marks):**

**Sample Size Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $Z_{\alpha/2} = 2.576$ (for 99% confidence), $\sigma = 1.1\%$, $E = 0.5\%$.

$$n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$

$$n = \left( \dfrac{2.576 \cdot 1.1}{0.5} \right)^2$$

$$n = \left( \dfrac{2.8336}{0.5} \right)^2 = (5.6672)^2 = 32.12$$

Round up: **$n = 33$ units** must be tested. (Full calculation = 4 marks)

**Verification Matrix (3 marks):**

| Design Input | Verification Method | Acceptance Criterion | Test Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor wavelength accuracy | Test (T) | 660 nm ± 10 nm and 940 nm ± 10 nm | TP-001 (spectrometer) |
| Display legibility | Inspection + Demo (I+D) | Visible at 1 m, 300 lux | TP-002 (visual acuity panel) |
| Software alarm logic | Analysis + Test (A+T) | 100% alarm trigger on simulated desat, no false alarms over 168 hr | TP-003 (software V&V per IEC 62304) |
| Battery endurance | Test (T) | ≥ 24 hr continuous operation on full charge | TP-004 (battery life test) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the components of a complete DVP document. For each section, justify its regulatory purpose with reference to ISO 13485 or FDA 21 CFR Part 820. (7 marks)

**(b)** A defibrillator manufacturer must verify a 99.5% reliability of the high-voltage discharge circuit with 95% confidence using zero-failure testing. Determine the required sample size. Justify why sample sizes in biomedical device verification often differ from those in consumer electronics. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — DVP Components (7 marks):**

A complete DVP contains **eleven sections** (1 mark each for naming + 4 marks for regulatory justification spread across):

1. **Header & Document Control:** Unique ID, revision, effective date. *Regulatory basis: ISO 13485 §4.2.4 (Control of documents).*

2. **Scope & Purpose:** Defines device, verification boundaries, exclusions. *FDA §820.30(c) (Design Input).*

3. **Referenced Standards:** ISO 13485, ISO 14971, product-specific standards (e.g., IEC 60601-1). *Regulatory basis: ISO 13485 §7.3.3.*

4. **Definitions & Acronyms:** Prevents misinterpretation. *Regulatory basis: §4.2.4.*

5. **Verification Strategy:** Defines which method applies to which input category. *FDA §820.30(f).*

6. **Verification Matrix (Traceability):** Inputs → Methods → AC → Protocols. *Regulatory basis: ISO 13485 §7.3.9 (Control of Design Changes) + §7.3.5.*

7. **Test Protocols:** Step-by-step procedures, apparatus, calibration. *FDA §820.30(f)(2).*

8. **Acceptance Criteria:** Quantitative/qualitative pass thresholds. *FDA §820.30(g) (Design Validation).*

9. **Sample Size Justification:** Statistical basis (Z-score, binomial, etc.). *Regulatory basis: ISO 13485 §8.2.6 + FDA guidance on statistically valid sample sizes.*

10. **Test Conditions:** Environmental envelope (temperature, humidity, altitude). *IEC 60601-1 environmental testing.*

11. **Roles, Responsibilities & Approval Signatures:** Owner accountability. *ISO 13485 §5.5 (Responsibility & Authority) + §4.2.4.*

**Part (b) — Sample Size for Defibrillator Reliability (7 marks):**

Given: $p = 0.995$ (target reliability), $C = 0.95$ (confidence), zero failures allowed.

$$n = \dfrac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(1-p)}$$

$$n = \dfrac{\ln(1-0.95)}{\ln(1-0.995)} = \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.005)}$$

$$n = \dfrac{-2.9957}{-5.2983} = 0.5654$$

Round up: **$n = 1$ unit suffices at this loose target.** (2 marks for the calculation)

However, the manufacturer must demonstrate reliability over the **device's full service life** (typically 7–10 years for defibrillators). If the failure probability per year is $p_y = 0.0005$, then over 10 years:
$$p_{10\text{yr}} = 1 - (1-p_y)^{10} = 1 - (0.9995)^{10} \approx 0.00499$$

Required $n$:
$$n = \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(1-0.005)} = \dfrac{-2.9957}{-0.005013} = 597.7$$

So **$n \approx 598$ zero-failure units** are required. (3 marks)

**Why sample sizes differ from consumer electronics (2 marks):**
- **Risk to patient life/safety** — failure can cause death (vs. inconvenience).
- **Regulatory stringency** — FDA/ISO requires statistical demonstration, not just "it works in the lab."
- **Lifetime duration** — implants must last decades, not 2-year consumer lifespans.
- **Closed-system control** — post-market failures are difficult to monitor/recall, so pre-market verification must be exhaustive.
- **Use environment** — implanted devices face biological, chemical, and mechanical stresses (corrosion, fatigue, biofilm) that consumer devices never encounter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks in DVP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation (–2 marks):** Verification = meeting **design inputs**; Validation = meeting **user needs/intended use**. This is the single most common error.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to round UP the sample size (–1 mark):** If $n = 32.12$, you MUST write $n = 33$. Never truncate. This violates statistical conservatism.
>
> 3. **Not stating the confidence level and Z-score (–2 marks):** Every sample size calculation must declare the assumed confidence (typically 95% → $Z = 1.96$, 99% → $Z = 2.576$).
>
> 4. **Skipping the Verification Matrix (–3 marks):** A DVP answer without a traceability matrix is incomplete. KTU expects at least 4 input rows.
>
> 5. **Mixing Test (T) and Analysis (A):** A is for **theoretical/mathematical** evaluation without new empirical data. T is for **new experimental data**. Examiners deduct 1 mark per misclassification.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Environmental Test Conditions:** A bench test answer must specify temperature, humidity, and atmospheric pressure.
>
> 7. **Missing Regulatory Citation:** Always cite **at least one** of: ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR §820, ISO 14971, or product-specific standard. A purely descriptive answer is marked down.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** A DVP is a **pre-approved, documented test protocol** proving design outputs meet design inputs. **(KTU Hot Topic – asked every cycle)**
- **Verification vs Validation:** "Right" (specs met) vs "Right device" (user needs met).
- **Four Verification Methods:** **I**nspection, **T**est, **A**nalysis, **D**emonstration. Memorize these as **I-T-A-D**.
- **Sample Size Formula (Mean-based):**
$$n = \left( \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$$
- **Sample Size Formula (Reliability/Zero-Failure):**
$$n = \dfrac{\ln(1-C)}{\ln(1-p)}$$
- **Z-values to memorize:** 90% → 1.645; 95% → 1.96; 99% → 2.576.
- **Always round UP** the sample size (conservative statistical convention).
- **Regulatory Anchors:** ISO 13485 §7.3.5/7.3.6, FDA 21 CFR §820.30(f), ISO 14971 (Risk), IEC 60601-1 (Electrical Safety), ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility).
- **Document Structure:** 11 sections — Header → Scope → Standards → Definitions → Strategy → Matrix → Protocols → Acceptance Criteria → Sample Size → Conditions → Sign-off.
- **Acceptance Criteria:** Must be **quantitative** and **measurable**. Never write "must work well" — write "must produce ≥ 5 L/min flow."
- **Traceability Matrix:** Each Input → Method → AC → Protocol. **100% coverage** is mandatory.
- **Environmental Defaults:** 23 °C ± 2 °C, 50% ± 10% RH per ISO/IEC 17025.
- **Risk-Based Verification:** Higher risk inputs require **larger sample sizes** and **stricter acceptance criteria** — link DVP entries to ISO 14971 risk levels.
- **Common Use Cases in KTU:** Sample size calculation for ECG accuracy, infusion pump flow rate, SpO₂ precision, defibrillator energy delivery, implant fatigue life.

### KTU "Golden Rule" for DVP Answers

> [!TIP]
> Always structure your Part B answer as: **(1) Define inputs numerically → (2) Choose verification methods with reasoning → (3) Calculate sample size with full formula → (4) Present traceability matrix → (5) State regulatory citation.** This five-step structure maps directly to the **5 marks × 2 sub-parts = 10 marks** plus **2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for regulatory anchor** = 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protocols

# 📡 PROTOCOLS IN BIOMEDICAL DEVICE DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEBMT752 – Design of Biomedical Devices**, a **Communication Protocol** is defined as a formally standardized set of **rules, syntax, semantics, and synchronization procedures** that govern the exchange of physiological data, control signals, and device parameters between medical instruments, sensors, controllers, and information systems (e.g., Electronic Health Records – EHRs). Protocols ensure **interoperability**, **data integrity**, **patient safety**, and **regulatory compliance** (per **FDA**, **IEC 60601-1**, and **ISO/IEEE 11073**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The course explicitly classifies biomedical protocols into three functional layers:
> 1. **Wired Serial Protocols** (RS-232, USB, Ethernet)
> 2. **Wireless Short-Range Protocols** (Bluetooth, BLE, ZigBee, Wi-Fi)
> 3. **Medical Information / Application-Layer Standards** (HL7, DICOM, ISO/IEEE 11073, MIB/IEEE 1073)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" View

Imagine a hospital where doctors (sensors) need to send prescriptions (physiological data) to the pharmacy (central monitoring station/EHR). Without a common **language, envelope format, addressing convention, and delivery confirmation**, the system collapses.

- **Protocol = The entire postal rulebook** (envelope size, language, return address, tracking, post office hierarchy).
- **Data packets = Letters** carrying ECG samples, SpO₂ readings, blood pressure values.
- **Physical medium = Trucks, planes, fiber cables, or radio waves.**

> [!NOTE]
> A "protocol" is **NOT** the same as a "physical medium". Bluetooth is *not* a cable — Bluetooth is a **protocol suite** that uses the 2.4 GHz ISM radio band as its physical carrier. Likewise, USB is a protocol that may use copper wires or even optical fiber.

---

### 1.3 Why Protocols are *Critical* in Biomedical Engineering

| Aspect | Impact on Patient Safety & Device Performance |
|---|---|
| **Data Integrity** | Bit-errors in an insulin pump control signal can be fatal → protocols use **CRC checksums**, **ACK/NACK handshakes**. |
| **Interoperability** | A pulse oximeter made by Philips must talk to a GE monitor → governed by **IEEE 11073**. |
| **Security & Privacy** | Wireless pacemakers face hacking threats → **AES-128**, **TLS 1.3** encryption mandated. |
| **Latency** | Defibrillator sync pulses require **<100 ms** delay → protocol stack must be deterministic. |
| **Power Budget** | Implantable devices (e.g., cochlear implant) require protocols like **MICS (Medical Implant Communication Service)** at **<25 µW**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regulatory constants you must memorize for KTU exams:**
> - **MICS Band:** **402 – 405 MHz** (allocated by FCC, ITU-R RS.1346).
> - **ISM Band (Bluetooth/Wi-Fi):** **2.400 – 2.4835 GHz** (industrial, scientific, medical).
> - **WBAN (IEEE 802.15.6):** **400 MHz**, **900 MHz**, **2.4 GHz** bands.
> - **Medical Device Classification (FDA):** Class I (low risk), II, III (highest — pacemakers).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Biomedical Communication Protocol

Biomedical protocols follow a **layered architecture** (loosely mapped to the **OSI 7-layer model**, but typically condensed into 4–5 layers for embedded medical devices):

1. **Physical Layer (PHY):** Defines voltage levels, frequencies, modulation (e.g., **OOK**, **FSK**, **GFSK**, **DSSS**).
2. **Data Link Layer (MAC):** Framing, error detection (CRC-8, CRC-16), medium access (CSMA/CA, TDMA).
3. **Network/Transport Layer:** Routing, packet sequencing, flow control.
4. **Application Layer:** Domain-specific semantics — e.g., **HL7 v2.x** messages, **IEEE 11073** object classes (numerics, waveforms, alerts).
5. **Security Layer (overlay):** AES-128/256, ECC, TLS 1.3.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Biomedical Protocols (with KTU-Focused Detail)

#### 2.2.1 RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)
- **Origin:** EIA/TIA-232-F (legacy: 1962).
- **Topology:** Point-to-point, single-ended.
- **Voltage swing:** ±**3 V to ±25 V** (logic 1 = -3 to -25 V, logic 0 = +3 to +25 V).
- **Max cable length:** **15 meters** at 19.2 kbps; **degrades** with baud rate.
- **Max data rate:** **115.2 kbps** (practical); theoretical 1 Mbps.
- **Use in biomed:** Legacy patient monitors, defibrillator service ports, ventilator calibration ports.
- **Limitation:** Not plug-and-play, susceptible to EMI from electrosurgical units (ESU).

#### 2.2.2 USB (Universal Serial Bus)
- **Versions relevant to medical devices:** USB 1.1 (12 Mbps), USB 2.0 (**480 Mbps** — *gold standard* for medical imaging transfer), USB 3.0/3.1 (5–10 Gbps).
- **Topology:** Tiered-star, hot-pluggable.
- **Power delivery:** Up to **5 V / 500 mA (USB 2.0)** or **5 V / 900 mA (USB 3.0)**.
- **Medical relevance:** USB-IF has a dedicated **USB Personal Healthcare Device Class (PHDC)** — IEC 62327 compliant.
- **Connector types in hospitals:** USB-A, USB-B, **USB-C** (emerging), and **isolated medical-grade USB** (galvanic isolation up to 5 kV).

#### 2.2.3 Bluetooth & BLE (IEEE 802.15.1)
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz ISM band, **79 channels** (Classic) or **40 channels** (BLE).
- **Modulation:** GFSK (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying), index = 0.28–0.35.
- **Hop rate:** **1600 hops/sec** (Classic) — provides FHSS interference rejection.
- **Data rate:** Classic BR=1 Mbps, EDR=2–3 Mbps; **BLE = 125 kbps – 2 Mbps**.
- **Range:** Class 1 = **100 m**, Class 2 = **10 m**, Class 3 = **1 m**.
- **Medical Profile:** **HDP (Health Device Profile)** replaced by **GATT-based services** in BLE 4.0+.
- **Power:** Classic ~**30 mA** peak; BLE ~**15 mA** peak → coin-cell operation feasible (e.g., pulse oximeter rings).

#### 2.2.4 ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)
- **Frequency:** 2.4 GHz (worldwide) or 868 MHz (Europe) / 915 MHz (USA).
- **Data rate:** **250 kbps** (2.4 GHz).
- **Range:** **10–100 m** indoors.
- **Topology:** Mesh (up to 65,000 nodes).
- **Medical use:** Hospital asset tracking, ambient assisted living (AAL), patient location monitoring.

#### 2.2.5 Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)
- **Bands:** 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E adds 6 GHz).
- **PHY rate:** 802.11n = **600 Mbps**; 802.11ac = **6.93 Gbps**; 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) = **9.6 Gbps**.
- **Medical use:** Wireless PACS image transfer, VoIP nurse calls, real-time telemetry.

#### 2.2.6 IEEE 11073 (Health Informatics – Point-of-Care Medical Device Communication)
- **Family:** ISO/IEEE 11073-1xxxx (formerly "VITAL" → "CEN ENV 13734" → "IEEE 1073").
- **Purpose:** Plug-and-play interoperability between personal health devices (glucose meters, BP cuffs, weighing scales) and compute engines (phones, gateways).
- **Optimization:** **Optimized Exchange Protocol (OXP)** — uses **binary encoding** (not ASCII) to reduce packet size by ~70%, crucial for low-power BLE.
- **Domain Information Model (DIM):** Object-oriented — *Medical Device System (MDS)*, *Metric* objects (numeric, RT-SA, enumeration).

#### 2.2.7 HL7 (Health Level Seven)
- **Versions:** **v2.x** (pipe-delimited text — still the *de facto* hospital EMR standard), **v3** (XML-based RIM), **FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** — RESTful JSON/XML.
- **Sample HL7 v2 message:**
  ```
  MSH|^~\&|MONITOR^001|HOSP|EMR|HOSP|20241125143022||ORU^R01|MSG00001|P|2.5
  PID|1||MRN12345||Doe^John||19700101|M
  OBX|1|NM|8867-4^Heart Rate^LN||72|/min|60-100|N
  ```
- **Role:** Application-layer semantics for **EHR/HIS** integration.

#### 2.2.8 DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)
- **Standard:** **NEMA** (National Electrical Manufacturers Association) → ISO **12052**.
- **Use:** CT, MRI, ultrasound, X-ray, PET.
- **Protocol stack:** DICOM upper layer over **TCP/IP** (port **104**); also **DICOMweb** (RESTful: WADO-RS, STOW-RS, QIDO-RS).
- **Key concepts:** *Service-Object Pair (SOP) class*, *Information Object Definition (IOD)*, *Transfer Syntax* (JPEG, JPEG-LS, JPEG 2000, lossless/lossy).

#### 2.2.9 MIB (Medical Information Bus) – IEEE 1073
- **Architecture:** Bedside device → **MBCC** (MIB Communication Controller) → **DHCP** (Device Host Communication Controller) → Hospital network.
- **Status:** Largely superseded by ISO/IEEE 11073 family.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all math expressions below to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | Use in Biomedical Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon–Hartley Theorem** (channel capacity) | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless) | Theoretical max data rate over medical telemetry channel (e.g., 2.4 GHz BLE with 2 MHz BW). |
| 2 | $N = 2 B \log_2 M$ | **Nyquist rate** (max symbol rate for noiseless channel) | $N$ = data rate (bps), $M$ = signal levels | Determines minimum bandwidth needed for ECG digitization (e.g., $M = 256$ levels, $B = 4$ kHz → $N = 64$ kbps). |
| 3 | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$ | **Log-distance path loss model** | $PL(d)$ = path loss (dB), $d$ = distance (m), $d_0$ = reference distance (m), $n$ = path loss exponent, $X_\sigma$ = shadow fading (dB, Gaussian) | WBAN design — on-body ($n \approx 3.1$), in-body ($n \approx 4.0$). |
| 4 | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{S/N}{R/B}$ | **Bit energy to spectral noise density** | $E_b$ = energy/bit, $N_0$ = noise power density, $R$ = bit rate | BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curves for FSK, BPSK modems in implants. |
| 5 | $t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bytes}} \times 8}{R_{\text{bps}}}$ | **Frame transmission time** | $t_{\text{frame}}$ in seconds, $N_{\text{bytes}}$ in bytes, $R$ in bps | Worst-case latency calculation for alarm packets. |
| 6 | $P_{\text{avg}} = V \times I_{\text{avg}}$ | **Average power consumption** | $V$ = supply voltage (V), $I_{\text{avg}}$ = average current (A) | Battery-life estimation ($T = C_{\text{batt}} / I_{\text{avg}}$). |
| 7 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | **Nyquist sampling criterion** | $f_s$ = sampling rate, $f_{\max}$ = highest signal frequency | ECG sampling ≥ 500 Hz; EMG ≥ 2 kHz. |
| 8 | $T_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{tx}} + t_{\text{queue}}$ | **End-to-end latency** | $t_{\text{prop}} = d/c$ (signal propagation), $t_{\text{tx}}$ = transmission time, $t_{\text{queue}}$ = queuing delay | Real-time alarm delivery (target <100 ms for Class III devices). |
| 9 | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{\sigma E^2}{\rho}$ | **Specific Absorption Rate** (RF safety) | SAR in W/kg, $\sigma$ = conductivity, $E$ = E-field, $\rho$ = tissue density | FCC/IEC limit = **1.6 W/kg** (head, averaged over 1 g). |
| 10 | $\text{BER}_{\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **Bit Error Rate for BPSK** | $Q$ = Q-function | Determines link reliability for implant telemetry. |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In wearable ECG monitors** (e.g., Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile): **BLE + IEEE 11073 PHDC** stack is used.
- **In MRI suite** (RF-hostile environment): Use **optical fiber-based protocols** or **RS-422/485 with galvanic isolation**, NOT Wi-Fi (RF interference with 1.5 T magnet).
- **In ICU ventilators:** **HL7 v2 ORU^R01** messages export breath-by-breath waveform data every **1 s** to the EMR.
- **In remote pacemaker follow-up:** **MICS band (402–405 MHz)** at **<25 µW EIRP** for safety against RF heating of leads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1 — Maximum Achievable Data Rate in a BLE Telemetry Channel

**Problem:** A wearable SpO₂ sensor uses Bluetooth Low Energy over a channel of bandwidth $B = 2$ MHz in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. The receiver measures a signal-to-noise ratio $S/N = 35$ (linear, i.e., 15.44 dB). Calculate:
- (a) The **Shannon channel capacity** $C$.
- (b) The **minimum bandwidth** required to support a 1 Mbps waveform data stream using 16-level signaling ($M = 16$).
- (c) The **frame transmission time** for a 256-byte IEEE 11073 packet at $R = 1$ Mbps.

#### (a) Shannon Capacity — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Identify the input parameters.

$$B = 2 \text{ MHz} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}, \quad \dfrac{S}{N} = 35$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Shannon–Hartley equation.

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the argument of the logarithm.

$$1 + \dfrac{S}{N} = 1 + 35 = 36$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $\log_2(36)$.

$$\log_2(36) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(36)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{1.5563}{0.30103} \approx 5.1699$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $B$.

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 2 \times 10^{6} \times 5.1699 \\
&\approx 10.34 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer (a):** The Shannon capacity is **$C \approx 10.34$ Mbps**. This is the *theoretical* upper bound — practical BLE achieves only **2 Mbps** (LE 2M PHY) due to modulation and coding constraints.

#### (b) Nyquist Minimum Bandwidth

**Step 1:** Identify inputs: $N = 1$ Mbps = $10^6$ bps, $M = 16$.

**Step 2:** Apply the Nyquist formula $N = 2 B \log_2 M$.

**Step 3:** Solve for $B$.

$$B = \dfrac{N}{2 \log_2 M} = \dfrac{10^6}{2 \times \log_2(16)} = \dfrac{10^6}{2 \times 4} = \dfrac{10^6}{8}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$B = 125{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 125 \text{ kHz}$$

> **Answer (b):** Minimum required bandwidth = **125 kHz**, well within the 2 MHz BLE channel allocation. *Margin is healthy → 16-FSK is feasible.*

#### (c) Frame Transmission Time

**Step 1:** Inputs: $N_{\text{bytes}} = 256$, $R = 10^6$ bps.

**Step 2:** Convert bytes → bits.

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 256 \times 8 = 2048 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $t_{\text{frame}} = N_{\text{bits}} / R$.

$$t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{2048}{10^6} = 2.048 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4:** Add typical protocol overhead (BLE L2CAP + ATT headers ≈ **4–10 bytes**). Adjusted packet ≈ 266 bytes.

$$t_{\text{frame, real}} \approx \dfrac{266 \times 8}{10^6} \approx 2.128 \text{ ms}$$

> **Answer (c):** Transmission time ≈ **2.13 ms** — safely below the 7.5 ms BLE connection interval → real-time SpO₂ streaming is achievable.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2 — On-Body Path Loss for a WBAN (Wireless Body Area Network)

**Problem:** A chest-worn ECG transmitter communicates with a wrist receiver at $d = 1.5$ m (LOS, on-body). Reference distance $d_0 = 0.1$ m, path loss at reference $PL(d_0) = 35$ dB, exponent $n = 2.8$ (typical for on-body LOS). Calculate the path loss $PL(d)$.

**Step 1:** State the log-distance model.

$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

(We drop $X_\sigma$ for the deterministic case.)

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$PL(d) = 35 + 10 \times 2.8 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5}{0.1}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio.

$$\dfrac{d}{d_0} = 15$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $\log_{10}(15)$.

$$\log_{10}(15) \approx 1.1761$$

**Step 5:** Multiply.

$$10 \times 2.8 \times 1.1761 = 28 \times 1.1761 \approx 32.93 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6:** Add to reference.

$$PL(d) = 35 + 32.93 = 67.93 \text{ dB}$$

> **Answer:** Path loss ≈ **68 dB**. This is consistent with published IEEE 802.15.6 on-body measurements.

---

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Matrix — Protocol Selection Trade-Offs

| Criterion | RS-232 | USB 2.0 | BLE 5.0 | ZigBee | Wi-Fi 6 | IEEE 11073 (OXP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Data Rate** | 115 kbps | 480 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps | 9.6 Gbps | 100 kbps (typical) |
| **Range** | 15 m (wired) | 5 m (wired) | 200 m (LE Coded) | 100 m | 100 m | Application-layer |
| **Power** | Low (no PHY encoding) | Medium (120 mA) | **Ultra-low** (5–15 mA) | Low (30 mA) | **High** (200–500 mA) | Depends on PHY |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point | Tiered star | Star / Mesh | **Mesh** | Star (AP-client) | Client–manager |
| **EMI Susceptibility** | High | Medium | Low (FHSS) | Low | Medium | PHY-dependent |
| **Medical Certifiability** | High (mature) | High (isolated variants) | **High** (FDA-cleared wearables) | Medium | Medium (RF in MRI!) | **High** (ISO 11073) |
| **Security** | None inherent | Optional | AES-128 (LE Secure) | AES-128 | WPA3 | Application-defined |
| **KTU Typical Use** | Service port | Imaging transfer | Wearables | Hospital tracking | EHR telemetry | Plug-and-play standard |
| **Cost** | $ < 0.50 | $1–2 | $1.5–3 | $2–4 | $5–15 | Stack royalty |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Selecting a Protocol for an Ambulatory ECG Holter Monitor

**Specifications (typical KTU design problem):**
- 5-lead ECG, sampling rate $f_s = 500$ Hz, 12-bit ADC.
- Continuous recording for 72 hours.
- Bluetooth tether to a smartphone for live display and alert.
- Rechargeable 3.7 V, 600 mAh Li-Po battery.

**Step 1 — Data rate required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{ECG}} &= (\text{leads}) \times f_s \times N_{\text{bits}} \\
&= 5 \times 500 \times 12 \\
&= 30{,}000 \text{ bps} = 30 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Add protocol overhead (50%):**

$$R_{\text{link}} = 30 \times 1.5 = 45 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Protocol choice:** BLE 1M PHY (1 Mbps) provides **22× margin** → robust against retransmissions.

**Step 4 — Power budget:**

$$T_{\text{battery}} = \dfrac{600 \text{ mAh}}{I_{\text{avg}}}$$

Assuming $I_{\text{avg}} = 10$ mA (BLE active + ADC + MCU):

$$T_{\text{battery}} = 60 \text{ h} = 2.5 \text{ days} \;\;\checkmark\;\;(\text{meets 72 h spec})$$

> **Conclusion:** BLE is the *correct* protocol selection for this design. Wi-Fi would deplete the battery in ~6 hours; RS-232 would tether the patient and prevent ambulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generic Biomedical Device Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER HL7 v2 FHIR OR DICOM OR IEEE 11073 DIM"]
    security["SECURITY LAYER AES 128 TLS 1 3 HIPAA Compliance"]
    transport["TRANSPORT LAYER TCP UDP Port 104 DICOM Port 2570 HL7"]
    network["NETWORK LAYER IPv4 IPv6 Routing for hospital LAN"]
    mac["DATA LINK LAYER MAC CSMA CA TDMA CRC 16 Checksum"]
    phy["PHYSICAL LAYER PHY Modulation FSK GFSK OOK QPSK Frequency 2 4 GHz or 402 to 405 MHz"]
    medium["MEDIUM Copper Cable RF Channel Optical Fiber"]
    patient["PATIENT BODY Sensors and Actuators"]
    
    patient --> phy
    phy --> medium
    medium --> mac
    mac --> network
    network --> transport
    transport --> security
    security --> appLayer
    
    appLayer -.feedback.-> patient
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — IEEE 11073 Communication Model (Manager–Agent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    agent["AGENT SIDE Medical Device Pulse Oximeter BP Cuff"]
    dim["DOMAIN INFORMATION MODEL DIM MDS Object Metric Object Numeric Real Time SA Enumeration"]
    oxp["OPTIMIZED EXCHANGE PROTOCOL OXP Binary Encoding ASN 1"]
    phyAgent["PHYSICAL LAYER BLE USB ZigBee"]
    
    manager["MANAGER SIDE Smartphone Gateway EMR"]
    dimM["DIM Parser"]
    oxpM["OXP Decoder"]
    phyM["PHY Receiver"]
    ehr["ELECTRONIC HEALTH RECORD HL7 FHIR Bridge"]
    
    agent --> dim
    dim --> oxp
    oxp --> phyAgent
    phyAgent -.wireless.-> phyM
    phyM --> oxpM
    oxpM --> dimM
    dimM --> manager
    manager --> ehr
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Protocol Selection Decision Tree for Biomedical Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start Design Selection"]
    q1{"Is patient implanted?"}
    q2{"Is continuous monitoring needed?"}
    q3{"Is high bandwidth imaging required?"}
    q4{"Is multi-hop mesh required?"}
    
    p1["USE MICS Band 402 to 405 MHz EIRP 25 microwatts"]
    p2["USE BLE 5.0 with IEEE 11073 PHDC"]
    p3["USE Wi Fi 6 or USB 3.0 for DICOM"]
    p4["USE ZigBee Mesh IEEE 802.15.4"]
    p5["USE RS 232 with Galvanic Isolation"]
    p6["USE USB 2.0 with Medical Grade Isolation"]
    
    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| p1
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| q3
    q2 -->|No| q4
    q3 -->|Yes| p2
    q3 -->|No| p5
    q4 -->|Yes| p4
    q4 -->|No| q6
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — HL7 v2.x ORU^R01 Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as Patient Monitor
    participant Interface as Interface Engine Mirth Rhapsody
    participant EMR as Hospital EMR Database
    
    Note over Sensor: ECG Lead II sampled at 250 Hz
    Sensor->>Interface: MSH PID OBX Loop every 1 second
    Note over Interface: Parses pipe delimited fields<br/>Validates against HL7 v2.5 schema
    Interface->>EMR: Insert Observation into table OBSERVATION_FACT
    EMR-->>Interface: ACK Acknowledgement
    Interface-->>Sensor: MSA Segment Accepted
    Note over Sensor: If NACK retry with exponential backoff
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — DICOM Imaging Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    modality["MODALITY CT Scanner or MRI"]
    pacs["PACS SERVER"]
    workstation["RADIOLOGIST WORKSTATION"]
    web["DICOMweb WADO RS Viewer"]
    
    modality -->|DICOM C STORE over TCP port 104| pacs
    pacs -->|DICOM C FIND C MOVE| workstation
    workstation -->|DICOM C STORE archive| pacs
    pacs -->|HTTP GET JPEG 2000| web
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern Reference:** This question bank models the **End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for the 2024 Scheme. *Part A* tests recall (Bloom Level 1–2); *Part B* tests application and analysis (Bloom Level 3–4). All questions are mapped to the Course Outcome **CO3 (Design and apply appropriate communication protocols for biomedical systems)**.

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Answer ANY TWO out of THREE, typically 1 × 3 = 3 marks; or 2 × 3 = 6 marks in quiz format)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) and Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE). Which one is preferred for a wearable continuous SpO₂ monitor and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Bluetooth Classic (BR/EDR) | Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Current** | ~30 mA | ~15 mA (advertising) → can drop to <1 µA in sleep |
| **Data Rate** | 1–3 Mbps | 125 kbps – 2 Mbps |
| **Latency** | 100 ms typical | 7.5 ms connection interval (down to 1.25 ms with Coded PHY) |
| **Modulation** | GFSK, π/4-DQPSK, 8-DPSK | GFSK only |
| **Use Case** | Audio streaming (stethoscopes), file transfer | Sensor telemetry (SpO₂, ECG, glucose) |

**Preferred:** **BLE** is preferred because it consumes ~1/10 the power of Classic Bluetooth, enabling multi-day battery life on coin cells or small Li-Po packs. The 1–2 Mbps data rate is more than sufficient for SpO₂ waveforms (sampling at 100 Hz × 16 bits = 1.6 kbps). [3 marks]

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the MICS band? State its frequency range, maximum EIRP, and one application.**

**Model Answer:**
- **MICS** = **Medical Implant Communication Service** (also called MedRadio in USA, allocated by **FCC Part 95**, ITU-R Recommendation **RS.1346**).
- **Frequency range:** **402 – 405 MHz**.
- **Maximum EIRP:** **25 microwatts (µW)** EIRP — this restriction limits heating of surrounding tissue.
- **Channelization:** 10 channels of 300 kHz each.
- **Application:** Communication between an **implanted pacemaker/defibrillator** and an external **programmer/reader** placed over the patient's chest during follow-up visits. [3 marks]

---

### 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Answer ANY ONE — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfalls):**
> 1. *Failing to convert units* (e.g., dBW ↔ Watts, MHz ↔ Hz) costs **2 marks minimum**.
> 2. *Forgetting protocol overhead* in frame-time calculations → lose 1 mark.
> 3. *Confusing "bit rate" with "symbol rate" or "baud"* → deduction of 1.5 marks.
> 4. *Citing MICS for non-implantable devices* (or Wi-Fi for implants) → major conceptual error → **3 marks lost**.
> 5. *No diagrams* for design questions → typically 1–2 mark deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **OSI-based layered architecture of a wireless biomedical telemetry system** using BLE. Explain the function of each layer. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wireless patient monitoring system transmits ECG data over a 2.4 GHz BLE channel with bandwidth $B = 2$ MHz. The received SNR is **20 dB**. Calculate:
   - (i) The **Shannon channel capacity**.
   - (ii) The **minimum transmit power** required at the sensor if the path loss $PL = 70$ dB and the receiver sensitivity is **−90 dBm**.
   - (iii) The **frame transmission time** for a 128-byte BLE packet at $R = 1$ Mbps. Comment on whether this is suitable for real-time ECG. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Layered Architecture — 7 marks**

A wireless biomedical telemetry system (e.g., BLE-based ECG monitor) is structured in **5 layers**, each responsible for a specific function:

1. **Application Layer (top):** Houses the **IEEE 11073 PHDC** profile or vendor-specific GATT services. Translates raw ECG samples (from the MCU) into standardized objects (e.g., `MDC_ECG_LEAD_II`) with units, timestamps, and patient ID. Sends alerts (e.g., ventricular fibrillation) using `MDC_EVT_ALARM`.

2. **Security Layer (overlay):** Manages **LE Secure Connections** pairing (Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman – P-256), **AES-128 CCM** encryption, and **device authentication** (HIPAA/GDPR compliance).

3. **Transport / Network Layer:** **L2CAP** (Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol) multiplexes logical channels, performs **Segmentation and Reassembly (SAR)**. **ATT** (Attribute Protocol) carries the data as *characteristics* with read/write/notify permissions.

4. **Data Link Layer (MAC):** Implements **CSMA/CA**, generates **CRC-24** for packet integrity, manages **connection events** at 7.5 ms intervals, and assigns **hop channels** per the channel map.

5. **Physical Layer (PHY):** **GFSK modulation** (BT = 0.5, modulation index 0.5) over 40 RF channels (f = 2402 + 2k MHz, k = 0…39). Bit rate = 1 Mbps (LE 1M), 2 Mbps (LE 2M), or 500/125 kbps (LE Coded).

**Functions:**
- *Encapsulation & decapsulation* of data at each layer.
- *End-to-end reliability* via ACKs and retransmissions.
- *Spectrum efficiency* via adaptive frequency hopping (AFH).
- *Power management* via advertising/scanning/connection states.

**Block Diagram (textual):**

```
[ECG Electrodes] → [Analog Front-End] → [MCU + ADC] 
                ↓
        [App Layer: 11073 PHDC]
                ↓
        [Security: AES-128]
                ↓
        [Transport: L2CAP + ATT]
                ↓
        [MAC: CSMA/CA + CRC]
                ↓
        [PHY: GFSK Modulator + 2.4 GHz RF]
                ↓
        [Antenna → Wireless Channel]
```

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Step (i) Shannon capacity:**

$$\text{SNR (linear)} = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) = 2 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(101)$$

$$\log_2(101) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{2.0043}{0.30103} \approx 6.658$$

$$C \approx 2 \times 10^6 \times 6.658 \approx 13.32 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step (ii) Transmit power:**

Receiver sensitivity: $P_{\text{rx}} = -90 \text{ dBm}$
Path loss: $PL = 70 \text{ dB}$

$$P_{\text{tx, dBm}} = P_{\text{rx, dBm}} + PL = -90 + 70 = -20 \text{ dBm}$$

$$P_{\text{tx, W}} = 10^{-20/10} \text{ mW} = 10 \text{ µW} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W}$$

**Step (iii) Frame transmission time:**

$$N_{\text{bits}} = 128 \times 8 = 1024 \text{ bits}$$

$$t_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{1024}{10^6} = 1.024 \text{ ms}$$

With BLE protocol overhead (preamble 1 byte, access address 4 bytes, header 2 bytes, CRC 3 bytes ≈ 10 bytes extra), total frame ≈ 138 bytes:

$$t_{\text{real}} \approx \dfrac{138 \times 8}{10^6} \approx 1.104 \text{ ms}$$

**Comment:** A 1.1 ms transmission time is **well-suited for real-time ECG**, which requires latency <100 ms for critical alarms. The BLE connection event (7.5 ms) can comfortably accommodate several such frames per interval.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **HL7 v2.x**, **HL7 FHIR**, and **DICOM** with respect to: data format, transport protocol, primary use case, and a typical hospital scenario. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Design a **WBAN** (Wireless Body Area Network) for an elderly patient with three sensors: a chest ECG, a wrist SpO₂, and an ankle-worn accelerometer (fall detection). For each sensor, justify the choice of:
   - (i) Communication protocol (BLE, ZigBee, ANT+, proprietary)
   - (ii) Data rate and duty cycle
   - (iii) Estimated battery life (assume 3 V, 250 mAh coin cell) **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Comparison of HL7 v2.x, FHIR, and DICOM — 7 marks**

| Feature | HL7 v2.x | HL7 FHIR | DICOM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Format** | Pipe-delimited ASCII (`|`) | JSON / XML (RESTful) | Binary IOD + Tag-Length-Value |
| **Transport** | MLLP (TCP, port 2570), LLP | HTTP/HTTPS (REST), OAuth 2.0 | TCP/IP (port 104), DICOMweb (HTTP) |
| **Standard Body** | HL7 International | HL7 International | NEMA / ISO 12052 |
| **Primary Use** | Hospital EMR ↔ HIS messaging | Mobile health apps, cloud APIs | Medical imaging (CT, MRI, US, X-ray) |
| **Sample Message** | `OBX\|1\|NM\|8867-4^HR^LN\|\|72\|/min` | `{"resourceType":"Observation","code":"8867-4","value":72}` | `(0008,1030) "CHEST CT"` |
| **Hospital Scenario** | ICU monitor sending vitals to EMR every 5 s | Patient accessing lab results via smartphone app | Radiologist downloading a 500 MB CT series for review |
| **Interop Score** | ★★★★ (legacy ubiquitous) | ★★★★★ (modern, mobile-first) | ★★★★★ (imaging gold standard) |
| **Version Example** | v2.5, v2.7 | R4 (v4.0.1), R5 | DICOM 3.0 (2024 edition) |

**Key Takeaway:** HL7 v2.x still dominates **legacy enterprise systems**; FHIR is the **future of mobile and cloud health IT**; DICOM is the **exclusive standard for medical imaging**.

**(b) WBAN Design — 7 marks**

| Sensor | Protocol Justification | Data Rate & Duty Cycle | Battery Life Calculation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chest ECG (continuous)** | **BLE 5.0** — lowest power, 1–2 Mbps, ubiquitous smartphone support. ANT+ is also possible but proprietary. | $f_s = 500$ Hz, 12 bits, 1 lead → **6 kbps**. Duty cycle: **100%** during monitoring (24 h). | $I_{\text{avg}} = 8$ mA (BLE active 50%, sleep 50%): $T = 250/8 \approx 31.25$ h ≈ **1.3 days** per coin cell. |
| **Wrist SpO₂ (intermittent)** | **BLE** — same as above, benefits from shared smartphone gateway. | $f_s = 100$ Hz, 16 bits, 1 ch → **1.6 kbps**. Duty cycle: spot-check, **5 min/day active**. | Average current: 0.05 mA (5 min / 1440 min). $T = 250 / 0.05 = 5000$ h ≈ **208 days** ≈ **7 months**. |
| **Ankle Accelerometer (fall detection)** | **ZigBee (802.15.4)** if mesh needed across home; otherwise **BLE** for smartphone tether. | $f_s = 50$ Hz, 12 bits, 3-axis → **1.8 kbps**. Duty cycle: **<1%** (event-driven). | Sleep current dominates. $I_{\text{avg}} \approx 0.02$ mA. $T = 250/0.02 = 12{,}500$ h ≈ **1.4 years**. |

**Network Topology:** A **star topology** with all three sensors connecting directly to a **smartphone gateway** is preferred for simplicity. If the patient lives alone and requires caregiver alerts beyond Bluetooth range, an **intermediate ZigBee/LoRa relay** is added.

**Justification Summary (per KTU rubric):**
- **Why BLE over ZigBee for ECG?** Lower latency (7.5 ms vs 15 ms), universal smartphone support, and lower per-node cost.
- **Why not Wi-Fi for the accelerometer?** Wi-Fi power draw (~200 mA) would drain the coin cell in <2 hours.
- **Why not all ZigBee?** ZigBee requires a coordinator and is overkill for 3 sensors.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEEP THIS LIST AS YOUR LAST-NIGHT REVISION SHEET.**

- [x] **RS-232** uses **±3 V to ±25 V** swing; max **115.2 kbps**, max **15 m** cable.
- [x] **USB 2.0** is **480 Mbps**; **USB 3.0** is **5 Gbps**; **USB PHDC** is the medical device class.
- [x] **Bluetooth Classic:** 79 channels, 1600 hops/s, 1–3 Mbps, 30 mA peak.
- [x] **BLE:** 40 channels, 1–2 Mbps, **AES-128** encryption, 7.5 ms connection interval.
- [x] **ZigBee (802.15.4):** 250 kbps, mesh, suitable for hospital asset tracking.
- [x] **Wi-Fi (802.11):** 2.4/5/6 GHz; avoid in MRI rooms (RF interference).
- [x] **MICS band: 402–405 MHz**, **25 µW EIRP**, used for implant telemetry.
- [x] **WBAN standard: IEEE 802.15.6**, with on-body path-loss exponent $n \approx 2.8$–3.1.
- [x] **IEEE 11073** = plug-and-play medical device communication; **OXP** = binary, low-overhead encoding.
- [x] **HL7 v2.x** = pipe-delimited ASCII; **HL7 FHIR** = JSON/XML REST.
- [x] **DICOM** uses **TCP port 104**; **DICOMweb** uses HTTP/REST.
- [x] **FDA SAR limit** = **1.6 W/kg** (1 g avg) — critical for wireless implants.
- [x] **Shannon-Hartley:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- [x] **Nyquist rate:** $N = 2B \log_2 M$ — minimum bandwidth for given bit rate.
- [x] **Frame time:** $t = N_{\text{bits}} / R$ — convert bytes to bits always.
- [x] **BLE is preferred over Classic BT** for wearables because of **~10× lower power** consumption.
- [x] **Galvanic isolation** (≥5 kV) is required when connecting patient-connected devices to hospital IT networks.
- [x] **Security mandate:** HIPAA, GDPR, and IEC 80001-2 require encryption for all wireless medical data in transit.
- [x] **Real-time constraint:** Patient-monitoring alarms must reach the clinician within **<100 ms** — choose protocols with low connection intervals (BLE, not Wi-Fi).
- [x] **DICOM SOP class** = Service-Object Pair — fundamental for image transfer (C-STORE, C-FIND, C-MOVE).
- [x] **HL7 trigger event ORU^R01** = "Observation Result" — used for unsolicited ECG/vital transmission.
- [x] **IEEE 11073 object classes:** MDS, Metric (Numeric, RT-SA = Real-Time Sample Array, Enumeration).
- [x] **Continua Health Alliance (now PCHA)** certifies devices that comply with IEEE 11073 + BLE + FHIR stack.
- [x] **Liability rule:** Always quote **IEC 60601-1** (general safety) and **IEC 60601-1-2** (EMC) when justifying protocol choice in exams.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 — PROTOCOLS | KTU 2024 SCHEME | PEBMT752**
