topics.title: Design Transfer Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Transfer Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Transfer** is the formal, documented stage of the design and development lifecycle in which the verified and validated **Design Output (Design Outputs)** is accurately translated into production specifications, manufacturing procedures, quality system requirements, and installation/servicing protocols, thereby transforming a prototype into a **manufacturable, inspectable, and supportable medical device** ready for commercial distribution.

According to **FDA 21 CFR § 820.30(h)** — *Design Transfer*, each manufacturer of a Class II or Class III medical device (and certain Class I devices subject to design controls) shall establish and maintain procedures to ensure that the device design is correctly translated into production methods, tooling, fixtures, and inspection/test methods.

The harmonized international standard **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.3.8) — Design and Development Transfer** mandates that the organization shall transfer the design and development outputs to manufacturing in a manner that verifies these outputs are suitable for manufacturing before they become part of the final device specification.

$$ \boxed{\text{Design Transfer} = f(\text{Design Outputs}, \text{Production Capability}, \text{Quality System})} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3 Learning Outcome Mapping**
> Upon completion of this topic, the student will be able to:
> - **CO3:** Explain the procedures, documentation, and regulatory requirements governing the transition of a medical device design from development to commercial production.
> - **CO4:** Appraise the role of Design History File (DHF), Device Master Record (DMR), and Device History Record (DHR) in ensuring design transfer integrity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Handover of a House Blueprint"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine an architect (Design Engineer) who has spent 3 years drawing a stunning house blueprint on a software tool. The drawings look perfect on the screen. Now imagine the architect hands over these drawings to a contractor (Manufacturing Team). What happens if the contractor can't read the symbols? What happens if the foundation depth isn't marked? What happens if the local soil conditions weren't factored in? The house might collapse. Design Transfer is the **"structured handover meeting"** where the architect sits with the contractor, the site engineer, the procurement officer, and the quality inspector to ensure **every drawing detail, every material specification, every tolerance, and every test procedure is unambiguous, buildable, and verifiable**. It is *not* a single event — it is a coordinated, multi-disciplinary process.

| Analogy Element | Real-World Counterpart (Biomedical Design) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Architect's blueprint | Verified & Validated **Design Outputs** |
| Handover meeting | **Design Transfer Plan & Review** |
| Site engineer | **Process / Manufacturing Engineer** |
| Procurement officer | **Supply Chain / Vendor Qualification** |
| Quality inspector | **Incoming / In-process / Final QC** |
| Building code inspector | **Regulatory Body (FDA / CDSCO / Notified Body)** |
| Final furnished house | **Finished, released medical device** |

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Regulatory Metrics

| Standard / Regulation | Issuing Body | Clause / Section | Mandate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **21 CFR 820.30(h)** | U.S. FDA | Design Transfer | Ensure design correctly translated into production |
| **ISO 13485:2016** | ISO | Clause 7.3.8 | Transfer outputs to manufacturing, verify suitability |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | ISO | Risk Management | Verify risks are acceptable post-transfer |
| **IEC 62366-1:2015** | IEC | Usability Engineering | Transfer usability engineering file |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | European Commission | Annex II, Annex III | Technical Documentation must reflect transferred design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Takeaway:** Design Transfer is a **regulatory gate** — a device cannot be commercially released until the transfer has been **documented, reviewed, and approved by responsible functions** in writing.

---

## 1.4 Where Does Design Transfer Sit in the Design Control Lifecycle?

> [!NOTE]
> Design Transfer is the **8th and final sub-element** of the Design Controls (per 21 CFR § 820.30) and is positioned **immediately after** Design Validation and **concurrent with** Design Changes / Design History File (DHF) compilation.

The design control waterfall (per FDA / KTU 2024 syllabus):

$$
\text{Design Plan} \rightarrow \text{Design Input} \rightarrow \text{Design Output} \rightarrow \text{Design Review} \rightarrow
$$
$$
\text{Design Verification} \rightarrow \text{Design Validation} \rightarrow \text{Design Transfer} \rightarrow \text{Design Changes}
$$

Each arrow represents a **formal, documented gate** that must be cleared before the next phase begins.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Design Transfer

The Design Transfer process is not a single activity — it is a **multi-pillar construct** comprising five interdependent engineering activities. Each pillar must be formally executed and recorded.

### Pillar 1 — Production Specification Generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Conversion of the verified **Design Outputs** (drawings, BOMs, schematics, software source code, sterilization parameters) into **manufacturing-level work instructions**, **traveler/routing sheets**, and **process flow diagrams** that shop-floor operators can execute without ambiguity.

**Key Activities:**
- Translation of engineering tolerances (e.g., $\pm 0.05$ mm) into **operator-readable work instructions**.
- Generation of **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)** and **PFMEAs** (Process Failure Mode and Effects Analyses).
- Creation of **acceptance criteria documents** for receiving, in-process, and final inspection.
- Definition of **critical-to-quality (CTQ)** parameters and their **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** limits.

**Engineering "Why":** A design engineer thinks in terms of *function*; a manufacturing engineer thinks in terms of *process*. The transfer process **bridges this semantic gap** by explicitly defining *how* the function will be *physically achieved* on the production line.

---

### Pillar 2 — Tooling, Fixtures, and Equipment Qualification

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Identification, design/fabrication, and **IQ/OQ/PQ qualification** (Installation, Operational, and Performance Qualification) of all jigs, fixtures, tooling, dies, gauges, and process equipment required to consistently produce the device at the design-specified tolerances.

The qualification lifecycle follows the classic V-model:

$$ \text{Design Qualification (DQ)} \rightarrow \text{Installation Qualification (IQ)} \rightarrow \text{Operational Qualification (OQ)} \rightarrow \text{Performance Qualification (PQ)} $$

For a medical device like an **infusion pump** or **blood pressure monitor**, examples include:
- Custom PCB test fixtures (bed-of-nails).
- Injection-molding dies for the plastic housing.
- Calibration fixtures for the pressure transducer.
- Reflow soldering profiles for SMT components.

---

### Pillar 3 — Supply Chain & Supplier Qualification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Formal qualification of all vendors and suppliers providing **critical components, raw materials, sub-assemblies, or services** (e.g., sterilization, packaging, label printing) to ensure their delivered goods/services consistently meet the **design input specifications**.

**Qualification Framework:**

| Qualification Level | Trigger | Required Evidence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initial Qualification** | New supplier or new part | Site audit, capability index $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ |
| **Ongoing Monitoring** | Active supply | Periodic audits, SCAR (Supplier Corrective Action Request) tracking |
| **Change Control** | Supplier process/material change | Formal re-qualification, design impact analysis |

The supplier's process capability is often quantified by the **Process Capability Index**:

$$ C_{pk} = \min\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right) $$

where $\mu$ is the process mean, $\sigma$ is the standard deviation, USL and LSL are the upper and lower specification limits (derived from the **design tolerance**).

> A $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is the industry-accepted threshold for medical device critical components; for **safety-critical** functions, $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ is often mandated.

---

### Pillar 4 — Quality System Integration

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Embedding all design transfer outputs into the organization's **Quality Management System (QMS)** so that they are accessible, controlled, version-locked, and actionable by production, quality, regulatory, and service personnel.

This involves populating:
- **Device Master Record (DMR)** — the complete recipe for building one device.
- **Quality System Regulation (QSR) procedures** — incoming inspection, line clearance, labeling control.
- **Document Control System (DCS)** — ensuring operators are working only to the *current approved revision*.
- **Training Matrix** — confirming operators are *trained and certified* on the latest procedures.

---

### Pillar 5 — Design History File (DHF) Closure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Final compilation, indexing, and archival of **all design and development records** — from initial user needs through transfer — into the regulatory **Design History File (DHF)**, which serves as the *objective evidence* that the device was developed in accordance with the approved design plan and applicable regulations.

The DHF is the **single most important regulatory artifact** in design transfer. Per 21 CFR § 820.30(j) and ISO 13485 §7.3.10, it must contain or reference all records necessary to demonstrate conformity. It is **audited by FDA inspectors during FDA 483 / QSIT inspections**.

---

## 2.2 The Design Transfer Plan — Structure

A robust Design Transfer Plan (DTP) is a **controlled, cross-functional document** typically authored jointly by R&D, Manufacturing Engineering, Quality, and Regulatory. Its structure:

| Section | Content | Owner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Scope** | Device name, model, intended use | R&D Lead |
| **2. References** | DHF, DMR, Risk Management File, Usability File | Document Control |
| **3. Inputs** | List of design outputs to be transferred | R&D |
| **4. Activities** | Pillar-by-pillar task list with milestones | Cross-functional |
| **5. Acceptance Criteria** | What constitutes "successful" transfer? | Quality |
| **6. Responsibilities** | RACI matrix | Project Manager |
| **7. Risk Assessment** | Identified transfer risks & mitigations | Risk Manager |
| **8. Schedule** | Gantt chart of transfer activities | Project Manager |
| **9. Approval Signatures** | Multi-functional sign-off | All functions |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Design Transfer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-know mathematical and procedural formulas** relevant to KTU ESE questions on this topic. Master these for full marks.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Dimensionless | Supplier / process qualification |
| 2 | Process Capability (centering) | $C_{p} = \dfrac{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless | Process spread assessment |
| 3 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$ | ppm | Six-Sigma quality benchmarking |
| 4 | First Pass Yield | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good units (no rework)}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100\%$ | \% | Line capability during PQ |
| 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | \% | End-to-end production yield |
| 6 | Design Transfer Readiness Index | $\text{DTRI} = \dfrac{\text{Critical activities completed}}{\text{Total critical activities}} \times 100\%$ | \% | Go / No-Go gate metric |
| 7 | Risk Priority Number (Transfer) | $\text{RPN}_{\text{transfer}} = S \times O \times D$ | Score 1–1000 | Prioritizing transfer risks |
| 8 | Gauge R&R (GRR) | $\% \text{GRR} = \dfrac{6\sigma_{\text{gage}}}{0.5(\text{USL}-\text{LSL})} \times 100\%$ | \% | Measurement system adequacy |
| 9 | Tolerance Stack-Up (linear) | $T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sqrt{t_{i}^{2}}$ (RSS) or $\sum t_{i}$ (worst-case) | mm / µm | Assembly fit verification |
| 10 | Regulatory Compliance Count | $N_{\text{compliant}} = \sum_{k} \mathbb{1}(R_{k})$ | Integer | Checklist audit of all regs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics transform the *qualitative* notion of "design is ready" into a *quantitative, defensible, and regulator-friendly* decision matrix. KTU examiners often pose numerical questions based on $C_{pk}$, FPY, or DPMO during design transfer case studies.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

| Application | Design Transfer Focus |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Cardiac Pacemaker** | Hermeticity testing, battery qualification, sterilization validation (EtO), supplier of lithium-iodine cells |
| **MRI Scanner** | Superconducting magnet ramp-down procedure, cryogen handling SOPs, RF shielding of production hall |
| **Insulin Pen** | Injection-molding tooling for 0.01 mL dose accuracy, dose-counter mechanism tolerance stack-up |
| **COVID-19 Rapid Antigen Kit** | Membrane printing process, conjugate pad cutting, humidity-controlled packaging line |
| **Surgical Robot (e.g., Da Vinci)** | Software build release, cybersecurity penetration testing, surgeon-training simulator production |
| **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** | Enzyme-coated needle electrode sensor lot qualification, Bluetooth pairing test fixtures |

In each case, **the design cannot ship** until transfer activities produce a **Device Master Record** that allows an *inexperienced operator* in a *clean room* to *consistently manufacture* a device that *matches* the validated unit.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Process Capability Index ($C_{pk}$) Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (KTU-style ESE question):** A medical device manufacturer is transferring a Class II **blood-glucose test strip** production line. The critical dimension is the **electrode width** on the strip. The design specifies a target of **1.00 mm** with design tolerances of **$\pm 0.05$ mm**. After 50 production batches, the process mean is $\mu = 1.005$ mm and the standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.012$ mm.
> **(a)** Calculate $C_{pk}$. **(b)** Determine if the process is capable at the standard $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ threshold. **(c)** If not, what is the maximum allowable $\sigma$ to achieve $C_{pk} = 1.33$?

### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Identify specification limits.
$$ \text{USL} = 1.00 + 0.05 = 1.05 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{LSL} = 1.00 - 0.05 = 0.95 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2:** Identify process parameters.
$$ \mu = 1.005 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma = 0.012 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 3:** Calculate the upper and lower one-sided capability indices.
$$ C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{1.05 - 1.005}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.045}{0.036} = 1.250 $$

$$ C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{1.005 - 0.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.055}{0.036} = 1.528 $$

**Step 4:** $C_{pk}$ is the minimum of the two.
$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.250, \ 1.528) = 1.250 $$

### Solution — Part (b)

Since $C_{pk} = 1.250 < 1.33$, the process is **NOT capable** at the medical-device standard. The process is being pulled toward the **upper specification limit** (note: $C_{pk,\text{upper}} < C_{pk,\text{lower}}$), indicating the **process mean is off-center** (it should ideally be at 1.00 mm, not 1.005 mm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students will state "$C_{pk} = 1.250$" and conclude "process is good." You must explicitly **compare against the threshold** (1.33 for medical devices, 1.67 for safety-critical) and **identify the offending side** of the distribution.

### Solution — Part (c)

We need to find the maximum $\sigma_{\max}$ such that the lower (worst) $C_{pk} = 1.33$. The minimum of the two expressions is the upper one (since the mean is shifted up). So:

$$ C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma_{\max}} \geq 1.33 $$

Solving for $\sigma_{\max}$:
$$ \sigma_{\max} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3 \times 1.33} = \frac{1.05 - 1.005}{3.99} = \frac{0.045}{3.99} = 0.01128 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 0.0113 \text{ mm} } $$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The current process must reduce its standard deviation by approximately **6%** (from 0.0120 to 0.0113 mm) — and ideally **re-center the mean** back to 1.000 mm — to be considered capable. This is a typical **design transfer gap** that the manufacturing team must close before the process is released to production.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) in a Multi-Step Assembly

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A **dialysis machine** assembly line has 5 sequential stations with the following First Pass Yields (FPY): 99.0%, 97.5%, 98.2%, 99.5%, 96.8%. Calculate the **Rolled Throughput Yield** and the **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** if the machine has 200 critical assembly opportunities.

**Step 1:** Convert FPY to decimal form.
$$ \text{FPY}_{1} = 0.990, \ \text{FPY}_{2} = 0.975, \ \text{FPY}_{3} = 0.982, \ \text{FPY}_{4} = 0.995, \ \text{FPY}_{5} = 0.968 $$

**Step 2:** Compute the Rolled Throughput Yield.
$$ \text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} \text{FPY}_{i} = 0.990 \times 0.975 \times 0.982 \times 0.995 \times 0.968 $$

Intermediate multiplications:
$$ 0.990 \times 0.975 = 0.96525 $$
$$ 0.96525 \times 0.982 = 0.94787 $$
$$ 0.94787 \times 0.995 = 0.94313 $$
$$ 0.94313 \times 0.968 = 0.91295 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{RTY} \approx 91.30\% } $$

**Step 3:** Calculate defects per unit.
$$ \text{Defects per unit} = 200 \times (1 - 0.9130) = 200 \times 0.0870 = 17.40 \text{ defects} $$

**Step 4:** Calculate DPMO.
$$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{17.40 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 200} = \frac{17{,}400{,}000}{200} = 87{,}000 \text{ ppm} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DPMO} \approx 87{,}000 \text{ defects per million opportunities} } $$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A Six-Sigma medical device operation targets DPMO $\leq 3.4$. At 87,000 DPMO, this is roughly **4.0-sigma** quality — a typical "design transfer baseline" that the team must improve through **Pareto-driven root cause analysis** (e.g., station 5 at 96.8% is the lowest FPY and likely the largest contributor).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert percentages to decimals **before** multiplying. Always show the explicit numerical chain in your KTU answer — partial marks are awarded for each correct intermediate step.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Design Transfer Readiness Index (DTRI)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A ventilator design transfer has 24 critical activities. As of the project review meeting, 19 are complete, 3 are in progress, and 2 are blocked. Calculate the **Design Transfer Readiness Index** and the **Go / No-Go status** if the company's transfer policy mandates DTRI $\geq 90\%$ AND zero blocked activities.

**Step 1:** Count completed critical activities.
$$ N_{\text{complete}} = 19 $$

**Step 2:** Calculate DTRI.
$$ \text{DTRI} = \frac{N_{\text{complete}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{19}{24} \times 100\% = 79.17\% $$

**Step 3:** Apply the Go / No-Go criteria.
$$ \text{DTRI} = 79.17\% < 90\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAIL} $$
$$ N_{\text{blocked}} = 2 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAIL} $$

**Conclusion:** **NO-GO.** The transfer must not proceed. The team must:
- Complete 3 more activities to reach $\text{DTRI} = 22/24 = 91.67\%$.
- Resolve the 2 blocked activities (typically a **CAPA** or **supplier audit** issue).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Key Transfer Artifacts

| Artifact | Full Form | Authored During | Purpose | Regulatory Anchor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DHF** | Design History File | Entire design cycle | Objective evidence of design control execution | 21 CFR 820.30(j) |
| **DMR** | Device Master Record | Design Transfer | Complete recipe to manufacture one device | 21 CFR 820.181 |
| **DHR** | Device History Record | Per production lot | As-built record of a specific manufactured lot | 21 CFR 820.184 |
| **RMF** | Risk Management File | Risk management process | Live risk register updated through lifecycle | ISO 14971 |
| **UEF** | Usability Engineering File | Usability engineering | Evidence of human-factors engineering | IEC 62366-1 |
| **TMF** | Technical Master File (EU) | Pre-market | Submission-ready technical documentation | EU MDR Annex II/III |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic (KTU Exam Tip):** **"D-H-M-R"** — **D**esign **H**istory, **M**aster **R**ecord, then the **R**unning lot record. Order = chronological + abstraction level.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Design Transfer Process Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the Design Transfer process, mapping the cross-functional interactions from validated design outputs to commercial release.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Verified and Validated Design Outputs] --> B[Design Transfer Plan Authored]
    B --> C{Cross-Functional Review}
    C -- Approved --> D[Pillar 1: Production Specification Generation]
    C -- Rejected --> A1[Iterate Design Outputs]
    A1 --> C

    D --> E[Pillar 2: Tooling and Equipment Qualification]
    E --> F[Pillar 3: Supplier Qualification]
    F --> G[Pillar 4: Quality System Integration]
    G --> H[Pillar 5: DHF Compilation and Closure]
    H --> I[Design Transfer Readiness Review]
    I --> J{All Gates Passed?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Device Master Record Released]
    J -- No --> L[Issue CAPA and Re-Execute]
    L --> D
    K --> M[Commercial Production Authorization]
    M --> N[Continuous Post-Market Surveillance]

    classDef gate fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef deliverable fill:#87cefa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef terminal fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class C,J gate
    class K,M deliverable
    class N terminal
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Yellow gates = decision/review points.
- Blue nodes = deliverables (artifacts produced).
- Green node = terminal state (commercial release).
- The **loop back** from `L → D` represents the **CAPA-driven iteration** mandated by 21 CFR 820.100.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — RACI Matrix of the Transfer Team

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R[Responsible Team]
        R1[Design Engineer]
        R2[Manufacturing Engineer]
        R3[Quality Engineer]
        R4[Regulatory Affairs]
        R5[Supply Chain]
    end

    A[Pillar 1: Production Spec] --> R1
    A --> R2
    A --> R3

    B[Pillar 2: Tooling Qual] --> R2
    B --> R3
    B --> R5

    C[Pillar 3: Supplier Qual] --> R5
    C --> R3

    D[Pillar 4: QMS Integration] --> R3
    D --> R2
    D --> R4

    E[Pillar 5: DHF Closure] --> R1
    E --> R3
    E --> R4

    classDef person fill:#ffefd5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 person
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Notice that the **Quality Engineer (R3)** appears in *all five* pillars. This is by design — Quality is the **integrative function** that ensures regulatory and procedural conformance across every transfer activity. KTU examiners frequently ask students to identify which function is the "common thread" — the answer is **Quality Assurance / Quality Engineering**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Approval Sequencing of the Design Transfer

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DE as Design Engineer
    participant ME as Manufacturing Engineer
    participant QE as Quality Engineer
    participant RA as Regulatory Affairs
    participant PM as Project Manager

    DE->>PM: Submit Transfer Plan (DTP)
    PM->>ME: Manufacturing Feasibility Review
    PM->>QE: QMS & Risk Impact Review
    PM->>RA: Regulatory Submission Readiness
    ME-->>PM: Feasibility Approval
    QE-->>PM: Quality Approval
    RA-->>PM: Regulatory Approval
    PM->>DE: Consolidated Approval to Proceed
    DE->>ME: Release DMR to Production
    ME->>QE: Initiate PQ Builds
    QE->>PM: PQ Result Report
    PM->>DE: Transfer Closure Approval
    DE->>QE: Archive DHF
```

**Reading the Sequence:**
- Notice the **two parallel review branches** (ME, QE, RA run concurrently after PM routes the DTP).
- The **gate-release gate** between `DE→ME` (DMR release) is the **single most critical signature** — once signed, the device becomes producible.
- DHF archival is the **final terminal step** and is subject to retention periods (typically **device life + 2 years**, or per regional regulation, e.g., **EU MDR: 10 years** for Class III; **21 CFR: 2 years post-market**).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Design Transfer" as applied to the design and development of medical devices. List any **three** regulatory clauses that govern this activity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Design Transfer is the documented stage in the design and development lifecycle in which the **verified and validated design outputs** are accurately translated into **production specifications, manufacturing procedures, tooling, inspection methods, and quality system requirements** so that the medical device can be reproducibly manufactured and serviced.

Three governing regulatory clauses:
1. **FDA 21 CFR § 820.30(h)** — Design Transfer (U.S. regulatory requirement).
2. **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3.8** — Design and development transfer (international standard).
3. **EU MDR 2017/745, Annex II §4** — Technical documentation including production information (European requirement).

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any two clauses with brief explanation, 1 mark for the third clause — full 3 marks requires all three.)*

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Device Master Record (DMR) and the Device History Record (DHR). Mention the regulatory clause for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DMR | DHR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | The **recipe** document containing all instructions, drawings, specifications, and procedures to manufacture one device. | The **as-built record** for a specific production lot/batch of devices. |
| **Scope** | Device-level (one set per device type/model). | Lot-level (one record per production batch). |
| **Created During** | Design Transfer. | Production. |
| **Regulatory Clause** | 21 CFR § 820.181. | 21 CFR § 820.184. |

*(Award: 1.5 marks for accurate DMR distinction, 1.5 marks for accurate DHR distinction with the correct clause number.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **five pillars of the Design Transfer process** for a medical device. For each pillar, state (i) its objective and (ii) at least two concrete activities.

**(b) [7 marks]** A medical device manufacturer is transferring a Class II **electronic sphygmomanometer (digital blood pressure monitor)** to production. The cuff pressure sensor has a design specification of **40 mmHg $\pm$ 2 mmHg** at a calibration point. Pilot production of 25 batches yields a mean of $\mu = 40.5$ mmHg with $\sigma = 0.45$ mmHg.
- **(i)** Calculate $C_{pk}$ and state whether the process is capable at the $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ threshold.
- **(ii)** If the process is NOT capable, suggest **two engineering actions** the transfer team should take before approving production release.
- **(iii)** The assembly has 4 sequential stations with FPYs of 98.5%, 97.0%, 99.2%, and 96.5%. Compute the **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** and the **DPMO** assuming 150 critical assembly opportunities per device.

---

### Model Answer — Question B-A (a) [7 marks]

**Pillar 1: Production Specification Generation**
- *Objective:* Convert design outputs into shop-floor executable procedures.
- *Activities:*
  1. Generate Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs) and detailed Work Instructions for each assembly step.
  2. Create in-process and final inspection Acceptance Criteria Documents (ACDs).
  3. Define Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) parameters and their Statistical Process Control (SPC) limits.

**Pillar 2: Tooling and Equipment Qualification**
- *Objective:* Ensure all production tooling, jigs, fixtures, and equipment can consistently produce to design tolerances.
- *Activities:*
  1. Execute IQ (Installation Qualification), OQ (Operational Qualification), and PQ (Performance Qualification) protocols on the cuff-inflation pump test rig.
  2. Qualify the SMT pick-and-place machine for the PCB assembly using a DOE study.
  3. Calibrate the pressure reference standard (dead-weight tester) traceable to national standards (e.g., NABL/NIST).

**Pillar 3: Supplier / Vendor Qualification**
- *Objective:* Ensure all critical components and services meet design specifications.
- *Activities:*
  1. Audit the manufacturer of the pressure sensor (MEMS-based) and qualify them via an Initial Sample Inspection Report (ISIR).
  2. Qualify the contract sterilizer (EtO) for the cuff bladder material biocompatibility.
  3. Establish a Supplier Quality Agreement (SQA) defining change-notification obligations.

**Pillar 4: Quality System Integration**
- *Objective:* Embed all design outputs into the QMS for production use.
- *Activities:*
  1. Release the DMR into the Document Control System (DCS) with version control.
  2. Update the incoming inspection procedure to include the cuff fabric lot-acceptance protocol.
  3. Train and certify production operators on the new assembly line; record in the Training Matrix.

**Pillar 5: Design History File (DHF) Closure**
- *Objective:* Compile and archive all design records to provide objective evidence of design control execution.
- *Activities:*
  1. Index all design input/output documents, verification/validation reports, and risk analyses into the DHF.
  2. Conduct the final Design Transfer Review meeting and archive the minutes.
  3. Lock the DHF under formal change control for the commercial life of the device.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part a):** Students often **list activities without linking them to the regulatory clause** (e.g., IQ/OQ/PQ to GAMP 5 or process validation). A complete 7-mark answer **must explicitly tie each pillar to a regulatory or standard basis** (21 CFR, ISO 13485, ISO 14971, etc.). Forgetting this loses approximately 1–2 marks.

---

### Model Answer — Question B-A (b) (i) [3 marks]

**Specification limits:**
$$ \text{USL} = 42 \text{ mmHg}, \quad \text{LSL} = 38 \text{ mmHg}, \quad \mu = 40.5 \text{ mmHg}, \quad \sigma = 0.45 \text{ mmHg} $$

**Upper one-sided index:**
$$ C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{42 - 40.5}{3 \times 0.45} = \frac{1.5}{1.35} = 1.111 $$

**Lower one-sided index:**
$$ C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{40.5 - 38}{3 \times 0.45} = \frac{2.5}{1.35} = 1.852 $$

**Overall $C_{pk}$:**
$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.111, \ 1.852) = 1.111 $$

**Conclusion:** $C_{pk} = 1.111 < 1.33$. **The process is NOT capable** at the medical-device threshold. The process is **off-center** (pulled toward the upper limit) — the mean should ideally be 40.0 mmHg, not 40.5.

---

### Model Answer — Question B-A (b) (ii) [2 marks]

**Two engineering actions:**

1. **Re-center the process mean:** Adjust the calibration setpoint of the inflation pump / pressure transducer assembly station so that $\mu$ returns to 40.0 mmHg. This single action could raise $C_{pk}$ above 1.33 immediately.

2. **Reduce process variability (σ):** Conduct a **Design of Experiments (DOE)** study on the cuff-inflation valve and pressure-line path to identify noise factors inflating σ. Implement poka-yoke (error-proofing) fixtures to reduce operator-to-operator variation, and re-validate the assembly torque on the cuff-port fitting.

*(A third acceptable action: tighten the supplier's incoming-acceptance limits for the pressure sensor, e.g., $\pm 1.0$ mmHg instead of $\pm 2.0$ mmHg, to give the assembly process less incoming variation to absorb.)*

---

### Model Answer — Question B-A (b) (iii) [2 marks]

**Step 1:** Compute RTY.
$$ \text{RTY} = 0.985 \times 0.970 \times 0.992 \times 0.965 $$
$$ = 0.985 \times 0.970 = 0.95545 $$
$$ = 0.95545 \times 0.992 = 0.94781 $$
$$ = 0.94781 \times 0.965 = 0.91463 $$
$$ \boxed{\text{RTY} \approx 91.46\% } $$

**Step 2:** Compute defects per unit.
$$ \text{Defects per unit} = 150 \times (1 - 0.9146) = 150 \times 0.0854 = 12.81 \text{ defects} $$

**Step 3:** Compute DPMO.
$$ \text{DPMO} = \frac{12.81 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 150} = \frac{12{,}810{,}000}{150} = 85{,}400 \text{ ppm} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DPMO} \approx 85{,}400 \text{ defects per million opportunities} } $$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A Six-Sigma target is DPMO $\leq 3.4$. The current process at ~85,400 DPMO is roughly 4.0-sigma. The team should perform **Pareto analysis** on station 4 (FPY 96.5% — lowest) to identify the dominant defect mode and drive continuous improvement post-transfer.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (Alternative Choice)
**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **Design History File (DHF)** in detail. State its purpose, contents, regulatory anchor, and explain the relationship between the **DHF, DMR, and DHR** with a clear schematic / table.

**(b) [7 marks]** A company is transferring a **pulse oximeter** to production. Outline a **Design Transfer Plan (DTP)** including:
- (i) The four mandatory sections of the DTP and their contents.
- (ii) The five acceptance criteria you would use to declare the transfer "successful."
- (iii) Briefly explain the **role of the Quality Engineer** as the integrative function in the transfer team.

*(Students should attempt ONLY ONE of Question B-A or Question B-B per KTU module-internal choice rules.)*

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General):**
> - For 14-mark questions, **always use sub-headings and tables** — unstructured essays lose 2–3 marks automatically.
> - **Always show the regulatory reference** (e.g., "21 CFR 820.30(h)") alongside each concept.
> - For numerical sub-parts, **show every intermediate step** in a numbered or aligned block — partial marks are awarded for each correct transition.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Design Transfer Process (Module 3, PEBMT752)**

- ☐ **Definition (1-liner):** Design Transfer = the documented translation of verified & validated design outputs into reproducible production.
- ☐ **Regulatory anchors:** **21 CFR 820.30(h)** + **ISO 13485 §7.3.8** + **EU MDR Annex II/III**.
- ☐ **Five pillars:** (1) Production Specs, (2) Tooling Qual, (3) Supplier Qual, (4) QMS Integration, (5) DHF Closure.
- ☐ **Three key records:** **DHF** (design-level), **DMR** (manufacturing recipe), **DHR** (per-lot as-built). Mnemonic: **D-H-M-R** in lifecycle order.
- ☐ **DMR Clause:** **21 CFR 820.181**; **DHR Clause:** **21 CFR 820.184**; **DHF Clause:** **21 CFR 820.30(j)**.
- ☐ **Qualification cascade:** **DQ → IQ → OQ → PQ** (V-model).
- ☐ **Capability threshold for medical devices:** **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** (general) / **$\geq 1.67$** (safety-critical).
- ☐ **$C_{pk}$ formula:** $C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \dfrac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$.
- ☐ **RTY formula:** $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ — multiplicative, not additive.
- ☐ **DPMO formula:** $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities per unit}}$.
- ☐ **Six-Sigma target:** DPMO $\leq 3.4$.
- ☐ **Integrative function:** **Quality Engineer** participates in *all* five transfer pillars.
- ☐ **Gate philosophy:** Transfer is a **gate**, not an event — multiple cross-functional approvals required.
- ☐ **Post-transfer obligations:** Continue with **CAPA**, **post-market surveillance**, and **change control**.
- ☐ **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:**
  - Confusing DMR and DHR.
  - Stating $C_p$ when asked for $C_{pk}$ (centring ignored).
  - Forgetting to multiply FPYs (not average them) for RTY.
  - Missing regulatory clause numbers in long answers.
  - Omitting intermediate numerical steps in calculation sub-parts.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Design Transfer Process (PEBMT752, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Software-Hardware Test Plan for Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Software-Hardware Test Plan (SW/HW Test Plan)** for medical devices is a formal, documented master document mandated by regulatory frameworks (IEC 62304, ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR Part 820, and IEC 60601-1) that defines the **scope, objectives, strategy, resources, schedule, criteria, and procedures** for systematically verifying and validating the integrated software and hardware subsystems of a medical device throughout its product lifecycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> A Software-Hardware Test Plan is a controlled, risk-based engineering artifact that prescribes how the embedded firmware, application software, sensors, actuators, and electrical subsystems of a medical device will be exercised, measured, and judged against specified requirements to demonstrate **safety**, **effectiveness**, and **regulatory compliance** prior to market release.

The test plan bridges two domains:
1. **Software Verification (per IEC 62304)** – confirms that software outputs match specifications ("Are we building the product right?").
2. **Hardware-Software Integration Validation (per IEC 60601-1 & FDA Guidance)** – confirms that the integrated device meets user needs and intended use ("Are we building the right product?").

## 1.2 Key Terminology Primer

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Verification** | Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that specified requirements have been fulfilled (Static & Dynamic). |
| **Validation** | Confirmation by examination and provision of objective evidence that the particular requirements for the intended use can be consistently fulfilled. |
| **SOUP** | Software Of Unknown Provenance (off-the-shelf, third-party, legacy code). |
| **Traceability Matrix** | A document linking requirements → design → code → test cases (bidirectional). |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after risk control measures per ISO 14971. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Airplane Pre-Flight Inspection**
> Imagine a commercial aircraft before takeoff. The **flight plan** (analogous to the Test Plan) lists every single system to be checked: fuel, hydraulics, avionics, cabin pressure, navigation software. Each checklist item has a **pass/fail criterion** and a **responsible engineer**. The test plan for a medical device works the same way: an implantable cardiac defibrillator's firmware controlling shock delivery, the hardware sensor detecting arrhythmias, and the user interface must all be tested against pre-defined acceptance criteria before the device ever touches a patient. If even one subsystem (e.g., the battery management firmware) fails its test case, the entire device is **grounded**.

## 1.4 The Three Regulatory Pillars Governing the Test Plan

1. **IEC 62304:2006/AMD1:2015** – Medical device software life cycle processes.
2. **ISO 13485:2016, Clause 7.3.7** – Design and development validation.
3. **FDA General Principles of Software Validation (2002)** & **Cybersecurity in Medical Devices (2023)**.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Highlighted in Bold

- **Software Safety Class A** – No injury or damage to health is possible.
- **Software Safety Class B** – Non-serious injury is possible.
- **Software Safety Class C** – Death or serious injury is possible.
- **Pass Criterion (PC)** – Quantitative threshold (e.g., $\geq 99.9\%$ arrhythmia detection sensitivity).
- **Test Coverage (C)** – Percentage of requirements exercised by test cases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Software Safety Classification (IEC 62304) – The Foundation of Test Rigor

The classification dictates **how rigorous** the test plan must be. The test plan author must classify every software item (SOUP, unit, integration) before writing a single test case.

| Safety Class | Possible Consequence | Required Test Activities (Minimum) |
|:---:|---|---|
| **Class A** | No injury | Unit testing, integration testing |
| **Class B** | Non-serious injury | Unit + Integration + System testing, code review, architecture review |
| **Class C** | Death / Serious injury | Class B + Detailed software system testing, hazard analysis, formal methods recommended, full traceability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** A single misclassification (e.g., labeling Class C code as Class A) is the **#1 reason for FDA 483 observations** in medical device software audits.

## 2.2 The V-Model of Software Verification & Validation

The test plan must explicitly map each test level to a corresponding design/requirement level on the **V-Model**:

```
Requirements  <------------>  User Acceptance Testing (Validation)
  |                                  ^
System Architecture <--------> System Testing (Verification)
  |                                  ^
Software Architecture <----> Software Integration Testing
  |                                  ^
Detailed Design <---------> Software Unit Testing
  |                                  ^
Implementation (Code) -------+
```

Each arrow on the V represents a **verification activity** with measurable test results. The right side of the V is executed **bottom-up** in the test plan: units → integration → system → acceptance.

## 2.3 Test Plan Architecture – The 12 Mandatory Sections (per IEEE 829-2008 adapted for Medical Devices)

A compliant SW/HW Test Plan **must** contain:

1. **Test Plan Identifier** – Unique document number, version, author.
2. **Introduction & References** – SOPs, regulations, related docs.
3. **Test Items** – List of software units, SOUP, hardware interfaces under test.
4. **Software/Hardware Features to Be Tested** – In-scope.
5. **Features Not to Be Tested** – Out-of-scope with justification.
6. **Test Approach** – Strategy (e.g., risk-based, requirements-based, equivalence partitioning).
7. **Pass/Fail Criteria** – Quantitative and qualitative.
8. **Suspension & Resumption Criteria** – When to halt testing (e.g., critical defect found).
9. **Test Deliverables** – Test cases, logs, traceability matrix, defect reports.
10. **Testing Tasks & Responsibility Matrix (RACI)** – Who does what.
11. **Environmental Needs** – Lab equipment, simulators (e.g., ECG simulator, phantom load).
12. **Schedule & Approval** – Gantt chart, sign-off matrix.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Metric | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Test Coverage (Requirement)** | $C_{req} = \dfrac{N_{tested}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Expressed as **%**; target $= 100\%$ for Class C. |
| 2 | **Code Coverage (Statement)** | $C_{stmt} = \dfrac{S_{executed}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Used in unit testing. |
| 3 | **Branch Coverage** | $C_{branch} = \dfrac{B_{executed}}{B_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Target $\geq 95\%$ for Class C. |
| 4 | **Defect Density** | $D_{density} = \dfrac{\text{Defects found}}{\text{KLOC}}$ | KLOC = Thousand Lines Of Code. |
| 5 | **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{d,i} - t_{i})}{n}$ | Hours; $t_{d,i}$ = detection time. |
| 6 | **Sensitivity (Diagnostic Device)** | $\text{Sens} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100\%$ | TP = True Positive, FN = False Negative. |
| 7 | **Specificity (Diagnostic Device)** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP} \times 100\%$ | TN = True Negative, FP = False Positive. |
| 8 | **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detectability (1–10). |
| 9 | **Statistical Confidence (95%)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | For validation sample size; $Z=1.96$, $e=$ margin of error. |
| 10 | **Hardware-Software Interface (HSI) Coverage** | $C_{HSI} = \dfrac{N_{HSI\_exercised}}{N_{HSI\_total}} \times 100\%$ | Critical for Class C. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics feed directly into the **DHF (Design History File)** and **Tech File** required by FDA & Notified Bodies. RPN drives risk-based test prioritization — the test plan must test the highest-RPN failure modes first.

## 2.5 Hardware-Software Integration Test Strategy (Risk-Based)

A medical device test plan must allocate test effort proportional to **risk**. The strategy proceeds in five layers:

1. **Static Analysis** (Code review, lint, MISRA-C compliance) – Class B & C.
2. **Unit Testing** – Each function/module in isolation.
3. **Integration Testing** – Software modules + hardware peripherals (ADC, GPIO, I²C, SPI buses).
4. **System Testing** – Full device in simulated-use environment.
5. **Clinical Simulation / Usability Validation** – With human factors per IEC 62366-1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often skip the **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** simulation phase. For Class C devices (e.g., ventilators, infusion pumps), HIL is **mandatory** to test fault responses (sensor failure, power loss, watchdog reset).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation #1: Sample Size for Validation Testing (Statistical Confidence)

A medical device test plan must justify how many test cycles are required to demonstrate, with **95% confidence**, that a device's reliability meets its specification. The derivation proceeds as follows.

**Given:** Required reliability $R_{target}$, confidence level $1 - \alpha$, zero failures observed.

**Step 1: Relation between Confidence, Sample Size, and Reliability**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 1 - (1 - R_{target})^{n}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $C$ = achieved confidence,
- $R_{target}$ = minimum required reliability per requirement,
- $n$ = number of independent test cycles (no failures allowed).

**Step 2: Solve for $n$**

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - C &= (1 - R_{target})^{n} \\
\ln(1 - C) &= n \cdot \ln(1 - R_{target}) \\
n &= \dfrac{\ln(1 - C)}{\ln(1 - R_{target})}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Numerical Worked Example**

For a **Class C infusion pump** requiring $R_{target} = 0.99$ and $C = 0.95$:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{\ln(1 - 0.95)}{\ln(1 - 0.99)} \\
n &= \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.01)} \\
n &= \dfrac{-2.9957}{-4.6052} \\
n &= 0.6505
\end{aligned}$$

Since $n$ must be an integer, round **up**: $n = 1$? No — re-evaluate. The correct interpretation is that 0 failures in $n$ trials gives $1-(1-R)^{n}$ confidence. For $R=0.99, C=0.95$, we need:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.01)^{n} &= 0.05 \\
n \cdot \ln(0.01) &= \ln(0.05) \\
n &= \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.01)} = \dfrac{-2.9957}{-4.6052} \approx 0.65
\end{aligned}$$

This indicates that for $R = 0.99$, achieving 95% confidence with **zero failures** requires:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.01)} \approx 0.65 \rightarrow \text{ at least } 1 \text{ trial}
\end{aligned}$$

To achieve $C = 0.95$ at $R = 0.99$ with **zero failures**, the standard formula (using the rule of three / exact binomial) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{\ln(1 - C)}{\ln(1 - R_{target})} = \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.01)} = 0.65
\end{aligned}$$

However, applying the **exact binomial** for the **rule of three** with 95% confidence, 0 failures, and desired failure rate $p \leq 0.01$:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.99)} = \dfrac{-2.9957}{-0.01005} \approx 298
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-derivation Correction:** The correct formula for **upper bound on failure rate** (Clopper-Pearson exact binomial) with $k=0$ failures, $C=0.95$ is $n = \ln(\alpha) / \ln(R_{target})$ where $\alpha = 0.05$. Re-evaluating: $n = \ln(0.05) / \ln(0.99) \approx 298$ test runs are required to state, with 95% confidence, that reliability is at least 99%.

**Step 4: Interpretation**

To claim 99% reliability with 95% statistical confidence, the test plan must schedule a **minimum of 298 zero-failure test cycles**. This number goes into Section 11 of the Test Plan (Environmental Needs / Test Cycles).

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation #2: Risk Priority Number (RPN) for Test Prioritization

**Step 1: Define the three input ratings (per FMEA / ISO 14971)**

For a **software bug** that could cause incorrect defibrillation energy delivery:
- $S$ (Severity if it occurs) = 10 (catastrophic — patient death)
- $O$ (Occurrence probability) = 3 (low — firmware bug in timing loop)
- $D$ (Detectability by current controls) = 5 (moderate — review may miss)

**Step 2: Compute RPN**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN} &= S \times O \times D \\
\text{RPN} &= 10 \times 3 \times 5 = 150
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compare against threshold**

If the test plan defines RPN threshold $= 100$, this failure mode **must** be tested as a **Class C test case** with 100% branch coverage on the timing module.

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation #3: Traceability Matrix Closure

The Test Plan requires **100% closure** of the traceability matrix: every requirement has at least one test case, every test case traces to at least one requirement, and every risk control measure has a verification test.

**Step 1: Define Matrix Entries**

Let $R$ = set of requirements, $T$ = set of test cases, $C$ = set of risk controls.

**Step 2: Closure Definition**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closure} &= \min\left(\dfrac{\vert T \cap R_{mapped} \vert}{\vert R \vert}, \dfrac{\vert R \cap T_{mapped} \vert}{\vert T \vert}\right) \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: For a device with 120 requirements, 145 test cases, fully bijective mapping**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Closure} &= \min\left(\dfrac{120}{120}, \dfrac{120}{145}\right) \times 100\% = \min(1, 0.8276) \times 100\% = 82.76\%
\end{aligned}$$

This signals **25 orphan test cases** — investigation required (either expand requirements or justify removal of test cases).

## 3.4 Comparative Test Plan Matrix: Software vs. Hardware-Software Integration

| Aspect | Pure Software Test Plan | Hardware-Software Integration Test Plan |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Standard** | IEC 62304 | IEC 62304 + IEC 60601-1 |
| **Test Bed** | PC simulator, mock objects | HIL rig, sensor simulators, phantom loads |
| **Key Defect Categories** | Logic errors, memory leaks | Timing violations, EMI susceptibility, signal integrity |
| **Test Types** | Unit, integration, system | Adds environmental, EMC, electrical safety, fault injection |
| **Pass Criteria Source** | SRS, SDS | SRS, SDS, Hardware Spec, Risk File |
| **Reviewer** | Software QA Lead | Software QA + Hardware QA + Risk Manager + Clinical SME |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Test Plan Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
