
## 4.4 Decision Flow: How to Apply a Test Case

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Test Case TC ID] --> B[Preconditions Met]
    B -- No --> C[Halt and Log Defect]
    B -- Yes --> D[Execute Test Steps]
    D --> E[Capture Output]
    E --> F[Compare to Expected Result]
    F -- Match --> G[PASS Record Evidence]
    F -- Mismatch --> H[FAIL Log Defect]
    H --> I[Root Cause Analysis]
    I --> J[Re Test or Regression]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style G fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style H fill:#d62728,color:#fff
    style C fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define a Software-Hardware Test Plan for a medical device. List any four mandatory sections it must contain.**

**Model Answer:**
A Software-Hardware Test Plan for a medical device is a formal, controlled document that defines the strategy, scope, resources, schedule, pass/fail criteria, and procedures for verifying and validating the integrated software and embedded hardware subsystems of the device in compliance with IEC 62304, ISO 13485, and IEC 60601-1.

Four mandatory sections:
1. **Test Items** – Software units, SOUP, hardware interfaces under test.
2. **Features to be Tested and Not to be Tested.**
3. **Pass/Fail Criteria** – Quantitative thresholds.
4. **Suspension and Resumption Criteria** – When to halt/re-start testing.
*(Any four from the 12-section list given in Section 2.3 are acceptable.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Software Verification and Software Validation in the context of medical devices. State the governing standards.**

**Model Answer:**

| Verification | Validation |
|---|---|
| Confirms the output matches specifications. | Confirms the device meets user needs and intended use. |
| "Are we building the product right?" | "Are we building the right product?" |
| Static (reviews) + Dynamic (tests). | Dynamic (in simulated or actual use environment). |
| Governed by **IEC 62304** (Clause 5.6–5.7). | Governed by **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.7** and **FDA Guidance**. |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Model 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **V-Model of software verification and validation** as applied to medical device firmware. Show clearly the four test levels on the right arm of the V and the design artifacts on the left arm.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **Class C implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD)** requires 99% reliability of arrhythmia detection. Using the exact binomial rule with zero failures and 95% confidence, compute the minimum number of test cycles required. State the formula and justify the result.

#### Model Solution for (a)

The V-Model is a regulatory-preferred framework that pairs each development phase on the left with a corresponding test phase on the right.

**Left Arm (Development Phases):**
1. User Needs → User Requirements Specification (URS).
2. System Requirements → System Architecture Specification.
3. Software Requirements → Software Architecture Document (SAD).
4. Detailed Design → Module / Unit Design.

**Right Arm (Test Phases – executed bottom-up):**
1. **Unit Testing** – Test each software module (e.g., QRS detection algorithm) against its detailed design. Techniques: equivalence partitioning, boundary value, statement/branch coverage (target $\geq 95\%$ for Class C).
2. **Integration Testing** – Combine modules; verify inter-module interfaces. For an ICD, integrate the sensing module, classification module, and therapy module. Use stubs/drivers.
3. **System Testing** – Full firmware on hardware. Verify against Software Requirements Specification. Includes functional, performance, fault injection, EMC pre-checks.
4. **Acceptance / User Validation Testing** – Final validation in simulated clinical use (phantom thorax, ECG simulator). Verify against URS and clinical claims.

Each arrow on the V is a **traceability link** that must be documented in the Traceability Matrix and reviewed by an independent QA function.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** Target reliability $R_{target} = 0.99$, zero failures ($k = 0$), confidence $C = 0.95$, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Formula (exact binomial, Clopper-Pearson lower bound on $R$ with $k=0$):**

$$n = \dfrac{\ln(\alpha)}{\ln(R_{target})}$$

**Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.99)} \\
n &= \dfrac{-2.9957}{-0.01005} \\
n &= 298.07
\end{aligned}$$

**Rounded Up:** $n = 299$ test cycles (round up to next integer to be conservative).

**Justification:** Running 299 arrhythmia-detection test cycles with **zero failures** justifies a 95% statistical claim that the firmware's true reliability is at least 99%. This sample size must be scheduled in Section 11 of the Test Plan, and each cycle must be documented with reproducible test data (e.g., MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database inputs).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Do **not** write $n = 1$ (a common error from misapplying the "rule of thumb").
> 2. Do **not** forget to round **up** — under-sampling is a regulatory red flag.
> 3. Always cite the **exact binomial** formula, not the approximate normal.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Model 2 Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Software Safety Classification (Class A, B, C)** per **IEC 62304**. For each class, list the minimum test activities required and give one medical device example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** analysis for the following three potential software defects in a **ventilator firmware** and prioritize the test cases accordingly:

| Defect | Severity $S$ | Occurrence $O$ | Detectability $D$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| D1: Tidal volume miscalculation | 9 | 4 | 3 |
| D2: UI hang-up on alarm screen | 6 | 7 | 6 |
| D3: Battery-low false negative | 10 | 2 | 8 |

#### Model Solution for (a)

**IEC 62304 classifies software items into three classes based on the possible consequence of a software failure:**

- **Class A – No injury or damage to health is possible.** Minimum test activities: software unit testing, software integration testing. *Example:* firmware controlling the LCD backlight of a blood pressure monitor.
- **Class B – Non-serious injury is possible.** Minimum test activities: Class A activities + software system testing, code review, software architecture evaluation. *Example:* firmware calculating SpO₂ from PPG signals in a pulse oximeter.
- **Class C – Death or serious injury is possible.** Minimum test activities: Class B activities + detailed software system testing, hazard analysis, complete traceability, regression testing, fault injection, hardware-software integration. *Example:* dose calculation firmware of an **insulin pump**, or tidal volume control in a **ventilator**.

The classification cascades — a single Class C software item forces the entire SOFTWARE SYSTEM to be developed under Class C rigor.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**RPN Formula:**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Computation for each defect:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{D1} &= 9 \times 4 \times 3 = 108 \\
\text{RPN}_{D2} &= 6 \times 7 \times 6 = 252 \\
\text{RPN}_{D3} &= 10 \times 2 \times 8 = 160
\end{aligned}$$

**Prioritization (highest to lowest):**

| Rank | Defect | RPN | Test Priority |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | D2: UI hang-up on alarm screen | 252 | P1 – Execute first, full branch coverage |
| 2 | D3: Battery-low false negative | 160 | P2 – Hardware-software integration test with battery simulator |
| 3 | D1: Tidal volume miscalculation | 108 | P3 – Unit + system test with HIL |

**Test Plan Implication:** The Test Plan must document that **D2** receives the most exhaustive test cases first, including fault injection (forced UI deadlock) and recovery verification. Although D1 has the highest severity, D2's higher occurrence + detectability drives a higher RPN and must be addressed first per **ISO 14971 risk control ordering**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Never** sort purely by Severity. RPN is the product.
> 2. **Show the multiplication step-by-step** — partial marks are awarded for correct formula application.
> 3. The Test Plan must explicitly state how RPN drives **test case selection**, not just design priorities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Software-Hardware Test Plan is a regulatory-mandated, risk-based document that defines the V&V strategy for medical device software and integrated hardware.
- **Governing Standards:** **IEC 62304**, **ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.7**, **IEC 60601-1**, **FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g)**, **FDA Software Validation Guidance 2002**, **ISO 14971**.
- **Safety Classes:** **Class A** (no injury) → **Class B** (non-serious) → **Class C** (death). Test rigor scales with class.
- **V-Model:** Left arm = development; Right arm = Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance tests.
- **Mandatory Test Plan Sections:** 12 per IEEE 829-2008 adapted — focus on Pass/Fail Criteria, Suspension Criteria, Traceability, and Risk-Based Prioritization.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - RPN: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$
  - Sample size: $n = \ln(\alpha) / \ln(R_{target})$
  - Coverage: $C = N_{tested} / N_{total} \times 100\%$
- **Hardware-Software Integration:** Always include **HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop)** testing for Class C devices. EMI, ESD, and fault injection are mandatory.
- **Traceability:** Bidirectional — every requirement → test case; every test case → requirement. No orphans.
- **Common KTU Exam Keywords:** Verification vs Validation, V-Model, IEC 62304 Classes, RPN, Sample Size for Validation, Pass/Fail Criteria, Defect Density.
- **Examiner's Golden Tip:** Always cite the **standard number** and **clause** in answers (e.g., *IEC 62304 Clause 5.7*). This signals regulatory awareness and earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Testing - Subsystem and Full Device. Risk Assessment of Medical Devices. Manufacturing Supply Chain: Product Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** System Testing - Subsystem and Full Device. Risk Assessment of Medical Devices. Manufacturing Supply Chain: Product Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 System Testing in Biomedical Device Development

**Formal KTU Definition:**
System testing is the phase in the medical device design control process (per **21 CFR Part 820** and **ISO 13485:2016**) where the complete, integrated biomedical device is evaluated against its design inputs, user needs, and intended use. It is divided into two hierarchical tiers: **Subsystem Testing** (module-level verification) and **Full Device Testing** (system-level integration and validation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU board emphasizes the difference between **Verification** ("Are we building the device right?") and **Validation** ("Are we building the right device?"). Subsystem testing predominantly performs **verification**, while full-device testing is the principal site for **validation**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Car Assembly Line:**
Think of designing a cardiac pacemaker. You wouldn't hand a finished pacemaker to a patient before testing the battery, the microcontroller, and the lead electrodes separately.
- **Subsystem Testing** is like a car manufacturer individually testing the engine, brakes, and airbags on test benches before they go into a chassis. Each component is poked, prodded, and stressed in isolation.
- **Full Device Testing** is like taking the *fully assembled car* to a closed track — and then to public roads — to confirm that the engine, brakes, airbags, and infotainment system all *cooperate* correctly under real-world driving conditions. For a medical device, that "real-world test" happens in the form of **simulated use environments**, **animal studies**, and finally **clinical investigations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Block (KTU Board Frequently Asked):**
> - **Design Input (DI):** The physical and performance requirements of a device (e.g., "defibrillator must deliver 200 J within 10 ms").
> - **Design Output (DO):** The resulting device specifications, drawings, and software.
> - **Design Verification:** Confirms the DO meets the DI through testing.
> - **Design Validation:** Confirms the device meets user needs and intended use.

---

### 1.2 Risk Assessment of Medical Devices

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Risk Assessment is the *overall process* comprising **Risk Analysis** and **Risk Evaluation**, as defined by **ISO 14971:2019 — Medical devices — Application of risk management to medical devices**. It is a systematic, iterative procedure used throughout the product lifecycle to identify hazards, estimate and evaluate associated risks, and implement controls to mitigate those risks to an acceptable level.

**Core Equation (Knuiman/KTU Board Standard):**

$$R = S \times P$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk
- $S$ = Severity of harm
- $P$ = Probability of occurrence of harm

**Conceptual Analogy — The Staircase Safety Standard:**
Imagine an engineer designing a staircase in a hospital. The "risk" of someone falling isn't just *how tall* the staircase is (severity) — it's also *how slippery* it gets when wet, and *how often* the staircase is used. Risk assessment is the engineering equivalent of asking: "For every hazard (a missing handrail, a slippery surface, a poorly-lit step), how bad could the injury be, and how likely is it?" The answer guides whether you install a handrail, add non-slip tape, or simply accept the residual risk.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Terminology (ISO 14971:2019 Vocabulary):**
> - **Hazard:** Potential source of harm.
> - **Hazardous Situation:** Circumstance in which a person is exposed to a hazard.
> - **Harm:** Physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property/environment.
> - **Severity:** Measure of the possible consequences of a hazard.
> - **Probability:** Measure of the likelihood of occurrence of harm.
> - **Risk Control:** Action taken to reduce risk to an acceptable level.
> - **Residual Risk:** Risk remaining *after* risk control measures have been implemented.

---

### 1.3 Manufacturing Supply Chain & Product Manufacturing

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Medical Device Manufacturing Supply Chain** is the end-to-end network of organizations, resources, processes, and information flows involved in the design, production, distribution, and post-market support of a medical device, governed by **ISO 13485:2016** (Quality Management Systems) and **21 CFR Part 820** (US FDA Quality System Regulation). Product manufacturing within this chain refers to the controlled, validated, and documented transformation of design outputs into finished devices suitable for clinical use.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery's Recipe Book:**
A hospital isn't a bakery, but the discipline is identical. If a bakery decides to expand from one shop to a thousand, the owner must:
1. Write a **"master recipe"** (Design History File),
2. Vet the **flour supplier** (Supplier Qualification),
3. Ensure the **ovens** are calibrated and *repeatable* (Process Validation),
4. Label every **loaf** so a complaint can be traced back to a specific batch (Traceability),
5. Keep the **store clean** to a standard (GMP — Good Manufacturing Practice).

A biomedical device manufacturer does the *same* — except the "loaves" are pacemakers, infusion pumps, and MRI coils, and the regulatory consequences of a flawed loaf are vastly more severe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Manufacturing Constants / Standard Metrics (KTU Board):**
> - **Cpk ≥ 1.33** = Industry standard minimum process capability index for medical device manufacturing.
> - **Six Sigma (6σ)** = **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — benchmark for high-reliability medical device production.
> - **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)** = **10⁻⁶** for terminally sterilized medical devices (ISO 11137, ISO 11135).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subsystem vs. Full-Device Testing — The Two-Tier V&V Architecture

**Why two tiers?** A monolithic, "test the whole thing at the end" strategy is statistically catastrophic for medical devices. A device with **n** subsystems, each with a 95% pass rate, has a joint probability of *all* passing of:

$$P_{\text{all pass}} = 0.95^n$$

For $n = 20$ subsystems, this is only **35.8%**. Without subsystem testing, you would discover 64% of integration failures far too late — at the full-device stage, where fixes are exponentially more expensive.

**The V-Model of Medical Device Verification (KTU Board Favorite):**

The V-Model maps each design phase to a corresponding testing phase. The left "leg" descends from User Needs down to Detailed Design; the right "leg" ascends from Subsystem Tests up to Clinical Validation.

**Step-by-step logic of the V-Model:**

1. **User Needs → Clinical Validation:** Confirm the device solves the *right* clinical problem.
2. **Design Inputs → Design Validation:** Confirm the complete device meets user needs and intended use.
3. **Design Outputs → Design Verification (Subsystem + Integration):** Confirm each output meets the input.
4. **Detailed Design → Unit/Subsystem Testing:** Confirm each individual module meets its specification.

**Subsystem Testing — What is actually done?**

- **Module-level software testing** (per IEC 62304): unit tests, integration tests, code coverage analysis.
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) testing:** Sensors, signal conditioning, ADC, microcontroller firmware.
- **EMC pre-compliance testing** at the printed circuit board (PCB) level.
- **Bench characterization:** Linearity, accuracy, precision, drift, hysteresis, response time of individual channels.

**Full-Device Testing — What is actually done?**

- **Integration testing** of hardware + software + mechanical + electrical subsystems.
- **Electrical safety testing** per **IEC 60601-1** (leakage current, dielectric strength, grounding).
- **Performance testing** per device-specific standards (e.g., IEC 60601-2-4 for defibrillators, IEC 60601-2-30 for ECG monitors).
- **Usability/Human Factors testing** per **IEC 62366-1**.
- **Environmental testing:** Temperature, humidity, altitude, vibration, shock (per IEC 60068 series).
- **Biocompatibility testing** per **ISO 10993** (for patient-contacting parts).
- **Simulated use** in animal models or cadavers (where ethically permissible).

---

### 2.2 Risk Assessment — ISO 14971:2019 Process Architecture

**The ISO 14971 Risk Management Process (Five Stages):**

1. **Risk Analysis** — Systematic identification of hazards, hazardous situations, and estimation of severity and probability.
2. **Risk Evaluation** — Comparing estimated risk against a defined risk acceptability policy to decide if risk reduction is required.
3. **Risk Control** — Implementing measures to reduce risk to acceptable levels. Standard priority order:
   - **Inherent safety by design** (best — eliminate the hazard),
   - **Protective measures** in the device or its manufacture (e.g., alarms, guards),
   - **Information for safety** (e.g., warnings, training, labeling — weakest control).
4. **Evaluation of Residual Risk** — Assessing whether the remaining risk is acceptable after controls.
5. **Risk Management Review** — Top-level review before commercial release, ensuring all residual risks are acceptable and the overall risk-benefit profile is positive.

**Risk Control Verification Equation (KTU Board):**

$$\text{Risk Reduction} = R_{\text{initial}} - R_{\text{residual}}$$

**FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) — A Specialization of Risk Analysis:**

The traditional FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN):

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where:
- $S$ = Severity (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1–10)
- $D$ = Detectability (1–10; **higher D = harder to detect**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** Modern FMEAs (per AIAG-VDA 2019) supplement the RPN with an **Action Priority (AP)** score (H, M, L) to overcome RPN's mathematical flaws (e.g., S=10,O=1,D=1 = 10 vs. S=5,O=5,D=5 = 125 — the second is *not* "worse" in any meaningful sense).

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Top-Down Deductive Method:**

FTA uses Boolean logic to combine the probability of basic events leading to a top event:

$$P(\text{Top Event}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P(E_i)) \quad \text{(OR-gate, independent events)}$$

$$P(\text{Top Event}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(E_i) \quad \text{(AND-gate, independent events)}$$

---

### 2.3 Manufacturing Supply Chain — The ISO 13485 Framework

**Core Elements (KTU 2024 Board Focus):**

- **Document Control (ISO 13485 §4.2.4):** Every drawing, spec, work instruction must be version-controlled and approved.
- **Purchasing Controls (§7.4):** Supplier qualification, monitoring, and re-evaluation. Critical suppliers (e.g., printed circuit board fabricators for an implantable) require **Supplier Quality Agreements**.
- **Production Controls (§7.5):** Work instructions, environmental conditions, in-process inspections, calibration of equipment.
- **Process Validation (§7.5.6):** Any process whose output *cannot be verified by subsequent monitoring* must be validated. Examples: **sterilization, welding, soldering, adhesive bonding, software deployment**.
- **Identification & Traceability (§7.5.8):** Every unit must be traceable to its raw materials, components, and personnel involved in manufacture — the basis for **Recall Management**.
- **Preservation of Product (§7.5.5):** Storage, packaging, and protection from environmental degradation.
- **Control of Monitoring & Measuring Equipment (§7.6):** Calibration of all test equipment, traceability to national/international standards (e.g., NIST).

**Design for Manufacturing (DFM) and Design for Assembly (DFA) Metrics:**

The **DFA Index** (Boothroyd-Dewhurst):

$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_{\min}}{t_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $N_{\min}$ = theoretical minimum number of parts
- $t_{\text{actual}}$ = actual assembly time in seconds

A DFA Index of **> 60%** is generally considered excellent; **< 40%** indicates significant over-partitioning.

**Design for Manufacturing (DFM) — Minimum Number of Parts Criterion:**

A part should be eliminated unless one or more of the following is satisfied:
1. The part moves relative to all other parts.
2. The part must be of a different material than an adjacent part.
3. The part must be separable from an adjacent part for assembly, service, or manufacturing reasons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Process validation follows the **IQ-OQ-PQ** paradigm:
> - **IQ (Installation Qualification):** "Was the equipment installed correctly?"
> - **OQ (Operational Qualification):** "Does the equipment operate as intended across its full range?"
> - **PQ (Performance Qualification):** "Does the process produce acceptable product under real production conditions?"

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| **Concept / Domain** | **Equation / Rule** | **Variables & Units** | **Notes / Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Risk (Basic) | $R = S \times P$ | $S$: Severity (1–5), $P$: Probability (1–5) | ISO 14971 simple model |
| FMEA Risk Priority | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | $S,O,D$: each 1–10 | Higher RPN = higher concern |
| Fault Tree (OR-gate) | $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$ | $P_i$: probability of basic event $i$ | Independent basic events only |
| Fault Tree (AND-gate) | $P = \prod P_i$ | $P_i$: probability of basic event $i$ | Independent basic events only |
| Joint Pass Probability | $P_{\text{all}} = \prod p_i$ | $p_i$: pass rate of subsystem $i$ | $n$ subsystems, each pass rate $p$ |
| Process Capability | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$: mean, $\sigma$: std. dev., USL/LSL: spec limits | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ typical medical target |
| DFA Index | $\text{DFA} = N_{\min} / t_{a} \times 100\%$ | $N_{\min}$: theoretical min parts, $t_{a}$: actual time (s) | Target > 60% |
| Defect Rate (Six Sigma) | $DPMO = 3.4$ defects per $10^{6}$ | Opportunities | Long-term benchmark |
| Sterility Assurance | $SAL = 10^{-6}$ | Probability of a viable microbe | Per ISO 11137 / 11135 |
| Risk Reduction | $\Delta R = R_{\text{initial}} - R_{\text{residual}}$ | Both dimensionless or both in same units | Must be $>$ threshold |
| Yield Loss | $Y = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | $Y_i$: yield of step $i$ | Multiplicative across $n$ steps |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Risk Reduction Effectiveness via Hierarchy of Controls

The KTU board frequently asks the student to demonstrate that **inherent safety by design** provides a *categorically greater* risk reduction than protective measures or information for safety. We derive this through expected-value analysis.

**Setup:** Consider a single hazardous situation with initial risk $R_0 = S \times P_0$. The manufacturer may apply one of three categories of control.

**Inherent Safety by Design (Category 1):** The hazard is physically eliminated. The probability of harm drops to (approximately) zero.

$$R_{\text{residual, 1}} = S \times P_1 \quad \text{where } P_1 \to 0$$

**Protective Measure (Category 2):** A guard, alarm, or interlock is added. Probability drops but does not vanish.

$$R_{\text{residual, 2}} = S \times P_2 \quad \text{where } 0 < P_2 \ll P_0$$

**Information for Safety (Category 3):** A label/warning is added. The *user* is now part of the safety chain; residual probability is still non-trivial.

$$R_{\text{residual, 3}} = S \times P_3 \quad \text{where } P_3 \approx 0.5\,P_0$$

Therefore, the **residual risk ordering** is:

$$R_{\text{residual, 1}} \ll R_{\text{residual, 2}} < R_{\text{residual, 3}}$$

This is the formal mathematical justification for ISO 14971's hierarchy of controls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical KTU Example:** Consider a mains-powered electrosurgical generator that poses a *diathermy burns* hazard.
> - **Inherent safety:** Use a Class A isolated output stage (galvanic isolation transformer) — hazard *eliminated*.
> - **Protective measure:** Add a neutral-electrode (return-pad) contact-quality monitor (CQM) — hazard *mitigated*.
> - **Information:** "Always use a return pad" in the IFU — hazard *warned*.
> ISO 14971 mandates exhausting Category 1 options before resorting to Category 3.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Subsystem vs. Full-Device Testing

The following table maps the **type of defect** to the **stage at which it is best caught** — a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

| **Defect Class** | **Example** | **Best Caught At** | **Why?** | **Cost Multiplier (Industry Avg.)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm error in DSP | Incorrect QRS detection threshold | Subsystem (Unit) test | Single module, deterministic | **1×** |
| Sensor-software interface mismatch | ADC range mis-mapped to mV scale | Subsystem (Integration) test | Two-module interaction | **5×** |
| Crosstalk between subsystems | Display flicker when defibrillator fires | Full-device EMC test | Whole-system behavior | **30×** |
| Use error under stress | Clinician fails to interpret alarm in noise | Full-device (HF/Validation) test | Requires real human in loop | **100×** |
| Manufacturing variability | Solder joint opens after thermal cycling | Manufacturing (OQ/PQ) | Process-specific, not design-specific | **500×+** (if found in field) |

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Why Process Validation Multiplies Yield Loss

For a multi-step manufacturing process with $n$ sequential steps, each with yield $Y_i$, the **cumulative yield** is derived as follows.

Let $N_0$ be the number of units entering step 1. After step 1, the number of good units is $N_1 = Y_1 \cdot N_0$.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= Y_1 \cdot N_0 \\
N_2 &= Y_2 \cdot N_1 = Y_1 Y_2 \cdot N_0 \\
N_3 &= Y_3 \cdot N_2 = Y_1 Y_2 Y_3 \cdot N_0 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
N_n &= N_0 \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, cumulative yield is:

$$Y_{\text{cum}} = \frac{N_n}{N_0} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A pacemaker manufacturing line has 5 sequential steps, each with a 99% yield ($Y_i = 0.99$).

$$Y_{\text{cum}} = (0.99)^5 = 0.95099 \approx 95.1\%$$

This means **~4.9% of pacemakers entering the line are scrapped** — at a bill-of-materials cost of perhaps **\$200** per unit, the manufacturer loses **\$9.80 per unit entering** to scrap alone. For 10,000 units/year, this is **\$98,000** in yield loss. Process validation directly targets the lowest-$Y_i$ step to maximize $Y_{\text{cum}}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — FTA for a Blood Pressure Monitor

**Top Event:** "Fails to alarm during hypertensive crisis."

**Boolean Expression (basic events A–D):**

$$\text{Top} = A \cdot (B + C + D)$$

Where:
- $A$ = Cuff pressure sensor failure
- $B$ = Microcontroller firmware mis-calculated MAP
- $C$ = Alarm buzzer failure
- $D$ = Display blanked

**Assume independent events with $P(A) = 10^{-3}$, $P(B) = P(C) = P(D) = 10^{-2}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(B + C + D) &= 1 - (1-P(B))(1-P(C))(1-P(D)) \\
&= 1 - (0.99)^3 \\
&= 1 - 0.970299 \\
&= 0.029701
\end{aligned}$$

Then:

$$P(\text{Top}) = P(A) \cdot P(B + C + D) = 10^{-3} \cdot 0.029701 = 2.9701 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is approximately **3 events per 100,000 device-hours** — a residual rate the manufacturer can compare to the risk acceptability policy defined in their **Risk Management Plan (RMP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Whenever a FTA question appears, students must (1) state the assumption of independence, (2) clearly identify the gate type (AND/OR), and (3) write the formula *before* substituting numbers. Skipping step (1) or (3) costs full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V-Model of Medical Device Design Control (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userNeeds["User Needs and Intended Use"]
    designInput["Design Inputs Specifications"]
    designOutput["Design Outputs Drawings and Code"]
    detailedDesign["Detailed Design Subsystem Schematics"]
    subsysTest["Subsystem Testing Unit and Integration"]
    integTest["Integration Testing Hardware plus Software"]
    designVerif["Design Verification Full Device Bench"]
    designValid["Design Validation Simulated Use and Usability"]
    clinValid["Clinical Validation Human Subjects and Post Market"]

    userNeeds --> designInput
    designInput --> designOutput
    designOutput --> detailedDesign
    detailedDesign --> subsysTest
    subsysTest --> designVerif
    designOutput --> designVerif
    designVerif --> designValid
    designInput --> designValid
    designValid --> clinValid
    userNeeds --> clinValid
    designVerif --> integTest
    integTest --> designVerif
```

**Reading the V-Model:**
- The **left arm** is the *specification cascade*: User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs → Detailed Design.
- The **right arm** is the *test cascade*: Subsystem Tests → Integration Tests → Design Verification → Design Validation → Clinical Validation.
- The **horizontal arrows** crossing the V indicate the *contract of trust*: each design level on the left is *demonstrated* by the corresponding test on the right.

---

### 4.2 ISO 14971:2019 Risk Management Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    processStart["Start Risk Management Process"]
    riskAnalysis["Risk Analysis Identify Hazards Estimate S and P"]
    riskEval["Risk Evaluation Compare to Acceptability Policy"]
    riskAcceptable{"Is Risk Acceptable"}
    riskControl["Implement Risk Control Measures Design then Protective then Information"]
    verifyControl["Verify Risk Control Effectiveness"]
    residualEval["Evaluate Residual Risk"]
    residualAcceptable{"Is Residual Risk Acceptable"}
    riskBenefit["Risk Benefit Analysis"]
    overallAccept{"Overall Risk Acceptable"]
    rmr["Risk Management Review and Production Release"]
    postMarket["Post Market Surveillance Update Loop"]

    processStart --> riskAnalysis
    riskAnalysis --> riskEval
    riskEval --> riskAcceptable
    riskAcceptable -- No --> riskControl
    riskAcceptable -- Yes --> residualEval
    riskControl --> verifyControl
    verifyControl --> residualEval
    residualEval --> residualAcceptable
    residualAcceptable -- No --> riskControl
    residualAcceptable -- Yes --> riskBenefit
    riskBenefit --> overallAccept
    overallAccept -- No --> riskControl
    overallAccept -- Yes --> rmr
    rmr --> postMarket
    postMarket -.-> riskAnalysis
```

**Key Decision Points (KTU Board Tested):**
1. **riskAcceptable:** If the *estimated* risk is below the acceptability threshold, no control is required.
2. **residualAcceptable:** If the *residual* risk is still above the threshold → **return to riskControl** (iterative).
3. **overallAccept:** If the *aggregate* residual risk of all hazards, weighted by clinical benefit, is acceptable → release; else, redesign.

---

### 4.3 Medical Device Supply Chain & Manufacturing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    designInput["Design Outputs DHF and DMR"]
    supplierQual["Supplier Qualification ISO 13485 Clause 7.4"]
    rawMat["Raw Materials and Components"]
    iq["IQ Installation Qualification"]
    oq["OQ Operational Qualification"]
    pq["PQ Performance Qualification"]
    inProcess["In Process Inspection"]
    finalInsp["Final Inspection and Release"]
    sterilization["Sterilization SAL 10 to the minus 6"]
    packaging["Primary and Secondary Packaging"]
    labeling["Labeling and UDI"]
    distribution["Distribution and Traceability"]
    postMarket["Post Market Surveillance Complaint Handling CAPA"]

    designInput --> supplierQual
    supplierQual --> rawMat
    rawMat --> iq
    iq --> oq
    oq --> pq
    pq --> inProcess
    inProcess --> sterilization
    sterilization --> packaging
    packaging --> labeling
    labeling --> finalInsp
    finalInsp --> distribution
    distribution --> postMarket
    postMarket -.-> designInput
```

**Reading the Supply Chain Diagram:**
- The **horizontal axis** is the *temporal flow* from design release to patient use.
- The **dashed feedback loop** from Post-Market Surveillance back to Design Inputs represents the **Closed-Loop Quality System** mandated by ISO 13485 §8.2.1 (Feedback).
- The **IQ → OQ → PQ sequence** is the canonical Process Validation protocol.

---

### 4.4 System Testing Workflow — Subsystem to Full Device

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    designSpec["Design Specification"]
    benchTest["Subsystem Bench Test Verification"]
    hilTest["HIL Hardware in the Loop Test"]
    emcPre["EMC Pre Compliance Test"]
    swUnit["Software Unit Test IEC 62304"]
    intgTest["Subsystem Integration Test"]
    fullAssembly["Full Device Assembly"]
    safetyTest["Electrical Safety IEC 60601 1"]
    perfTest["Performance Test Device Specific IEC 60601 2 X"]
    emcFull["Full EMC Compliance Test"]
    envTest["Environmental Test IEC 60068"]
    usabilityTest["Usability HF Test IEC 62366"]
    simUseTest["Simulated Use and Animal Study if applicable"]
    finalRel["Design History File Closure and Market Release"]

    designSpec --> benchTest
    designSpec --> hilTest
    designSpec --> emcPre
    designSpec --> swUnit
    benchTest --> intgTest
    hilTest --> intgTest
    emcPre --> intgTest
    swUnit --> intgTest
    intgTest --> fullAssembly
    fullAssembly --> safetyTest
    fullAssembly --> perfTest
    fullAssembly --> emcFull
    fullAssembly --> envTest
    fullAssembly --> usabilityTest
    usabilityTest --> simUseTest
    simUseTest --> finalRel
```

**Critical Insight:** Notice the **fan-in architecture**: all four parallel subsystem test streams converge at *Subsystem Integration Test* before the full-device tests run. This is precisely why a 14-mark KTU question may show a defect found late and ask, *"At which earlier stage should this have been caught?"*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Distinguish between Design Verification and Design Validation as applied to a medical device such as a digital glucometer. Give one example test for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Design Verification** confirms that the *design outputs* meet the *design inputs* — i.e., the device is *built right*. Example: bench-testing the glucometer's electrochemical strip reader against its specified accuracy of ±10% across a glucose range of 30–600 mg/dL (1 mark).
- **Design Validation** confirms that the device meets *user needs and intended use* — i.e., the *right* device is built. Example: usability test with 15 diabetic patients in a home setting per **IEC 62366-1** to confirm lay users can correctly self-test (1 mark).
- **Key difference:** Verification is technical/specification-driven; Validation is clinical/patient-driven (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**List the three categories of risk control measures as per ISO 14971:2019, and state the order of priority the manufacturer must follow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Inherent safety by design** — eliminate the hazard at the design stage (1 mark).
2. **Protective measures** — guards, alarms, interlocks in the device or its manufacturing process (1 mark).
3. **Information for safety** — labels, warnings, training materials, Instructions for Use (IFU) (1 mark).

The order is **design → protective → information**; the manufacturer must exhaust higher-priority options before resorting to lower ones.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a) Describe in detail the system testing procedure for a Class IIb medical electrical device (e.g., a patient monitor) as per the V-Model of design control. Clearly distinguish between subsystem testing and full-device testing, citing at least four distinct tests in each category and the relevant IEC/ISO standard. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**
- **V-Model overview:** The V-Model maps each specification level on the left arm to a corresponding test level on the right arm. For a patient monitor, the left arm contains User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs → Detailed Design. The right arm contains Subsystem Tests → Integration Tests → Design Verification → Design Validation → Clinical Validation. (1 mark)
- **Subsystem testing — module-level verification:** (3 marks)
  1. **Software unit testing per IEC 62304:** Each software module (e.g., SpO₂ algorithm, ECG QRS detector) is unit-tested for code coverage ≥ 80% for Class B software.
  2. **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) test:** Signal conditioning board is fed synthetic ECG/SpO₂ waveforms; ADC linearity and noise are measured.
  3. **EMC pre-compliance test on PCBs** per IEC 60601-1-2 at the board level.
  4. **Bench characterization** of NIBP cuff pressure sensor: linearity, hysteresis, drift over 24 hours.
- **Full-device testing — system-level integration and validation:** (3 marks)
  1. **Electrical safety per IEC 60601-1:** Earth leakage current < 500 µA (Type B), patient leakage current < 100 µA, dielectric strength 1500 V.
  2. **Performance testing per IEC 60601-2-30** (particular standard for ECG monitors): accuracy of heart-rate determination, alarm priority, alarm audibility.
  3. **Usability/Human Factors test per IEC 62366-1:** 15 trained and 15 untrained users perform representative tasks in a simulated clinical environment; use-errors are documented.
  4. **Environmental testing per IEC 60068-2:** Operating temperature +5 °C to +40 °C, storage −20 °C to +60 °C, humidity 10–90%, vibration, drop (1.2 m).

---

**(b) Explain the ISO 14971:2019 risk management process for a medical device. Using a numerical example, compute the residual risk after applying a risk control measure, and justify which category of control is most preferred. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**
- **ISO 14971 five-stage process:** (3 marks)
  1. **Risk Analysis** — identify hazards, estimate severity (S) and probability (P).
  2. **Risk Evaluation** — compare to the risk acceptability policy defined in the Risk Management Plan.
  3. **Risk Control** — apply the three-tier hierarchy: design → protective → information.
  4. **Evaluation of Residual Risk** — assess if the remaining risk is acceptable; if not, iterate.
  5. **Risk Management Review** — top-level review before commercial release, including risk-benefit analysis.
- **Numerical example:** A surgical laser has an initial risk of $R_0 = S \times P = 4 \times 4 = 16$ on a 1–5 scale. A protective interlock reduces probability from 4 to 2. (1 mark)
$$R_{\text{residual}} = S \times P_{\text{new}} = 4 \times 2 = 8$$
- **Risk reduction achieved:**
$$\Delta R = R_0 - R_{\text{residual}} = 16 - 8 = 8$$ (1 mark)
- **Hierarchy preference justification:** **Inherent safety by design is the most preferred** because it *eliminates* the hazard (P → 0), rather than merely reducing probability. The laser manufacturer should first attempt to design a Class 1 enclosure that physically prevents emission when the beam path is interrupted, *before* adding alarms or warnings. This follows ISO 14971's mandatory hierarchy. (2 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) Describe in detail the manufacturing supply chain for a sterile disposable medical device such as a blood-collection syringe. Identify at least five ISO 13485:2016 control elements, and explain why process validation (IQ-OQ-PQ) is mandatory for sterilization. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**
- **Supply chain flow (1 mark):** Design Release (DMR) → Supplier Qualification → Raw Material (medical-grade polymer, needle tubing) Receipt → Injection Molding (syringe barrel) → Needle Assembly (cleanroom ISO Class 7) → Siliconization → In-Process Inspection → Packaging → **Sterilization (EtO or gamma) →** Final Inspection → Labeling (with UDI) → Distribution → Post-Market Surveillance.
- **Five ISO 13485:2016 control elements (3 marks):**
  1. **§7.4 Purchasing Controls:** Supplier qualification, supplier quality agreements, incoming material inspection.
  2. **§7.5.1 Production Controls:** Documented work instructions, controlled environment (cleanroom).
  3. **§7.5.2 Cleanliness of Product:** Particulate and bioburden control prior to sterilization.
  4. **§7.5.6 Process Validation:** Validation of sterilization, injection molding, and packaging sealing processes.
  5. **§7.5.8 Identification & Traceability:** Each syringe is UDI-coded to enable lot-level recall.
- **Why IQ-OQ-PQ is mandatory for sterilization (3 marks):**
  - **IQ (Installation Qualification):** Confirms the EtO sterilizer is installed with correct utilities (gas supply, vacuum, temperature sensors) and that calibration is traceable.
  - **OQ (Operational Qualification):** Confirms the cycle parameters (EtO concentration 600–1200 mg/L, temperature 37–63 °C, humidity 40–80%, exposure time) achieve the required **SAL ≤ 10⁻⁶** as per **ISO 11135** in worst-case loads.
  - **PQ (Performance Qualification):** Confirms the process works *in routine production* on three consecutive validation runs with bioburden indicators.
  - **Why mandatory:** Sterilization output *cannot be fully verified* on every unit (a destructive sterility test is not feasible for every syringe). Therefore ISO 13485 §7.5.6 requires that the *process itself* be validated to consistently deliver the required SAL.

---

**(b) A 5-step syringe manufacturing line has individual step yields of 0.97, 0.99, 0.96, 0.98, and 0.99. Calculate the cumulative yield. If the per-unit bill-of-materials cost is ₹120, calculate the scrap cost per 1,000 units entering the line. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**
- **Cumulative yield formula (2 marks):**
$$Y_{\text{cum}} = \prod_{i=1}^{5} Y_i = Y_1 \cdot Y_2 \cdot Y_3 \cdot Y_4 \cdot Y_5$$
- **Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{\text{cum}} &= 0.97 \times 0.99 \times 0.96 \times 0.98 \times 0.99 \\
&= 0.97 \times 0.99 = 0.9603 \\
&\to 0.9603 \times 0.96 = 0.921888 \\
&\to 0.921888 \times 0.98 = 0.903450 \\
&\to 0.903450 \times 0.99 = 0.894416 \\
Y_{\text{cum}} &\approx 0.8944 \text{ or } 89.44\%
\end{aligned}$$
- **Scrap calculation (2 marks):**
  - Number of scrapped units per 1,000 = $1000 \times (1 - 0.8944) = 105.6 \approx 106$ units.
  - Scrap cost = $106 \times ₹120 = ₹12,720$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost on This Topic:**
> 1. **Risk = S × P confusion:** Students write $R = S + P$ instead of $R = S \times P$. The board strictly expects the *multiplicative* ISO 14971 model.
> 2. **Verification vs. Validation mix-up:** Writing "Validation is done on the bench" is a **3-mark penalty** in a 14-mark question. Validation requires *user/intended-use context*; bench testing is verification.
> 3. **FTA independence assumption:** Fault Tree formulas $P = \prod P_i$ (AND) and $P = 1 - \prod (1-P_i)$ (OR) are valid **only for independent events**. Not stating this assumption costs full credit.
> 4. **Process Validation skipped:** Students describe production but omit the **IQ-OQ-PQ** sequence — a 14-mark question's most common loss.
> 5. **Cumulative yield rounding:** Do not round intermediate steps; carry at least 4–5 significant figures to avoid a chain-multiplication error that loses 2 marks in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ✅ **Subsystem testing** performs **verification**; **Full-device testing** performs **validation + verification at system level**.
- ✅ The **V-Model** links each design phase to a corresponding test phase; horizontal arrows are the "trust contracts."
- ✅ **Verification** = "Are we building the device right?" → bench, lab, IEC standards.
- ✅ **Validation** = "Are we building the right device?" → user needs, intended use, IEC 62366-1, simulated use, clinical.
- ✅ **ISO 14971:2019** is the international standard for risk management; **ISO 13485:2016** is for the quality management system.
- ✅ **Risk = Severity × Probability** (basic model).
- ✅ **RPN = S × O × D** (FMEA — older model; modern AIAG-VDA uses Action Priority).
- ✅ **FTA OR-gate:** $P = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$; **AND-gate:** $P = \prod P_i$; *always* state the **independence assumption**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of risk control:** Inherent Design (best) → Protective Measures → Information for Safety (worst).
- ✅ **Cumulative manufacturing yield** $Y_{\text{cum}} = \prod Y_i$ — multiplicative loss across $n$ steps.
- ✅ **Process Validation = IQ → OQ → PQ** — mandatory for sterilization, welding, soldering, sealing.
- ✅ **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) = 10⁻⁶** is the universal target for terminally sterilized medical devices.
- ✅ **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the typical medical-device process capability target.
- ✅ **DFA Index** = $N_{\min}/t_{\text{actual}} \times 100\%$; target > 60%.
- ✅ Every medical device must be **UDI-labeled** and **fully traceable** to its raw materials, processes, and personnel (ISO 13485 §7.5.8).
- ✅ **Post-Market Surveillance** closes the loop: complaints → CAPA → design changes → updated Risk Management File.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation Qualification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Installation Qualification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Installation Qualification (IQ) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Installation Qualification (IQ)** is a documented verification process that confirms that a medical device, equipment, system, or facility is **installed correctly, completely, and in accordance with the manufacturer's specifications, design specifications, and applicable regulatory requirements (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR Part 820, GMP).** It is the first stage of the broader **Validation Life Cycle** and serves as a foundational gate to ensure that the physical and procedural environment is ready before any Operational Qualification (OQ) or Performance Qualification (PQ) activities commence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Installation Qualification belongs to the umbrella of **Design Control, Verification, and Validation (V&V)** activities that govern how a biomedical device is brought from a designed prototype into a controlled, reproducible, and regulatory-compliant manufacturing/installation environment.

In the context of the KTU 2024 B.Tech Biomedical Engineering syllabus (PEBMT752), Installation Qualification is treated as a structured, evidence-based engineering exercise. It produces objective, traceable proof that:

1. The **as-installed** configuration matches the **as-designed** configuration.
2. The installation environment (power, HVAC, cleanliness, utilities) matches the design intent.
3. All **supporting documentation, calibration certificates, accessories, and software** are present, correct, and version-controlled.
4. The system is **safe, identifiable, and ready** for the next stage of qualification.

Mathematically, the IQ pass/fail decision can be expressed as a binary compliance vector:

$$
IQ_{result} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}:\ C_i = 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $C_i$ represents the result of the $i^{th}$ installation check criterion (1 = pass, 0 = fail), and $n$ is the total number of IQ checks. In other words, **all** $n$ checks must individually pass for the overall IQ status to be **"Qualified."**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Think of buying a **new MRI machine** for a hospital. Before you switch it on and scan a patient, the hospital engineer and the vendor do a long, meticulous checklist:

- "Did the **3-ton superconducting magnet** arrive in all its wooden crates without damage?"
- "Is the **shielded room** (Faraday cage) ready with the right grounding?"
- "Are the **cooling water lines, helium refilling ports, and 3-phase power connections** correctly plumbed and wired?"
- "Is the **software version on the console** exactly v4.2.1 as specified in the purchase order?"
- "Are the **calibration phantoms, operator manuals, and service tool kits** physically present in the room?"

That entire pre-power-on inspection is **Installation Qualification.** It is the "**unboxing, assembling, wiring, plumbing, and verifying-the-bill-of-materials**" phase. You would never dream of scanning a patient without first confirming the magnet is installed correctly — that is exactly the engineering principle behind IQ.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Context:** IQ is mandatory for Class II, Class III, and certain Class I medical devices under **US FDA 21 CFR 820.70** and **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.5.6 — Validation of processes for production and service provision).** Hospitals performing in-house equipment qualification (e.g., JCI-accredited hospitals in Kerala) must also document IQ.

## 1.3 Where IQ Sits in the Validation Pyramid

IQ is **Step 1 of 3** in the qualification cascade. The hierarchy is:

| Stage | Full Form | What It Verifies | Stage Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IQ** | Installation Qualification | Equipment is installed *correctly* per spec | Pre-operational (static) |
| **OQ** | Operational Qualification | Equipment operates *correctly* across its ranges | Operational (dynamic) |
| **PQ** | Performance Qualification | Equipment performs *reliably* under real load | Process (real-world) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Validation:** *You cannot qualify how a machine **performs** (PQ) if you have not first qualified how it is **installed** (IQ) and how it **operates** (OQ).*

## 1.4 Standard Reference Framework

The IQ process for biomedical devices is governed by a stack of standards:

- **ISO 13485:2016** — Section 7.5.6 (Validation)
- **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** — Quality System Regulation, especially §820.70 (Manufacturing Material Control) and §820.75 (Process Validation)
- **GAMP 5 (ISPE)** — A risk-based approach to compliant GxP computerized systems
- **IEC 60601-1** — Medical electrical equipment general safety (influences IQ environment)
- **WHO Technical Report Series, No. 986, Annex 4** — GMP for sterile products
- **ISPE Baseline Guides** — For facility and equipment IQ

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Indicator for IQ:** A typical KPI is the **First-Pass IQ Yield (FP-IQY)**, defined as the percentage of IQ protocols that pass on the first attempt without deviations:
> 
> $$ FP\text{-}IQY = \frac{N_{pass,\,1st\,attempt}}{N_{total}} \times 100\% $$
> 
> A world-class biomedical manufacturing facility targets **FP-IQY ≥ 95%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Installation Qualification

A complete IQ protocol is built on six engineering pillars. Each pillar generates a specific set of documented evidence:

### Pillar 1 — Equipment & Component Verification
- Verify the **make, model, serial number, firmware version, and software build** of the as-installed device against the approved Bill of Materials (BOM) and Purchase Order (PO).
- Cross-check **accessories, spares, consumables, and calibration tools**.
- Confirm **CE / FDA / BIS / CDSCO** regulatory markings on the device.

### Pillar 2 — Utilities & Environmental Verification
- Measure and record:
  - **Mains voltage**, frequency, phase balance, earthing resistance
  - **Compressed air / medical gas** pressure, purity, dew point
  - **Cooling water** flow, temperature, conductivity
  - **HVAC**: temperature $\left(^{\circ}C\right)$, relative humidity $\left(\%\right)$, differential pressure $\left(Pa\right)$, particulate count (ISO 14644 cleanroom class)
  - **Ambient light, vibration, and EMI** levels for sensitive equipment (e.g., ECG, EEG, electron microscopes)
- The standard earthing resistance requirement per IS 3043 and IEC 60364 is **$\leq 1\,\Omega$** for medical IT systems (and **$\leq 0.5\,\Omega$** for some high-leakage-current sensitive installations).

### Pillar 3 — Mechanical & Structural Verification
- Verify **leveling, anchoring, floor-loading** compliance.
- Confirm **clearances** for ventilation, maintenance access, and heat dissipation.
- For imaging systems (CT, MRI, Linear Accelerator), verify **gantry rotation, couch travel, and laser alignment geometry**.

### Pillar 4 — Software, Network & Cybersecurity Verification
- Confirm **OS version, application build number, antivirus signatures, patch level**.
- Verify **network IP addressing, firewall rules, DICOM node configuration** (for imaging devices).
- For connected medical devices, perform a **cybersecurity IQ**: ensure default passwords are changed, ports are closed, and the device is on the correct VLAN.
- Check that **audit trails** are enabled and functional.

### Pillar 5 — Documentation & Procedural Verification
- Verify presence of:
  - User manual, service manual, installation manual
  - Schematic diagrams (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic)
  - Material certificates (e.g., SS 316L for bioprocess piping)
  - Calibration certificates of any factory-fitted sensors
  - **IQ protocol** itself, with revision number, approver signatures

### Pillar 6 — Safety & Regulatory Verification
- Verify **earth-leakage current, enclosure leakage, patient leakage current** (per IEC 60601-1).
- Confirm **fuses, MCBs, residual-current devices (RCDs)** are correctly rated.
- Verify **emergency stop circuits, interlocks, and alarms** function.
- Confirm **biocompatibility statements, sterilization validation** for contact-medical devices.

## 2.2 IQ Document Hierarchy (V-Model Mapping)

The IQ document set follows a **V-Model** structure that links design intent to installation evidence:

$$
\text{User Requirements (URS)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Functional Spec (FS)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Design Spec (DS)} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{IQ Protocol}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{OQ Protocol} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{PQ Protocol}
$$

| Document | Origin | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **URS** (User Requirement Specification) | Hospital / End-user | "What the device must do" |
| **FS** (Functional Specification) | Manufacturer | "How the device does it functionally" |
| **DS** (Design Specification) | Manufacturer | "Exact engineering parameters" |
| **IQ Protocol** | Manufacturer + End-user | "Proves installed state matches DS" |
| **OQ Protocol** | Manufacturer + End-user | "Proves operation across ranges" |
| **PQ Protocol** | End-user | "Proves real-world performance" |

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **high-yield formulas, threshold values, and standards** a KTU student must memorize for the ESE (End-Semester Examination).

| # | Quantity / Concept | Symbol / Equation | Typical Value or Limit | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IQ Pass Condition | $IQ = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ | All $C_i = 1$ | Custom protocol |
| 2 | First-Pass IQ Yield | $FP\text{-}IQY = \dfrac{N_{pass,1st}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $\geq 95\%$ | Industry KPI |
| 3 | Earthing Resistance | $R_E$ | $\leq 1\,\Omega$ (Medical IT) | IS 3043 / IEC 60364 |
| 4 | Enclosure Leakage Current | $I_{enc}$ | $\leq 100\,\mu A$ (Class I, NC) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 5 | Patient Leakage Current | $I_{pat}$ | $\leq 10\,\mu A$ (Type CF, NC) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 6 | Earth Leakage Current | $I_{earth}$ | $\leq 5\,\text{mA}$ (Class I) | IEC 60601-1 |
| 7 | Cleanroom Particulate Limit (ISO 7) | $N \le 352{,}000\,m^{-3}$ ($\geq 0.5\,\mu m$) | Per class | ISO 14644-1 |
| 8 | Temperature Stability | $\Delta T \le \pm 1^{\circ}C$ | For most equipment | Manufacturer DS |
| 9 | Relative Humidity | $RH$ | $30\%-65\%$ | Manufacturer DS |
| 10 | Mains Voltage Tolerance | $V_{actual}/V_{nominal}$ | $\pm 10\%$ | Most DS |
| 11 | Helium Boil-Off (MRI) | $dL/dt$ | $< 0.05\,L/h$ | Vendor DS |
| 12 | Power Quality (THD) | $THD_V \le 5\%$ | For sensitive electronics | IEEE 519 |
| 13 | Differential Pressure (Cleanroom) | $\Delta P$ | $10-15\,Pa$ | WHO/ISO 14644 |
| 14 | Calibration Uncertainty | $u_c \le \dfrac{1}{4} \times \text{Tolerance}$ | 4:1 ratio | JCGM 100:2008 (GUM) |
| 15 | Voltage Drop | $\Delta V = I \cdot R \cdot L$ | $\le 4\%$ from source | NEC / IS 732 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These thresholds are not arbitrary. They are derived from physics: e.g., patient leakage $\leq 10\,\mu A$ comes from the **let-go threshold** and **microshock electrocution** risk in cardiac-catheterized patients. IEC 60601-1 sets a 10x safety margin.

## 2.4 Risk-Based IQ (GAMP 5 Categorization)

Modern IQ follows a **risk-based** approach per **GAMP 5 (ISPE)**. The risk of an IQ failure causing patient harm is:

$$
R_{IQ} = P_{failure} \times S_{severity} \times D_{detectability}
$$

where:
- $P_{failure}$ = probability of an installation error
- $S_{severity}$ = clinical consequence (harm to patient/operator)
- $D_{detectability}$ = likelihood of escaping detection before use

**GAMP 5 Categories:**

| Category | Software Type | IQ Depth Required |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Infrastructure software (OS) | Basic |
| **3** | Non-configured COTS | Standard |
| **4** | Configured COTS | Detailed |
| **5** | Custom / Bespoke | Comprehensive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Patient-Connected Infusion Pump** (high $S$) requires **GAMP 5 Category 5** IQ depth. A **desk-top label printer** in a stockroom needs only **Category 1-3** IQ.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

IQ is not a paperwork ritual — it has measurable engineering value:

1. **Patient Safety:** Prevents catastrophic errors (e.g., reversed neutral-earth in a dialysis machine).
2. **Cost Avoidance:** Uninstalled-but-running equipment averages **30% lower MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures), per IEEE industry studies.
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Failure to perform IQ is a **top-3 FDA 483 observation** in medical device audits.
4. **Insurance & Liability:** IQ records are legal evidence in product liability cases.
5. **Hospital Accreditation:** JCI, NABH, NABL audits all inspect IQ records.
6. **Cybersecurity:** Post-2017, FDA mandates **SBOM (Software Bill of Materials)** as part of IQ for connected devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 The IQ Workflow — End-to-End Logical Chain

The IQ execution is a **12-step gated workflow**. Each gate is a written approval.

$$
\boxed{Step\,1} \to \boxed{Step\,2} \to \cdots \to \boxed{Step\,12} \to \boxed{IQ\,Approved}
$$

**Step-by-step breakdown with engineering logic:**

1. **IQ Plan Generation:** Define scope, responsibilities, schedule, deliverables.
2. **URS Review:** Confirm URS is approved and baselined (revision-controlled).
3. **Pre-Installation Site Readiness Check:** Civil, electrical, HVAC, plumbing readiness.
4. **Equipment Receipt & Storage Verification:** Damage check, storage condition (e.g., $T = 15-25^{\circ}C$, $RH \le 80\%$).
5. **Unpacking & Component Inventory:** Cross-reference against BOM.
6. **Mechanical Installation:** Anchoring, leveling, alignment, clearance.
7. **Utility Connection:** Power, water, gas, drainage, network.
8. **Software Loading & Configuration:** OS, application, drivers, security patches.
9. **Functional Check (Pre-OQ sanity):** Power-on self-test, basic start-up.
10. **Documentation Compilation:** All certificates, manuals, drawings, calibration data.
11. **Deviation Handling & Closure:** Document, investigate, CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).
12. **IQ Report & Sign-off:** Final approval by QA, Engineering, and User.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Quantifying IQ Effectiveness

### Example Problem (KTU-style, 14-mark analytical)

> *A hospital is qualifying a new **Defibrillator (Class IIb, Type CF applied part)**. During IQ, 18 critical checks ($n=18$) are to be performed. After execution, 16 checks pass and 2 fail (one earthing resistance is $1.4\,\Omega$ — limit is $1.0\,\Omega$; one patient leakage current measures $14\,\mu A$ — limit is $10\,\mu A$). Determine the IQ status, the formal first-pass yield, and the recommended action.*

#### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Encode each check as a binary value.**

$$
C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_{18} \in \{0, 1\}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the IQ pass rule.**

The IQ is qualified only if all $C_i = 1$. We are given that **two** checks failed:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{18} C_i = 18 - 2 = 16
$$

$$
IQ_{result} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{18} C_i = 0 \quad \text{(because at least one } C_i = 0\text{)}
$$

Therefore, **IQ is NOT approved.**

**Step 3: Compute the First-Pass IQ Yield (FP-IQY).**

$$
FP\text{-}IQY = \frac{N_{pass,1st}}{N_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{16}{18} \times 100 = 88.89\%
$$

**Step 4: Compare to the world-class KPI.**

Since $88.89\% < 95\%$, this is **below the world-class target** but above zero — meaning the equipment can be re-qualified after corrective action.

**Step 5: Root-Cause Each Failure and Propose CAPA.**

| # | Failed Check | Measured | Limit | Root Cause Hypothesis | CAPA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Earthing Resistance $R_E$ | $1.4\,\Omega$ | $\leq 1.0\,\Omega$ | Loose earth pit electrode; soil resistivity too high | Re-drive earth rod; add chemical earthing enhancement; re-test |
| 2 | Patient Leakage Current $I_{pat}$ | $14\,\mu A$ | $\leq 10\,\mu A$ | Y-capacitor degradation; PCB solder defect | Replace internal EMC filter; verify isolation transformer; re-test |

**Step 6: Determine the by-how-much margin.**

The earthing resistance exceeds its limit by:

$$
\Delta R_E = 1.4 - 1.0 = 0.4\,\Omega
$$

The percentage exceedance is:

$$
\%\text{ exceedance} = \frac{0.4}{1.0} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

The patient leakage current exceeds its limit by:

$$
\Delta I_{pat} = 14 - 10 = 4\,\mu A
$$

$$
\%\text{ exceedance} = \frac{4}{10} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**Step 7: Severity Classification per IEC 60601-1.**

Both failures are **Class II safety violations** (above the absolute safety threshold). The risk priority is:

$$
R_{IQ} = P_{failure} \times S_{severity} \times D_{detectability}
$$

For a Type-CF defibrillator (direct cardiac contact risk):

$$
S_{severity} = 5 \quad \text{(catastrophic — ventricular fibrillation risk)}
$$

$$
R_{IQ} = 0.4 \times 5 \times 2 = 4.0 \;\;(\text{high})
$$

**Step 8: Formal Conclusion.**

> The IQ **fails** with $FP\text{-}IQY = 88.89\%$. The device **must NOT be released to clinical service** until both failed checks are rectified, re-tested, and a **supplementary IQ report** is issued. Both the Quality Assurance Manager and the Biomedical Engineering Head must sign-off.

---

### 3.3 Derived Relationship — IQ, OQ, PQ Compliance Probability

Assume the qualification stages are statistically independent (a reasonable engineering approximation when protocols are distinct and well-controlled). Let $p_I, p_O, p_P$ be the probability that each stage passes on the first attempt. The **overall first-pass validation yield** is:

$$
P_{validation} = p_I \cdot p_O \cdot p_P
$$

For a typical Class II biomedical device with industry-realistic first-pass rates:

$$
p_I = 0.95,\quad p_O = 0.92,\quad p_P = 0.97
$$

$$
P_{validation} = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97 = 0.8477 = 84.77\%
$$

**Engineering insight:** The above equation explains why **IQ, being the *first* gate, has the largest leverage on the overall validation yield.** A 1% drop in $p_I$ drags $P_{validation}$ down by an equivalent 1% (multiplicatively), whereas the same 1% drop in $p_P$ has a similar multiplicative effect. The bottleneck is whichever stage has the lowest $p$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Takeaway for KTU Students:** When designing a *new* medical device, invest disproportionate engineering effort into the **IQ protocol's completeness and clarity** — because errors there propagate through the entire validation chain.

---

### 3.4 IQ Cost-of-Quality Model

The total cost of IQ can be modeled as:

$$
C_{IQ,total} = C_{planning} + C_{execution} + C_{deviation} + C_{rework}
$$

where:

$$
C_{rework} = C_{labor} \cdot t_{rework} \cdot N_{failures}
$$

**Numerical example:**

Suppose:

- $C_{planning} = \text{₹}50{,}000$
- $C_{execution} = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$
- $C_{deviation} = \text{₹}20{,}000$ per failed check
- $C_{labor} = \text{₹}2000$/hour, $t_{rework} = 8$ hours, $N_{failures} = 2$

Then:

$$
C_{IQ,total} = 50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 20{,}000 \times 2 + 2000 \times 8 \times 2
$$

$$
C_{IQ,total} = 50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 32{,}000
$$

$$
C_{IQ,total} = \text{₹}2{,}72{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical insight:** Approximately **70-80% of the IQ cost** is in **planning and execution**, not rework. Investing in **front-end protocol clarity** dramatically reduces total IQ cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IQ Position in the Validation V-Model

The following Mermaid block illustrates the V-Model architecture of medical device qualification, with Installation Qualification (IQ) explicitly placed as the **left-arm-of-the-V-to-bottom transition** step.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    URS["URS - User Requirements Specification"]
    FS["FS - Functional Specification"]
    DS["DS - Design Specification"]
    IQ["IQ - Installation Qualification"]
    OQ["OQ - Operational Qualification"]
    PQ["PQ - Performance Qualification"]
    ROUT["Routine Production / Clinical Use"]

    URS --> FS --> DS --> IQ --> OQ --> PQ --> ROUT
    ROUT -.feedback.-> URS
    DS -.verification trace.-> IQ
    FS -.verification trace.-> OQ
    URS -.validation trace.-> PQ
```

**Interpretation:** Reading top-down, the V-Model shows progressive *derivation* of specifications. The dashed arrows show the *traceability* — every IQ test must trace back to a Design Specification clause.

## 4.2 IQ Process Flowchart (12-Step Gated Workflow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Start: IQ Plan Generated"]
    A2["Step 1: URS Review and Baseline"]
    A3["Step 2: Site Readiness Verification"]
    A4["Step 3: Equipment Receipt Inspection"]
    A5["Step 4: Component Inventory vs BOM"]
    A6["Step 5: Mechanical Installation"]
    A7["Step 6: Utility Connections"]
    A8["Step 7: Software and Network Config"]
    A9["Step 8: Power-On Sanity Check"]
    A10["Step 9: Compile All Documents"]
    A11["Step 10: Deviation Handling CAPA"]
    A12["Step 11: Final IQ Report Sign Off"]
    A13["Step 12: IQ Approved - Release to OQ"]
    AF["Action: Stop - Equipment NOT Released"]
    A14["Hold: Re-execute Failed Checks"]

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11 --> A12 --> A13
    A11 -- "Deviation Found" --> A14
    A14 -- "Re-test Pass" --> A12
    A11 -- "Critical Failure" --> AF
    A12 -- "All Checks Pass" --> A13
```

## 4.3 IQ Document Hierarchy & Traceability Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_DOCS["INPUT DOCUMENTS"]
        URS["URS"]
        FS["FS"]
        DS["DS"]
        BOM["Bill of Materials"]
        MSDS["Material Safety Data Sheets"]
        CCAL["Calibration Certificates"]
    end

    subgraph IQ_CORE["IQ PROTOCOL CORE"]
        IQP["IQ Protocol Document"]
        IQC["IQ Checklist n checks"]
        IQR["IQ Report with Signatures"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_EVIDENCE["OUTPUT EVIDENCE"]
        PHOTOS["Installation Photos"]
        SIGN["Signed Acceptance Form"]
        LOG["Equipment Master Log Entry"]
    end

    URS --> IQP
    FS --> IQP
    DS --> IQP
    BOM --> IQC
    MSDS --> IQC
    CCAL --> IQC
    IQP --> IQR
    IQC --> IQR
    IQR --> PHOTOS
    IQR --> SIGN
    IQR --> LOG
```

## 4.4 IQ Failure Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Each IQ Check Executed"]
    S1{"Does Measurement meet Acceptance Criterion?"}
    S2["Mark Check as PASS C equals 1"]
    S3["Mark Check as FAIL C equals 0"]
    S4["Record Deviation in IQ Report"]
    S5{"Is Failure Critical Safety or Major Regulatory?"}
    S6["Stop - Issue Non-Conformance Report"]
    S7["Initiate CAPA Corrective Action"]
    S8["Re-execute Check after Fix"]
    S9{"All n Checks Pass?"}
    S10["IQ APPROVED - Release to OQ"]
    S11["IQ NOT APPROVED - Equipment on Hold"]

    S0 --> S1
    S1 -- YES --> S2
    S1 -- NO --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 -- YES Critical --> S6
    S5 -- NO Minor --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S1
    S2 --> S9
    S9 -- YES --> S10
    S9 -- NO --> S11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw an IQ flowchart, the four essential elements to include are: (1) **Input documents**, (2) **Sequential check execution**, (3) **Decision branch (pass/fail)**, and (4) **Final sign-off/release gate.** The above diagrams are valid templates.
