<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBMT752]`

**Q: Define Installation Qualification (IQ) as applied to medical device design. List any four IQ verification activities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Installation Qualification is the documented verification process which confirms that a medical device, its accessories, utilities, and supporting infrastructure are **installed correctly, completely, and in accordance with the manufacturer's design specifications, the purchase order, and applicable regulatory requirements (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR Part 820).** IQ is the first phase of the validation cascade, preceding Operational Qualification (OQ) and Performance Qualification (PQ).

**Four IQ verification activities (1.5 marks, 0.375 each):**

1. **Equipment Identification:** Make, model, serial number, firmware/software version verified against the Bill of Materials and Purchase Order.
2. **Utility Verification:** Mains voltage, frequency, earthing resistance, compressed-air pressure, cooling-water flow measured and recorded.
3. **Mechanical Installation Check:** Leveling, anchoring, clearances, and (for imaging devices) gantry/couch alignment verified.
4. **Documentation Verification:** User manual, service manual, calibration certificates, schematic diagrams, and IQ protocol itself confirmed present and revision-controlled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award 1.5 marks for a clean, regulation-cited definition. Do not paraphrase loosely — anchor your answer to **ISO 13485 / 21 CFR 820**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBMT752]`

**Q: Differentiate between IQ, OQ, and PQ with one distinguishing focus for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Stage | Stage Type | Distinguishing Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **IQ (Installation Qualification)** | Pre-operational / Static | Verifies the equipment is **installed correctly** per design specs — confirms "right equipment, right place, right connections." |
| **OQ (Operational Qualification)** | Operational / Dynamic | Verifies the equipment **operates correctly** across its full operational range — confirms "all controls, alarms, set-points work as specified." |
| **PQ (Performance Qualification)** | Process / Real-world | Verifies the equipment **performs reliably** under actual production/clinical load — confirms "consistently produces acceptable output in routine use." |

**One-line summary (1 mark):** *IQ asks: "Is it installed right?" OQ asks: "Does it work right?" PQ asks: "Does it keep working right under real use?"*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Part B questions carry 14 marks and **must** offer an internal choice. The two independent options below are calibrated to that format. Each long answer is split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBMT752, Module 3]`

**Q: A hospital is performing Installation Qualification of a newly installed hemodialysis machine (Class IIb, Type BF applied part).**

**(a)** Prepare a **comprehensive IQ checklist** with at least 12 distinct verification items grouped into logical categories. (7 marks)

**(b)** During execution, **3 out of 12** checks failed: (i) earth leakage current measured $6.8\,\text{mA}$ against a limit of $5.0\,\text{mA}$, (ii) dialysate temperature set-point drifts by $1.8^{\circ}C$ over 30 minutes against an acceptance criterion of $\leq 1.0^{\circ}C$, and (iii) the operator manual version is **v3.0** instead of the contracted **v3.2**. Determine the IQ status, calculate the first-pass IQ yield, and outline a step-by-step CAPA plan. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comprehensive IQ Checklist (7 marks — 0.5 to 0.75 mark per item, with 1 mark for logical grouping)**

**Category 1: Equipment Identification (1 mark)**
1. Make, model, serial number of the dialysis machine verified against PO.
2. Software/firmware version (e.g., HD-console v6.4.2) confirmed.
3. Accessories present: blood tubing set, dialyzer holders, BP cuff, temperature probe, conductivity cell.
4. Regulatory markings (CE / FDA / CDSCO) present on nameplate.

**Category 2: Utilities (1.5 marks)**
5. Mains voltage: $230\,\text{V} \pm 10\%$, $50\,\text{Hz} \pm 1\,\text{Hz}$ measured at socket.
6. Earthing resistance: $R_E \leq 1.0\,\Omega$ measured at equipment ground stud.
7. Water inlet pressure: $1.5-6.0\,\text{bar}$, RO water conductivity $\leq 5\,\mu S/cm$.
8. Drain connection: free fall, no back-pressure, air-break present.

**Category 3: Environmental (1 mark)**
9. Room temperature: $20-25^{\circ}C$; relative humidity: $30-65\%$.
10. Floor load: machine dry weight $90\,\text{kg}$ + water $30\,\text{kg}$ = $120\,\text{kg}$ on floor of rated capacity.

**Category 4: Mechanical (1 mark)**
11. Machine levelled (bubble level centered $\pm 0.5^{\circ}$); wheels locked.
12. Clearance: $\geq 0.5\,\text{m}$ on rear and sides for ventilation.

**Category 5: Safety (1 mark)**
13. Enclosure leakage $\leq 100\,\mu A$ (NC, Class I) measured per IEC 60601-1.
14. Patient leakage current $\leq 100\,\mu A$ (Type BF, NC) measured.
15. Earth leakage $\leq 5\,\text{mA}$ measured; fuse ratings match DS.

**Category 6: Documentation (1 mark)**
16. User manual, service manual, water-treatment manual, schematics, calibration certificates all present and revision-controlled.
17. **Operator manual version verified as v3.2** (not v3.0) — *(this is the very check that fails in part b).*

**Category 7: Functional Sanity (0.5 mark)**
18. Power-on self-test (POST) completes without error; alarms (air-in-line, blood-leak, conductivity, temperature) trigger in self-test mode.

**(b) Status, Yield, and CAPA (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Encode each check (1 mark):**

$$
n = 12 \text{ (critical sub-checks considered)}, \quad \sum C_i = 12 - 3 = 9
$$

**Step 2 — IQ status (1 mark):**

$$
IQ_{result} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{12} C_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{IQ NOT Approved}
$$

**Step 3 — First-Pass IQ Yield (1 mark):**

$$
FP\text{-}IQY = \frac{9}{12} \times 100 = 75.00\%
$$

This is **well below** the world-class benchmark of $\geq 95\%$, indicating a serious qualification issue.

**Step 4 — Quantitative analysis of each failure (2 marks):**

| Check | Measured | Limit | Absolute Exceedance | % Exceedance | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Earth leakage current | $6.8\,\text{mA}$ | $\leq 5.0\,\text{mA}$ | $1.8\,\text{mA}$ | $36\%$ | **Critical** (Class I safety) |
| Dialysate temp drift | $1.8^{\circ}C$ | $\leq 1.0^{\circ}C$ | $0.8^{\circ}C$ | $80\%$ | **Critical** (patient hyperthermia risk) |
| Operator manual version | v3.0 | v3.2 | (version mismatch) | N/A | **Major** (documentation) |

**Step 5 — Step-by-step CAPA (2 marks):**

1. **Issue a Non-Conformance Report (NCR)** for each of the three deviations and tag the equipment with a red "**IQ Hold**" label.
2. **Earth leakage ($6.8\,\text{mA}$):** Engage the manufacturer's service engineer. Inspect Y-capacitors and line-filter; tighten earth stud; replace if degradation found. Re-test with a medical-grade safety analyzer (e.g., Fluke ESA612).
3. **Dialysate temperature drift:** Calibrate the temperature sensor (PT-100 / RTD); check the heating element PID controller; verify mixing chamber thermistor. Perform a 4-hour soak test at $37.0^{\circ}C$ set-point; log temperature every 5 min.
4. **Operator manual version:** Procure v3.2 from the vendor; replace v3.0 in the equipment file; record the version-update in the Equipment Master Log.
5. **Re-execute** the three failed IQ checks; document results in a **Supplementary IQ Report.**
6. **Sign-off:** QA Head, Biomedical Engineering Head, and Hospital Safety Officer must countersign the supplementary report before the equipment is released to OQ.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students routinely lose 2-3 marks by (i) failing to **quantify** the exceedance (always show $\Delta$ and $\%$), (ii) omitting the **risk-based severity classification**, and (iii) forgetting to call out the **regulatory standard** (IEC 60601-1) explicitly. Avoid these traps.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[Alternate Choice, KTU 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**Q: As a Biomedical Engineer involved in the design transfer of a new patient monitor (multi-parameter: ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, IBP, Temperature) to manufacturing:**

**(a)** Explain the **purpose, scope, and key deliverables of Installation Qualification** in this context, citing the relevant regulatory standards. (7 marks)

**(b)** Develop a **risk-based IQ test plan** using the GAMP 5 categorization approach. For each category, list at least 2 specific IQ checks. Also discuss what actions must be taken if an IQ deviation is classified as **"Critical"** versus **"Minor."** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Purpose, Scope, and Deliverables of IQ (7 marks)**

**Purpose (2 marks):** The purpose of IQ in this multi-parameter patient monitor design transfer is to **objectively verify, document, and certify** that the as-manufactured devices are received, installed, configured, and ready for operational qualification in a manner that:

- Conforms to the **Design History File (DHF)** per **21 CFR 820.30**.
- Meets the **User Requirements Specification (URS)** agreed with the receiving hospital.
- Satisfies the **process validation** clause of **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.5.6)**.
- Aligns with **GAMP 5** for the software subsystem.
- Complies with **IEC 60601-1** (general safety), **IEC 60601-2-30** (NIBP), **IEC 60601-2-49** (multi-parameter), and **ISO 14971** (risk management).

**Scope (2.5 marks):**

The scope of IQ for this monitor includes the following elements:
1. **Hardware Subsystem:** Main unit, display module, battery, power supply, ECG/NIBP/SpO₂ modules, cables, and transducers.
2. **Software Subsystem:** Embedded firmware, GUI application, alarm management module, network stack, and cybersecurity configuration.
3. **Utilities:** Mains power, UPS/battery backup, Ethernet network, and HL7/DICOM interface gateway.
4. **Environment:** ICU/step-down unit installation site, including temperature, humidity, EMI, and ergonomic mounting (bed-rail, wall-mount, roll-stand).
5. **Documentation:** URS, FS, DS, BOM, user manual, service manual, calibration certificates, and software build manifest.

**Key Deliverables (2.5 marks):**

1. **Approved IQ Protocol** — with revision number, author, reviewer, and approver signatures.
2. **Executed IQ Checklist** — all checks with measured values, pass/fail status, and instrument IDs used.
3. **Supplementary Deviation Reports** — for any failures, with root cause, CAPA, and re-test results.
4. **IQ Summary Report** — overall pass/fail statement, statistical first-pass yield, list of approved deviations, and final sign-off page.
5. **Equipment Master Log Entry** — registration of the monitor with its unique Equipment ID, location, and custodian.
6. **Photographic Evidence** — installed configuration photos, nameplate close-up, and signature of install engineer.

**(b) Risk-Based IQ Test Plan & Deviation Handling (7 marks)**

**GAMP 5 Categorization (2 marks):**

| Subsystem | GAMP Category | IQ Depth |
|---|---|---|
| Linux/RTOS on the patient monitor | **Category 1** (Infrastructure OS) | Basic — verify version, license, patch level |
| Commercial alarm engine (COTS, not modified) | **Category 3** (Non-configured COTS) | Standard — install per vendor manual, verify version |
| Vendor's HL7/DICOM gateway (configured for hospital) | **Category 4** (Configured COTS) | Detailed — verify config, network settings, message round-trip |
| Custom alarm-management firmware (in-house) | **Category 5** (Custom / Bespoke) | Comprehensive — full functional IQ + code-build verification |

**Sample IQ Checks per Category (2.5 marks):**

**Category 1 (OS):**
- (i) OS build matches DS, e.g., "MedOS v2.4.7 build 1891."
- (ii) Read-only filesystem enforced; root password rotated from default.

**Category 3 (Alarm engine):**
- (iii) Alarm engine v8.1 installed; license file present.
- (iv) Audible alarm SPL measured at $1\,\text{m}$: $50-80\,\text{dB(A)}$ adjustable.

**Category 4 (HL7/DICOM gateway):**
- (v) Hospital VLAN IP, subnet mask, gateway, DNS configured and `ping` successful to ADT server.
- (vi) HL7 ADT message round-trip test passes; patient demographics appear on monitor within $5$ seconds.

**Category 5 (Custom firmware):**
- (vii) Firmware SHA-256 hash matches the build server's recorded hash.
- (viii) Signed-boot verification passes; no unsigned modules loaded.
- (ix) Alarm priority logic verified: tachycardia at $150\,\text{bpm}$ triggers HIGH-priority alarm within $2$ seconds.
- (x) Cybersecurity: default SSH password changed; firewall rules block all inbound except HL7/DICOM ports.

**Deviation Handling — Critical vs Minor (2.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Critical Deviation | Minor Deviation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Failure that **directly endangers patient or operator safety**, or violates a regulatory must-pass clause. | Failure that is a **documentation, labelling, or non-safety** issue, or a deviation that does not affect clinical performance. |
| **Examples** | Patient leakage current above IEC 60601-1 limit; alarm volume below audibility threshold; cybersecurity default password still active. | Operator manual revision mismatch; cosmetic scratch on enclosure; cable label printing typo. |
| **Action** | (1) **Immediate stop** of further qualification; (2) issue **NCR**; (3) engage vendor / engineering for **root cause analysis**; (4) implement **CAPA**; (5) re-execute the check; (6) escalate to **Hospital Safety Officer + QA Head + Manufacturer's regulatory team**; (7) **mandatory** re-test of all related checks (impact analysis); (8) document in supplementary IQ report. | (1) Document the deviation in the IQ report; (2) issue a **field action** to correct (e.g., reprint label); (3) obtain QA approval for conditional release; (4) verify correction in next scheduled re-qualification. |
| **Release Decision** | **Cannot release** to OQ until closure. | **May release** to OQ with a recorded deviation, subject to closure within a defined time window. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students **confuse "Critical" with "Major"**. In GAMP / GxP terminology, **"Critical"** refers to direct patient/operator safety impact, while **"Major"** refers to a regulatory non-compliance without immediate safety impact. Use the precise term expected in the marking scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Installation Qualification**

- [ ] **Definition:** IQ = documented verification that equipment is installed correctly per design specs and regulatory requirements (ISO 13485 §7.5.6, 21 CFR 820.70).
- [ ] **Validation Cascade:** **IQ → OQ → PQ** (in that order; never skip or reorder).
- [ ] **Golden Rule:** All $C_i = 1$ for IQ to pass; **any single failure blocks release.**
- [ ] **IQ Formula:** $IQ_{result} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{n} C_i$; $FP\text{-}IQY = \dfrac{N_{pass,1st}}{N_{total}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Six Pillars of IQ:** (1) Equipment ID, (2) Utilities, (3) Mechanical, (4) Software/Network, (5) Documentation, (6) Safety.
- [ ] **Key Safety Limits (memorize):** Earthing $\leq 1\,\Omega$ (medical IT); Earth leakage $\leq 5\,\text{mA}$; Patient leakage $\leq 10\,\mu A$ (Type CF, NC); Enclosure leakage $\leq 100\,\mu A$ (Class I, NC).
- [ ] **GAMP 5 Categories:** 1 (Infra OS), 3 (Non-configured COTS), 4 (Configured COTS), 5 (Custom). Higher category = more IQ depth.
- [ ] **V-Model:** URS → FS → DS → IQ → OQ → PQ; every IQ check must trace back to a DS clause.
- [ ] **Document Set:** IQ Protocol, IQ Checklist, IQ Report, Supplementary Deviation Reports, Equipment Master Log Entry, Photographic Evidence.
- [ ] **Deviation Handling:** Issue NCR → Root Cause → CAPA → Re-test → Supplementary Report → Sign-off (QA + BME Head + Safety Officer).
- [ ] **Risk Severity:** $R_{IQ} = P_{failure} \times S_{severity} \times D_{detectability}$ — for connected patient-contact devices, $S = 5$ (catastrophic).
- [ ] **Validation Yield:** $P_{validation} = p_I \cdot p_O \cdot p_P$ (assuming independent stages); IQ has the **first gate leverage** on overall yield.
- [ ] **Cost Insight:** ~70-80% of IQ cost is in **planning and execution**, not rework. Invest in front-end protocol clarity.
- [ ] **First-Pass Yield Target:** World-class biomedical manufacturing targets $FP\text{-}IQY \geq 95\%$.
- [ ] **Standards Stack (for citation in answers):** ISO 13485:2016, 21 CFR Part 820, GAMP 5, IEC 60601-1, IEC 60601-2-x series, ISO 14644-1 (cleanrooms), IS 3043 (earthing), ISO 14971 (risk management), WHO TRS 986 Annex 4.
- [ ] **Common 3-Mark Question Triggers:** "Define IQ," "Differentiate IQ/OQ/PQ," "List four IQ verifications," "Name the regulatory standard."
- [ ] **Common 14-Mark Question Triggers:** "Prepare an IQ checklist for [device]," "Determine IQ status from given measurements," "Develop a GAMP 5 risk-based IQ plan," "Outline CAPA for a failed IQ."
- [ ] **Pitfalls to Avoid:** Confusing IQ with OQ; forgetting to cite the standard; omitting the % exceedance calculation; missing the sign-off roles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operational Qualification and Performance Qualification (IQ/OQ/PQ) Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Operational Qualification and Performance Qualification (IQ/OQ/PQ) Protocols # MODULE 3 — Operational Qualification and Performance Qualification (IQ/OQ/PQ) Protocols ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Installation Qualification (IQ), Operational Qualification (OQ), and Performance Qualification (PQ)** constitute the three sequential, documented verification stages mandated by **ISO 13485:2016 (Clause 7.5.6 — Validation of Processes for Production and Service Provision)** and **FDA 21 CFR §820.75 — Process Validation**, used to demonstrate that a biomedical device (or the manufacturing/clinical process that produces or operates it) has been correctly installed, functions correctly across its intended operating ranges, and consistently performs as intended under real-world clinical or production conditions. In the context of **PEBMT752 (Design of Biomedical Devices)**, IQ/OQ/PQ protocols are the *engineering bridge* between the device's **Design History File (DHF)** and its actual deployment in a hospital, clinical laboratory, or patient environment. They form the backbone of the **Verification & Validation (V&V)** lifecycle demanded by the **V-Model** of medical device development. | Stage | Question Answered | Regulatory Anchor | |-------|-------------------|-------------------| | **IQ** | *Was it installed correctly?* | FDA 21 CFR §820.170, ISO 13485 §7.5.5 | | **OQ** | *Does it work as designed?* | FDA 21 CFR §820.75(a), IEC 62304 §5.7 | | **PQ** | *Does it work in the real world?* | FDA 21 CFR §820.75(b), ISO 14971 risk file | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "New ICU Ventilator" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a hospital purchases a **new MRI machine** for ₹12 crore. Before scanning the first patient, three things must happen: > > 1. **IQ — "Unboxing & Setup":** Engineers physically install the magnet, route the helium coolant lines, connect the **3-phase 415 V** power supply, mount the RF coils, and verify the serial numbers on the cryostat match the packing list. *Did we unpack it right?* > > 2. **OQ — "Bench-Test on the Workbench":** A biomedical engineer places **QA phantoms** (test objects of known geometry) inside the bore. They sweep the static field through **0.5 T → 1.5 T → 3.0 T**, test the SAR (Specific Absorption Rate) alarm, the quench interlock, the patient table limit switches. *Does every button and sensor behave to spec?* > > 3. **PQ — "First Real Scan":** A radiologist performs a real scan on a volunteer. Image Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), Geometric Accuracy, and Slice Position Accuracy are measured against clinical acceptance thresholds over a continuous **30-day** period. *Does it reliably diagnose a real person every single day?* > > **IQ = Receipt, OQ = Bench, PQ = Bedside.** --- ### 1.3 Where IQ/OQ/PQ Sits in the Medical Device Lifecycle The protocol stack is positioned **after design verification** and **before market release**: $$ \text{User Needs} \rightarrow \text{URS} \rightarrow \text{Design Input} \rightarrow \text{Design Output} \rightarrow \text{DQ} \rightarrow \text{IQ} \rightarrow \text{OQ} \rightarrow \text{PQ} \rightarrow \text{Market Release} $$ - **URS** = User Requirements Specification - **DQ** = Design Qualification (verifies the design *itself* meets the URS) - **IQ/OQ/PQ** = Verifies the *realized physical instance* meets the qualified design > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For biomedical devices (Class B, C, D as per **EU MDR 2017/745 Annex VIII**), IQ/OQ/PQ protocols are **mandatory** before clinical use. Skipping any stage can lead to FDA Form 483 observations during audits. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The V-Model of Validation — Theoretical Foundation The IQ/OQ/PQ stack follows the right-hand leg of the classic **V-Model (ISO/IEC 15288)**: | Left Leg (Specification → Down) | Right Leg (Verification → Up) | |----------------------------------|--------------------------------| | User Requirements Specification | **Acceptance Testing (PQ)** | | Functional Requirements Specification | **System Qualification (OQ)** | | Design Specification | **Subsystem Verification** | | Component Specification | **Unit Verification (IQ-level)** | The **right leg is where IQ, OQ, and PQ live**. Each test traces back to a *specific requirement* on the left leg, guaranteeing **traceability**. --- ## 2.2 Installation Qualification (IQ) — Detailed Breakdown **Definition:** IQ provides *documented evidence* that the equipment has been **received, identified, installed, and configured** in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, the purchase order, and any site-specific engineering drawings. ### IQ Protocol Structure (6 Mandatory Sections) 1. **Equipment Identification** — make, model, serial number, firmware version, asset tag. 2. **Pre-Installation Verification** — site readiness (power, HVAC, floor loading, EMI shielding). 3. **Installation Verification** — physical placement, utility hookup (voltage, water, gas), network IP assignment. 4. **Calibration of Installed Instruments** — traceable to **NABL / NIST** standards. 5. **Software Installation & Configuration** — OS version, application build hash, license activation. 6. **Documentation Handover** — Operation Manual, Service Manual, Certificate of Conformity. ### Key IQ Acceptance Criteria - Power supply voltage within ± **10%** of nominal. - Ambient temperature **18 °C to 24 °C** (for imaging devices). - Relative humidity **30% to 70%** non-condensing. - Earth resistance ≤ **1 Ω** (per IS 3043). > [!NOTE] > IQ **does not** test whether the device *functions* — only that it is *installed* correctly. Functional testing belongs to OQ. --- ## 2.3 Operational Qualification (OQ) — Detailed Breakdown **Definition:** OQ provides documented evidence that the equipment operates as intended across its **entire specified operating range** and that its **control, alarm, and safety systems** function correctly under *simulated but not production* conditions. ### OQ Test Categories | Category | Example Test | Acceptance Criterion | |----------|--------------|----------------------| | **Functional Test** | Heart rate range of patient monitor | 30 BPM to 250 BPM, ±2 BPM | | **Alarm Test** | SpO₂ low alarm | Triggers within 5 s when SpO₂ < 90% | | **Safety Test** | Defibrillator energy delivery | 200 J ± 15% into 50 Ω test load | | **Boundary Test** | Infusion pump flow rate | 0.1 mL/h to 1200 mL/h, ±2% | | **Failure Mode Test** | Mains power loss | Battery backup engages within 0.5 s | | **EMC/Safety (Electrical)** | Patient leakage current | ≤ 100 µA (CF-type applied part) | ### OQ Statistical Sampling (For Multi-Unit Installations) For $n$ identical units, OQ is typically performed on a **3-batch sample** (per FDA Process Validation Guidance 2011): $$ n_{\text{OQ, sample}} = \max\left(3,\ \lceil 0.10 \times N \rceil\right) $$ where $N$ is the total number of identical units installed. The *bracketing approach* (worst-case + best-case + mid-point) is preferred over random sampling for high-risk devices. --- ## 2.4 Performance Qualification (PQ) — Detailed Breakdown **Definition:** PQ provides documented evidence that the equipment **consistently performs as intended under actual clinical or production conditions** using real samples, real operators, and the actual environment where the device will be deployed. ### PQ Protocol — Three Sub-Levels 1. **PQ — Performance Verification (PV):** Single-batch challenge test with actual product/patient. 2. **PQ — Process Performance Qualification (PPQ):** Multiple consecutive batches (typically 3) demonstrating consistency. 3. **PQ — Continued Process Verification (CPV):** Ongoing monitoring post-launch (per FDA 2011 Guidance Stage 3). ### PQ Acceptance Metrics for Biomedical Devices | Metric | Symbol | Typical Formula | Threshold | |--------|--------|-----------------|-----------| | **Diagnostic Sensitivity** | $Se$ | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | ≥ 95% (Class III devices) | | **Diagnostic Specificity** | $Sp$ | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | ≥ 90% | | **Positive Predictive Value** | $PPV$ | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Reported | | **Repeatability (CV)** | $CV$ | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$ | ≤ 5% | | **Reproducibility** | $R$ | $R = 2.77 \times \sigma_{\text{between-run}}$ | ≤ 10% | | **Bias** | $B$ | $B = \bar{x} - x_{\text{ref}}$ | Within total allowable error | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Symbol | Unit | Application | |---|---------------------|--------|------|-------------| | 1 | Diagnostic Sensitivity | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | dimensionless | PQ clinical accuracy | | 2 | Diagnostic Specificity | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | dimensionless | PQ clinical accuracy | | 3 | Repeatability (CV) | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}\times 100$ | % | PQ precision check | | 4 | Bias | $B = \bar{x} - x_{\text{ref}}$ | Same as measurand | PQ trueness check | | 5 | Total Allowable Error | $TE_a = B\% + 2 \cdot CV\%$ | % | PQ pass/fail criterion | | 6 | OQ Sample Size | $n_{\text{OQ}} = \max(3,\ \lceil 0.10 N \rceil)$ | integer | Multi-unit installs | | 7 | SNR (Imaging PQ) | $SNR = \dfrac{\mu_{\text{ROI}}}{\sigma_{\text{background}}}$ | dB | MRI/CT PQ | | 8 | Patient Leakage Current | $I_{\text{leak}}$ | µA | OQ electrical safety | | 9 | Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | dimensionless | IQ/OQ failure mode linkage | | 10 | Confidence Interval (95%) | $CI = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | varies | PQ statistical release | > [!WARNING] > In markdown tables, absolute-value bars **must** be written as `\vert x \vert` — never as `|x|` — to prevent column-syntax breakage during KTU PDF export. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility In a **Class IIb/III** device company (say, a glucose monitor or dialysis machine), the IQ/OQ/PQ package is the *legal artifact* that: 1. Gets attached to the **Design Dossier** (EU MDR) or **510(k) / PMA submission** (US FDA). 2. Survives a **Notified Body Audit** (TÜV SÜD, BSI, DEKRA). 3. Provides the legal basis for **post-market surveillance** claims. 4. Drives the **Master Validation Plan (MVP)** document hierarchy. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping: This topic directly satisfies **CO3** (*"Apply validation and qualification protocols in the design of medical devices in compliance with regulatory standards"*) and contributes to **CO5** (*"Evaluate device safety and risk in compliance with ISO 14971"*). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Deriving a Complete IQ/OQ/PQ Protocol for a Blood Pressure Monitor To consolidate the theory, we will now **derive, step by step**, the exact IQ/OQ/PQ protocol structure for a **digital non-invasive blood pressure (NIBP) monitor** intended for clinical use in a Kerala government hospital. --- ## 3.1 Step 1 — Establish the User Requirements Specification (URS) > The URS is the *input* to the entire validation chain. | URS ID | Requirement | |--------|-------------| | URS-01 | Measure systolic BP in the range **60 to 250 mmHg** with ±3 mmHg accuracy. | | URS-02 | Measure diastolic BP in the range **40 to 200 mmHg** with ±3 mmHg accuracy. | | URS-03 | Cycle time for one reading ≤ **45 seconds**. | | URS-04 | Battery operation ≥ **6 hours** continuous. | | URS-05 | Audible alarm when systolic > **180 mmHg** or < **90 mmHg**. | | URS-06 | Operate from **230 V, 50 Hz** mains supply. | --- ## 3.2 Step 2 — Derive the Design Qualification (DQ) Tests For each URS row, we identify **which IQ / OQ / PQ test** verifies it. This is the *traceability matrix* (KTU examiners love this): | URS ID | Verification Stage | Test Type | |--------|--------------------|-----------| | URS-01 (accuracy) | **PQ** | Clinical correlation vs. mercury sphygmomanometer on 30 patients | | URS-02 (accuracy) | **PQ** | Same as URS-01 for diastolic | | URS-03 (cycle time) | **OQ** | Bench test with simulator at 5 pressure points | | URS-04 (battery) | **OQ** | Full discharge curve test | | URS-05 (alarm) | **OQ** | Trigger-level calibration check | | URS-06 (mains) | **IQ** | Voltage measurement at socket, earth continuity check | > **Logic:** IQ checks *static infrastructure*. OQ checks *dynamic functional limits*. PQ checks *clinical usefulness*. --- ## 3.3 Step 3 — Derive the OQ Statistical Acceptance Criterion The NIBP monitor's measurement accuracy must fall within a **95% confidence interval** of the true value. We use the standard clinical-statistics derivation: $$ CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$ Suppose OQ bench tests on a **calibrated pressure simulator** at 5 set points (50, 100, 150, 200, 250 mmHg), repeated **6 times each** ($n=6$ per set point). Standard deviation $\sigma = 1.2$ mmHg. $$ CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{6}} $$ $$ 1.96 \cdot \frac{1.2}{\sqrt{6}} = 1.96 \cdot 0.4899 = 0.9603 \approx 0.96 \text{ mmHg} $$ Therefore: $$ CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm 0.96 \text{ mmHg} $$ Since URS-01 requires ±3 mmHg, and the OQ confidence interval of ±0.96 mmHg is well *within* the ±3 mmHg envelope, the OQ **PASS criterion is satisfied at the bench level**. > **Transition logic:** A narrower bench CI is necessary but **not sufficient** for clinical release. PQ must additionally confirm that the same device, on real human arms of varying circumference (pediatric to adult thigh), still achieves ±3 mmHg. --- ## 3.4 Step 4 — Derive the PQ Diagnostic Accuracy via 2×2 Contingency For PQ, we recruit **$n = 100$ patients** and compare the device against a **mercury sphygmomanometer (gold standard)**. Hypothetical result table: | | Gold Standard (+) Hypertension | Gold Standard (−) Normal | Total | |----------------|-------------------------------:|-------------------------:|------:| | **Device (+)** | $TP = 54$ | $FP = 4$ | $58$ | | **Device (−)** | $FN = 6$ | $TN = 36$ | $42$ | | **Total** | $60$ | $40$ | $100$ | Applying the formula sheet: $$ Se = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{54}{54+6} = \frac{54}{60} = 0.9000 $$ $$ Sp = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{36}{36+4} = \frac{36}{40} = 0.9000 $$ $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{54}{54+4} = \frac{54}{58} \approx 0.9310 $$ $$ NPV = \frac{TN}{TN+FN} = \frac{36}{36+6} = \frac{36}{42} \approx 0.8571 $$ > **PQ Decision Rule (per URS):** Se and Sp must both be ≥ **0.85** for a screening-grade NIBP. The result **passes** (Se = 0.90, Sp = 0.90). --- ## 3.5 Step 5 — Risk-Based Linking (ISO 14971) Every IQ/OQ/PQ failure must be mapped to the **Risk Management File (RMF)**. The classic FMEA score: $$ RPN = S \times O \times D $$ Suppose during OQ we discover that at a **230 V → 240 V mains surge**, the alarm speaker fails. Severity $S = 8$ (delayed treatment), Occurrence $O = 3$, Detectability $D = 5$: $$ RPN = 8 \times 3 \times 5 = 120 $$ Since $RPN > 100$, this is a **RED** flag. A **deviation report (DR)** is filed, the design is reworked (added surge suppressor), and OQ is re-executed until $RPN \leq 100$. > This is why **OQ failures cannot be 'patched'** — they must trigger a **change control** and re-validation. --- ## 3.6 Complete Protocol Execution Flow — Analytical Matrix $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: URS Approved} \rightarrow \text{VMP Issued} \\ &\text{Step 2: IQ Executed} \rightarrow \text{IQ Report Approved} \\ &\text{Step 3: OQ Executed} \rightarrow \text{DRs Resolved} \rightarrow \text{OQ Report Approved} \\ &\text{Step 4: PQ Executed (3 batches minimum)} \rightarrow \text{PQ Report Approved} \\ &\text{Step 5: Validation Summary Report (VSR)} \rightarrow \text{Released for Clinical Use} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic (for exam answers):** *"IOP-VSR"* — IQ, OQ, PQ, then the Validation Summary Report. --- # 4.
