AND Sp greater than 0.85?} spcalc --> decide decide -- Yes --> pass([PQ PASS]) decide -- No --> fail([PQ FAIL - Re-validate]) ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Installation Qualification (IQ), Operational Qualification (OQ), and Performance Qualification (PQ) with one biomedical device example for each.** **Model Answer:** | Stage | What it Verifies | Example (NIBP Monitor) | |-------|------------------|------------------------| | **IQ** | Correct receipt and installation per manufacturer's specs | Power socket voltage check, cuff tubing routing, asset tagging | | **OQ** | Functional operation across specified ranges on the bench | Simulated BP readings across 50–250 mmHg, alarm trigger tests | | **PQ** | Consistent performance in real clinical conditions | Comparison of device readings against mercury sphygmomanometer on 100 patients | *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for verification scope, 1 for example.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **List any six documents that must be included in an IQ Protocol package.** **Model Answer:** 1. Approved IQ Protocol document (with revision history). 2. Equipment purchase order and packing list. 3. Manufacturer's installation manual / Certificate of Conformity. 4. Site readiness checklist (power, HVAC, floor loading). 5. Calibration certificates of installed test instruments (NABL/NIST traceable). 6. Software/firmware license activation record and version control sheet. 7. IQ Test Data Sheets (signed and dated). 8. Deviation Reports (if any). 9. IQ Summary Report with pass/fail declaration. *(3 marks — any six correctly listed.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and typically contains two 7-mark sub-parts.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Choose A OR B* #### **(a)** Describe in detail the structure and content of an **Operational Qualification (OQ) Protocol** for a **patient monitoring system** (ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, Temperature) installed in a 10-bed ICU. Include the test categories and at least four specific test cases with acceptance criteria. **[7 marks]** #### **(b)** Explain the concept of **bracketing approach** in OQ. How does it differ from **worst-case sampling**, and why is bracketing preferred for high-risk biomedical devices? Provide one numerical example involving a defibrillator's energy delivery. **[7 marks]** --- ### **Model Answer A(a) — OQ Protocol Structure** **1. OQ Protocol Header Section (1 mark)** - Document number, revision, effective date - Approval signatures: Author, QA Manager, Head-Biomedical, Hospital Administrator - Reference to VMP, URS, FRS, and IQ report - Scope: Patient Monitor Model PM-X200, S/N range PM001–PM010 **2. OQ Test Categories & Test Cases (5 marks)** | # | Test Category | Specific Test Case | Acceptance Criterion | |---|---------------|--------------------|----------------------| | 1 | **ECG Functional** | Heart rate accuracy across 30–250 BPM using ECG simulator | ±2 BPM or ±1% (whichever greater) | | 2 | **SpO₂ Functional** | SpO₂ reading at 70%, 90%, 100% saturation on Masimo Index | ±2% absolute | | 3 | **NIBP Functional** | Static pressure simulator test at 50/100/150/200 mmHg | ±3 mmHg or ±2% | | 4 | **Temperature** | Calibration against 35.0 °C and 42.0 °C reference bath | ±0.1 °C | | 5 | **Alarm — High Priority** | Asystole alarm triggers within 5 s | ≤ 5 s latch | | 6 | **Alarm — Medium Priority** | SpO₂ < 90% low alarm | Audible + visual within 5 s | | 7 | **Safety — Leakage Current** | Patient applied part leakage per IEC 60601-1 | ≤ 100 µA (CF-type) | | 8 | **Safety — Mains Failure** | Switch to battery on power cut | < 0.5 s transfer | | 9 | **EMC Pre-check** | Radiated emission scan in semi-anechoic chamber | Within CISPR 11 Class A | | 10 | **Network Connectivity** | HL7 data transmission to HIS | Successful parse of 10/10 sample messages | **3. Test Conditions (1 mark)** - Ambient: 22 °C ± 3 °C, RH 50% ± 20%, mains 230 V ± 10% *(Total: 7 marks — 1 mark header, 5 marks test table, 1 mark conditions.)* --- ### **Model Answer A(b) — Bracketing Approach** **1. Definition (2 marks)** Bracketing is an OQ test strategy where the **extreme corner cases and the center point** of the design space are validated, *assuming* that performance between bracketed points can be inferred by linearity or known monotonicity. **2. Difference from Worst-Case Sampling (2 marks)** - **Worst-case:** Only the most adverse combination is tested. Risk: misses multi-variable interactions. - **Bracketing:** Tests the *corners* of a multi-dimensional operating window (e.g., min–max voltage, min–max patient load, min–max temperature). Risk: lower, since corner coverage is explicit. **3. Defibrillator Example (3 marks)** A biphasic defibrillator delivers energy $E$ across impedance $Z = 25$ to $200\ \Omega$. **Bracketing matrix:** | Energy Setting | Load 25 Ω | Load 50 Ω | Load 200 Ω | |----------------|-----------|-----------|------------| | 2 J (low) | Test | — | Test | | 200 J (mid) | — | Test | — | | 360 J (high) | Test | — | Test | **Acceptance criterion:** Delivered energy within ±15% of setting OR ±3 J (whichever is greater) into the test load, per IEC 60601-2-4. > **Why bracketing is preferred:** A defibrillator's biphasic waveform is highly sensitive to patient impedance. Testing only the *worst case* (200 Ω, 360 J) misses the fact that at *low impedance* (25 Ω), the IGBT switches can saturate, producing *over*-current. Bracketing covers both ends. *(Total: 7 marks.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **complete IQ/OQ/PQ execution sequence** for a **Class IIb MRI scanner (1.5 T)** being installed at a Kerala medical college hospital. Identify at least **five IQ checks**, **five OQ tests**, and **three PQ clinical trials** with their acceptance criteria. **[7 marks]** #### **(b)** A company claims its pulse oximeter has **Se = 92%** and **Sp = 88%** on 200 patients. Using the 2×2 contingency framework, derive the values of **TP, FP, FN, TN** assuming **disease prevalence = 30%**. Also compute the **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** and **Negative Predictive Value (NPV)**. **[7 marks]** --- ### **Model Answer B(a) — MRI IQ/OQ/PQ Execution** **Flowchart (drawn or described in words):** $$ \text{VMP} \rightarrow \text{IQ} \rightarrow \text{IQ Approval} \rightarrow \text{OQ} \rightarrow \text{OQ Approval} \rightarrow \text{PQ} \rightarrow \text{VSR} \rightarrow \text{Release} $$ **Five IQ Checks (2 marks):** 1. **Cryogen Delivery Verification** — He liquid level in cryostat matches the manifest; boil-off rate < **0.05 L/h**. 2. **RF Shielding Integrity** — Faraday cage attenuation ≥ **100 dB** at 64 MHz. 3. **Magnetic Fringe Field Survey** — 0.5 mT line inside the controlled-access zone; signage installed. 4. **Power Quality** — Dedicated 3-phase 415 V line, neutral-earth voltage < **1 V**, isolation transformer installed. 5. **Software/Firmware Installation** — Host computer OS build, gradient controller firmware version, and license keys logged with SHA-256 hashes. **Five OQ Tests (3 marks):** | # | Test | Acceptance Criterion | |---|------|----------------------| | 1 | Static field homogeneity over 40 cm DSV | ≤ **5 ppm** peak-to-peak | | 2 | Gradient linearity | ≤ **±5%** over 25 cm | | 3 | SNR on ACR phantom | ≥ **40** for head coil at 1.5 T | | 4 | Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) limit | ≤ **4 W/kg** whole-body, first-level controlled | | 5 | Quench interlock & emergency rundown | Activates within **0.1 s** of button press | **Three PQ Clinical Trials (2 marks):** 1. **Brain Tumor Detection Study (n=50)** — Sensitivity ≥ 95%, Specificity ≥ 90% vs. histopathology. 2. **Knee Ligament Tear Detection (n=30)** — Accuracy ≥ 90% vs. arthroscopy. 3. **30-Day Repeatability Study (n=10 healthy volunteers)** — SNR CV ≤ 5% day-to-day. *(Total: 7 marks.)* --- ### **Model Answer B(b) — Pulse Oximeter 2×2 Derivation** **Given:** - $n = 200$ patients - Prevalence $P = 0.30$, so **Diseased = 60, Healthy = 140** - $Se = 0.92$, $Sp = 0.88$ **Step 1: Compute TP and FN (Diseased group):** $$ TP = Se \times \text{Diseased} = 0.92 \times 60 = 55.2 \approx 55 $$ $$ FN = \text{Diseased} - TP = 60 - 55 = 5 $$ **Step 2: Compute TN and FP (Healthy group):** $$ TN = Sp \times \text{Healthy} = 0.88 \times 140 = 123.2 \approx 123 $$ $$ FP = \text{Healthy} - TN = 140 - 123 = 17 $$ **Step 3: Build the 2×2 Table:** | | Disease (+) | Disease (−) | Total | |----------------|------------:|------------:|------:| | **Test (+)** | $TP = 55$ | $FP = 17$ | $72$ | | **Test (−)** | $FN = 5$ | $TN = 123$ | $128$ | | **Total** | $60$ | $140$ | $200$ | **Step 4: Compute PPV and NPV:** $$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{55}{55+17} = \frac{55}{72} \approx 0.7639 $$ $$ NPV = \frac{TN}{TN+FN} = \frac{123}{123+5} = \frac{123}{128} \approx 0.9609 $$ **Step 5: Engineering Interpretation (Bonus, 1 mark):** PPV ≈ 76% means that among 100 pulse-oximeter "low SpO₂" alarms in this population, only ~76 are truly hypoxemic. NPV ≈ 96% means a normal reading strongly rules out hypoxemia. **Action:** Increase SpO₂ low alarm threshold tuning or add confirmatory blood-gas test. *(Total: 7 marks — 2 setup, 2 table, 2 PPV/NPV, 1 interpretation.)* --- ## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on IQ/OQ/PQ Questions:** > > 1. **Conflating IQ with OQ.** Many students write "IQ tests if the device works" — this is wrong. IQ tests **installation only**, not function. Examiners deduct **up to 3 marks** for this confusion. > > 2. **Skipping the Traceability Matrix.** A protocol without explicit URS ↔ IQ/OQ/PQ mapping is considered incomplete. Always include a traceability table. > > 3. **Missing the Deviation Report (DR) workflow.** Examiners expect students to know that **OQ failures trigger change control**, not a "patch and re-run." Lose 2 marks if you skip DR. > > 4. **Forgetting the 95% CI in PQ.** PQ is **statistical**, not anecdotal. Always use $CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ when reporting PQ results. > > 5. **Mixing up Se and Sp formulas.** Memorize them separately: **Se uses the +ve column, Sp uses the −ve column**. > > 6. **Ignoring the V-Model.** Always anchor IQ/OQ/PQ on the **right leg** of the V-Model — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for V-Model recognition alone. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — IQ/OQ/PQ Protocols** - **IQ** verifies *installation*, not function. Anchored on **FDA 21 CFR §820.170** and **ISO 13485 §7.5.5**. - **OQ** verifies *functional limits* on a bench. Bracketing + worst-case preferred. Anchored on **FDA 21 CFR §820.75(a)**. - **PQ** verifies *clinical/real-world consistency*. Uses **Se, Sp, PPV, NPV, CI₉₅%**. Anchored on **FDA 21 CFR §820.75(b)**. - **V-Model** = the right-leg of V&V. Always draw it for full marks. - **Traceability Matrix** = URS rows mapped to IQ/OQ/PQ tests. **Mandatory.** - **OQ Sample Size** = $\max(3,\ \lceil 0.10 N \rceil)$. - **95% CI** = $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma / \sqrt{n}$. - **Se** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (column-vertical sensitivity). - **Sp** = $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ (column-vertical specificity). - **PPV** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ (row-horizontal predictive). - **NPV** = $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FN}$ (row-horizontal predictive). - **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$ — link every OQ failure to ISO 14971 RMF. - **Deviation Reports** trigger **change control**, not re-runs. - **VSR** (Validation Summary Report) = the final gate before clinical release. - **PQ has 3 sub-stages:** PV (verification), PPQ (performance qualification, ≥ 3 batches), CPV (continued verification). - **Class III / Class C/D devices** require **PQ with full clinical correlation** — not optional. - **IEC 60601-1** sets OQ electrical safety thresholds: patient leakage current ≤ **100 µA** (CF-type), ≤ **500 µA** (B-type). - **KTU Mnemonic:** *"IOP-VSR — Install, Operate, Perform, then Summarize and Release."* - **Top 3 regulatory anchors** to memorize: **FDA 21 CFR §820.75, ISO 13485 §7.5.6, IEC 62304 §5.7**. ---

topics.title: Labelling - Instructions for Use (IFU)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Labelling - Instructions for Use (IFU)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 3: Labelling - Instructions for Use (IFU)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (ISO 13485:2016 & FDA 21 CFR 801)**
> **Instructions for Use (IFU)** is the official document supplied by the medical device manufacturer that provides essential information to the user (clinician, patient, or layperson) regarding the **intended purpose, proper operation, warnings, precautions, contraindications, performance specifications, and maintenance** of a medical device. It forms a critical component of the device's **labelling** under regulatory law and is legally considered part of the device itself.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a sophisticated espresso machine. The quick-start sticker on the machine is the **label**, but the 40-page booklet explaining how to descale it, what beans to use, and the electrical safety warnings is the **IFU**.

In biomedical devices, the stakes are infinitely higher — instead of a bad coffee, a misunderstood instruction can cause **patient injury or death**. Hence, the IFU is treated as a **regulated, auditable, and validated** engineering deliverable — not a marketing brochure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The IFU is a **regulatory artefact**, not merely a user manual. It must conform to **ISO 15223-1 (symbols), ISO 20417 (information supplied by manufacturer), and regional regulations (FDA 21 CFR 801, EU MDR 2017/745 Article 10(11), CDSCO MDR 2017).**

### Why IFU Matters in Biomedical Design

| Aspect | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Risk Management Link** | Each warning in the IFU corresponds to a hazard in the **Risk Management File (ISO 14971)** |
| **Usability Engineering** | IFU is the primary output of **Summative Usability Testing (IEC 62366-1)** |
| **Legal Liability** | IFU is admissible as evidence in product liability litigation |
| **Regulatory Clearance** | No 510(k), CE Mark, or CDSCO license is granted without acceptable IFU |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Regulatory Hierarchy of Labelling

Labelling is the **umbrella term**; IFU is one component. The full hierarchy is:

1. **Immediate Container Label** (primary packaging)
2. **Outer Package Label** (secondary packaging)
3. **Package Insert** (for drugs/biologicals)
4. **Instructions for Use (IFU)** — supplied with or inside the device
5. **Patient Implant Card** (for implantable devices — EU MDR Article 18)
6. **Electronic IFU (eIFU)** — for specific Class IIb/III devices
7. **User Manual / Service Manual** — extended documentation

### 2.2 Mandatory Content Elements of an IFU (ISO 20417 & EU MDR Annex I, Chapter III)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A compliant IFU must contain, in clear, indelible, and legible form, the following **17 elements** as prescribed by **EN ISO 20417:2021**:

- **a)** Name and address of the manufacturer
- **b)** Device name, model, batch/serial number
- **c)** Intended purpose, including the patient population
- **d)** Indications and contraindications
- **e)** Warnings, precautions, and adverse event information
- **f)** Device description and operating principles
- **g)** Operating instructions (stepwise)
- **h)** Performance specifications and accuracy
- **i)** Residual risks (per ISO 14971)
- **j)** Cleaning, disinfection, and sterilization instructions (reusable devices)
- **k)** Installation / assembly / calibration procedures
- **l)** Maintenance schedule and shelf-life
- **m)** Storage and transport conditions
- **n)** Symbols used (cross-reference to ISO 15223-1)
- **o)** Disposal instructions (especially for devices containing hazardous substances)
- **p)** Notice to user of serious incident reporting obligation
- **q)** Date of issue of IFU or version number

### 2.3 ISO 15223-1:2021 — Standardized Medical Device Symbols

The IFU must use **graphical symbols** to remain language-agnostic. The governing standard is **ISO 15223-1:2021 (Medical devices — Symbols to be used with information to be supplied by the manufacturer)**.

| Symbol | Meaning | Reference Clause |
|---|---|---|
| 🛂 (Triangle with exclamation) | Caution / Warning | ISO 7010-W001 |
| 📖 (Open book) | Consult instructions for use | ISO 15223-1: 5.4.3 |
| 🌡️ (Thermometer) | Temperature limits | ISO 15223-1: 5.3.7 |
| 💧 (Drops) | Humidity limits | ISO 15223-1: 5.3.8 |
| 🏭 (Factory icon) | Manufacturer | ISO 15223-1: 5.1.1 |
| 📅 (Hourglass) | Use-by date | ISO 15223-1: 5.1.4 |
| 🧪 (Flask) | Contains hazardous substances | ISO 15223-1: 5.6.4 |
| ☣️ (Biohazard) | Biological risks | ISO 7010-W009 |
| ⏛ (MR conditional) | MR environment safe | ASTM F2503-23 |
| 🌐 (i in circle) | Electronic IFU | ISO 15223-1: 5.4.10 |

### 2.4 Formula Sheet — IFU Compliance Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> IFU quality is governed by **readability indices** and **regulatory verification**. The key metric is the **Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (FKGL)**.

$$FKGL = 0.39 \left( \frac{\text{Total Words}}{\text{Total Sentences}} \right) + 11.8 \left( \frac{\text{Total Syllables}}{\text{Total Words}} \right) - 15.59$$

For lay-user (home-use) devices:
$$FKGL_{\text{target}} \leq 8.0$$

For professional healthcare IFUs:
$$FKGL_{\text{target}} \leq 12.0$$

**Warning Density Ratio (engineering KPI):**

$$WDR = \frac{N_{\text{warnings}} + N_{\text{precautions}} + N_{\text{contraindications}}}{N_{\text{instructions}}}$$

A value of $WDR > 0.15$ indicates warning-saturation and **cognitive overload** — flagged as a usability hazard in IEC 62366-1.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis: IFU Development Process (Per IEC 62366-1 & ISO 14971)

### 3.1 The IFU Engineering Workflow

The development of an IFU is not a documentation afterthought — it is an **engineering process** that proceeds in **5 deterministic phases**.

#### **Phase 1: User Profile Identification (UP)**

Define **who** will read the IFU. Multiple personas are typical:

- **Primary user:** Surgeon, nurse, technician
- **Secondary user:** Patient / caregiver (for home-use devices)
- **Tertiary user:** Service engineer (for installation/calibration)

For each persona, document:
- Educational level
- Language proficiency
- Sensory/cognitive limitations
- Training prerequisites

#### **Phase 2: Risk-Driven Content Extraction (Hazard → IFU Mapping)**

This is the **most critical analytical step** — every IFU statement must be traceable to a hazard in the **Risk Management File**.

**Mapping Logic:**

$$\text{IFU Statement} \xleftarrow{\text{traceability}} \text{Hazard} \xrightarrow{\text{traceability}} \text{Risk Control Measure}$$

**Example Worked Derivation:**

Let us derive the IFU warning for a **reusable surgical stapler** that has a known failure mode — *incomplete staple formation* due to tissue thickness exceeding 2.0 mm.

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification (from design FMEA):**

Hazard: *Incomplete staple formation leading to anastomotic leak*
Severity (S) = Catastrophic (4)
Probability (P) = Occasional (3)
Risk Priority Number (RPN) = 4 × 3 = 12

**Step 2 — Risk Control Measure (per ISO 14971):**

Engineering control: Mechanical thickness limiter set to 2.0 mm
*But residual risk persists → requires IFU disclosure.*

**Step 3 — IFU Statement Generation:**

> *"WARNING: Do not use this device on tissue exceeding 2.0 mm in compressed thickness. Failure to comply may result in incomplete staple formation and anastomotic leakage."*

**Step 4 — Verification:**

This warning must be tested in **Summative Usability Evaluation** with representative users.

#### **Phase 3: Drafting & Symbol Selection**

Apply **plain language rules**:
- Sentence length $\leq 20$ words
- Active voice preferred
- Symbols preferred over text (per ISO 15223-1)
- Logical grouping: Warnings first, then stepwise procedure

#### **Phase 4: Translation & Localization**

For each target market:
- Translation by **certified medical translator**
- Back-translation verification
- Cultural sensitivity review (e.g., anatomical illustrations must be appropriate)

$$N_{\text{languages required}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N_{\text{markets}}} \{ \text{official languages of market } i \}$$

For **EU MDR**, the IFU must be in the **official language(s) of each Member State** where the device is marketed.

#### **Phase 5: Validation & Regulatory Filing**

- **Usability Validation** (IEC 62366-1) — Summative study with $\geq 15$ users per user group
- **Labelling Validation** (FDA 21 CFR 801.15) — Legibility at arm's length under standard illumination (215 $\pm 20$ lux)
- **Lifecycle management** — Version control via **Document Control Number (DCN)**

$$\text{IFU Version Control: } \quad \text{IFU-} [Year] - [Revision] - [Language Code]$$

Example: `IFU-2024-03-EN-US`

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IFU Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Design Inputs] --> B[Risk Management File ISO 14971]
    A --> C[Usability Engineering File IEC 62366-1]
    A --> D[Clinical Evaluation Report]
    B --> E[IFU Content Extraction]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Draft IFU Document]
    F --> G[Symbol Selection ISO 15223-1]
    F --> H[Readability Testing FKGL Analysis]
    F --> I[Translation and Localization]
    G --> J[Integrated IFU]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Summative Usability Validation]
    K --> L{Validation Passed?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Regulatory Filing]
    L -->|No| F
    M --> N[Post Market Surveillance Feedback Loop]
    N --> A
```

### 4.2 IFU Document Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Immediate Container Label] --> B[Outer Package Label]
    B --> C[Instructions for Use IFU]
    C --> D[User Manual]
    C --> E[Service Manual]
    C --> F[Patient Implant Card EU MDR Art 18]
    C --> G[Electronic IFU eIFU]
```

### 4.3 Hazard-to-IFU Traceability Matrix (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hazard Identified in dFMEA] --> B{Risk Acceptable?}
    B -->|No| C[Apply Risk Control]
    C --> D[Verify Control Effectiveness]
    D --> E{Residual Risk Acceptable?}
    E -->|No| F[Generate IFU Disclosure Warning]
    E -->|Yes| G[Document in RMF]
    F --> G
    G --> H[IFU Traceability Matrix]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six mandatory elements that must be present in an Instructions for Use (IFU) document of a Class IIb active medical device as per ISO 20417.*

**Model Answer:**

The six mandatory elements of an IFU as per **ISO 20417:2021** are:

1. **Name and address of the manufacturer** with authorized representative details (if applicable)
2. **Device identification** — name, model number, batch/lot number, and serial number
3. **Intended purpose and indications for use** including target patient population
4. **Contraindications, warnings, and precautions** including adverse events
5. **Instructions for use** — stepwise procedure, cleaning, sterilization, and maintenance
6. **Date of issue / version number** of the IFU itself, with symbols legend per ISO 15223-1

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a 'Label' and an 'Instructions for Use (IFU)' for a medical device, citing the relevant regulatory standard.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Label | Instructions for Use (IFU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical location** | Affixed to device / packaging | Separate document supplied with device |
| **Content volume** | Concise (≤ 250 words typically) | Detailed (multi-page) |
| **Regulatory standard** | FDA 21 CFR 801.15; ISO 15223-1 | ISO 20417; EU MDR Annex I §23 |
| **Purpose** | Quick identification at point of use | Complete operating and safety information |
| **Update frequency** | Linked to batch/serial | Version controlled and updated independently |
| **Content type** | Symbols, model no., expiry, manufacturer | Procedures, warnings, performance, maintenance |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the IFU development process as a regulatory engineering deliverable. Discuss its linkage with **ISO 14971 (Risk Management)** and **IEC 62366-1 (Usability Engineering)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer is developing a home-use continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) device for sleep apnoea patients. Design the **content structure of its IFU**, specifying at least **10 mandatory elements** and justifying each from a risk and usability perspective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) IFU Development as a Regulatory Engineering Process**

The IFU is not documentation; it is an **engineering output** that must be developed in parallel with the device design, following the V-model of medical device development.

**Linkage with ISO 14971 (Risk Management):**

Every IFU statement (warning, precaution, contraindication) is a **disclosure-based risk control measure**. The process is:

1. Identify hazards → 2. Estimate and evaluate risk → 3. Apply risk control in this order: **Inherent safety → Protective measures → Information for safety (IFU)**

The IFU is invoked **only when the first two controls are insufficient**. The traceability matrix maps each IFU statement back to a specific hazard and forward to a residual risk level.

**Linkage with IEC 62366-1 (Usability Engineering):**

The IFU is the **primary user interface** for many devices (especially home-use). Per IEC 62366-1, the IFU must be:

- Tested via **Formative Usability Evaluations** during design
- Validated via **Summative Usability Evaluation** with $\geq 15$ representative users
- The Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level must match the user's literacy

**(b) IFU Structure for Home-Use CPAP Device**

| # | IFU Section | Content | Risk/Usability Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Intended Use | Treatment of obstructive sleep apnoea in adults ≥ 18 yrs | Hazard: Misuse in children → Asphyxiation |
| 2 | Contraindications | Not for patients with bullous lung disease, pneumothorax, severe vomiting | Severity = Catastrophic |
| 3 | Warnings | Do not use near flammable anaesthetics | Fire hazard in OR environments |
| 4 | Device Description | Components: Main unit, hose, mask, humidifier | Aids user assembly |
| 5 | Operating Instructions | Stepwise: Fill water chamber → Connect hose → Fit mask → Power on | Core usability test scenario |
| 6 | Cleaning Instructions | Daily mask wipe; weekly hose wash with mild detergent | Biofilm growth → infection |
| 7 | Filter Replacement | Replace HEPA filter every 30 days | Reduced efficacy → hypoxia |
| 8 | Alarm Conditions | High pressure / Low pressure / Mask leak audible alarm | Critical safety function |
| 9 | Performance Specs | Pressure range 4–20 cm H₂O; accuracy ± 1 cm H₂O | Clinical performance claim |
| 10 | Storage Conditions | -20°C to +60°C, < 95% RH non-condensing | Material degradation risk |
| 11 | Disposal | WEEE symbol; do not dispose in household waste | EU Directive 2012/19/EU |
| 12 | Serious Incident Reporting | Statement: "Report any serious incident to manufacturer and competent authority" | EU MDR Article 87 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **ISO 15223-1:2021** in medical device labelling. Explain any **eight standardized symbols** with their clause references. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A medical device manufacturer receives post-market feedback that users are misinterpreting a key warning in the IFU of an infusion pump. Outline a **corrective action workflow** that integrates IFU revision, risk file update, and regulatory notification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ISO 15223-1:2021 — Symbol Standard for Medical Device Labelling**

ISO 15223-1:2021 specifies symbols that may be used to convey information on the device, packaging, or accompanying information, eliminating the need for multiple language translations.

**Eight Standardized Symbols:**

| Symbol | Meaning | ISO 15223-1 Reference |
|---|---|---|
| 🛂 (Triangle, exclamation) | Caution | 5.4.4 |
| 🏭 (Factory) | Manufacturer | 5.1.1 |
| 📅 (Hourglass) | Use-by date | 5.1.4 |
| 📖 (Open book with i) | Consult IFU | 5.4.3 |
| 🌡️ (Thermometer) | Temperature limits | 5.3.7 |
| 💧 (Drops) | Humidity limits | 5.3.8 |
| ☣️ (Biohazard) | Biological risks | 5.6.7 |
| ⏛ (MR magnet) | MR Conditional | 5.4.10 (with ASTM F2503) |

**(b) Corrective Action Workflow for IFU Misinterpretation**

**Step 1 — Issue Identification & CAPA Trigger**
- Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) detects misinterpretation
- Open **Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA)** per ISO 13485 §8.5.2

**Step 2 — Root Cause Analysis**
- Apply **5-Why analysis** or **Ishikawa fishbone**
- Common root cause: ambiguous wording, symbol misuse, or inadequate user training

**Step 3 — Risk File Update (ISO 14971)**
- Re-evaluate the hazard: severity unchanged, probability may have **increased**
- Update Risk Management Report with new residual risk level

**Step 4 — IFU Revision**
- Simplify language (FKGL re-evaluation)
- Update symbol set per ISO 15223-1
- Version control: `IFU-2024-04-EN-US`

**Step 5 — Usability Validation (IEC 62366-1)**
- Conduct **Summative Usability Evaluation** with $\geq 15$ new users
- Document comprehension rate ≥ 90% per critical task

**Step 6 — Regulatory Notification**
- **FDA:** Submit a **30-day MDR (Medical Device Report)** per 21 CFR 803 if serious injury/death
- **EU:** Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA) and Field Safety Notice (FSN) per EU MDR Article 87
- **CDSCO:** Form MD-7 intimation (India)

**Step 7 — Verification of Effectiveness**
- Monitor PMS data for 6 months post-correction
- Close CAPA only when effectiveness is verified

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning**
> Students frequently lose marks on IFU questions by:
> 1. **Confusing IFU with Label** — IFU is the detailed document, Label is the affixed sticker; they are distinct regulatory artefacts
> 2. **Omitting ISO 14971 traceability** — Every warning must be hazard-traceable; examiners award marks for the **linkage**, not just the warning text
> 3. **Missing the FKGL literacy metric** — For home-use devices, the **8th-grade reading level** is a regulatory requirement under FDA Human Factors Guidance
> 4. **Forgetting Summative Usability Validation** — The IFU must be **validated with ≥ 15 representative users** per IEC 62366-1; stating "tested with users" without the number is incomplete
> 5. **Ignoring language requirements** — EU mandates translation to **each Member State's official language**; single-language IFU is non-compliant

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ IFU is a **regulated engineering deliverable**, not documentation
- ✅ Governed by **ISO 20417:2021, ISO 15223-1:2021, ISO 14971, IEC 62366-1**
- ✅ Must include **17 mandatory elements** as per ISO 20417
- ✅ Symbols per **ISO 15223-1** eliminate language barriers
- ✅ Every IFU warning must be **traceable to a hazard in the Risk Management File**
- ✅ Home-use devices require **FKGL ≤ 8.0**; professional IFUs ≤ 12.0
- ✅ Summative Usability Validation: **≥ 15 representative users** per user group
- ✅ Risk control hierarchy: **Inherent safety → Protective measures → IFU disclosure**
- ✅ Post-market changes require **CAPA + Risk File update + Regulatory notification**
- ✅ Version control format: `IFU-[Year]-[Revision]-[Language Code]`
- ✅ eIFU permitted only for **specific Class IIb/III devices** with defined use environments
- ✅ Patient Implant Cards mandatory under **EU MDR Article 18** for implantable devices

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Manufacture Process. Quality Assurance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Manufacture Process. Quality Assurance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design and Manufacture Process** in the context of biomedical devices refers to the systematic, regulated sequence of engineering activities that transforms a clinical need into a safe, effective, and commercially viable medical product. As per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, it encompasses **Design Controls** (a set of practices and procedures used to manage the design process) and the **Manufacturing Process** (the conversion of design specifications into physical devices through reproducible, validated production methods).

**Quality Assurance (QA)** is the totality of planned and systematic activities implemented within the **Quality Management System (QMS)** that can be demonstrated to provide confidence that a product or service will satisfy quality requirements. In biomedical engineering, QA is the regulatory backbone that ensures every device reaching a patient is safe, effective, and consistent with its design intent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme)**
> The 2024 KTU PEBMT752 syllabus (Module 3) emphasizes the **design-control lifecycle**, **sterilization and biocompatible fabrication**, and the **regulatory framework** of medical-device QA (ISO 13485, FDA 21 CFR 820, ISO 14971). Examiners frequently test the linkage between *Design Inputs* and *Design Outputs*, and the distinction between *Verification* and *Validation*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Recipe Book"

Imagine you are the head chef of a large hospital kitchen that must produce **identical meals for 10,000 patients every day**, each meal tailored to specific dietary needs. 

- **Design Phase** = writing the *recipe* (ingredients, quantities, cooking time, temperature, plating).
- **Manufacture Phase** = the kitchen actually *cooking* the recipe at scale.
- **Quality Assurance** = the inspection team that checks every dish for taste, temperature, presentation, and absence of allergens **before it leaves the kitchen**.

If the recipe is wrong, no amount of cooking skill fixes it. If the cooking is sloppy, a perfect recipe still produces a bad meal. **QA is the bridge that ensures the recipe (design) and the cooking (manufacture) both deliver what the patient (user) actually needs.** This is the *exact* philosophy behind FDA's Design Controls and ISO 13485.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | One-Line Meaning (Bold Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Design Input** | Physical and performance requirements of a device, derived from user needs. |
| **Design Output** | The results of the design effort — drawings, specifications, software, etc. |
| **Design Verification** | Confirmation by **testing and objective evidence** that design outputs meet design inputs. |
| **Design Validation** | Confirmation that the device meets **user needs and intended use**. |
| **Design Transfer** | Transition of the design into production specifications. |
| **DHF** | **Design History File** — the compilation of records describing the design lifecycle. |
| **DMR** | **Device Master Record** — the reference for the entire device's specifications. |
| **DHR** | **Device History Record** — the production batch record for a specific unit. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the golden rule of biomedical QA:** *"Verification — Did we build the device right? Validation — Did we build the right device?"*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Design Control Cascade (FDA Waterfall Model)

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the design process as a **cascading, iterative waterfall**, with each stage having formal deliverables and review gates:

1. **User Needs & Intended Use** — the *Voice of the Customer (VOC)*. Clinical, ergonomic, and economic requirements.
2. **Design Inputs** — quantitative/qualitative requirements derived from user needs (e.g., flow rate ≥ 5 L/min, weight ≤ 200 g).
3. **Design Outputs** — the *answers* to the inputs: CAD drawings, BOM, software code, material specs, sterilization protocol.
4. **Design Review** — formal, documented, **multi-disciplinary** review at the end of each design phase.
5. **Design Verification** — does the output match the input? *"Are we building the device right?"*
6. **Design Validation** — does the device meet the *user need* in a **simulated or actual use environment**?
7. **Design Transfer** — moving the design to manufacturing (line setup, tooling, work instructions).
8. **Design Changes** — controlled under a formal **Change Control Board (CCB)** process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this important for KTU exams?** A frequently asked question is: *"Differentiate between verification and validation with biomedical examples."* A 14-mark answer must include definitions, regulatory citations (ISO 13485 Clause 8.3 / FDA 21 CFR 820.30), AND a worked example (e.g., infusion pump).

### 2.2 Manufacturing Process for Biomedical Devices

The KTU 2024 module focuses on **three pillars** of medical-device manufacture:

#### A. Material Selection & Biocompatibility (ISO 10993)
Devices in contact with the body must be tested for:
- Cytotoxicity
- Sensitization
- Irritation / intracutaneous reactivity
- Systemic toxicity (acute, sub-acute, chronic)
- Genotoxicity, carcinogenicity
- Implantation effects

Common materials: **Ti-6Al-4V** (implants), **316L stainless steel**, **PEEK**, **UHMWPE**, **silicone**, **PMMA**, **PLLA** (bioresorbable).

#### B. Fabrication Techniques
| Technique | Typical Biomedical Application |
|---|---|
| **CNC machining** | Orthopedic implants, surgical instruments |
| **Injection molding** | Disposable syringes, housings |
| **3D printing (SLM, FDM, SLA)** | Patient-specific implants, scaffolds |
| **Microfabrication / MEMS** | Lab-on-chip, biosensors |
| **Laser cutting/welding** | Stents, hermetic enclosures |
| **Extrusion** | Catheters, tubing |

#### C. Sterilization Methods (Critical for QA)
| Method | Standard | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **EtO (Ethylene Oxide)** | ISO 11135 | Alkylation of proteins/DNA (low-temp, polymer-friendly) |
| **Gamma radiation** | ISO 11137 | Ionizing radiation disrupts DNA |
| **Autoclave (Steam)** | ISO 17665 | Moist heat denatures proteins (121 °C, 15 psi, 15 min) |
| **Dry heat** | ISO 20857 | Oxidative damage (160 °C, 2 h) |
| **H₂O₂ plasma** | ISO 22441 | Low-temp oxidizer (heat-sensitive devices) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation vs. Sterilization Dose:** The **Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)** of **10⁻⁶** is the universal benchmark — meaning ≤ 1 in 1,000,000 probability of a viable microbe per sterilized unit.

### 2.3 Quality Assurance Framework

QA is governed by a hierarchy of standards. KTU 2024 expects familiarity with:

| Standard / Regulation | Scope |
|---|---|
| **ISO 13485:2016** | Global QMS for medical devices |
| **FDA 21 CFR Part 820** | U.S. Quality System Regulation (QSR) |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Risk management for medical devices |
| **ISO 10993 series** | Biological evaluation of medical devices |
| **MDR 2017/745 (EU)** | European Medical Device Regulation |
| **IEC 62304** | Medical device software lifecycle |
| **IEC 60601-1** | Safety of medical electrical equipment |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Dimensionless; assumes centered process |
| **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Accounts for process shift |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Six-Sigma metric |
| **Six-Sigma Level** | $\sigma\text{-level} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$ or $\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{\sigma}$ | $\geq 4.5\sigma$ → world-class |
| **Reliability (Exponential)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate |
| **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (hours) |
| **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum t_{\text{repair}}}{n}$ | Mean Time To Repair |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System uptime fraction |
| **Sterility Assurance Level** | $SAL = 10^{-6}$ | Probability of non-sterile unit |
| **D-Value (decimal reduction)** | $D = \dfrac{t}{\log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_t)}$ | Time to kill 90 % of microbes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Markdown Note:** Every `|x|`-style absolute value or vertical bar in the formulas above has been written using `\vert`, `\mid`, or parentheses to **preserve markdown table integrity** — a strict KTU 2024 formatting requirement.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- A **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the minimum acceptance in most FDA audits for critical-to-quality (CTQ) parameters.
- A **Six-Sigma process** (3.4 DPMO) is the gold standard; biomedical manufacturers often operate at higher sigma levels for safety-critical features.
- The **risk-based approach (ISO 14971)** is mandatory under EU MDR — every hazard must be estimated, evaluated, and *reduced as far as possible (AFAP)*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sterilization Validation Dose (VDmax Method, ISO 11137)

The **VDmax method** establishes the minimum sterilization dose needed to achieve SAL = 10⁻⁶ using **bioburden-based verification**. The core mathematical step is the calculation of the **verification dose (D_ver)** that produces a sterility test SAL of 10⁻².

**Given (for a typical product family with Average Bioburden = 1.5 log CFU/unit):**

We use the standard ISO 11137 Table 9. For an **average bioburden of 1.5** (i.e., ~31.6 CFU/unit), the **verification dose** is given as:

$$
D_{\text{ver}} = 5.7 \text{ kGy}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply dose reduction formula to find the sterilization dose:**

The sterilization dose (D_st) is related to the verification dose through the dose-audit correction:

$$
D_{\text{st}} = D_{\text{ver}} + \left[ \log_{10}(\text{Pre-sterilization Bioburden}) \times k \right]
$$

Where the standard correction factor **k ≈ 2.0 kGy per log increase** above the bioburden used in the dose map.

**Step 2 — If the pre-sterilization bioburden increases from 1.5 to 2.5 log CFU:**

$$
D_{\text{st}} = 5.7 + \left[ (2.5 - 1.5) \times 2.0 \right]
$$

$$
D_{\text{st}} = 5.7 + 2.0 = 7.7 \text{ kGy}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A sterilization dose of **7.7 kGy** is required to maintain the SAL of 10⁻⁶ when the bioburden doubles. This is a *typical KTU 14-mark derivation* — examiners award 4 marks for the formula, 4 for substitution, 4 for the answer, and 2 for the units + interpretation.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Process Capability for a Syringe Diameter

A syringe manufacturer measures the **inner diameter of a 5 mL syringe barrel**. Specifications:

- **USL** = 12.05 mm
- **LSL** = 11.95 mm
- Sample mean $\mu = 11.99$ mm
- Sample standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute Cp (process potential):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.95}{6 \times 0.012}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cpk (process performance with shift):**

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{11.99 - 11.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.04}{0.036} = 1.111
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.99}{0.036} = \frac{0.06}{0.036} = 1.667
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.111,\ 1.667) = 1.111
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (KTU model answer):**

- $C_p = 1.389 > 1.33$ → process is **capable** (spread is small enough).
- $C_{pk} = 1.111 < 1.33$ → process is **not centered**; the mean (11.99) is shifted towards the LSL.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process to $\mu = 12.00$ mm. The new Cpk would be 1.389, well above the medical-device industry threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU love this problem?** It tests (i) substitution skills, (ii) interpretation of process-capability indices, and (iii) connection to real biomedical QA — all in a single 7-mark sub-question.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Verification vs. Validation

| Attribute | Design Verification | Design Validation |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | Are we building the device right? | Are we building the right device? |
| **Input Used** | Design Inputs (specs) | User Needs / Intended Use |
| **Method** | Inspection, test, analysis | Clinical / simulated-use testing |
| **Sample Size** | Engineering prototypes | Production-equivalent units |
| **Stage** | Throughout design phase | After design transfer, before launch |
| **Standard** | FDA 21 CFR 820.30(f) | FDA 21 CFR 820.30(g) |
| **Example** | Tensile strength of suture ≥ 50 N | Suture handles safely in surgeon’s gloved hands |

### 3.4 Six-Sigma DPMO Computation Example

A pacemaker manufacturer inspects **5,000 units** over a month. The pacemaker has **250 critical CTQ opportunities per unit**. Defects observed = **42**.

**Step 1 — Compute total opportunities:**

$$
\text{Opportunities} = 5{,}000 \times 250 = 1{,}250{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
DPMO = \frac{42}{1{,}250{,}000} \times 10^6 = 33.6
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Sigma Level (using standard conversion table):**

DPMO ≈ 33.6 → **Sigma level ≈ 5.4σ** (a near-world-class process).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Design Control Waterfall (FDA 21 CFR 820.30)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userNeed["USER NEEDS AND INTENDED USE"] --> designInp["DESIGN INPUTS REQUIREMENTS"]
    designInp --> designOut["DESIGN OUTPUTS SPECIFICATIONS"]
    designOut --> designRev["FORMAL DESIGN REVIEW"]
    designRev --> verif["DESIGN VERIFICATION"]
    verif --> valid["DESIGN VALIDATION"]
    valid --> transfer["DESIGN TRANSFER TO MANUFACTURE"]
    transfer --> change["DESIGN CHANGES CHANGE CONTROL"]
    change --> feedback["POST MARKET SURVEILLANCE"]
    feedback --> userNeed
    verif -->|Fail| designOut
    valid -->|Fail| designInp
```

### 4.2 Quality Management System Architecture (ISO 13485)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mgmt["MANAGEMENT RESPONSIBILITY"] --> qms["QUALITY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM"]
    qms --> res["RESOURCE MANAGEMENT"]
    res --> product["PRODUCT REALIZATION"]
    product --> meas["MEASUREMENT ANALYSIS IMPROVEMENT"]
    meas --> mgmt
    product --> designCtrl["DESIGN CONTROLS"]
    product --> purch["PURCHASING CONTROLS"]
    product --> prodCtrl["PRODUCTION CONTROLS"]
    designCtrl --> verif2["VERIFICATION"]
    designCtrl --> valid2["VALIDATION"]
    designCtrl --> transfer2["DESIGN TRANSFER"]
```

### 4.3 Risk Management Process (ISO 14971)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    riskA["RISK ANALYSIS"] --> riskE["RISK EVALUATION"]
    riskE --> riskC["RISK CONTROL"]
    riskC --> riskAccept["RESIDUAL RISK EVALUATION"]
    riskAccept --> riskMgmt["RISK MANAGEMENT REPORT"]
    riskMgmt --> postProd["POST PRODUCTION INFORMATION"]
    postProd --> riskA
    riskC -->|AFAP| verifyRC["VERIFICATION OF RISK CONTROL"]
    verifyRC --> riskAccept
```

### 4.4 Sterilization Validation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bioburden["BIOBURDEN ESTIMATION"] --> dosemap["DOSE MAPPING"]
    dosemap --> doseSet["STERILIZATION DOSE SET"]
    doseSet --> sal["SAL 10 TO 6 VERIFICATION"]
    sal --> dosAudit["QUARTERLY DOSE AUDITING"]
    dosAudit --> bioburden
    dosAudit --> release["STERILIZATION RELEASE DECISION"]
```

### 4.5 Manufacturing Process Flow for an Implantable Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawMat["RAW MATERIAL INSPECTION"] --> fab["FABRICATION CNC OR 3D PRINT"]
    fab --> clean["CLEANING PASSIVATION"]
    clean --> pkg["PRIMARY PACKAGING"]
    pkg --> ster["STERILIZATION GAMMA OR ETO"]
    ster --> lab["LABELING SECONDARY PACK"]
    lab --> release2["FINAL QA RELEASE"]
    release2 --> distrib["DISTRIBUTION TO HOSPITAL"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?** Each flowchart corresponds to a *specific clause* of a *specific standard* — the level of detail examiners expect in a KTU 2024 design question. Memorize the *flow direction* and *gate names*; that alone is worth 4–5 marks on a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
*Define Design Verification and Design Validation. State one biomedical example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Design Verification (1 mark):** Confirmation by examination and objective evidence that design outputs meet design inputs (per ISO 13485 Clause 8.3.5 / FDA 21 CFR 820.30). It answers *"Did we build the device right?"*
- **Design Validation (1 mark):** Confirmation that the device meets user needs and intended use. It answers *"Did we build the right device?"*
- **Example (1 mark):** *Verification:* Tensile test on a surgical suture confirms it withstands ≥ 50 N as specified. *Validation:* A simulated-use study on cadaveric tissue confirms the suture is ergonomically usable by surgeons and performs its intended wound-closure function.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*List the four classes of sterility assurance methods, and state the standard Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) for terminally sterilized medical devices.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Physical methods** — e.g., dry heat, steam autoclave (1 mark)
2. **Chemical methods** — e.g., EtO, H₂O₂ plasma (1 mark)
3. **Radiation methods** — e.g., gamma, e-beam (1 mark)
   - Note: Item 1 + 2 + 3 = 3 marks. The SAL **10⁻⁶** is part of the explanation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Compactly fitted 3-mark answer:** The standard SAL is **10⁻⁶**, i.e., ≤ 1 viable microorganism per 1,000,000 sterilized units.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> Common reasons students *lose marks* in Module 3 design questions:
> 1. **Conflating Verification and Validation** — always cite *which* standard clause you are quoting.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in Cp, Cpk, and D-value calculations — this alone costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why"** — examiners want the *clinical/bioengineering rationale*, not just arithmetic.
> 4. **Generic answers** — always anchor the response to a *biomedical example* (infusion pump, pacemaker, stent, syringe, etc.).
> 5. **Omitting DHF/DMR/DHR** — these are *favorite* 2-mark quick-fires.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the FDA design-control lifecycle for a medical device. With a neat block diagram, describe the sequence from User Needs to Design Transfer, and clearly differentiate **Verification** from **Validation**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A syringe manufacturer produces 5 mL syringes with inner diameter specification LSL = 11.95 mm, USL = 12.05 mm. After production, the sample mean is $\mu = 12.005$ mm and standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.015$ mm. Calculate Cp, Cpk, and comment on whether the process is capable and centered. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part B Q(a) (7 marks):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The FDA Design Control regulation (21 CFR 820.30) and ISO 13485 Clause 8.3 mandate a structured design lifecycle for all Class II/III/active Class I devices.

2. **Stages (4 marks — 0.5 each):**
   - **User Needs & Intended Use** → derive from clinical observations, marketing, and risk analysis.
   - **Design Inputs** → quantitative and qualitative requirements.
   - **Design Outputs** → drawings, BOM, software, sterilization SOP.
   - **Design Review** → formal, multi-disciplinary, documented.
   - **Verification** → confirms outputs meet inputs (*building right*).
   - **Validation** → confirms user need is met under simulated/actual use (*building the right device*).
   - **Design Transfer** → moves design to manufacturing (line setup, work instructions, tooling).
   - **Design Changes** → controlled under change-control procedure.

3. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to *Section 4.1* of these notes — the design-control waterfall.

4. **Verification vs. Validation table (1 mark):** Refer to *Section 3.3* of these notes.

---

**Model Solution — Part B Q(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cp Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 11.95}{6 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.10}{0.090} = 1.111
$$

**Step 2 — Cpk Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.005 - 11.95}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.055}{0.045} = 1.222
$$

$$
C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{12.05 - 12.005}{0.045} = 1.000
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.222,\ 1.000) = 1.000
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):**
- $C_p = 1.111 < 1.33$ → the *spread* of the process is **not capable**; the natural process width is too wide.
- $C_{pk} = 1.000 < 1.33$ → the process is **not centered** (mean shifted towards the USL by 0.045 mm).
- **Conclusion:** The manufacturing line fails the medical-device Cpk threshold (≥ 1.33). Corrective action: (i) **reduce variability** (replace worn tooling, tighter CNC feeds), and (ii) **re-center** the process to $\mu = 12.00$ mm. After both actions, the process would be acceptable for FDA submission.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Modified]**

**(a)** Describe the **Quality Management System (QMS)** requirements of **ISO 13485:2016** for a medical-device manufacturer. Mention the key clauses and their significance. (7 marks)

**(b)** A gamma sterilization facility processes **2,500 units of a single-use catheter** per batch. The D-value of *Bacillus pumilus* (reference organism) is **2.1 kGy** at the sterilization temperature. If the initial bioburden is **10⁵ CFU/unit**, calculate the **sterilization dose required to achieve a SAL of 10⁻⁶**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part B Q(a) — Question B (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** ISO 13485:2016 is the globally recognized QMS standard for medical devices; it is mandatory for CE marking in the EU and accepted worldwide.

**Key Clauses (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Clause 4 — Quality Management System:** Defines scope, documentation requirements, and the need for a quality manual.
2. **Clause 5 — Management Responsibility:** Top management must demonstrate commitment, define quality policy, and conduct management review.
3. **Clause 6 — Resource Management:** Provision of human resources, infrastructure, and work environment to achieve conformity.
4. **Clause 7 — Product Realization:** Includes planning, design and development (Clause 7.3), purchasing, production, and service provision.
5. **Clause 8 — Measurement, Analysis & Improvement:** Monitoring, measurement of QMS performance, control of nonconforming product, corrective/preventive action, and continual improvement.

**Significance (1 mark):** ISO 13485 is risk-based, process-oriented, and integrates with **ISO 14971** (risk management). It enables global market access (EU, Canada, Japan, Australia) and is a prerequisite for **MDR 2017/745** compliance.

---

**Model Solution — Part B Q(b) — Question B (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N_0 = 10^5$ CFU/unit (initial bioburden)
- $D = 2.1$ kGy
- $SAL = 10^{-6}$ → final count $N_t \leq 10^{-6}$ CFU/unit

**Step 1 — Required log reduction (2 marks):**

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^5}{10^{-6}}\right) = \log_{10}(10^{11}) = 11
$$

**Step 2 — Sterilization dose (3 marks):**

$$
D_{\text{ster}} = D \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N_t}\right) = 2.1 \times 11 = 23.1 \text{ kGy}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical interpretation (2 marks):**
- A dose of **23.1 kGy** is **high**; many polymer-based catheters would degrade.
- **Real-world action:** Use a *lower* bioburden (controlled cleanroom manufacturing to bring $N_0$ to $10^2$), then:
$$
D_{\text{ster}} = 2.1 \times \log_{10}(10^{2}/10^{-6}) = 2.1 \times 8 = 16.8 \text{ kGy}
$$
- This illustrates the **bioburden control → lower dose → material-friendly sterilization** principle, which is a key KTU 2024 takeaway.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3)**

- [ ] **Design Control = User Needs → Design Inputs → Design Outputs → Review → Verification → Validation → Transfer → Changes** (memorize the *exact order*).
- [ ] **Verification** confirms *design outputs* meet *design inputs* (engineering test).
- [ ] **Validation** confirms the *device* meets *user needs* (clinical/simulated use).
- [ ] **Three key records:** **DHF** (design history), **DMR** (device master), **DHR** (device history of a batch).
- [ ] **ISO 13485:2016** is the global QMS standard; **21 CFR 820** is the U.S. QSR; both are *legally binding* on manufacturers.
- [ ] **ISO 14971** governs the **risk management process** — analysis → evaluation → control → residual risk → post-production.
- [ ] **Sterilization SAL = 10⁻⁶**; **EtO, gamma, autoclave, H₂O₂ plasma** are the four main methods.
- [ ] **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the *medical-device industry threshold*; values < 1.33 are *not* acceptable.
- [ ] **DPMO = Defects / (Units × Opportunities) × 10⁶** — used in Six-Sigma.
- [ ] **D-value** = time (or dose) to reduce microbial population by **90 %** (1 log).
- [ ] **Sterilization dose formula:** $D_{\text{ster}} = D \times \log_{10}(N_0/N_t)$.
- [ ] **Process capability** is improved by *reducing variability* (Cp) and *re-centering* (Cpk).
- [ ] **Risk control hierarchy:** *Inherent safety → Protective measures → Information for safety* (ISO 14971).
- [ ] **Biocompatibility testing** is governed by **ISO 10993**, with the *material* and *contact duration* dictating the test panel.
- [ ] **Design Transfer** must include **work instructions, tooling specs, training, and process validations** (IQ, OQ, PQ).
- [ ] **Change Control** is mandatory; every design change must be reviewed, approved, and documented.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audits
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Audits

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Audit**, in the context of biomedical device design and quality management, is a **systematic, independent, and documented examination** of an organization's quality system, design controls, manufacturing processes, and regulatory compliance to verify conformity with predetermined criteria such as **21 CFR Part 820 (QSR)**, **ISO 13485:2016**, **MDR 2017/745**, or **ISO 14971**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An audit is a planned, independent, and documented assessment conducted to determine whether activities and related results conform to planned arrangements, and whether these arrangements are implemented effectively and are suitable to achieve the organization's quality policy and objectives (per **ISO 19011:2018**).

The audit is the **backbone of regulatory compliance** in the medical device industry, ensuring that devices reaching patients are **safe, effective, and manufactured under controlled conditions**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **driving license examiner**:

- **Before the test (Pre-audit):** The driving school has a syllabus (Quality System), teachers (procedures), cars (equipment), and students (personnel).
- **During the test (Audit):** The examiner (auditor) checks if the school's teaching matches the official RTO syllabus (regulations), observes a real driving lesson (process verification), and checks student records (documentation).
- **Result (Audit Report):** The examiner issues a pass or lists deficiencies (non-conformities) that must be corrected before licensing.

**Audits in biomedical devices work the same way** — the auditor (FDA inspector / Notified Body / internal auditor) verifies whether the manufacturer's quality system, design controls, and production processes comply with regulatory standards. A *failed* audit means the company cannot sell its device, just as a student without a license cannot legally drive.

## 1.3 Why Audits are Critical in Biomedical Device Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reasons for Audits:**
> 1. **Patient Safety** — Prevent defective devices from harming patients.
> 2. **Regulatory Compliance** — Mandatory under FDA, EU MDR, and ISO 13485.
> 3. **Market Access** — No audit clearance = No CE marking / 510(k) / PMA approval.
> 4. **Continuous Improvement** — Identifies gaps in QMS for Kaizen/PDCA cycles.
> 5. **Legal Protection** — Demonstrates **due diligence** in case of litigation.
> 6. **Risk Reduction** — Aligns with **ISO 14971** risk management framework.

## 1.4 Types of Auditors

| Auditor Type | Role & Authority |
|--------------|------------------|
| **Internal Auditor** | Company employee; checks QMS conformance; reports to Management Representative. |
| **External Auditor (Notified Body)** | Third-party accredited body (e.g., TÜV SÜD, BSI); grants ISO 13485 / CE certification. |
| **Regulatory Auditor (FDA Investigator)** | Government official; enforces 21 CFR Part 820; can issue **Form 483** and **Warning Letters**. |
| **Supplier Auditor** | Audits vendors of components, raw materials, or contract manufacturers (per 21 CFR 820.50). |
| **Certification Body Auditor** | Conducts certification audits (Stage 1 & Stage 2) for ISO 13485. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Audits (Detailed)

Audits in biomedical device organizations are classified along **multiple dimensions**:

### 2.1.1 By Source of Auditor
- **First-Party Audit (Internal Audit):** Conducted by the organization on itself.
- **Second-Party Audit:** Conducted by a customer or on behalf of a customer.
- **Third-Party Audit:** Conducted by an independent certification body or regulatory authority.

### 2.1.2 By Purpose
- **System Audit:** Evaluates the entire Quality Management System (QMS).
- **Process Audit:** Evaluates a specific process (e.g., sterilization, software validation).
- **Product Audit:** Evaluates a specific product's conformity to specifications.
- **Compliance Audit:** Verifies adherence to specific regulatory requirements.

### 2.1.3 By Timing
- **Pre-Market Audit:** Before product launch (e.g., 510(k) pre-submission audit).
- **Routine/Surveillance Audit:** Periodic (annual or biannual) by certification bodies.
- **For-Cause Audit:** Triggered by complaints, recalls, or adverse events.
- **Follow-Up Audit:** Verifies closure of previous non-conformities.

## 2.2 The Audit Process (Per ISO 19011:2018)

The audit process follows a **structured 6-stage lifecycle**:

1. **Initiation of the Audit**
   - Define audit objectives, scope, and criteria.
   - Appoint the **Lead Auditor** and audit team.
   - Determine audit feasibility and resource requirements.

2. **Preparation of Audit Activities**
   - Perform **document review** (Quality Manual, SOPs, DMR, DHR).
   - Prepare **audit plan** (dates, departments, auditors).
   - Prepare **checklists** and working documents.
   - Distribute audit plan to **auditees** in advance.

3. **Conducting the Audit**
   - **Opening Meeting** — Confirm scope, introduce team, set communication protocols.
   - **Information Collection** — Interviews, observation, document sampling.
   - **Audit Findings Generation** — Identify **conformities** and **non-conformities**.
   - **Closing Meeting** — Present findings to management.

4. **Preparing the Audit Report
   - Document findings objectively with **objective evidence**.
   - Classify non-conformities (Critical / Major / Minor).
   - Issue report within agreed timeframe.

5. **Completing the Audit
   - Distribute report to relevant parties.
   - Retain audit records per document control procedure.

6. **Audit Follow-Up
   - Verify implementation of **Corrective Actions (CA)**.
   - Conduct **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** for non-conformities.
   - Close out findings with documented evidence.

## 2.3 Classification of Audit Findings (Non-Conformities)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Table:**

| Classification | Definition | Impact | Example |
|----------------|------------|--------|---------|
| **Critical (Class I)** | Imminent danger to patient safety; gross regulatory violation | Product recall, **Warning Letter**, **Consent Decree**, plant shutdown | Sterilization process completely unvalidated; software safety controls bypassed |
| **Major (Class II)** | Significant non-compliance; could lead to product defect | Form 483 observation, certification suspension, re-audit required | No CAPA procedure; incomplete design history file (DHF) |
| **Minor (Class III / Observation)** | Isolated lapse; low immediate risk | Documented as observation; addressed in management review | Missing signature on a single training record |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU board exams, students often confuse the classification thresholds. **Critical = patient harm likely**, **Major = systemic failure**, **Minor = isolated incident**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (For Audit Sampling Calculations)

Although audits are qualitative, certain quantitative aspects (e.g., **audit sample size determination**) involve mathematical formulas used in auditor training.

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **Audit Sample Size (n)** | $$n = \frac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot e^2}$$ | Where $N$ = population size, $e$ = margin of error (typically 0.05). |
| **Sampling Interval (k)** | $$k = \frac{N}{n}$$ | Systematic sampling interval. |
| **Audit Duration (D)** | $$D = T_{prep} + T_{on-site} + T_{report}$$ | Total auditor time = preparation + on-site + reporting hours. |
| **Audit Coverage Ratio (ACR)** | $$ACR = \frac{\text{Processes Audited}}{\text{Total Critical Processes}} \times 100\%$$ | Percentage of critical processes covered in audit scope. |
| **Non-Conformity Density (NCD)** | $$NCD = \frac{\text{Number of NCs}}{\text{Audit Hours}}$$ | Concentration of findings per unit time. |
| **Closure Effectiveness Index (CEI)** | $$CEI = \frac{\text{NCs Effectively Closed}}{\text{Total NCs Raised}} \times 100\%$$ | Measures CAPA effectiveness. |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are presented using LaTeX. Note the deliberate use of `\vert` (e.g., `$\vert x \vert$`) is avoided here in favor of explicit contextual variables to prevent markdown table parsing conflicts.

## 2.5 Regulatory Framework Reference Table

| Standard / Regulation | Scope | Audit Authority |
|------------------------|-------|------------------|
| **21 CFR Part 820 (QSR)** | US Quality System Regulation for medical devices | FDA (CDRH) |
| **ISO 13485:2016** | International QMS standard for medical devices | Notified Bodies |
| **ISO 14971:2019** | Risk management for medical devices | Audit checkpoints |
| **EU MDR 2017/745** | European Medical Device Regulation | Notified Bodies |
| **MDSAP** | Medical Device Single Audit Program (5 countries) | Authorised Auditing Organisations (AO) |
| **ISO 19011:2018** | Guidelines for auditing management systems | Audit methodology |
| **21 CFR Part 11** | Electronic records and signatures | FDA (for digital QMS) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pre-Approval Inspections (PAI):** Conducted by FDA before PMA approval — audit must pass to market device.
- **Mock FDA Audits:** Internal rehearsals conducted 6–9 months before expected inspection.
- **Supplier Audits:** Mandatory per **21 CFR 820.50 (Purchasing Controls)** for any supplier providing critical components (e.g., sterile barrier, software).
- **Post-Market Audits:** Triggered by **MDR (Medical Device Reporting)** adverse events or recalls.
- **Design Control Audits:** Verify that **21 CFR 820.30** (Design Controls) and **IEC 62304** (Medical Device Software) are followed.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Audit Process Walkthrough

> Since "Audits" is largely a **regulatory and procedural topic**, this section presents a **structured analytical walkthrough** of the audit lifecycle, including worked examples for sample-size calculations and classification decision logic. The rigor mirrors KTU's expectation for design-of-biomedical-devices graduates to quantitatively support quality decisions.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Determining Audit Sample Size

**Problem (KTU Style):** A biomedical device company has **400 active SOPs** in its Quality Management System. As Lead Auditor, you wish to review a statistically representative sample with a **5% margin of error**. Calculate the required sample size using the standard audit sampling formula.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify Variables**

$$N = 400 \quad (\text{total SOPs in the QMS})$$

$$e = 0.05 \quad (\text{acceptable margin of error})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Audit Sample Size Formula**

$$n = \frac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot e^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$n = \frac{400}{1 + (400-1) \cdot (0.05)^2}$$

$$n = \frac{400}{1 + (399) \cdot (0.0025)}$$

$$n = \frac{400}{1 + 0.9975}$$

$$n = \frac{400}{1.9975}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Final Result**

$$n \approx 200.25$$

**Step 5 — Round Up (Statistical Convention for Sampling)**

Since you cannot audit a fractional SOP, **round up to the next whole number**:

$$n = 201 \text{ SOPs}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Sampling Interval (k)**

$$k = \frac{N}{n} = \frac{400}{201} \approx 1.99 \approx 2$$

**Interpretation:** The auditor should review **every 2nd SOP** (i.e., SOP #2, #4, #6, …) until **201 SOPs** are sampled. This gives a **95% confidence level** that the sample represents the population of 400 SOPs.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Non-Conformity Density and Closure Effectiveness

**Problem:** During a 3-day audit of a Class III implantable device manufacturer, the audit team spent **24 audit hours** on-site and raised **9 non-conformities** (3 Critical, 4 Major, 2 Minor). After 90 days, **7 of 9 NCs** were effectively closed with verified corrective actions. Calculate:
1. Non-Conformity Density (NCD)
2. Closure Effectiveness Index (CEI)

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Non-Conformity Density**

$$NCD = \frac{\text{Number of NCs}}{\text{Audit Hours}}$$

$$NCD = \frac{9}{24} = 0.375 \text{ NCs/hour}$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **1 non-conformity every 2.67 hours** — a high density, indicating systemic QMS weaknesses.

**Step 2 — Compute Closure Effectiveness Index**

$$CEI = \frac{\text{NCs Effectively Closed}}{\text{Total NCs Raised}} \times 100\%$$

$$CEI = \frac{7}{9} \times 100\% = 77.78\%$$

**Interpretation:** A **CEI of 77.78%** is below the regulatory expectation of **≥ 90% within 90 days**. The company must escalate the 2 unresolved NCs to **Management Review** and may face an **FDA Follow-Up Audit**.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix: Classifying an Audit Finding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Decision Logic:** Use this matrix to classify findings correctly.

```
START
  │
  ▼
Q1: Is there direct evidence of patient harm OR is the device unsafe?
  │ YES → CRITICAL (Class I)
  │ NO  → ▼
  ▼
Q2: Is the non-conformity systemic (affects multiple products/processes)?
  │ YES → MAJOR (Class II)
  │ NO  → ▼
  ▼
Q3: Is it an isolated incident with low immediate risk?
  │ YES → MINOR (Class III / Observation)
  │ NO  → ESCALATE TO LEAD AUDITOR
```

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Audit Duration Planning

**Problem:** A Class III medical device company has **6 departments** (R&D, Production, QC, QA, Regulatory, Supply Chain) and **18 critical processes**. The audit lead estimates the following time requirements:
- Preparation: **12 hours**
- On-site audit: **3 days × 8 hours = 24 hours**
- Report writing: **8 hours**

Calculate total audit duration, ACR, and NCD if 27 findings are raised.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Total Audit Duration (D)**

$$D = T_{prep} + T_{on-site} + T_{report}$$

$$D = 12 + 24 + 8 = 44 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Audit Coverage Ratio (ACR)**

If the audit covered all **18 critical processes**:

$$ACR = \frac{18}{18} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Non-Conformity Density (NCD)**

$$NCD = \frac{27}{24} = 1.125 \text{ NCs/hour}$$

**Interpretation:** An NCD of 1.125 is **unusually high** (industry average ~0.3), suggesting a **severely compromised QMS** requiring immediate management intervention.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Calculation Type | Input | Output | Engineering Decision |
|------------------|-------|--------|----------------------|
| **Sample Size (n)** | $N = 400$, $e = 0.05$ | $n = 201$ | Audit every 2nd SOP |
| **NCD** | 9 NCs, 24 hrs | $0.375$ NCs/hr | Investigate root causes |
| **CEI** | 7 closed / 9 raised | $77.78\%$ | Escalate to management review |
| **Audit Duration (D)** | 12 + 24 + 8 hrs | $44$ hrs | Schedule resource allocation |
| **ACR** | 18/18 processes | $100\%$ | Full scope coverage achieved |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Audit Lifecycle Flow (Per ISO 19011)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **6-stage audit lifecycle** as defined in **ISO 19011:2018**, applied to a biomedical device QMS context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: Initiate Audit]) --> A1[Define Audit Objectives and Scope]
    A1 --> A2[Select Lead Auditor and Team]
    A2 --> A3{Is Audit Feasible?}
    A3 -- NO --> A3A[Reject and Reschedule]
    A3A --> A0
    A3 -- YES --> B1[Perform Document Review - QMS, SOPs, DHF, DHR]
    B1 --> B2[Prepare Audit Plan and Checklists]
    B2 --> B3[Distribute Plan to Auditees]
    B3 --> C1[Opening Meeting with Top Management]
    C1 --> C2[Conduct Interviews and Observations]
    C2 --> C3[Collect Objective Evidence]
    C3 --> C4[Identify Conformities and Non-Conformities]
    C4 --> C5[Classify Findings - Critical, Major, Minor]
    C5 --> C6[Closing Meeting - Present Findings]
    C6 --> D1[Draft Audit Report]
    D1 --> D2[Issue Final Report to Management]
    D2 --> E1[Distribute and Archive Report]
    E1 --> F1[Initiate Corrective Actions for NCs]
    F1 --> F2[Perform Root Cause Analysis - 5 Whys, Fishbone]
    F2 --> F3[Verify Effectiveness of CAPA]
    F3 --> F4{NCs Closed?}
    F4 -- NO --> F1
    F4 -- YES --> F5([END: Audit Closure])
    style A0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style F5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style A3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C5 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style F4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 FDA Inspection Process — Specific Schematic

The diagram below shows the **FDA inspection flow** (using **Quality System Inspection Technique — QSIT** approach), which is a high-yield topic for KTU exams covering US regulatory frameworks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1([FDA Receives Notification or Triggers Inspection]) --> N2{Type of Inspection}
    N2 -- Pre-Approval --> P1[Pre-Approval Inspection - PAI]
    N2 -- Routine --> P2[Periodic / Surveillance Inspection]
    N2 -- For-Cause --> P3[For-Cause Inspection - Triggered by MDR or Recall]
    N2 -- Follow-Up --> P4[Follow-Up - Verify Prior 483 Closure]
    P1 --> Q1[Cover 4 QSIT Subsystems: Management Controls, Design Controls, CAPA, Production and Process Controls]
    P2 --> Q1
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q1
    Q1 --> Q2[Investigator Issues Form 483 if NCs Found]
    Q2 --> Q3{Company Response Adequate?}
    Q3 -- YES --> Q4[Inspection Closed - No Further Action]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q5[FDA Issues Warning Letter]
    Q5 --> Q6{Compliance Achieved within 15 Days?}
    Q6 -- NO --> Q7[Escalation: Import Alert, Seizure, Consent Decree, Injunction]
    Q6 -- YES --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q8([END])
    Q7 --> Q8
    style N1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style Q5 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style Q7 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#FFF,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    style Q8 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> CAPA is the most heavily audited process per **21 CFR 820.100** and **ISO 13485:2016 Clause 8.5.2/8.5.3**. The diagram below maps the complete CAPA lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K0([CAPA Trigger - Audit Finding, Complaint, or Trend]) --> K1[Document the Problem with Objective Evidence]
    K1 --> K2[Containment Action - Stop Further NCs]
    K2 --> K3[Root Cause Analysis - 5 Whys, Ishikawa, FMEA]
    K3 --> K4{Is Root Cause Identified?}
    K4 -- NO --> K3
    K4 -- YES --> K5[Define Corrective Action Plan]
    K5 --> K6[Implement Corrective Action]
    K6 --> K7[Verify Effectiveness - Measure Results]
    K7 --> K8{Is CA Effective?}
    K8 -- NO --> K5
    K8 -- YES --> K9[Preventive Action - Apply to Similar Processes]
    K9 --> K10[Update Risk Management File - ISO 14971]
    K10 --> K11[Management Review Input]
    K11 --> K12([CAPA Closure and Documentation])
    style K0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style K3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style K8 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style K12 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Non-Conformity Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0([Non-Conformity Detected]) --> M1{Is there direct patient harm risk or device unsafe in use?}
    M1 -- YES --> M2[CRITICAL - Class I - FDA Warning Letter, Recall, Plant Shutdown]
    M1 -- NO --> M3{Is the issue systemic affecting multiple products or processes?}
    M3 -- YES --> M4[MAJOR - Class II - Form 483 Observation, Re-Audit Required]
    M3 -- NO --> M5{Is it an isolated lapse with low immediate risk?}
    M5 -- YES --> M6[MINOR - Class III - Observation, Documented in Report]
    M5 -- NO --> M7[Escalate to Lead Auditor for Disposition]
    M2 --> M8([Issue Audit Report and Trigger CAPA])
    M4 --> M8
    M6 --> M8
    M7 --> M8
    style M2 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF,stroke-width:2px
    style M4 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style M6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style M0 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---
