> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** European Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**European Standards** in the context of biomedical device design refer to the harmonized technical specifications, regulatory frameworks, and conformity assessment procedures that a medical device must satisfy before it can be legally placed on the market within the European Economic Area (EEA). The cornerstone of this regulatory system is **Regulation (EU) 2017/745**, commonly known as the **European Union Medical Device Regulation (EU MDR)**, which superseded the legacy Directives 90/385/EEC (AIMD) and 93/42/EEC (MDD) on **26 May 2021**.

Complementing this legal framework are the **harmonized standards** published by the three European Standards Organizations:
- **CEN** (European Committee for Standardization)
- **CENELEC** (European Committee for Electrotechnical Standardization)
- **ETSI** (European Telecommunications Standards Institute)

When a manufacturer demonstrates compliance with these harmonized standards, they receive a **presumption of conformity** with the corresponding Essential Requirements of the EU MDR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The European Standards framework is mandatory knowledge for Module 4 because biomedical devices exported to or designed for the European market must legally comply with EU MDR 2017/745, the In Vitro Diagnostic Regulation (IVDR 2017/746), and the Medical Devices Directive (legacy MDD 93/42/EEC) for transition devices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the European Standards system like a **driver's license test for medical devices**. Before you can legally drive (sell a device) in Europe, your vehicle (the device) must pass a series of rigorous checks: a mechanical fitness test (safety), an emissions check (biocompatibility), a road behavior test (clinical performance), and proper documentation (Technical File). The EU MDR is the "driving test rules," CE Marking is the "license plate," and the Notified Body acts as the "official testing inspector" who verifies everything. Just as a license plate issued in one EU country is recognized across all EU nations, a single CE Mark allows free movement of the device across all **27 EU member states** plus EEA countries (Norway, Iceland, Liechtenstein).

### 1.3 Key Definitions and Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **CE Marking** | Mandatory conformity mark indicating compliance with EU legislation |
| **Notified Body** | Independent, accredited organization authorized to perform conformity assessments |
| **Harmonized Standard** | A European standard adopted following a mandate from the European Commission |
| **Declaration of Conformity (DoC)** | Document where manufacturer declares the device meets all applicable EU legislation |
| **U** (Unique Device Identifier) | Alphanumeric code for unambiguous device identification |
| **EUDAMED** | European Database on Medical Devices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Statistic:** Under the EU MDR, **Class Is, Im, Ir, IIa, IIb, and III devices** all require Notified Body involvement for certification, a substantial expansion from the legacy MDD framework.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The EU MDR 2017/745 Framework - Structural Breakdown

The EU MDR is organized into **10 Chapters and 123 Articles** plus **17 Annexes**. The logical workflow for a manufacturer seeking CE Marking is:

- **Step 1: Device Classification** — Determine the risk class (I, IIa, IIb, III) using **Annex VIII Rule Set**.
- **Step 2: Conformity Assessment Route Selection** — Choose between Annex IX (Quality Management System + Technical Documentation Assessment), Annex X (Type Examination + Production Quality Assurance), or Annex XI (Production Quality Assurance).
- **Step 3: Compilation of Technical Documentation** — Build the **Technical File (TF)** per Annexes II and III.
- **Step 4: Clinical Evaluation** — Conduct a Clinical Evaluation per **Article 61** and **Annex XIV**, supported by Clinical Investigation data under **ISO 14155**.
- **Step 5: Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)** — Establish PMS system, Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR), and Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF) per **Article 84**.
- **Step 6: UDI Assignment & EUDAMED Registration** — Assign UDI-DI (device identifier) and UDI-PI (production identifier) and register in EUDAMED.
- **Step 7: Declaration of Conformity & CE Marking** — Issue DoC and affix CE Mark with Notified Body number (where applicable).

### 2.2 Risk-Based Classification Rules

The classification follows a **rule-based system** in Annex VIII, considering:
- Duration of contact (transient $\leq$ 60 min, short-term 60 min–30 days, long-term $>$ 30 days)
- Invasiveness (non-invasive, body orifice, surgically invasive, implantable)
- Active vs. non-active therapeutic/diagnostic devices
- Critical anatomical regions (central circulatory system, central nervous system)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Class Rule (non-invasive)** | Class I if transient use; else IIa, IIb, or III based on contact type | Categorical |
| **Invasive Duration Rule** | $\Delta t \leq 60\,min \Rightarrow$ Class I transient; $60\,min < \Delta t \leq 30\,days \Rightarrow$ short-term; $\Delta t > 30\,days \Rightarrow$ long-term | minutes/days |
| **Conformity Assessment Cost (approx.)** | $C_{total} = C_{QMS} + C_{TF} + C_{Audit} + C_{Clinical}$ | EUR |
| **Benefit-Risk Ratio** | $R_{BR} = \dfrac{B_{clinical}}{R_{residual}}$ where $R_{residual} \leq R_{acceptable}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Statistical Sample Size (Clinical)** | $n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}$ | Subjects |
| **Risk Acceptability** | $R = S \times P \times D$ where $S$ = Severity, $P$ = Probability, $D$ = Detectability | Risk score |
| **Transition Timeline** | MDD $\to$ MDR cut-off date = **26 May 2024** for legacy devices | Date |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In industry, understanding classification (e.g., a Class III implantable pacemaker vs. a Class I stethoscope) directly dictates the regulatory burden. A Class III device may incur certification costs of **EUR 200,000–500,000**, while a Class I self-certified device costs only the technical file preparation.

### 2.4 Harmonized Standards Architecture

The most critical harmonized standards for biomedical device design under the EU MDR include:

- **EN ISO 13485:2016** — Quality Management Systems for Medical Devices
- **EN ISO 14971:2019** — Application of Risk Management to Medical Devices
- **EN IEC 60601-1** — Medical Electrical Equipment Safety (the "60601 family")
- **EN ISO 10993 series** — Biological Evaluation of Medical Devices
- **EN ISO 14155:2020** — Clinical Investigation of Medical Devices
- **EN 62366-1:2015** — Application of Usability Engineering to Medical Devices
- **EN ISO 15223-1:2021** — Symbols for Medical Device Labeling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Harmonized"?** The European Commission publishes the official list of harmonized standards in the **Official Journal of the European Union (OJEU)**. Compliance confers a legal **presumption of conformity** — meaning if you follow EN ISO 14971 correctly, the regulator presumes you have met the relevant Essential Safety and Performance Requirements (Annex I).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Common Biomedical Devices

Because European Standards Module 4 involves regulatory logic rather than pure numerical calculation, the "derivation" here is a structured decision matrix showing how the EU MDR Annex VIII rules map real devices to risk classes.

| Device Example | Non-invasive? | Invasive Duration | Active? | Anatomical Contact | Rule Applied | **Final Class** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stethoscope | Yes (Rule 1) | Transient | No | Skin | Rule 1 | **Class I** |
| Surgical Gloves | Yes (transient) | Short-term | No | Body orifice/skin | Rule 5 | **Class I** |
| Contact Lens (long-term) | No | Long-term | No | Body orifice | Rule 5 | **Class IIa** |
| Insulin Pen | Yes | Transient | Active therapeutic | Skin | Rule 9, 12 | **Class IIa** |
| Blood Pressure Monitor | Yes | Transient | Active diagnostic | Skin | Rule 10 | **Class IIa** |
| Hip Implant | No | Long-term | No | Bone | Rule 8 | **Class III** |
| Pacemaker (implantable) | No | Long-term | Active therapeutic | Central circulatory | Rule 8, 22 | **Class III** |
| X-Ray Machine | Yes | Transient | Active diagnostic | External | Rule 10, 22 | **Class IIb** |
| Surgical Laser | Yes | Transient | Active therapeutic | External | Rule 9 | **Class IIb** |
| Sutures (absorbable) | No | Short-term | No | Internal tissue | Rule 7 | **Class IIb** |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Conformity Assessment Route Selection

The selection of the conformity assessment route is a deterministic decision flow. Let us derive the correct route for a hypothetical **Class IIb active therapeutic device** (e.g., a ventilator):

**Step 1 — Identify Risk Class:**

The ventilator is an **active therapeutic device** intended to administer or exchange energy. Per Annex VIII Rule 9, active therapeutic devices intended to administer or exchange energy in a potentially hazardous way to/from the body are **Class IIb**.

**Step 2 — Map Class to Required Routes:**

From the EU MDR framework, the manufacturer has two alternative options for Class IIb:

- **Option A (Annex IX):** Full Quality Management System audit + Technical Documentation Assessment by Notified Body.
- **Option B (Annex X + XI):** Type Examination (Annex X) + Production Quality Assurance (Annex XI) — sample-based.

**Step 3 — Apply Quality Management Foundation:**

$$
C_{route} = C_{QMS} \cup C_{TF} \cup C_{Clinical}
$$

where every route must include QMS certification under **EN ISO 13485**.

**Step 4 — Determine Notified Body Number:**

The Notified Body issues a unique 4-digit identification number (e.g., **CE 0123** for TÜV SÜD Product Service GmbH). This number must appear adjacent to the CE Mark on the device labeling.

**Step 5 — Conclusion for Ventilator:**

$$\boxed{\text{Class IIb Ventilator: Route = Annex IX (QMS + TDA) with NB 0123}}$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Risk Classification Decision Tree (Logical Derivation)

For a non-invasive transient device (Rule 1 of Annex VIII):

**Step 1:** Check duration of use.

$$
\Delta t \leq 60\,min \Rightarrow \text{Apply Rule 1, Class I}
$$

**Step 2:** If duration exceeds 60 minutes, check contact type (direct vs. indirect).

$$
60\,min < \Delta t \leq 30\,days \Rightarrow \text{Class I (long-term use exception per Rule 1)}
$$

**Step 3:** For devices that channel or store body liquids, escalate.

$$
\text{Channeling blood/Organs} \Rightarrow \text{Class IIa minimum}
$$

**Step 4:** For modifications of body fluids (e.g., dialyzer, oxygenator), escalate further.

$$
\text{Body fluid modification in closed loop} \Rightarrow \text{Class IIb}
$$

**Step 5:** For devices contacting the central nervous system or central circulatory system.

$$
\text{CNS/CCS contact} \Rightarrow \text{Class III (highest risk)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Insight:** Although the classification is categorical, it follows a **monotonic escalation principle** — once a device meets a higher-class criterion, it cannot be down-classified. This is mathematically analogous to a **partial order** in lattice theory, where Class I $<$ IIa $<$ IIb $<$ III.

### 3.4 Statistical Sample Size for Clinical Evaluation (KTU Quantitative Component)

For Post-Market Clinical Follow-up (PMCF) studies under the EU MDR, the minimum sample size for demonstrating performance is calculated as:

$$
n = \dfrac{Z_{\alpha/2}^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{E^{2}}
$$

where:
- $Z_{\alpha/2}$ = critical Z-value for confidence level (e.g., **1.96** for 95% CI)
- $p$ = expected proportion (e.g., clinical success rate = **0.85**)
- $E$ = margin of error (e.g., **0.05**)

**Numerical Evaluation for a Pacemaker PMCF Study:**

$$
n = \dfrac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot 0.85 \cdot (1 - 0.85)}{(0.05)^{2}}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{3.8416 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.15}{0.0025}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{3.8416 \cdot 0.1275}{0.0025}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{0.4898}{0.0025}
$$

$$
\boxed{n \approx 196\, \text{subjects}}
$$

This means at least **196 patients** must be enrolled in the PMCF study for statistical validity at 95% confidence with a $\pm$5% margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CE Marking Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Device Design Complete] --> B[Classify Device per Annex VIII]
    B --> C{Risk Class?}
    C -->|Class I Non-Sterile Non-Measuring| D[Self Certification]
    C -->|Class Is Im Ir| E[Notified Body Sterile or Measurement]
    C -->|Class IIa| F[Annex IX or XI Route]
    C -->|Class IIb| G[Annex IX or X plus XI Route]
    C -->|Class III| H[Annex IX Full TDA plus NB]
    D --> I[Prepare Technical File]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compile Clinical Evaluation Report]
    J --> K[Establish PMS and PMCF Plan]
    K --> L[Assign UDI DI and UDI PI]
    L --> M[Register in EUDAMED]
    M --> N[Issue Declaration of Conformity]
    N --> O[Affix CE Mark with NB Number]
    O --> P[Market Surveillance Ongoing]
```

### 4.2 EU MDR Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Regulatory Layer]
        R1[EU MDR 2017/745]
        R2[EU IVDR 2017/746]
    end
    subgraph L2[Harmonized Standards Layer]
        S1[EN ISO 13485 QMS]
        S2[EN ISO 14971 Risk]
        S3[EN IEC 60601 Safety]
        S4[EN ISO 10993 Biocompatibility]
        S5[EN ISO 14155 Clinical]
    end
    subgraph L3[Assessment Layer]
        A1[Notified Body]
        A2[Competent Authority]
        A3[EUDAMED Database]
    end
    subgraph L4[Manufacturer Output]
        O1[Technical File]
        O2[Declaration of Conformity]
        O3[CE Mark Affixed]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> M1[Market Access in EEA]
```

### 4.3 Risk-Based Conformity Assessment Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Patient Safety Priority] --> Q[Device Risk Classification]
    Q --> R1[Class I Low Risk]
    Q --> R2[Class IIa Low to Moderate]
    Q --> R3[Class IIb Moderate to High]
    Q --> R4[Class III High Risk]
    R1 --> S1[Self Declaration Only]
    R2 --> S2[NB Audit Required]
    R3 --> S3[NB plus TDA Required]
    R4 --> S4[NB plus Full Clinical plus Design Examination]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **CE Marking** and explain its significance under the EU MDR 2017/745.

**Model Answer:**

CE Marking is a mandatory conformity mark affixed to a medical device indicating that the device complies with all applicable European Union legislation, primarily the **Medical Device Regulation (EU) 2017/745**. Its significance includes:

- It is a **legal prerequisite** for placing a medical device on the EU/EEA market.
- It indicates the device has undergone the appropriate **conformity assessment procedure** based on its risk class.
- For Class Is, IIa, IIb, and III devices, the CE Mark must be accompanied by the **4-digit Notified Body identification number**.
- A single CE Mark allows **free movement** of the device across all 27 EU member states plus EEA countries.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a **Notified Body**? State its role in medical device certification under European Standards.

**Model Answer:**

A Notified Body is an **independent, third-party conformity assessment organization** designated by an EU member state and notified to the European Commission. Its role includes:

- Conducting **conformity assessments** (QMS audits, Technical Documentation Assessment, Type Examination).
- Issuing **certificates** required for CE Marking of Class Is, IIa, IIb, and III devices.
- Performing **periodic surveillance audits** to ensure continued compliance.
- Operating under the oversight of the **national Competent Authority** and the **Medical Device Coordination Group (MDCG)**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Classify the following biomedical devices according to the EU MDR 2017/745 risk-based classification system, justifying each with the relevant Annex VIII rule: **(7 marks)**
- (i) An implantable cardiac pacemaker
- (ii) A blood pressure monitoring cuff
- (iii) A surgical scalpel
- (iv) An absorbable suture
- (v) A class I non-sterile elastic bandage
- (vi) An MRI diagnostic scanner
- (vii) A dialysis machine

**(b)** Describe in detail the **conformity assessment routes** available under the EU MDR 2017/745 for a Class IIb active therapeutic device. Compare Annex IX and Annex X routes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Device Classification:**

| Device | Classification | Annex VIII Rule Applied | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Implantable cardiac pacemaker** | **Class III** | Rule 8 + Rule 22 | Active implantable device in direct contact with central circulatory system; intended to administer energy |
| **(ii) Blood pressure monitoring cuff** | **Class IIa** | Rule 10 | Active diagnostic device providing energy to patient; non-hazardous measurement |
| **(iii) Surgical scalpel** | **Class I** | Rule 6 | Surgically invasive transient instrument |
| **(iv) Absorbable suture** | **Class IIb** | Rule 7 | Surgically invasive short-term device intended to have biological effect on body |
| **(v) Elastic bandage (non-sterile)** | **Class I** | Rule 1 | Non-invasive transient use |
| **(vi) MRI diagnostic scanner** | **Class IIb** | Rule 22 | Active diagnostic device emitting ionizing or hazardous radiation |
| **(vii) Dialysis machine** | **Class IIb** | Rule 3 | Modifies composition of body fluids |

**Part (b) — Conformity Assessment Routes for Class IIb:**

Under the EU MDR 2017/745, the manufacturer of a Class IIb active therapeutic device has **two alternative conformity assessment routes**:

**Route 1 — Annex IX (Quality Management System + Technical Documentation Assessment):**

This route requires the manufacturer to establish and maintain a **full Quality Management System** certified by a Notified Body against **EN ISO 13485:2016**. Additionally, the Notified Body conducts a **Technical Documentation Assessment (TDA)** for each device category or representative sample, verifying compliance with Annex I Essential Safety and Performance Requirements. This route allows the manufacturer to use the same QMS across multiple devices and gives greater control over design changes (with notification procedures).

**Route 2 — Annex X + XI (Type Examination + Production Quality Assurance):**

This route is a **two-stage process**. Under **Annex X**, the Notified Body performs a **Type Examination** — examining a representative sample of the device for conformity with the Essential Requirements. Under **Annex XI**, the Notified Body audits the **production quality assurance system**, ensuring that manufactured devices conform to the approved type.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Annex IX | Annex X + XI |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | QMS + TDA | Type + Production QMS |
| **Sample Required** | No (sample-based TDA) | Yes (type sample) |
| **Design Change Control** | More flexible via notification | Stricter; requires re-type-examination |
| **Best For** | Continuous product lines | Limited production runs |
| **Cost Profile** | Lower per device, higher initial | Higher per device, lower initial |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently confuse **Annex IX with Annex X**. Remember: **Annex IX = QMS + Technical Documentation Assessment** (no type sample needed), while **Annex X = Type Examination** (a physical/sample-based review). Also, do NOT write "CE Certification" — the correct terminology is **"CE Marking"** (the device is marked, the certificate is issued). Marks are deducted for this error.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the structure of the **EU MDR 2017/745**, listing its key chapters and describing the **General Safety and Performance Requirements (Annex I)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **harmonized standards** in demonstrating conformity. List at least **five harmonized standards** relevant to biomedical device design with their primary purpose. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Question B:**

**Part (a) — EU MDR Structure:**

The EU MDR 2017/745 is organized into **10 Chapters and 17 Annexes**, comprising **123 Articles**. The key chapters are:

| Chapter | Content | Key Articles |
|---|---|---|
| **Chapter I** | Scope and Definitions | Articles 1–4 |
| **Chapter II** | Making Available on Market and Putting into Service | Articles 5–10 |
| **Chapter III** | Identification, Traceability, and Registration of Devices | Articles 27–34 |
| **Chapter IV** | Notified Bodies | Articles 35–48 |
| **Chapter V** | Classification and Conformity Assessment | Articles 51–60 |
| **Chapter VI** | Clinical Evaluation and Clinical Investigations | Articles 61–82 |
| **Chapter VII** | Post-Market Surveillance and Vigilance | Articles 83–92 |
| **Chapter VIII** | Governance and Cooperation | Articles 93–101 |
| **Chapter IX** | Confidentiality, Data Protection, and Penalties | Articles 102–113 |
| **Chapter X** | Final Provisions | Articles 114–123 |

**Annex I — General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR):**

Annex I replaces the legacy MDD "Essential Requirements" and expands from **13 requirements to 23 requirements**. Key GSPRs include:

- **GSPR 1:** Devices must achieve performance intended by manufacturer.
- **GSPR 2:** Risk management throughout lifecycle (per EN ISO 14971).
- **GSPR 3:** Risk/benefit analysis must favor benefits.
- **GSPR 4–6:** Risk reduction inherent in design, protective measures, and information for safety.
- **GSPR 10:** Must not compromise health and safety of patients, users, or others.
- **GSPR 23:** Labeling and information requirements.

**Part (b) — Harmonized Standards:**

**Role of Harmonized Standards:**
Harmonized standards are European standards developed by CEN, CENELEC, or ETSI following a **mandate from the European Commission**. When a manufacturer applies a harmonized standard, they receive a **presumption of conformity** with the corresponding Essential Requirements of the EU MDR. This significantly reduces the regulatory burden by replacing the need for the manufacturer to independently demonstrate compliance with each requirement.

**Five Key Harmonized Standards for Biomedical Device Design:**

| Standard | Full Title | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **EN ISO 13485:2016** | Quality Management Systems | Establishes QMS for medical device design, production, and servicing |
| **EN ISO 14971:2019** | Risk Management | Defines process for risk analysis, evaluation, control, and post-production review |
| **EN IEC 60601-1** | Medical Electrical Equipment Safety | General safety and essential performance for electromedical equipment |
| **EN ISO 10993-1** | Biological Evaluation | Framework for biocompatibility assessment of body-contacting materials |
| **EN ISO 14155:2020** | Clinical Investigation | GCP for design, conduct, recording, and reporting of clinical investigations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **CE Marking** (a mark on the device) with **EC Certificate** (the document issued by Notified Body).
> 2. Misstating the transition date — it is **26 May 2024** (end of MDR transition period), not 26 May 2021.
> 3. Forgetting that **Class I non-sterile, non-measuring** devices do NOT require Notified Body involvement (self-declaration).
> 4. Saying "FDA" or "510(k)" when asked about European Standards — these are **US** frameworks, not European.
> 5. Missing the **Annex VIII** rule reference when justifying a device classification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EU MDR 2017/745** replaced MDD 93/42/EEC and AIMD 90/385/EEC; full application began **26 May 2021**, transition end **26 May 2024**.
- **CE Marking** is the mandatory conformity mark for accessing the EU/EEA market.
- **Classification:** Class I $<$ IIa $<$ IIb $<$ III, based on **Annex VIII Rule Set**.
- **Class I non-sterile non-measuring** = self-declared; all other classes need **Notified Body**.
- **Conformity Routes:** Annex IX (QMS + TDA), Annex X (Type Exam), Annex XI (Production QA).
- **GSPR (Annex I)** replaces the legacy "Essential Requirements" — now **23 requirements**.
- **Harmonized Standards** = CEN / CENELEC / ETSI standards providing **presumption of conformity**.
- **Key Standards:** EN ISO 13485 (QMS), EN ISO 14971 (Risk), EN IEC 60601-1 (Electrical Safety), EN ISO 10993 (Biocompatibility), EN ISO 14155 (Clinical).
- **EUDAMED** is the central European database for device registration, UDI, vigilance, and certificates.
- **UDI system:** UDI-DI (Device Identifier) + UDI-PI (Production Identifier).
- **Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC):** A new requirement under Article 15 of EU MDR.
- **EUDAMED components:** Actor registration, UDI registration, certificates, vigilance, clinical investigations.
- **Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC)** — required under Article 15 to ensure compliance with the regulation.
- **EUDAMED components** — Actor registration, UDI database, certificate module, vigilance module, clinical investigation module.
- **Substantial changes** under MDR require re-certification, unlike MDD which was more lenient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain European Standards," structure your answer around **three pillars**: (1) **Regulatory Framework** (EU MDR + IVDR), (2) **Classification & Conformity Assessment**, (3) **Harmonized Standards for Presumption of Conformity**. This triad covers the majority of marks in KTU Module 4 questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO) - ISO 13485/14971
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO) - ISO 13485/14971

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> The **International Organisation for Standardisation (ISO)** is an independent, non-governmental international body that develops and publishes voluntary consensus-based international standards across nearly every industry, including medical devices, healthcare, engineering, and quality management. Founded on **23 February 1947** and headquartered in **Geneva, Switzerland**, ISO operates through a network of **169 national standards bodies**, and its standards are recognized in **167+ countries** worldwide.

For medical device engineering under the KTU 2024 PEBMT752 syllabus, two ISO standards are of paramount importance:
- **ISO 13485** – Quality Management Systems for Medical Devices
- **ISO 14971** – Application of Risk Management to Medical Devices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**
> Both ISO 13485 and ISO 14971 are **harmonized standards** under the EU Medical Device Regulation (**MDR 2017/745**) and are accepted globally as the foundational regulatory reference for medical device manufacturers seeking **CE marking**, **USFDA 510(k)**, and **CDSCO (India)** approvals.

---

### 1.2 ISO 13485: Medical Devices — Quality Management Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **ISO 13485:2016** is the internationally recognized standard that specifies requirements for a **Quality Management System (QMS)** where an organization needs to demonstrate its ability to provide medical devices and related services that **consistently meet customer requirements** and applicable **regulatory requirements**. The current version was published in **2016**, replacing ISO 13485:2003, and the **transition deadline** was **28 February 2019**.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The "Hospital Kitchen" Analogy

Imagine a hospital kitchen preparing meals for patients. Every chef (employee) must:
- Wear gloves and a hairnet (process controls),
- Use a specific recipe approved by a dietician (design controls),
- Record the temperature of every dish (documented information),
- Be inspected by a health officer (regulatory compliance), and
- Recall a meal if even one patient reports a stomach upset (post-market surveillance).

The kitchen itself doesn't *sell* food for profit — it produces a *life-critical product*. **ISO 13485 is the rulebook** that tells every hospital kitchen (medical device manufacturer) exactly how to run a clean, traceable, and accountable operation. It does not promise the food is *delicious*; it promises the food is **safe, consistent, and traceable** — which is precisely what a medical device must be.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from ISO 9001**
> ISO 13485 is a **standalone standard**, but it is largely based on the structure of **ISO 9001**. However, it places **far greater emphasis on regulatory compliance, traceability, risk management, and document control**. While ISO 9001 is generic and applicable to *any* industry, ISO 13485 is **sector-specific** to medical devices.

---

### 1.3 ISO 14971: Medical Devices — Application of Risk Management

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> **ISO 14971:2019** is the international standard that specifies a **process for a manufacturer to identify the hazards** associated with medical devices, including **in vitro diagnostic (IVD) medical devices**, to **estimate and evaluate the associated risks**, to **control these risks**, and to **monitor the effectiveness of the controls** throughout the **total product life cycle (TPLC)**. The current third edition was published in **December 2019** and is recognized as the **de facto global standard for medical device risk management**.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The "Road Trip Safety" Analogy

Consider a family planning a long road trip:
- **Step 1 – Identify Hazards**: Tire blowout, driver fatigue, bad weather, engine failure.
- **Step 2 – Estimate Risk**: Probability × Severity for each hazard.
- **Step 3 – Evaluate Acceptability**: Is the risk acceptable for our family?
- **Step 4 – Implement Controls**: Service the car, take breaks, check weather forecast, buy roadside assistance.
- **Step 5 – Monitor Effectiveness**: Did the controls actually work? Should we change them?

**ISO 14971 is exactly this risk-management process, but applied to a medical device** — from initial concept design (analogous to "planning the trip") to post-market surveillance (analogous to "monitoring the journey in real time"). The "family's safety" is the **patient's safety**, and the "car" is the **medical device**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Both Standards Work Together**
> ISO 13485 *mandates* the use of a risk-based approach throughout the QMS (Clause 8, Risk-Based Thinking). ISO 14971 *provides the methodology* for implementing that risk-based approach. Therefore, **ISO 14971 is the "engine" and ISO 13485 is the "vehicle"** — they are inseparable in modern medical device design.

---

### 1.4 Why These Standards Matter for Biomedical Engineers

| Stakeholder | Benefit of ISO 13485/14971 Compliance |
|---|---|
| **Patients** | Safer medical devices, fewer adverse events, traceable recalls |
| **Manufacturers** | Streamlined global market access, reduced regulatory burden |
| **Regulators (USFDA, EU, CDSCO)** | Standardized, predictable review processes |
| **Biomedical Engineers (YOU)** | Structured design process, clear documentation, professional accountability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric Highlight**
> The **ISO 13485 minimum documentation requirement** includes **6 mandatory documented procedures**: (1) Control of Documents, (2) Control of Records, (3) Internal Audit, (4) Control of Nonconforming Product, (5) Corrective Action, and (6) Preventive Action. This is a high-yield fact for KTU short-answer questions.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ISO 13485:2016 — Structural Framework

ISO 13485:2016 contains **8 normative clauses (0–8)**, of which **Clauses 4–8** contain the *auditable* requirements. The structure follows the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** of quality management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Structure (Memorize this list)**
> - **Clause 0** – Introduction
> - **Clause 1** – Scope
> - **Clause 2** – Normative references
> - **Clause 3** – Terms and definitions
> - **Clause 4** – Quality Management System (QMS general, documentation requirements)
> - **Clause 5** – Management responsibility (commitment, customer focus, planning, review)
> - **Clause 6** – Resource management (human resources, infrastructure, work environment, contamination control)
> - **Clause 7** – Product realization (planning, customer-related processes, design & development, purchasing, production, control of monitoring & measuring equipment)
> - **Clause 8** – Measurement, analysis, and improvement (monitoring, control of nonconforming product, CAPA)

### 2.2 Core Components of ISO 13485:2016 (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why** these clauses exist and **how** a manufacturer implements them — explained in KTU board-exam language.

#### 2.2.1 Clause 4 — Quality Management System
- The organization must **document a QMS**, maintain its effectiveness, and retain records.
- The QMS must include **6 mandatory documented procedures**.
- **Documentation hierarchy**: Quality Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records.
- **Why**: A medical device is only as safe as the *documented process* that produced it. A brain surgeon's hands (engineer) may be skilled, but without a documented procedure, the result is non-reproducible.

#### 2.2.2 Clause 5 — Management Responsibility
- **Top management** must demonstrate **leadership and commitment** (Clause 5.1).
- The **management representative** (Clause 5.5.2) is responsible for the QMS and for reporting performance.
- **Management review** (Clause 5.6) must occur at least **every 12 months**, with **12 mandatory inputs** documented.
- **Why**: Without management commitment, a QMS is just paperwork. Engineers, technicians, and inspectors must have *executive-level authority* to halt production if patient safety is at risk.

#### 2.2.3 Clause 6 — Resource Management
- Personnel performing work affecting product quality must be **competent**, based on **education, training, skills, and experience** (Clause 6.2).
- The **work environment** must be controlled — this includes **cleanliness, contamination control, temperature, humidity, lighting, and EMI shielding** (Clause 6.4).
- **Why**: A sterile surgical suture manufactured in a dusty environment is unsafe regardless of design quality. Environmental controls are **non-negotiable**.

#### 2.2.4 Clause 7 — Product Realization (The Engineering Heart)
- **Clause 7.3 — Design and Development** is the single most important clause for a KTU biomedical engineering student.
- Design and development must be **planned**, and the organization must control **8 stages**:
  1. Design and development planning
  2. Design and development inputs
  3. Design and development outputs
  4. Design and development review
  5. Design and development verification
  6. Design and development validation
  7. Design and development transfer
  8. Control of design and development changes
- **Design History File (DHF)** — a complete record of all design activities.
- **Why**: A pacemaker designed without *user need analysis* (input), *bench testing* (verification), and *clinical evaluation* (validation) is an accident waiting to happen.

#### 2.2.5 Clause 8 — Measurement, Analysis, and Improvement
- **Monitoring & measurement** (Clause 8.2) — including **post-market surveillance (PMS)**.
- **Internal audit** (Clause 8.2.4) — at least once per year.
- **Control of nonconforming product** (Clause 8.3) — explicit, no ambiguity allowed.
- **CAPA** (Corrective and Preventive Action, Clause 8.5.2 and 8.5.3).
- **Why**: Devices fail. The question is not "Will it fail?" but "How quickly will we detect and correct the failure?" Clause 8 is the *immune system* of the QMS.

---

### 2.3 ISO 14971:2019 — Risk Management Process (The 6-Step Engine)

ISO 14971:2019 defines a **risk management process** consisting of **6 major activities**, applied iteratively throughout the product life cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the 6 ISO 14971 Steps — Every KTU Question Will Test This**
> 1. **Risk Analysis** – Identify hazards, estimate severity and probability.
> 2. **Risk Evaluation** – Compare estimated risk against acceptability criteria.
> 3. **Risk Control** – Implement measures to reduce risk to acceptable level.
> 4. **Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk Acceptability** – Is the *combined* residual risk acceptable?
> 5. **Risk Management Review** – Formal review before commercial release.
> 6. **Production and Post-Production Activities** – Monitor real-world performance, update risk file.

### 2.4 Core Risk Concepts and Formulas (The KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Management Vocabulary — Definitions for KTU Answers**
> - **Hazard**: A potential source of harm.
> - **Hazardous Situation**: A circumstance in which a hazard can lead to harm.
> - **Harm**: Physical injury or damage to the health of people, or damage to property or the environment.
> - **Severity**: A measure of the possible consequences of a hazard.
> - **Probability of Occurrence**: The likelihood that a hazard will materialize.
> - **Risk**: The combination of the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.

**The fundamental risk equation** (the most tested formula in KTU risk-management questions):

$$
Risk = Severity \times Probability
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk Acceptability Decision Rule**
> A risk is considered **acceptable** if and only if:
> - **Individual residual risk** is acceptable, AND
> - **Overall residual risk** is acceptable, AND
> - The **benefit-risk ratio** is favorable.
>
> If any condition fails, **additional risk control measures** must be applied and the process iterated.

**The risk control hierarchy** (in descending order of effectiveness — high-yield for KTU short answers):

$$
\text{Inherently safe design} > \text{Protective measures (in device)} > \text{Information for safety (labelling)}
$$

| Risk Reduction Method | Effectiveness | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Inherently safe design | **Highest** | Use a battery voltage too low to cause harm |
| Protective measures (in device) | High | Insulated casing, current limiters |
| Information for safety (labelling) | Lowest (last resort) | Warning labels, training manuals |

**Risk Acceptability Matrix (Typical 3×3 Grid):**

| Probability \ Severity | Negligible (S1) | Marginal (S2) | Critical (S3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequent (P1) | Medium | High | **Unacceptable** |
| Occasional (P2) | Low | Medium | High |
| Remote (P3) | Negligible | Low | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Risk Management File Contents (KTU Exam Favourite)**
> A compliant ISO 14971 **Risk Management File (RMF)** must contain:
> 1. Risk management policy
> 2. Risk management plan
> 3. Risk analysis results (hazards, hazardous situations, harm)
> 4. Risk evaluation results
> 5. Risk control measures implemented
> 6. Verification of risk control effectiveness
> 7. Residual risk evaluation
> 8. Risk management review report
> 9. Production and post-production monitoring data

---

### 2.5 The Relationship: ISO 14971 ↔ ISO 13485

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield KTU question topic — appears in almost every past paper**

| ISO 14971 Concept | Mapped to ISO 13485 Clause |
|---|---|
| Risk analysis & control | Clause 7.1 (Planning of product realization) |
| Design & development | Clause 7.3 |
| Purchasing controls | Clause 7.4 (Risk of suppliers) |
| Production & service controls | Clause 7.5 |
| Post-market surveillance | Clause 8.2.1 (Feedback) |
| CAPA | Clause 8.5.2 / 8.5.3 |

The **Risk Management File (RMF)** is part of the **Medical Device File (MDF)** required by ISO 13485 Clause 4.2.4.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **CE Marking (EU MDR 2017/745)**: Compliance with ISO 13485 and ISO 14971 is **mandatory** for any device sold in the European Union.
- **USFDA 21 CFR Part 820**: Functionally harmonized with ISO 13485.
- **CDSCO (India)**: ISO 13485 certification is the standard for Indian medical device licensing.
- **Implantable devices (pacemakers, stents, prosthetics)**: ISO 14971 risk analysis is **non-negotiable** because harm severity is extreme.
- **In-vitro diagnostics (IVDs)**: Included under ISO 14971:2019 for the first time.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From "Hazard" to "Acceptable Residual Risk" (The Complete ISO 14971 Workflow)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **analytical chain** that transforms a vague engineering concern ("This could hurt a patient") into a defensible regulatory conclusion ("This residual risk is acceptable because…"). This is the single most important derivation for KTU long-answer questions.

We proceed through the **6-step ISO 14971 process**, with the *current state vector* explicitly tracked at each stage.

**Initial State (t = 0): Device Concept**
- Inputs: User need, indication for use, intended use, target population.
- Outputs: Initial device description, initial regulatory classification.

---

**STEP 1 — Risk Analysis**

$$
\text{Output of Step 1} = \{(H_i, S_i, P_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}
$$

where:
- $H_i$ = the *i*-th identified hazard,
- $S_i$ = severity class of harm caused by $H_i$,
- $P_i$ = probability class that $H_i$ leads to harm.

**Detailed logic per row:**
- For each device part / function / use scenario, ask: *What could go wrong? What harm could it cause?*
- Estimate $S_i$ and $P_i$ from:
  - Historical data (complaint databases, MAUDE / FDA adverse event database),
  - Clinical literature,
  - Standards (e.g., IEC 60601-1 for electrical safety),
  - Expert opinion,
  - FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) tables.

**Worked example (Pacemaker):**
- $H_1$: Lead fracture → $S_1$ = Catastrophic (death), $P_1$ = Occasional → $R_1$ = High.
- $H_2$: Battery depletion → $S_2$ = Catastrophic, $P_2$ = Remote → $R_2$ = Medium.
- $H_3$: Skin irritation at implant site → $S_3$ = Marginal, $P_3$ = Frequent → $R_3$ = Medium.

---

**STEP 2 — Risk Evaluation**

$$
\text{Decision}(R_i) = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT}, & \text{if } R_i \le R_{\text{acceptable, policy}} \\ \text{REDUCE}, & \text{if } R_i > R_{\text{acceptable, policy}} \end{cases}
$$

**Logic expansion:**
- The manufacturer's **risk management policy** defines the acceptability threshold.
- Any $R_i$ above the threshold **must enter Step 3 (Risk Control)**.
- A documented **risk evaluation rationale** is required for every decision.

**Applied to our pacemaker:**
- $R_1$ (lead fracture) = High → **REDUCE**.
- $R_2$ (battery depletion) = Medium → depends on policy; conservative policy → **REDUCE**.
- $R_3$ (skin irritation) = Medium → **REDUCE** if possible.

---

**STEP 3 — Risk Control**

$$
R_{\text{post-control}, i} = R_{\text{pre-control}, i} \times (1 - E_{\text{control}, i})
$$

where $E_{\text{control}, i} \in [0, 1]$ is the **effectiveness** of the chosen control measure.

**Logic expansion:**
- Apply the **hierarchy of controls** (inherently safe design → protective measures → information for safety).
- For each control, verify that the **new risk** is acceptable.
- Verify that the control itself does **not introduce new hazards** (this is often missed by students — and is a KTU board favourite trap).

**Applied to our pacemaker:**
- $H_1$ (lead fracture): Use a more fatigue-resistant conductor → $E_{\text{control}} = 0.7$ → $R_{\text{post}}$ drops to Low.
- $H_2$ (battery depletion): Use a 10-year cell with a low-battery alarm → $E_{\text{control}} = 0.6$ → $R_{\text{post}}$ drops to Low.
- $H_3$ (skin irritation): Use a biocompatible titanium alloy per **ISO 5832-2** → $E_{\text{control}} = 0.8$ → $R_{\text{post}}$ drops to Negligible.

---

**STEP 4 — Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk Acceptability**

$$
R_{\text{overall, residual}} = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{n} R_{\text{post-control}, i}
$$

where $\oplus$ denotes a **defining operator** (the manufacturer defines how to combine individual risks — typically by review of the **risk-benefit analysis**).

**Logic expansion:**
- A medical device may have dozens of *individual* residual risks that are all "low," but their *combination* may not be acceptable.
- The manufacturer must prepare a **risk-benefit analysis report** demonstrating that the **medical benefit** outweighs the **overall residual risk**.
- If the overall risk is **not acceptable**, the manufacturer must **go back to Step 3** and add more controls, or **re-design the device** (go back to ISO 13485 Clause 7.3 design review).

---

**STEP 5 — Risk Management Review**

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{RELEASE}, & \text{if all residual risks acceptable AND } R_{\text{overall, residual}} \text{ acceptable AND } B > R_{\text{overall, residual}} \\ \text{DO NOT RELEASE}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $B$ is the **medical benefit**.

**Logic expansion:**
- Before the device is released to market, a **formal review** is conducted.
- The **Risk Management Report** is produced and approved.
- The **Risk Management File (RMF)** is locked and submitted as part of the technical documentation.

---

**STEP 6 — Production and Post-Production Activities**

$$
R_{\text{real-world}, i}(t) = f(R_{\text{post-control}, i}, \text{complaint rate}(t), \text{incident data}(t), \text{clinical follow-up}(t))
$$

**Logic expansion:**
- Once the device is in the market, the manufacturer must **continuously collect** data from:
  - Customer complaints,
  - Adverse event reports,
  - Field service reports,
  - Clinical follow-up studies,
  - Regulatory agency advisories.
- If $R_{\text{real-world}, i}(t)$ **rises above the acceptability threshold**, the manufacturer must:
  - Initiate a **CAPA** (ISO 13485 Clause 8.5.2),
  - Issue a **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)** or **recall**,
  - **Update the RMF** and **Risk Management Report**,
  - **Re-validate** the device (ISO 13485 Clause 7.3.7 → 7.5.6).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Insulin Infusion Pump

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **type of calculation KTU may ask in a 14-mark question** on ISO 14971 application.

**Scenario**: A portable insulin pump is being designed. The risk management team has identified two main hazards and wants to compute the post-control risk reduction.

**Given data:**

| Hazard ID | Hazard | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | $R_{\text{pre}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Battery overheating → burn to skin | 3 (Critical) | 2 (Occasional) | 6 |
| H2 | Air bubbles in insulin line → embolism | 3 (Critical) | 1 (Remote) | 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Risk score = Severity × Probability** (assuming a 3×3 matrix).

**Risk acceptability threshold (from policy):** $R_{\text{acceptable}} \le 3$.

---

**Step 1 – Risk Analysis**: Already done (H1, H2 identified).

**Step 2 – Risk Evaluation**:
$$
R_{\text{pre,H1}} = 3 \times 2 = 6 > 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{REDUCE}
$$
$$
R_{\text{pre,H2}} = 3 \times 1 = 3 \le 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT} \text{ (subject to additional monitoring)}
$$

**Step 3 – Risk Control (H1 only)**:
- Selected control: Replace Li-ion with thermally protected LiFePO₄ cell + thermal cutoff circuit.
- Estimated effectiveness: $E_{\text{control,H1}} = 0.7$.

**Step 3 – Compute Post-Control Risk**:
$$
R_{\text{post,H1}} = R_{\text{pre,H1}} \times (1 - E_{\text{control,H1}})
$$

$$
R_{\text{post,H1}} = 6 \times (1 - 0.7) = 6 \times 0.3 = 1.8
$$

**Step 3 – Verify Acceptability**:
$$
R_{\text{post,H1}} = 1.8 \le 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ACCEPT}
$$

**Step 4 – Overall Residual Risk**:
- Both H1 ($R_{\text{post}} = 1.8$) and H2 ($R_{\text{pre}} = 3$) are now individually acceptable.
- The benefit (lifelong insulin delivery with precision) clearly outweighs the combined residual risk.
- **Conclusion**: Overall residual risk **ACCEPTABLE**.

**Step 5 – Risk Management Review**: Approved for release.

**Step 6 – Post-Market**: Set up PMS to monitor:
- Field reports of any thermal events.
- Pump recall database.
- Quarterly trending of $R_{\text{real-world}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight**
> Notice how the calculations are *simple* but the **decision-making logic** is dense. KTU examiners award full 14 marks only when students clearly state: (1) the *policy threshold*, (2) the *control hierarchy choice* justification, (3) the *verification of no new hazards*, and (4) the *overall benefit-risk reasoning*. Skipping any of these forfeits 2–3 marks.
