## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, PEBMT752]**
**Differentiate between Basic Safety and Essential Performance with one biomedical engineering example each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Basic Safety** is defined under IEC 60601-1 Clause 3.10 as the *absence of unacceptable risk directly caused by physical hazards* (electrical shock, mechanical injury, thermal burn, fire, radiation) when the ME equipment is used under normal condition and single fault condition.
>
> *Example:* The enclosure of an ECG machine must not become LIVE during a single fault in the basic insulation (electrical Basic Safety).
>
> **Essential Performance** is defined under IEC 60601-1 Clause 3.27 as the *performance of a clinical function, where loss or degradation beyond manufacturer-specified limits results in an unacceptable risk*.
>
> *Example:* A defibrillator must deliver the programmed energy within ±15% or ±2 J (whichever is greater) into a 50 Ω test load — failing to do so is a loss of Essential Performance.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBMT752]**
**Explain the meaning of Type BF and Type CF applied parts. Why is Type CF mandatory for direct cardiac application? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Type BF (Body Floating):** Applied part that is electrically isolated (floating) from earth and from other accessible conductive parts of the equipment. It is *not* intended for direct cardiac application. *Examples:* SpO₂ finger probe, NIBP cuff, ultrasound imaging probe.
>
> **Type CF (Cardiac Floating):** Applied part with the **highest degree of isolation** from earth, specifically intended for direct contact with the heart. It is also **defibrillation-proof**, i.e., it can withstand a 5000 V DC defibrillator discharge without damage or unsafe voltage transfer.
>
> **Why CF is mandatory for cardiac application:** Microamp-level currents (≥ 10 µA) passing through the heart can induce **ventricular fibrillation**. The CF leakage limit of 0.01 mA (10 µA) in normal condition is exactly at this fibrillation threshold, providing the necessary margin of safety. Examples include intracardiac catheters and pacing leads.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (University ESE Pattern — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEBMT752]*

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the classification of Medical Electrical Equipment into **Class I, Class II, and Class III** based on protection against electric shock. Mention one limitation of Class II in hospitals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A patient monitor has a 250 V AC rms working voltage, insulation resistance $R_{\text{ins}} = 10\ G\Omega$, and a stray capacitance $C_{\text{ins}} = 220\ pF$ between LIVE and the chassis. Calculate the resulting enclosure leakage current at 50 Hz and verify whether it satisfies the IEC 60601-1 Type B limit under Normal Condition. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Class I Equipment**

The equipment relies on:
- **Basic insulation** for primary protection (between LIVE/NEUTRAL and accessible metal parts).
- **Protective Earth (PE) conductor** as the secondary protection.
- If basic insulation fails, fault current flows through PE → blows line fuse → safety achieved.

*Sketch description:* A metal chassis ECG machine with a 3-pin plug (L, N, E). The chassis is bonded to the E pin.

**Step 2 — Class II Equipment**

The equipment uses:
- **Double insulation** (basic + supplementary) OR **reinforced insulation**.
- **No PE conductor required.**
- Plug is **2-pin only**.

*Sketch description:* Same ECG machine internally, but with double-insulated plastic enclosure and 2-pin plug.

**Step 3 — Class III Equipment**

The equipment is powered by a **Safety Extra-Low Voltage (SELV)** source, $\le 24\ V_{AC}\ \text{rms}$ or $\le 60\ V_{DC}$, from an internal SELV transformer or battery. Above SELV voltages do not exist inside the equipment.

*Sketch description:* A battery-operated digital thermometer or patient worn telemetry tag.

**Step 4 — Limitation of Class II in Hospitals**

> [!NOTE]
> Class II devices cannot be used as a *patient environment equipotential bonding point*. During microshock-sensitive procedures (cardiac catheterisation), the *absence* of a PE path means chassis potential can float and capacitive-couple to the patient. Hence, in the cardiac-protected environment (per IEC 60364-7-710), Class I equipment with verified PE is preferred and Class II is generally avoided.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Given data**

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = 250\ V, \quad R_{\text{ins}} = 10 \times 10^{9}\ \Omega, \quad C_{\text{ins}} = 220 \times 10^{-12}\ F, \quad f = 50\ Hz
$$

**Step 2 — Capacitive reactance**

$$
X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_{\text{ins}}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 220 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{6.9115 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
= 1.4469 \times 10^{7}\ \Omega \approx 14.47\ M\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Parallel combination of $R_{\text{ins}}$ and $X_C$**

$$
Z_{\text{ins}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{ins}} \times (-jX_C)}{R_{\text{ins}} - jX_C}
$$

Taking magnitudes:

$$
\vert Z_{\text{ins}} \vert = \dfrac{R_{\text{ins}} \cdot X_C}{\sqrt{R_{\text{ins}}^{2} + X_C^{2}}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{(10 \times 10^{9})(1.4469 \times 10^{7})}{\sqrt{(10 \times 10^{9})^{2} + (1.4469 \times 10^{7})^{2}}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1.4469 \times 10^{17}}{\sqrt{10^{20} + 2.0935 \times 10^{14}}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1.4469 \times 10^{17}}{\sqrt{1.0000 \times 10^{20}}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1.4469 \times 10^{17}}{10^{10}}
$$

$$
= 1.4469 \times 10^{7}\ \Omega
$$

(This confirms $X_C \ll R_{\text{ins}}$, so the impedance is dominated by $X_C$ as expected.)

**Step 4 — Leakage current**

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{rms}}}{\vert Z_{\text{ins}} \vert} = \dfrac{250}{1.4469 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
= 1.728 \times 10^{-5}\ A = 17.28\ \mu A = 0.0173\ mA
$$

**Step 5 — Verification against IEC 60601-1 limit**

Type B Enclosure Leakage Limit (Normal Condition) = **0.1 mA**.

$$
I_{\text{leak}} = 0.0173\ mA \le 0.1\ mA \quad \checkmark\ \text{PASS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The patient monitor's design comfortably satisfies IEC 60601-1 Type B enclosure leakage under normal condition. A wide safety margin of approximately **5.8×** exists between measured and limit values.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, PEBMT752]* *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Define **Single Fault Condition (SFC)** as per IEC 60601-1. List any **four** SFCs that must be considered during type testing, and explain why each is relevant to medical equipment safety. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hospital biomedical engineer performs periodic safety testing of a 16-slice CT scanner. The CT is a **Class I, Type B** equipment. During the test:
- Mains voltage = $230\ V_{AC}\ \text{rms}$
- Protective Earth resistance measured = $0.08\ \Omega$
- Earth leakage current under NC = $3.2\ mA$

Determine if the equipment passes the IEC 60601-1 safety test. Justify with calculations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Definition:**

> **Single Fault Condition (IEC 60601-1, Clause 3.85):** *A condition in which a single means for reducing a risk is defective OR an abnormal condition is present.*

The ME equipment must remain safe even when **only one** protection layer fails (multiple simultaneous failures are not normally required to be considered, except for risks where a single fault could lead directly to another).

**Four SFCs relevant to type testing:**

1. **Interruption of the Protective Earth (PE) conductor:** Verifies that Class I equipment remains safe without PE — this is why **double insulation** or reinforced insulation is required for the patient pathway (the MOPP concept).

2. **Interruption of any one supply conductor (LIVE or NEUTRAL):** Verifies that loss of either conductor does not cause unsafe leakage currents to flow through patient or operator.

3. **Short-circuit of any one basic insulation layer:** Tests whether supplementary insulation alone is sufficient to limit leakage below 0.5 mA (enclosure) or 0.5 mA (patient Type B).

4. **Reverse polarity of the mains supply (LIVE and NEUTRAL interchanged):** Ensures that internal fusing and insulation continue to function regardless of plug orientation, since medical plugs are not always keyed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each is relevant:** Each SFC tests a *different physical protection layer*. A device passing all four SFCs demonstrates *defence-in-depth* — no single point of failure can cause an unsafe condition.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Check Protective Earth Resistance against IEC 60601-1 limit**

$$
R_{\text{PE, measured}} = 0.08\ \Omega \quad \text{vs.} \quad R_{\text{PE, max allowed}} = 0.1\ \Omega
$$

Since $0.08\ \Omega \le 0.1\ \Omega$, the protective earth continuity **passes**.

**Step 2 — Check Earth Leakage Current against Type B limit**

Type B Earth Leakage Limit (Normal Condition) = **5 mA**.

$$
I_{\text{earth, NC}} = 3.2\ mA \le 5\ mA \quad \checkmark\ \text{PASS}
$$

**Step 3 — Touch voltage verification (bonus calculation for full marks)**

The chassis touch voltage during a hypothetical fault:

$$
V_{\text{touch}} = I_{\text{earth, NC}} \times R_{\text{PE}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.08 = 2.56 \times 10^{-4}\ V = 0.256\ mV
$$

Far below the 50 V AC touch voltage limit. **Excellent safety margin.**

**Step 4 — Overall Conclusion**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CT scanner PASSES the IEC 60601-1 periodic safety test** for Class I Type B equipment under normal condition. Both PE resistance and earth leakage current are within limits with comfortable margins. The equipment may continue in clinical service.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Safety Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MOOP and MOPP.** Remember: MOOP = Operator, MOPP = Patient. The patient pathway demands 2 MOPPs.
> 2. **Forgetting the NC vs SFC distinction.** NC = everything working normally. SFC = one protection layer intentionally failed.
> 3. **Mixing up the patient leakage limit for CF.** It is **0.01 mA** (NC), NOT 0.1 mA. CF is 10× stricter than B/BF.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Always write "mA" or "µA" — answers in bare Amperes are treated as incomplete.
> 5. **Writing "earthed" when describing Type BF or CF.** These are *floating* applied parts, isolated by design.
> 6. **In leakage calculations, skipping the capacitive reactance term.** Stray capacitance always matters at 50 Hz.
> 7. **Not stating which clause of IEC 60601-1 is being referenced** in theory answers — examiners reward clause citations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4:**
>
> - **Basic Safety** = no unacceptable direct physical hazard (IEC 60601-1 Clause 3.10).
> - **Essential Performance** = clinical function whose loss causes unacceptable risk (Clause 3.27).
> - **Class I** uses Basic Insulation + PE; **Class II** uses Double/Reinforced Insulation; **Class III** uses SELV only.
> - **Type B** = body, earthed; **Type BF** = body floating; **Type CF** = cardiac floating + defibrillation-proof.
> - **MOOP** = protection for the operator (1 MOOP sufficient for mains-earth).
> - **MOPP** = protection for the patient (2 MOPPs needed for mains-patient).
> - **Single Fault Condition** = one protection layer intentionally failed during testing.
> - **Earth leakage** limits: $\le 5\ mA$ (NC), $\le 10\ mA$ (SFC).
> - **Enclosure leakage** limits: $\le 0.1\ mA$ (NC), $\le 0.5\ mA$ (SFC).
> - **Patient leakage (B, BF)**: $\le 0.1\ mA$ (NC), $\le 0.5\ mA$ (SFC).
> - **Patient leakage (CF)**: $\le 0.01\ mA$ (NC), $\le 0.05\ mA$ (SFC).
> - **Hipot test voltage**: 1500 V AC for 1 MOPP, 4000 V AC for 2 MOPPs, 5000 V DC for defibrillation-proof.
> - **PE resistance must be $\le 0.1\ \Omega$.**
> - **Risk Management** follows ISO 14971: Identify → Estimate → Evaluate → Control → Monitor.
> - **Creepage $\ge$ Clearance** in polluted / humid hospital environments; both double for 2 MOPP.
> - **Microshock** threshold = 10 µA directly through the heart — hence CF rating is mandatory for intracardiac devices.
> - **Defibrillation-proof** applied parts must survive 5000 V DC across the patient without unsafe voltage transfer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intellectual Property (IP) - Protection for Medical Devices. Steps for Patent Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT752 - DESIGN OF BIOMEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intellectual Property (IP) - Protection for Medical Devices. Steps for Patent Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intellectual Property (IP)** refers to the legally recognized exclusive rights accorded to creators/inventors over their intangible creations of the mind. In the context of **biomedical devices**, IP encompasses statutory protections—primarily **patents, trademarks, copyrights, trade secrets, and industrial designs**—that safeguard innovations ranging from novel implantable electrodes and diagnostic algorithms to biocompatible coatings and surgical instrument geometries.

Under the **Indian Patents Act, 1970** (as amended in 2005, aligned with TRIPS), a **patent** is a statutory right granted for an invention that is a **new product or process**, capable of **industrial application**, and involves an **inventive step** (i.e., non-obvious to a person skilled in the art). The standard term is **20 years** from the date of filing, subject to payment of renewal fees.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Recipe vs. The Restaurant"** 🍳🏪
> Imagine you invent a unique cardiac stent delivery system after 5 years of R&D costing ₹50 crores. Without IP protection, any competitor can simply reverse-engineer your device the moment it hits the market and sell identical copies at a fraction of your cost—undercutting you commercially and eroding your ROI.
> A **patent** acts like a **government-issued "exclusive recipe card"**. For 20 years, you are the only chef who can commercially cook that exact "dish" (device). Others may study, analyze, or even improve upon it, but they cannot sell, make, or import it without your permission (a license). For medical devices, where regulatory approval alone costs millions, the patent is the only mechanism that allows inventors to recoup their research investment and continue funding life-saving innovation.

### 1.3 Why IP is Critical in Medical Devices (Key Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Justifications for IP Protection in Biomedical Engineering:**
> 1. **High R&D Recovery:** Medical device development typically requires **3–7 years** and **$10–100 million** in investment. Patents ensure ROI.
> 2. **Regulatory Linkage:** FDA 510(k) or CE Mark approvals are public, but the underlying technical innovation is shielded only by patents.
> 3. **Patient Safety Linkage:** Trademarks prevent "look-alike" counterfeit devices (e.g., fake pacemakers) that endanger lives.
> 4. **University/Startup Commercialization:** KTU students' thesis-stage prototypes need IP protection before industry licensing.
> 5. **Cross-border Protection:** PCT (Patent Cooperation Treaty) filings enable protection in **150+ countries** simultaneously.

### 1.4 Types of IP Most Relevant to Medical Devices

| IP Type | Protects | Medical Device Example | Typical Term |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Patent** | Inventions (devices, methods, materials) | A novel drug-eluting stent composition | **20 years** |
| **Trademark** | Brand identity, logos, names | "Medtronic" name/logo on a pacemaker | **10 years** (renewable) |
| **Copyright** | Software, manuals, documentation | ECG analysis algorithm source code | **Life of author + 60 years** |
| **Trade Secret** | Confidential know-how | Proprietary biocompatible polymer formulation | Indefinite (if kept secret) |
| **Industrial Design** | Aesthetic shape/ornament | Ergonomic grip pattern of a surgical forceps | **10–15 years** |
| **Geographical Indication** | Origin-based product identity | Specific herbal extract region | **10 years** (renewable) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Patentability Requirements (The "Three Pillars")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an invention related to a medical device to be patentable in India, it MUST satisfy three cumulative conditions under **Section 2(1)(j)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. These are derived directly from the **TRIPS Agreement (Article 27)**.

**Pillar 1 — Novelty (Section 2(1)(l))**
- The invention must not have been **anticipated by prior art** anywhere in the world (absolute novelty, not just in India).
- *Biomedical Example:* A new pulse oximetry algorithm must not exist in any published paper, conference proceeding, or earlier patent.

**Pillar 2 — Inventive Step / Non-Obviousness (Section 2(1)(ja))**
- The invention must not be obvious to a **"person skilled in the art" (PSA)**.
- *Biomedical Example:* Combining a standard sphygmomanometer with Bluetooth is **obvious** (likely not patentable). Combining it with **AI-based arrhythmia detection using PPG signals** is **non-obvious** (potentially patentable).

**Pillar 3 — Industrial Application / Utility (Section 2(1)(ac))**
- The invention must be capable of being **made or used in an industry**.
- *Biomedical Example:* A surgical suture must demonstrably work and have a practical medical use.

### 2.2 Non-Patentable Subject Matter (Section 3 & 4)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exclusions for Biomedical Innovators** (must memorize for KTU exams):
> 1. **Methods of treatment** of humans/animals (surgical, therapeutic, diagnostic) are **not patentable** in India. *However, devices used IN these methods ARE patentable.*
> 2. **New uses of known substances** are generally not patentable (unlike in the US/EU where second medical use patents exist).
> 3. **Atomic energy-related inventions** and **contrary to public order/morality** inventions are excluded.
> 4. **Plants and animals** (other than microorganisms) and **essentially biological processes** are excluded.
> 5. **Mathematical methods or business methods** per se are excluded—BUT when integrated into a medical device producing a technical effect, they ARE patentable.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Patent Process Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the key quantitative parameters a KTU student must memorize for ESE questions on the patent process.

| Parameter / Metric | Value / Formula | Governing Law / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **Patent Term (India)** | **20 years** from date of filing | Indian Patents Act, 1970, Sec 53 |
| **First Renewal Due** | **8th year** from filing | Sec 53(2) |
| **Subsequent Renewals** | Every year (max 20 renewals) | Rule 80 |
| **Convention Application Priority Period** | **12 months** from first filing | Paris Convention, Art 4C |
| **PCT National Phase Entry Deadline** | **30 / 31 months** from priority date | PCT Art 22/39 |
| **Expedited Examination Timeline** | **18 months** (vs. 5–7 years normal) | Rule 24C |
| **Working Statement (India)** | **Form 27**, due every financial year | Sec 146(2) |
| **Compulsory License Threshold** | **3 years** after grant + non-working | Sec 84 |
| **Grace Period for Disclosure** | **12 months** (India allows pre-filing disclosure) | Sec 31 amended 2005 |
| **Annual Patent Filing Growth (India)** | ~**15–20%** CAGR | IP India Statistics |
| **Average Pendency at IPO** | **4–6 years** for examination | CGPDTM data |
| **Patent Cost (India, individual)** | ₹**1,600–8,000** filing fee (varies by entity) | First Schedule, Patents Rules 2003 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for a Biomedical Engineer:**
> - **Strategic patenting** before publishing a thesis is critical—**publication kills novelty**.
> - The **12-month grace period** in India gives KTU students a buffer to file after academic publication.
> - **Design-around strategies** by competitors (e.g., redesigning around a claim) highlight the importance of writing **broad independent claims** during patent drafting.
> - **Evergreening** (patenting minor modifications of existing drugs) is a moral/ethical issue actively debated in Indian IP jurisprudence (e.g., Novartis-Gleevec case).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Patent Process & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The 7-Stage Patent Process Flow (India)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **exhaustive step-by-step patent process** in India, from invention conception to post-grant maintenance. This is a **frequent 14-mark KTU question**.

---

**Step 1: Invention Disclosure & Prior Art Search (Pre-filing Phase, ~2–6 weeks)**

This is the conceptual starting point. The inventor documents the invention in a signed, witnessed lab notebook and conducts a **prior art search** across databases (Indian Patent Advanced Search System — **IPAS**, **Espacenet, Google Patents, PubMed, IEEE Xplore**).

$$
\text{Patentability Decision} = f(\text{Novelty}, \text{Non-Obviousness}, \text{Utility})
$$

If all three pillars are satisfied, proceed to filing. A **provisional specification** may be filed first to establish the **priority date** (this locks in the filing date for 12 months while the inventor refines the invention).

---

**Step 2: Drafting the Patent Application (Specification)**

The application has two mandatory parts:

- **Title of Invention**
- **Specification** comprising:
  - **Background / Field of Invention**
  - **Summary of Invention**
  - **Brief Description of Drawings** (if any)
  - **Detailed Description** (enables a PSA to reproduce the invention — this is the **enablement requirement**)
  - **Claims** (defines the legal scope of protection — the most litigated part)
  - **Abstract** (≤150 words)

**Form 1** (Application for Patent), **Form 2** (Provisional/Complete Specification), **Form 5** (Declaration of Inventorship), and **Form 26** (Power of Attorney, if filed through a patent agent) are submitted.

---

**Step 3: Filing & Acknowledgment (Date of Filing, ~1 day)**

The application is filed either:
- **Physically** at one of the **4 Patent Offices** (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata), OR
- **Online via e-filing portal** of the Indian Patent Office (preferred).

The **date of filing** is the official priority date. A filing receipt with **Application Number** is issued.

---

**Step 4: Publication (18 months from Priority Date / Filing Date)**

Under **Section 11A**, the application is **automatically published** in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months**. From this date, the applicant gains **"provisional rights"** — they can claim reasonable damages from infringers (not royalty before grant, per the 2020 amendment).

The applicant may request **early publication** via **Form 9** (₹2,500 fee), reducing the wait to approximately **1 month**.

---

**Step 5: Request for Examination (RFE, within 48 months of priority date)**

The applicant files **Form 18** along with the prescribed fee to request substantive examination. The Patent Office assigns the application to an examiner. The **48-month deadline** is a hard cutoff — missing it results in **automatic withdrawal**.

---

**Step 6: Substantive Examination & First Examination Report (FER)**

The examiner (a subject-matter expert) reviews the application against:
- Prior art databases
- Patentability criteria (novelty, inventive step, industrial application)
- Formal requirements and unity of invention

The examiner issues a **First Examination Report (FER)** citing objections under various sections. The applicant has **6 months (extendable by 3 months)** to respond with **Form 8** amendments, arguments, or both.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Outcome Paths:**
> - **Objections replied → Allowed** → proceeds to grant
> - **Objections not satisfied** → **Hearing** scheduled (Form 14)
> - **Still unsatisfied** → Application **refused** (appealable to IPAB/High Court)

---

**Step 7: Grant of Patent & Sealing**

Once accepted, the **Controller** grants the patent. The patent number is published in the **Patent Office Journal**, and a **Certificate of Patent** is issued. The patent must be renewed annually starting from the **8th year**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Patent Filing Routes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below compares the three principal patent filing routes relevant for KTU biomedical engineering students/innovators.

| Parameter | Direct National (India) | Paris Convention Route | PCT (International) Route |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filing Location** | Indian Patent Office | National office of each country | WIPO (Geneva) |
| **Initial Cost** | Lowest (₹1,600+) | Moderate | Highest (PCT fees + attorney) |
| **Time to Decision** | 4–6 years (India) | Varies per country | **30/31 months** deferral |
| **Geographic Scope** | India only | Multiple countries via priority | **150+ countries** via national phase |
| **Priority Period** | N/A (first filing) | **12 months** from first filing | **12 months** (Paris) → **30/31 months** (national) |
| **Best Use Case** | India-only product | Select 2–5 key markets | Global commercialization planned |
| **KTU Student Applicability** | ✅ Most relevant | Limited (high cost) | Strategic (university tech transfer) |

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example: Patent Term Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Walkthrough for KTU Exam Practice:**

A biomedical startup in Kerala files a patent application for a novel **continuous glucose monitoring sensor** on **15 March 2024**. The patent office grants the patent on **20 September 2027**. Calculate:
1. The **expiration date** of the patent.
2. The **first renewal deadline**.
3. The **status** of the patent on 1 January 2032.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Expiration Date} = \text{Filing Date} + 20 \text{ years}
$$
$$
= 15 \text{ March 2024} + 20 \text{ years} = \mathbf{15 \text{ March 2044}}
$$

$$
\text{First Renewal} = \text{Grant Date} + 8 \text{ years (latest)} \text{ OR Filing} + 8\text{th year anniversary}
$$
$$
\text{Filing} + 8 \text{ years} = 15 \text{ March 2032} \quad (\text{this is the mandatory first renewal window})
$$
$$
\therefore \text{First renewal fee due on or before: } \mathbf{15 \text{ March 2032}}
$$

$$
\text{Status on 1 Jan 2032:} \text{ Patent valid; first renewal pending in 2.5 months.}
$$

If renewal fee is **NOT paid by 15 March 2032 + 6-month grace period = 15 September 2032**, the patent **lapses** (Form 4 with surcharge may restore within **18 months** under Rule 81).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Indian Patent Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a Mermaid `flowchart TD` block depicting the complete sequential patent process. All node IDs follow the alpha-prefix rule and use plain uppercase alphanumeric labels (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Invention Disclosure] --> A2[Prior Art Search IPAS Espacenet]
    A2 --> A3{Patentable Check Novelty Inventive Step Utility}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Stop or Reframe Invention]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[Step 2: Draft Specification Form 1 Form 2 Form 5]
    A5 --> A6{Provisional or Complete Filing}
    A6 -- Provisional --> A7[File Provisional Lock Priority Date 12 Months]
    A6 -- Complete --> A8[Step 3: File at Indian Patent Office]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Step 4: Publication After 18 Months Automatic]
    A9 --> A10{Early Publication Requested Form 9}
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[Published in 1 Month]
    A10 -- No --> A12[Published in 18 Months]
    A11 --> A13[Step 5: Request Examination Form 18 within 48 Months]
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14[Step 6: Substantive Examination by IPO Examiner]
    A14 --> A15{First Examination Report FER Issued}
    A15 --> A16{Applicant Response Form 8 within 6 Months}
    A16 -- Amendments Made --> A17{Controller Decision}
    A16 -- No Response --> A18[Application Abandoned]
    A17 -- Accepted --> A19[Step 7: Patent Granted Certificate Issued]
    A17 -- Objections Remain --> A20[Hearing Scheduled Form 14]
    A20 -- Resolved --> A19
    A20 -- Not Resolved --> A21[Application Refused Appeal to IPAB High Court]
    A19 --> A22[Patent Number Published in Patent Journal]
    A22 --> A23{Renewal Fees from 8th Year}
    A23 -- Paid --> A24[Patent in Force up to 20 Years]
    A23 -- Not Paid --> A25[Patent Lapse Grace Period 6 Months Surcharge]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — IP Protection Architecture for a Medical Device

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CORE[MEDICAL DEVICE INNOVATION ECOSYSTEM]
        B1[Core Invention: Novel Pacemaker Lead] --> B2[Patent Protection 20 Years]
        B1 --> B3[Trade Secret: Manufacturing Process Knowhow]
        B1 --> B4[Copyright: Embedded Firmware Source Code]
        B1 --> B5[Trademark: Brand Name Logo]
        B1 --> B6[Industrial Design: Ergonomic Casing Shape]
    end

    subgraph REG[REGULATORY AND COMMERCIAL LAYER]
        B2 --> C1[FDA 510k Clearance]
        B2 --> C2[CE Marking EU MDR]
        B2 --> C3[CDSCO India Registration]
        B5 --> C4[Market Recognition and Anti Counterfeit]
    end

    subgraph ENF[ENFORCEMENT AND MONETIZATION]
        B2 --> D1[Licensing to OEMs Royalty Income]
        B2 --> D2[Cross Licensing with Competitors]
        B2 --> D3[Infringement Litigation Cease and Desist]
        B3 --> D4[Confidentiality Agreements with Suppliers]
    end

    B1 --> E1[Patient Safety and Public Health Outcome]
    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    C4 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Architecture:**
> The first subgraph (CORE) shows that a single medical device innovation draws on **all five IP forms simultaneously**. The second subgraph (REG) demonstrates how IP interacts with regulatory approvals—patents do NOT replace FDA/CE/CDSCO clearances, but they create a competitive moat. The third subgraph (ENF) outlines the four major monetization/defense mechanisms. The terminal node `E1` shows the ultimate engineering-societal goal: a protected, regulated, monetized innovation that reaches patients safely.

### 4.3 Mermaid Timeline Diagram — Patent Lifecycle

```mermaid
gantt
    title Medical Device Patent Lifecycle (Indian Jurisdiction)
    dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD
    axisFormat %Y

    section Pre Filing
    Invention and Prior Art Search    :done, 2024-01-01, 60d
    Drafting Specification            :active, 2024-03-01, 90d

    section Filing to Publication
    File Patent Application          :crit, 2024-06-01, 1d
    Publication 18 Months            :2025-12-01, 1d

    section Examination
    Request Examination Form 18     :2025-12-15, 1d
    Substantive Examination          :2026-01-01, 365d
    Response to FER                  :2026-12-01, 180d

    section Grant and Renewal
    Patent Granted                   :milestone, 2027-12-01, 1d
    First Renewal 8th Year           :milestone, 2032-06-01, 1d
    Patent Expiry 20 Years           :milestone, 2044-06-01, 1d
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Intellectual Property. List the four major forms of IP protection relevant to biomedical devices with one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Intellectual Property (IP) is the legal recognition of exclusive rights over intangible creations of the mind. The four major forms relevant to biomedical devices are:
1. **Patent** — protects inventions; e.g., a novel drug-eluting coronary stent (20-year term).
2. **Trademark** — protects brand identity; e.g., the "Medtronic" wordmark and logo on a pacemaker generator.
3. **Copyright** — protects software and literary works; e.g., the source code of ECG analysis software.
4. **Trade Secret** — protects confidential know-how; e.g., proprietary silicone bonding process for a cochlear implant.
*(Award 0.5 marks for definition + 0.5 marks per correct IP type with example = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. State the three patentability criteria under the Indian Patents Act, 1970, with one biomedical example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Under Section 2(1)(j) and 2(1)(l)/(ja)/(ac):
1. **Novelty** — the invention must not be anticipated by any prior art globally. *Example:* A new nano-coating for catheters not described in any publication.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — must not be obvious to a person skilled in the art. *Example:* Combining an accelerometer with a defibrillator to predict falls is non-obvious; simply adding Bluetooth is obvious.
3. **Industrial Application** — must be capable of being made or used in an industry. *Example:* A surgical stapler with a working prototype demonstrating anastomosis.
*(1 mark per criterion with valid biomedical example = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — Module 4 Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for obtaining a patent in India under the Indian Patents Act, 1970. Mention all relevant forms, fees, and statutory deadlines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The patent process in India follows a **seven-stage procedure**:

**Stage 1: Invention Disclosure & Prior Art Search**
- Document the invention in a witnessed lab notebook.
- Conduct prior art search using **IPAS (Indian Patent Advanced Search System)**, **Espacenet**, and **Google Patents**.
- Verify the three patentability criteria: novelty, inventive step, industrial application.

**Stage 2: Drafting the Specification**
- File **Form 1** (Application), **Form 2** (Provisional or Complete Specification), **Form 5** (Declaration of Inventorship), and **Form 26** (Power of Attorney, if agent is engaged).
- Specification must include: title, field, background, summary, brief description of drawings, detailed description (enabling disclosure), claims, and abstract (≤150 words).
- **Provisional specification** locks priority date for **12 months** during which the complete specification must be filed.

**Stage 3: Filing**
- File online via the IPO e-filing portal or physically at one of the 4 patent offices.
- **Date of filing** = priority date. Acknowledgment and Application Number issued.
- Filing fee ranges from ₹**1,600 (student/individual)** to ₹**8,000 (large entity)**.

**Stage 4: Publication (Section 11A)**
- Automatic publication in the **Patent Office Journal after 18 months**.
- Early publication request via **Form 9** (₹2,500) reduces wait to **~1 month**.
- Provisional rights accrue from publication date.

**Stage 5: Request for Examination**
- File **Form 18** within **48 months** of priority date.
- Without this, the application is **deemed abandoned**.

**Stage 6: Substantive Examination**
- Examiner issues **First Examination Report (FER)** with objections.
- Applicant responds with **Form 8** (amendments/arguments) within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months).
- Hearing under **Form 14** if objections persist.

**Stage 7: Grant of Patent**
- **Controller of Patents** accepts the application.
- Patent number published in the Journal; **Certificate of Patent** issued.
- First renewal due in the **8th year**; subsequent renewals annual.
- Term: **20 years** from filing date.

*(Allocate 1 mark per stage with form/deadline = 7 marks)*

---

**Q.A (b)** Discuss the importance of intellectual property protection in the medical device industry. What are the special IP challenges specific to software-enabled medical devices? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Importance of IP in Medical Devices (3.5 marks):**
1. **R&D Cost Recovery:** Development of a Class III device costs $50–100M and 5+ years. Patents provide 20 years of exclusivity to recoup investment.
2. **Investor Attraction:** VCs and grants require demonstrable IP portfolios.
3. **Market Exclusivity:** Combined with regulatory approvals (FDA 510(k), CE Mark, CDSCO), patents create a "regulatory moat."
4. **Licensing Revenue:** Universities like APJ KTU can license patented student innovations to industry for royalty income.
5. **Anti-Counterfeiting:** Trademarked devices prevent dangerous look-alike counterfeits.
6. **Cross-Border Trade:** PCT and Paris Convention filings protect innovations in 150+ countries.

**IP Challenges in Software-Enabled Medical Devices (3.5 marks):**
1. **Software Patent Eligibility:** Pure algorithms are excluded under **Section 3(k)** of the Indian Patents Act, 1970. However, software producing a **technical effect in a medical device** (e.g., AI-based arrhythmia detection) is patentable. The test is **"technical contribution"** beyond mere mathematical method.
2. **Copyright vs. Patent Overlap:** Source code is copyrighted automatically; but the underlying method patented separately.
3. **Open-Source Dependencies:** Many medical device firmware use Linux or RTOS—licensing terms (GPL, MIT) impose IP obligations.
4. **Trade Secret Leakage:** Employee turnover in software-heavy startups risks trade secret exposure (e.g., proprietary ML model weights).
5. **AI/ML Model IP:** Black-box AI models are difficult to enable-disclose for patents (must reveal the architecture); model weights are best protected as trade secrets.
6. **Update Cycles:** Continuous software updates raise questions about continuation patents and whether updates infringe existing claims.

*(Allocate ~0.5 marks per point = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**Q.B (a)** Compare the three principal patent filing routes (Direct National, Paris Convention, and PCT) for a biomedical startup. Recommend the most suitable route for a KTU-incubated medical device startup targeting both the Indian and European markets. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Direct National (India) | Paris Convention | PCT (WIPO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Time to Decision | 4–6 years | Varies per country | 30/31 months deferral |
| Geographic Scope | India only | Multiple countries | 150+ countries |
| Best For | India-only product | 2–5 key markets | Global commercialization |

*(Use `vert` or `mid` for any absolute values to avoid markdown breaking.)*

**Recommendation (3 marks):**
For a **KTU-incubated startup** targeting **India and the EU** with a **novel diagnostic device**:

1. **Recommended Route: PCT followed by National Phase Entry in India + EPO.**
2. **Rationale:**
   - **Phase 1:** File a **PCT application** at WIPO (with India as receiving office) within **12 months** of any provisional filing. This provides **30/31 months** of deferral.
   - **Phase 2:** Use this 30-month window to conduct **market validation, regulatory planning (CE Mark under EU MDR, CDSCO registration)**, and secure **investor funding**.
   - **Phase 3:** Enter **national phase** in India (direct, no translation) and at the **European Patent Office (EPO)** with a validated European patent.
3. **Why not direct national?** A 4–6 year examination in India would be wasteful if the startup's primary market is the EU.
4. **Why not Paris Convention alone?** Paris requires filing in each country within 12 months, causing immediate cost pressure; PCT allows strategic deferral.

*(1 mark comparison + 2 marks recommendation logic = 7 marks)*

---

**Q.B (b)** What is the compulsory licensing provision in India? Under what conditions can the Controller of Patents grant a compulsory license for a medical device patent? Discuss with reference to the **NATCO v. Bayer (Nexavar)** judgment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A **compulsory license (CL)** is a statutory authorization granted by the **Controller of Patents** allowing a third party to manufacture, sell, or import a patented invention **without the patent holder's consent**, subject to royalty payment. It is enshrined in **Section 84 of the Indian Patents Act, 1970**, derived from **TRIPS Article 31**.

**Conditions for Grant (3.5 marks):**
The Controller may grant a CL if **any of the following** is satisfied:
1. The **reasonable requirements of the public** have not been met with respect to the patented invention.
2. The patented invention is **not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price**.
3. The patented invention is **not worked in the territory of India** (3 years after grant).
4. The applicant has attempted to **negotiate a voluntary license** with the patentee on reasonable commercial terms and failed (typically within 6 months).

Additional conditions: The CL is **non-exclusive**, **non-transferable** (except with the business), and the licensee must **pay royalty** to the patentee.

**NATCO v. Bayer (Nexavar, 2012) Case Study (2 marks):**
- **Patent:** Bayer's Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar) — kidney/liver cancer drug.
- **Issue:** Bayer priced Nexavar at ₹2.8 lakh/month, unaffordable for Indian patients.
- **Application:** Natco Pharma applied for CL citing non-availability at affordable price.
- **Decision:** The Controller granted the **first-ever Indian CL** in March 2012, fixing royalty at **6% of net sales** to Bayer.
- **Principles laid down:**
  - Patentees have a **"privilege, not a right"** to price arbitrarily.
  - Affordability is a key public interest consideration.
  - Adequate compensation to the patent holder is still mandated.
- **KTU relevance:** This case is **landmark** for medical device startups dealing with **life-saving but expensive innovations** (e.g., advanced prosthetics, gene therapy vectors).

*(1.5 + 3.5 + 2 = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing patent TERM (20 years) with RENEWAL schedule** — students often say "renewal starts at year 1" (WRONG: first renewal is mandatory only in the 8th year).
> 2. **Mixing up Indian and US patent terms** — US used to have 17 years from grant; now both are 20 years from filing, but students cite outdated info.
> 3. **Forgetting "methods of treatment" exclusion in India** — medical device method claims are patentable, but treating a disease by a method is NOT.
> 4. **Citing 35 USC or 21 CFR (US-specific) for KTU exams** — KTU expects Indian Patents Act references.
> 5. **Omitting form numbers** — always mention Form 1, 2, 5, 18, 26, 27 by number.
> 6. **Missing the 48-month examination request deadline** — this is the most commonly missed hard cutoff.
> 7. **Conflating copyright (automatic) with patent (requires grant)** — students often write "my code is patented" when they mean copyrighted.
> 8. **Not distinguishing between FER response timeline (6 months) and grace period** — these are separate concepts.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — IP Protection & Patent Process**

- **IP** = intangible creations of the mind; for medical devices, it primarily means **patents + trademarks + trade secrets + copyright + designs**.
- **Patent = 20 years** from filing date in India; **first renewal in 8th year**; subsequent annual.
- **3 Patentability Pillars:** Novelty + Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness) + Industrial Application.
- **Methods of treatment** of humans/animals are **NOT patentable** in India (Section 3(i)); devices USED in such methods ARE patentable.
- **Pure software/algorithms** are excluded (Section 3(k)) but **software producing a technical effect** in a medical device IS patentable.
- **Key Forms:** Form 1 (Application), Form 2 (Specification), Form 5 (Inventorship), Form 9 (Early Publication), Form 18 (Request Examination — within 48 months), Form 26 (Power of Attorney), Form 27 (Working Statement, annual).
- **Statutory Deadlines to Memorize:**
  - **12 months** — Paris Convention priority / India grace period for pre-filing disclosure.
  - **18 months** — automatic publication.
  - **6 months** — FER response (extendable by 3 months).
  - **30/31 months** — PCT national phase entry.
  - **48 months** — Request for Examination deadline.
- **Three Patent Routes:** Direct National (India only, cheapest), Paris Convention (12-month priority across select countries), PCT (30-month deferral, 150+ countries, global commercialization).
- **Compulsory License (Section 84):** Triggered by non-working, unaffordable pricing, or unmet public demand; landmark case **NATCO v. Bayer (Nexavar, 2012)** established 6% royalty benchmark.
- **PCT is the gold standard** for KTU-incubated medical device startups targeting global markets — defer national costs, validate the market, then enter national phase.
- **Trade secrets protect indefinitely** if confidentiality is maintained — useful for manufacturing know-how, ML model weights, and proprietary polymer formulations.
- **Trademarks prevent counterfeiting** — critical for patient safety in medical devices.
- **First-to-file system** in India (not first-to-invent like old US law) — file EARLY.
- **Always conduct a prior art search** using IPAS, Espacenet, and Google Patents before investing in drafting.
- **Working Statement (Form 27)** is an annual obligation — non-filing is a punishable offence under the Act.
- **Examiner = "person skilled in the art" (PSA)** — the legal benchmark for non-obviousness.
- **Section 3(d) anti-evergreening** provision — incremental modifications of known substances (e.g., new polymorphic forms of existing drugs) face heightened scrutiny; this is less restrictive for medical **devices** but relevant for drug-device combination products.
